## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the bias column $x_0 = 1$ in the design matrix $X$.** The Normal Equation $\theta = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y$ requires the augmented matrix. Many students submit a matrix missing the leading 1s, losing 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing the cost factor $\frac{1}{2m}$ with $\frac{1}{m}$ or $\frac{1}{2n}$.** The denominator is the number of training examples $m$, not the number of features $n$. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Writing the gradient update as $\theta_j := \theta_j + \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ (positive sign).** This moves *uphill* (maximization), not *downhill* (minimization). Always use the **minus** sign.
>
> 4. **Asynchronous parameter updates.** Update *all* $\theta_j$ from old values, then assign simultaneously. In-place sequential updates break convergence guarantees.
>
> 5. **Claiming that Gradient Descent always gives the "exact" answer.** It gives an *approximate* optimum within numerical tolerance $\epsilon$. Only the Normal Equation gives a *closed-form* exact solution.
>
> 6. **Not stating the invertibility assumption** of $X^{T}X$ in the Normal Equation. Examiners expect you to mention this assumption and its failure modes.
>
> 7. **Confusing the learning rate $\alpha$ with regularization parameter $\lambda$.** They are different hyperparameters: $\alpha$ controls step size in gradient descent; $\lambda$ controls penalty strength in regularized regression.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Hypothesis (vectorized):** $h_\theta(x) = \theta^{T} x$ with $x_0 = 1$ implicit.
- **Cost Function (MSE):** $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$. The $\frac{1}{2}$ is for cleaner calculus; it does not change the optimum.
- **Cost Function (vectorized):** $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} (X\theta - y)^{T}(X\theta - y)$.
- **Gradient (vectorized):** $\nabla_\theta J = \frac{1}{m} X^{T}(X\theta - y)$.
- **Gradient Descent Update:** $\theta := \theta - \frac{\alpha}{m} X^{T}(X\theta - y)$. **Always** simultaneous.
- **Normal Equation:** $\theta = (X^{T} X)^{-1} X^{T} y$. Closed-form, no $\alpha$, no scaling, no iterations.
- **Learning Rate:** Small $\alpha \Rightarrow$ slow; Large $\alpha \Rightarrow$ diverge; just right $\Rightarrow$ monotonic convergence.
- **Feature Scaling (Z-score):** $x_j := (x_j - \mu_j) / s_j$. Accelerates gradient descent.
- **Convergence Criterion:** $\vert J(\theta)^{(t)} - J(\theta)^{(t-1)} \vert \lt 10^{-6}$.
- **Non-Invertibility Causes:** Redundant (linearly dependent) features, or $n \ge m$.
- **Remedy for Non-Invertibility:** Delete redundant features, OR use **regularization** (Ridge: add $\lambda I$ to $X^{T}X$).
- **Polynomial Features:** $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x + \theta_2 x^2 + \theta_3 x^3$. Model is *linear in $\theta$* but *nonlinear in $x$*.
- **Trade-off Table:** Use Normal Equation when $n \le 10^3$; use Gradient Descent (or SGD) when $n \ge 10^4$.
- **Convexity Guarantee:** $J(\theta)$ is convex because its Hessian $\frac{1}{m} X^{T} X$ is positive semi-definite. Hence gradient descent converges to the **global** minimum.
- **KTU Board Pattern:** Always show *all matrix transposes and dimensions* explicitly; examiners award partial credit for clean, well-labeled steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification - Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification - Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification — The Formal Definition

In **Supervised Machine Learning**, problems are divided into two major families:

- **Regression** — predicting a continuous real-valued output (e.g., house price, temperature).
- **Classification** — predicting a discrete categorical label (e.g., spam / not-spam, malignant / benign, pass / fail).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification (KTU Definition):** A supervised learning task in which the target variable $y$ takes values from a small, discrete, and finite set of categories, and the goal is to learn a mapping $h_\theta(x): \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \{0, 1, \dots, K\}$ that assigns each input vector to its most probable class label.

### 1.1.1 Types of Classification Problems

| Type | Number of Classes | Example |
|------|-------------------|---------|
| **Binary Classification** | 2 (e.g., 0 / 1) | Email → Spam or Ham |
| **Multiclass Classification** | $> 2$, mutually exclusive | Digit recognition (0–9) |
| **Multi-label Classification** | $> 2$, not mutually exclusive | Movie genre tagging |

## 1.2 Why Linear Regression Fails for Classification

A student often asks: *"Can I just use linear regression and threshold at 0.5?"* The answer is **no**, and here is why:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. Linear regression produces continuous outputs $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, not bounded probabilities.
> 2. Outliers can drastically shift the regression line, ruining the decision boundary.
> 3. There is no probabilistic interpretation of the output as $P(y=1 \mid x)$.

This is where **Logistic Regression** comes in — despite its name, it is a **classification algorithm**, not a regression algorithm.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Sigmoid Squeeze

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine squeezing a water pipe. The water pressure (raw linear score $z = \theta^T x$) can be very high, but a *throttle valve* (the **sigmoid function**) compresses it into the range $(0, 1)$. This compressed value is a *probability*. Logistic Regression is essentially Linear Regression *plugged into* a probability-squashing throttle.

Formally, Linear Regression gives us a real number $z \in \mathbb{R}$, and we map it through the **Logistic (Sigmoid) Function** to obtain a probability in $(0, 1)$.

## 1.4 The Logistic (Sigmoid) Function

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

where $z = \theta^T x$ is the linear combination of inputs and parameters.

### Key Properties of $\sigma(z)$

- **Range:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ for all $z \in \mathbb{R}$ — never reaches 0 or 1 exactly.
- **Monotonically increasing** and **S-shaped (sigmoidal)**.
- **Symmetric about the origin:** $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$.
- **Derivative (very useful for gradient descent):**

$$
\frac{d}{dz}\sigma(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)
$$

## 1.5 The Hypothesis Representation

For binary classification where $y \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$
h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}
$$

Interpretation:

$$
h_\theta(x) = P(y = 1 \mid x; \theta)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output $h_\theta(x)$ is the **estimated probability that $y = 1$** given input $x$ and parameters $\theta$. Consequently, $P(y = 0 \mid x; \theta) = 1 - h_\theta(x)$.

**The decision rule** becomes:

$$
\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } h_\theta(x) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{if } h_\theta(x) < 0.5 \end{cases}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decision Boundary — The Geometric Heart of Logistic Regression

The decision boundary is the locus where the hypothesis is exactly 0.5, i.e., where $\sigma(z) = 0.5 \Rightarrow z = 0$.

Therefore, the **decision boundary** is the curve (or hyperplane) defined by:

$$
\theta^T x = 0
$$

### 2.1.1 Linear Decision Boundary Example

Suppose $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2)$, and we learn $\theta = [-3, 1, 1]^T$.

The boundary is: $-3 + x_1 + x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 + x_2 = 3$.

This is a **straight line** in 2D — a **linear classifier**.

### 2.1.2 Non-Linear Decision Boundary

By using polynomial features, we can produce curved boundaries:

$$
\theta^T x = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \theta_3 x_1^2 + \theta_4 x_2^2
$$

If $\theta = [-1, 0, 0, 1, 1]^T$, the boundary is $x_1^2 + x_2^2 = 1$, a **circle**.

> [!TIP]
> The decision boundary is a property of the **hypothesis and parameters**, **not** of the training set. Once $\theta$ is fixed, the boundary is fixed.

## 2.2 Cost Function — Why MSE Fails

Using the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with the sigmoid produces a **non-convex** cost function riddled with local minima — gradient descent cannot guarantee global convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solution:** Logistic Regression uses the **Log-Loss (Cross-Entropy) cost function**, which is convex for logistic regression, guaranteeing a single global minimum.

The cost for a **single training example** is:

$$
\text{Cost}(h_\theta(x), y) = \begin{cases} -\log(h_\theta(x)) & \text{if } y = 1 \\ -\log(1 - h_\theta(x)) & \text{if } y = 0 \end{cases}
$$

### Intuition Behind the Log-Loss

- If $y = 1$ and $h_\theta(x) \to 1$ → cost $\to 0$ (happy!).
- If $y = 1$ and $h_\theta(x) \to 0$ → cost $\to +\infty$ (penalize heavily!).
- If $y = 0$ and $h_\theta(x) \to 0$ → cost $\to 0$.
- If $y = 0$ and $h_\theta(x) \to 1$ → cost $\to +\infty$.

This is the **maximum likelihood principle** in disguise.

## 2.3 The Simplified Cost Function

Combining both cases into one elegant expression:

$$
\text{Cost}(h_\theta(x), y) = -y \log\bigl(h_\theta(x)\bigr) - (1 - y) \log\bigl(1 - h_\theta(x)\bigr)
$$

The **total cost function** $J(\theta)$ over $m$ training examples:

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log\bigl(h_\theta(x^{(i)})\bigr) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log\bigl(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})\bigr) \right]
$$

## 2.4 Gradient Descent Update Rule

The same update rule as linear regression, **but with a different $h_\theta(x)$**:

$$
\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)
$$

Computing the partial derivative (using the sigmoid derivative identity), we get:

$$
\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **remarkable similarity** to the linear regression update rule! The *only* difference is the definition of $h_\theta(x)$ — for linear regression $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$, but for logistic regression $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula** |
|------------|-------------|-------------|
| $\sigma(z)$ | Sigmoid function | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ |
| $h_\theta(x)$ | Hypothesis (probability of $y=1$) | $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$ |
| Decision rule | Class label | $\hat{y} = 1$ if $h_\theta(x) \geq 0.5$, else $0$ |
| Decision boundary | Locus of 0.5 probability | $\theta^T x = 0$ |
| Cost (single) | Log-loss | $-y \log(h) - (1-y)\log(1-h)$ |
| Cost (total) | Mean cross-entropy | $-\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\bigl[y^{(i)}\log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h^{(i)})\bigr]$ |
| Gradient | Parameter update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h^{(i)} - y^{(i)})\,x_j^{(i)}$ |
| Sigmoid derivative | Used in backprop | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ |
| Regularization | L2 penalty term | $\dfrac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^2$ |
| One-vs-All | Multiclass extension | Train $K$ binary classifiers; pick max $h_\theta^{(k)}(x)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse:** Logistic regression is a **discriminative** classifier. It directly models $P(y \mid x)$, in contrast to **generative** classifiers (e.g., Naive Bayes, GDA) which model $P(x \mid y)$ and $P(y)$.

## 2.6 Multiclass Classification — One-vs-All (One-vs-Rest)

When $y \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ with $K > 2$:

1. For each class $k \in \{1, \dots, K\}$, train a binary classifier $h_\theta^{(k)}(x)$ treating class $k$ as positive and the rest as negative.
2. For a new input $x$, predict the class with the highest probability:

$$
\hat{y} = \underset{k}{\arg\max}\; h_\theta^{(k)}(x)
$$

## 2.7 Regularization — Preventing Overfitting

To prevent high-variance (overfitting), add a penalty term to the cost:

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h^{(i)})\right] + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^2
$$

**Convention:** Penalty is applied to $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$ but **not** to the bias $\theta_0$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Sigmoid Function from Log-Odds

### Setup

The **odds** of an event with probability $p$ is defined as $\dfrac{p}{1-p}$. The **log-odds** (or logit) is:

$$
\text{logit}(p) = \log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)
$$

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Assume the log-odds is a linear function of $x$:**

$$
\log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \theta^T x
$$

**Step 2 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$
\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\theta^T x}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $p$ (which is $h_\theta(x)$):**

$$
p = (1 - p)\, e^{\theta^T x}
$$

$$
p = e^{\theta^T x} - p\, e^{\theta^T x}
$$

$$
p\,(1 + e^{\theta^T x}) = e^{\theta^T x}
$$

$$
p = \frac{e^{\theta^T x}}{1 + e^{\theta^T x}}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{-\theta^T x}$:**

$$
p = \frac{1}{e^{-\theta^T x} + 1} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Logistic regression is therefore the model obtained by **inverting the logit** of a linear predictor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Cross-Entropy Cost Function via Maximum Likelihood

### Setup

Assume the $m$ training examples are **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)**. The probability of correctly classifying all examples is the **likelihood**:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \theta)
$$

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Write the per-example probability:**

Since $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$, we use the Bernoulli distribution:

$$
P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \theta) = \bigl(h_\theta(x^{(i)})\bigr)^{y^{(i)}} \cdot \bigl(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})\bigr)^{(1 - y^{(i)})}
$$

(Check: if $y^{(i)} = 1$, this reduces to $h_\theta(x^{(i)})$; if $y^{(i)} = 0$, it reduces to $1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})$.)

**Step 2 — Form the likelihood:**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \bigl(h^{(i)}\bigr)^{y^{(i)}} \bigl(1 - h^{(i)}\bigr)^{(1 - y^{(i)})}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural log (log-likelihood, easier to maximize):**

$$
\ell(\theta) = \log \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a minimization problem (negate):**

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\ell(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h^{(i)}) \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Cross-entropy loss is the **negative log-likelihood** of the data under a Bernoulli assumption. This is why it is the *natural* loss for binary classification.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gradient Descent Update

We need $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$.

### Step-by-step

**Step 1 — Differentiate one term of the loss:**

Let $h = \sigma(z)$ and $z = \theta^T x$. The chain rule gives:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}\bigl[-y\log h - (1-y)\log(1-h)\bigr]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply log-derivative $\frac{d}{dh}\log h = \frac{1}{h}$:**

$$
= -y \cdot \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j} - (1-y) \cdot \frac{1}{1-h} \cdot \left(-\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Use sigmoid derivative $\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j} = h(1-h)\,x_j$:**

$$
= -y(1-h)\,x_j + (1-y)\,h\,x_j
$$

**Step 4 — Factor:**

$$
= \bigl[-y + yh + h - yh\bigr] x_j = (h - y)\,x_j
$$

**Step 5 — Average over all $m$ examples:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\bigl(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\bigr)\,x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 6 — Update rule:**

$$
\boxed{\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\bigl(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\bigr)\,x_j^{(i)}}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

Consider a **toy dataset** for passing an exam based on hours studied.

| Hours ($x_1$) | Pass ($y$) |
|---------------|------------|
| 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 |
| 3 | 1 |
| 4 | 1 |

Suppose we initialize $\theta_0 = -3$, $\theta_1 = 1$. Compute one gradient descent step with $\alpha = 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Compute predictions for each example** using $h(x) = \sigma(\theta_0 + \theta_1 x)$:

- $x=1$: $h = \sigma(-3 + 1) = \sigma(-2) = 0.119$
- $x=2$: $h = \sigma(-3 + 2) = \sigma(-1) = 0.269$
- $x=3$: $h = \sigma(-3 + 3) = \sigma(0) = 0.500$
- $x=4$: $h = \sigma(-3 + 4) = \sigma(1) = 0.731$

**Step 2 — Compute error $(h - y)$:**

- $0.119 - 0 = 0.119$
- $0.269 - 0 = 0.269$
- $0.500 - 1 = -0.500$
- $0.731 - 1 = -0.269$

**Step 3 — Compute gradient for $\theta_0$** (where $x_0 = 1$):

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{4}(0.119 + 0.269 - 0.500 - 0.269) = \frac{1}{4}(-0.381) = -0.095
$$

**Step 4 — Compute gradient for $\theta_1$** (using $x_1$ values):

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl[(0.119)(1) + (0.269)(2) + (-0.500)(3) + (-0.269)(4)\bigr]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{4}(0.119 + 0.538 - 1.500 - 1.076) = \frac{1}{4}(-1.919) = -0.480
$$

**Step 5 — Update parameters:**

$$
\theta_0 := -3 - 0.1 \times (-0.095) = -2.9905
$$

$$
\theta_1 := 1 - 0.1 \times (-0.480) = 1.048
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both parameters increased, indicating that the model correctly learned to push the sigmoid rightward (more hours → higher pass probability).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Logistic Regression Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Features x] --> B[Linear Combination z = thetaT x]
    B --> C[Sigmoid Function sigma z]
    C --> D[Probability h theta x in 0 1]
    D --> E{Decision Threshold 0.5}
    E -- Greater or Equal --> F[Predicted Class y hat equals 1]
    E -- Less than --> G[Predicted Class y hat equals 0]
    D --> H[Compare with True Label y]
    H --> I[Cross Entropy Loss J theta]
    I --> J[Gradient Computation partial J partial theta]
    J --> K[Update theta j]
    K --> B
    I --> L{Converged}
    L -- Yes --> M[Final Classifier]
    L -- No --> J
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Geometric Intuition of Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feature Space R2] --> B[Linear Boundary thetaT x equals 0]
    A --> C[Nonlinear Boundary Polynomial Features]
    B --> D[Class 1 Region]
    B --> E[Class 0 Region]
    C --> F[Circular Region]
    C --> G[Outside Region]
    D --> H[Classify x as 1]
    E --> I[Classify x as 0]
    F --> H
    G --> I
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — One-vs-All Multiclass Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multiclass Input with K classes] --> B[Class 1 vs Rest]
    A --> C[Class 2 vs Rest]
    A --> D[Class K vs Rest]
    B --> E[Classifier h1 x]
    C --> F[Classifier h2 x]
    D --> G[Classifier hK x]
    E --> H[Compute All Probabilities]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[argmax over k]
    I --> J[Final Predicted Class]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Convexity of Log-Loss vs Non-Convexity of MSE on Sigmoid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Error Surface for theta] --> B[Using Mean Squared Error]
    A --> C[Using Cross Entropy Loss]
    B --> D[Non Convex Surface]
    C --> E[Convex Surface Bowl Shaped]
    D --> F[Many Local Minima]
    E --> G[Single Global Minimum Guaranteed]
    F --> H[Gradient Descent Unreliable]
    G --> I[Gradient Descent Converges]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Cost Function Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[theta parameter vector] --> B[Compute h theta x for all m]
    B --> C[Compute log h term]
    B --> D[Compute log 1 minus h term]
    C --> E[Multiply by y]
    D --> F[Multiply by 1 minus y]
    E --> G[Sum and Negate]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Add Regularization Term]
    H --> I[Average over m]
    I --> J[Output J theta]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw or describe the architecture of logistic regression, always emphasize the **two-stage** nature — first a linear transformation, then a nonlinear squashing function. This is the essence of a **Generalized Linear Model (GLM)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Define the sigmoid function. Why is it used in logistic regression instead of the step function?**

**Model Answer:**

The sigmoid function is defined as:

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

It maps any real input $z \in \mathbb{R}$ to the open interval $(0, 1)$, producing a smooth, differentiable, and strictly increasing curve.

> The **step function** is **not** used in logistic regression because it is not differentiable at $z = 0$, which makes gradient-based optimization impossible. The sigmoid, in contrast, is **smoothly differentiable everywhere**, which allows us to use **gradient descent** to learn the parameters $\theta$. Additionally, its output is interpretable as a probability.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**What is the decision boundary in logistic regression? Write its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer:**

The **decision boundary** is the surface in the feature space that separates regions classified as $y = 1$ from those classified as $y = 0$. It corresponds to the locus where the hypothesis equals $0.5$.

Since $\sigma(z) = 0.5$ if and only if $z = 0$, the decision boundary is given by:

$$
\theta^T x = 0
$$

> It is a property of the **hypothesis parameters** $\theta$, not of the training data. With polynomial features, this can be a circle, ellipse, or any other shape.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the cost function used in logistic regression from the maximum likelihood principle. (7 Marks)**

**(b) For a binary classification problem with the following data, compute one step of gradient descent. Initialize $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$. (7 Marks)**

| $x_1$ | $y$ |
|-------|-----|
| 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 1 |
| 5 | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the cost function:**

The likelihood of observing the dataset under the Bernoulli model is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \bigl(h_\theta(x^{(i)})\bigr)^{y^{(i)}} \bigl(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})\bigr)^{(1 - y^{(i)})}
$$

Taking log:

$$
\log \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]
$$

To convert to a minimization, we negate and average:

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]
$$

> This is the **cross-entropy loss**, derived rigorously from the principle of maximum likelihood.

**(b) One step of gradient descent:**

With $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$, we have $z = 0$ for all examples, hence $h = \sigma(0) = 0.5$ for all.

**Compute error $(h - y)$ for each example:**

- $x=1, y=0$: error = $0.5 - 0 = 0.5$
- $x=3, y=1$: error = $0.5 - 1 = -0.5$
- $x=5, y=1$: error = $0.5 - 1 = -0.5$

**Gradient for $\theta_0$ (with $x_0 = 1$):**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{3}(0.5 - 0.5 - 0.5) = \frac{-0.5}{3} \approx -0.1667
$$

**Gradient for $\theta_1$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{3}\bigl[(0.5)(1) + (-0.5)(3) + (-0.5)(5)\bigr] = \frac{1}{3}(0.5 - 1.5 - 2.5) = \frac{-3.5}{3} \approx -1.1667
$$

**Update parameters:**

$$
\theta_0 := 0 - 0.1 \times (-0.1667) = 0.01667
$$

$$
\theta_1 := 0 - 0.1 \times (-1.1667) = 0.11667
$$

> After the update, $\theta_1$ has become positive, meaning the model has learned that higher $x_1$ values correlate with class $y = 1$, exactly as expected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) Explain the One-vs-All approach for multiclass classification using logistic regression. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the problem of overfitting in logistic regression. Derive the regularized cost function. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) One-vs-All (One-vs-Rest) Approach:**

When the target variable has $K > 2$ classes, we reduce the multiclass problem into $K$ independent binary classification problems.

**Algorithm:**

1. For each class $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$, train a separate logistic regression classifier $h_\theta^{(k)}(x)$ that treats class $k$ as the positive class ($y = 1$) and **all other classes** as the negative class ($y = 0$).
2. This produces $K$ probability estimates.
3. For a new input $x$, the predicted class is the one with the **highest probability**:

$$
\hat{y} = \underset{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}}{\arg\max}\; h_\theta^{(k)}(x)
$$

**Example:** For digit recognition with $K = 10$ (digits 0 to 9), we train 10 classifiers. A new image is assigned to the digit whose classifier outputs the highest probability.

> This method is simple, scalable, and works well in practice, though it assumes the classes are **mutually exclusive**.

**(b) Overfitting and Regularization:**

**Overfitting** occurs when the model learns the training data too well — including its noise — leading to poor generalization on unseen test data. Symptoms include a very complex, jagged decision boundary that fits every training point.

**Mitigation — L2 Regularization:**

We add a penalty proportional to the squared magnitude of the parameters to the cost function:

$$
J_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h^{(i)})\right] + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^2
$$

> The parameter $\lambda$ (regularization strength) controls the trade-off:
> - **Large $\lambda$** → underfitting (high bias, simple model).
> - **Small $\lambda$** → overfitting (high variance, complex model).
> - **Just right $\lambda$** → good generalization.

The penalty is **not** applied to the bias term $\theta_0$. The corresponding gradient update for $j \geq 1$ becomes:

$$
\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \left[ \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\theta_j \right]
$$

> This effectively shrinks $\theta_j$ towards zero, simplifying the model.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing cost function with loss function** — the cost $J(\theta)$ is the *average* over all $m$ examples, whereas the loss is for *one* example. The $\frac{1}{m}$ factor is **mandatory** in the cost.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the log-loss — without it, you are *maximizing* the wrong quantity, and the model trains in the wrong direction.
>
> 3. **Applying linear regression update rule blindly** — many students write the update rule of linear regression (with $h = \theta^T x$) inside logistic regression problems. The correct $h$ is $\sigma(\theta^T x)$, and graders *will* deduct marks.
>
> 4. **Missing the regularization term** in part (b) when part (a) asked for the regularized cost — this is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Using the step function** as the activation in derivation questions — always use the **sigmoid** because the problem demands differentiability for gradient descent.
>
> 6. **One-vs-All confusion** — many students write "One-vs-One" by mistake. The correct KTU terminology is **One-vs-All (OvA)** or **One-vs-Rest (OvR)**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Logistic Regression:**
>
> - **Logistic regression is a classification algorithm**, not a regression algorithm, despite its name.
>
> - The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ squashes the linear output to a probability in $(0, 1)$.
>
> - The **hypothesis** $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$ represents $P(y=1 \mid x; \theta)$.
>
> - **Decision rule:** predict $1$ if $h_\theta(x) \geq 0.5$, else $0$.
>
> - **Decision boundary:** the curve where $\theta^T x = 0$.
>
> - **Cost function** is the **cross-entropy / log-loss**, derived from the maximum likelihood of a Bernoulli distribution:
>   $$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h^{(i)})\right]$$
>
> - **Gradient descent update:**
>   $$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$$
>
> - **Sigmoid derivative** identity: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ — useful for derivations.
>
> - **Multiclass extension** uses **One-vs-All**: train $K$ classifiers, pick the one with max probability.
>
> - **Regularization** adds $\frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\theta_j^2$ to prevent overfitting.
>
> - **Convexity:** the cross-entropy cost is convex in $\theta$ — gradient descent is guaranteed to find the global minimum.
>
> - **Probabilistic interpretation:** Logistic regression is a **discriminative** model; it models $P(y \mid x)$ directly.
>
> - **Vectorized implementation** uses $\theta^T X$ for the linear part and the element-wise sigmoid for the squashing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naïve Bayes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Naïve Bayes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Naïve Bayes** is a probabilistic supervised machine learning classifier based on **Bayes' Theorem**, which applies the theorem with a strong (naïve) assumption of **conditional independence** between every pair of features given the class label. Despite this oversimplified assumption, it performs remarkably well in many real-world applications such as spam filtering, sentiment analysis, document classification, and medical diagnosis.

Mathematically, a Naïve Bayes classifier assigns an instance $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ to the class $c^*$ that maximizes the posterior probability:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "naïve" assumption states that the effect of a particular feature $x_i$ on the class $c$ is independent of the values of the other features. This is what makes the algorithm computationally tractable and the term "naïve".

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Doctor's Diagnosis**
>
> Imagine a doctor diagnosing whether a patient has the flu. The doctor considers multiple symptoms: fever, cough, body ache, and sore throat. Although in reality these symptoms are correlated (e.g., fever and body ache often occur together), the doctor, like a Naïve Bayes classifier, **treats each symptom independently** when calculating the probability of flu. The doctor asks:
> 1. "Out of 100 patients I see, how many have flu?" → **Prior probability $P(\text{Flu})$**
> 2. "Among those with flu, what fraction had a fever?" → **Likelihood $P(\text{Fever} \mid \text{Flu})$**
> 3. "Among those with flu, what fraction had a cough?" → **Likelihood $P(\text{Cough} \mid \text{Flu})$**
> 4. Multiply them all together with the prior.
>
> The class (Flu or Not Flu) with the highest combined probability wins — this is precisely the Naïve Bayes decision rule.

### 1.3 Historical Context

- Rooted in **Thomas Bayes'** work on inverse probability (1763).
- Popularized for text classification in the 1990s (e.g., spam filtering).
- Considered one of the simplest yet surprisingly accurate baseline classifiers in the ML practitioner's toolkit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundation: Bayes' Theorem

The classification rule is rooted in Bayes' theorem, which expresses the posterior in terms of the prior, likelihood, and evidence:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Where:
- $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ = **Posterior** — probability of class $c$ given the observed features.
- $P(c)$ = **Prior** — probability of class $c$ from training data.
- $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$ = **Likelihood** — probability of observing $\mathbf{x}$ given class $c$.
- $P(\mathbf{x})$ = **Evidence** — overall probability of the feature vector (constant for all classes, hence ignored during maximization).

### 2.2 The Naïve Independence Assumption

Computing $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid c)$ directly requires estimating a joint distribution over all feature combinations, which is infeasible for large $n$. The Naïve Bayes assumption states:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

This is the **conditional independence assumption**: each feature $x_i$ is conditionally independent of every other feature $x_j$ given the class $c$.

### 2.3 The Naïve Bayes Decision Rule

Substituting the independence assumption into Bayes' rule:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of $c$, it can be dropped from the maximization.

### 2.4 Laplace Smoothing (Zero-Frequency Problem)

If a feature value never co-occurs with a class in training, $P(x_i \mid c) = 0$, which zeroes out the entire product. To avoid this, **Laplace (add-1) smoothing** is applied:

$$P(x_i = v \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i = v, c) + 1}{\text{count}(c) + \vert V_i \vert}$$

Where $\vert V_i \vert$ is the number of distinct values feature $x_i$ can take.

### 2.5 Types of Naïve Bayes Classifiers

| **Variant** | **Data Type** | **Distribution Assumed** | **Common Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Multinomial NB** | Discrete counts (e.g., word frequencies) | Multinomial | Text classification, NLP |
| **Bernoulli NB** | Binary/Boolean features | Bernoulli (Multivariate) | Document classification (word presence/absence) |
| **Gaussian NB** | Continuous features | Gaussian (Normal) | Medical data, sensor data, iris dataset |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(c \vert \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(c) \, P(\mathbf{x} \vert c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Posterior in terms of prior, likelihood, evidence |
| Naïve Bayes Rule | $c^* = \arg\max_{c} \, P(c) \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$ | MAP decision rule |
| Conditional Independence | $P(x_1, \dots, x_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$ | The naïve assumption |
| Prior | $P(c) = \dfrac{N_c}{N}$ | $N_c$ = samples in class $c$, $N$ = total samples |
| Likelihood (Categorical) | $P(x_i = v \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(x_i = v, c)}{\text{count}(c)}$ | MLE estimate |
| Laplace Smoothing | $P(x_i = v \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(x_i = v, c) + 1}{\text{count}(c) + \vert V_i \vert}$ | Add-1 smoothing |
| Gaussian Likelihood | $P(x_i \mid c) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_c^2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x_i - \mu_c)^2}{2\sigma_c^2}\right)$ | For continuous features |
| Log-Sum Trick | $\log P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid c)$ | Avoids numerical underflow |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **NLP and Text Mining:** Spam detection, sentiment analysis, news categorization.
- **Bioinformatics:** Disease classification from gene expression data.
- **Cybersecurity:** Intrusion detection systems, malware classification.
- **Recommendation Systems:** User classification based on behavioral features.
- **Medical Diagnosis:** Predicting diseases from patient symptoms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Naïve Bayes Matters:** Even though the conditional independence assumption is often violated in real data, Naïve Bayes often yields competitive performance. It is extremely **fast**, requires **less training data**, handles **high-dimensional spaces** well, and is highly **interpretable** — making it a strong baseline in industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Naïve Bayes Decision Rule from First Principles

We start with the goal: assign a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ to the most probable class $c^* \in C$.

**Step 1: Posterior formulation.**
The optimal class (under 0-1 loss) is the one with the highest posterior probability:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 2: Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 3: Note that $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant** for all classes, so it does not affect the argmax. We get:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$$

**Step 4: Apply the chain rule of probability** to the joint likelihood:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid c, x_1) \cdot P(x_3 \mid c, x_1, x_2) \cdots$$

**Step 5: Apply the naïve conditional independence assumption.** All features are independent given the class:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) &= P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid c) \cdot P(x_3 \mid c) \cdots P(x_n \mid c) \\
&= \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Combine the prior and the product of likelihoods:**

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

This is the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Naïve Bayes decision rule**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: The Play Tennis Dataset

This is the **canonical KTU/University ML example** used in many board examinations.

**Training Data (14 days):**

| **Day** | **Outlook** | **Temperature** | **Humidity** | **Wind** | **Play Tennis** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1  | Sunny   | Hot   | High   | Weak   | No  |
| 2  | Sunny   | Hot   | High   | Strong | No  |
| 3  | Overcast| Hot   | High   | Weak   | Yes |
| 4  | Rain    | Mild  | High   | Weak   | Yes |
| 5  | Rain    | Cool  | Normal | Weak   | Yes |
| 6  | Rain    | Cool  | Normal | Strong | No  |
| 7  | Overcast| Cool  | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8  | Sunny   | Mild  | High   | Weak   | No  |
| 9  | Sunny   | Cool  | Normal | Weak   | Yes |
| 10 | Rain    | Mild  | Normal | Weak   | Yes |
| 11 | Sunny   | Mild  | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 12 | Overcast| Mild  | High   | Strong | Yes |
| 13 | Overcast| Hot   | Normal | Weak   | Yes |
| 14 | Rain    | Mild  | High   | Strong | No  |

---

**Step A: Compute Prior Probabilities.**
Counting classes: Yes = 9 days, No = 5 days, Total = 14.

$$P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{9}{14} \approx 0.6429$$

$$P(\text{No}) = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.3571$$

---

**Step B: Compute Conditional Probabilities for each feature.**

*Outlook:*

$$P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2}{9}, \quad P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{4}{9}, \quad P(\text{Rain} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3}{9}$$

$$P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3}{5}, \quad P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{0}{5}, \quad P(\text{Rain} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{2}{5}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Zero-Frequency Alert:** $P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = 0/5 = 0$. This will annihilate the product. We will need Laplace smoothing in Step D.

*Temperature:*

$$P(\text{Hot} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2}{9}, \quad P(\text{Mild} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{4}{9}, \quad P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3}{9}$$

$$P(\text{Hot} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{2}{5}, \quad P(\text{Mild} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{2}{5}, \quad P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{1}{5}$$

*Humidity:*

$$P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3}{9}, \quad P(\text{Normal} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{6}{9}$$

$$P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{4}{5}, \quad P(\text{Normal} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{1}{5}$$

*Wind:*

$$P(\text{Weak} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{6}{9}, \quad P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3}{9}$$

$$P(\text{Weak} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{2}{5}, \quad P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3}{5}$$

---

**Step C: Test Instance Classification (Without Smoothing).**

Classify a new day: $\mathbf{x} = (\text{Outlook=Sunny}, \text{Temp=Cool}, \text{Humidity=High}, \text{Wind=Strong})$.

*Posterior for "Yes":*

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}) &\propto P(\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) \\
&= \frac{9}{14} \cdot \frac{2}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \\
&= \frac{486}{91854} \approx 0.00529
\end{aligned}$$

*Posterior for "No":*

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) &\propto P(\text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) \\
&= \frac{5}{14} \cdot \frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{3}{5} \\
&= \frac{180}{8750} \approx 0.02057
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision:** Since $P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) > P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x})$, the classifier predicts **Play Tennis = No**.

---

**Step D: Classification with Laplace Smoothing (Demonstrating the Concept).**

For demonstration, suppose the test instance is $\mathbf{x} = (\text{Outlook=Overcast}, \text{Temp=Cool}, \text{Humidity=High}, \text{Wind=Strong})$.

Without smoothing, $P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = 0/5 = 0$, forcing $P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) = 0$.

Apply Laplace smoothing: $\vert V_{\text{Outlook}} \vert = 3$ (Sunny, Overcast, Rain).

$$P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{0 + 1}{5 + 3} = \frac{1}{8}$$

$$P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto \frac{9}{14} \cdot \frac{4}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \approx 0.00687$$

$$P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto \frac{5}{14} \cdot \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{3}{5} \approx 0.00214$$

Now the prediction flips to **Yes**, demonstrating how Laplace smoothing prevents the zero-frequency problem from over-influencing classification.

### 3.3 Summary Table: Posterior Comparison for Test Instance (Step C)

| **Class** | **Prior** | **$\cdot\, P(\text{Outlook})$** | **$\cdot\, P(\text{Temp})$** | **$\cdot\, P(\text{Humidity})$** | **$\cdot\, P(\text{Wind})$** | **= Posterior $\propto$** | **Prediction** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Yes | $9/14$ | $2/9$ | $3/9$ | $3/9$ | $3/9$ | $0.00529$ | — |
| No  | $5/14$ | $3/5$ | $1/5$ | $4/5$ | $3/5$ | $0.02057$ | $\star$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Naïve Bayes Inference Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional flow of the Naïve Bayes classification process — from training data ingestion to final class prediction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    trainA[Training Dataset] --> priB[Compute Prior P_c]
    trainA --> likC[Compute Likelihood P_x_given_c]
    priB --> mulD[Multiply Prior and Likelihoods]
    likC --> mulD
    mulD --> comE[Compare Posteriors for All Classes]
    comE --> mapF[Select Class with Maximum Posterior]
    mapF --> outG[Output Predicted Class Label]
    testX[Test Instance Features] --> likC
    testX --> mapF
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Naïve Bayes Underlying Probability Flow

This diagram maps the conceptual probability relationships exploited by the Naïve Bayes algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prior1[Prior P_c] --> post3[Posterior P_c_x]
    evi2[Evidence P_x] --> post3
    lik3[Likelihood P_x_c] --> post3
    post3 --> rule4[MAP Decision Rule argmax]
    rule4 --> dec5[Final Class Label]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decomposition of Joint Likelihood

This block-level schematic shows how the joint likelihood is decomposed into a product of independent feature-wise likelihoods.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    jointA[Joint P_x1_x2_x3_given_c] --> fx1B[Feature 1 P_x1_given_c]
    jointA --> fx2C[Feature 2 P_x2_given_c]
    jointA --> fx3D[Feature 3 P_x3_given_c]
    fx1B --> prodE[Naive Product]
    fx2C --> prodE
    fx3D --> prodE
    prodE --> nbf[Naive Bayes Score]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The top diagram shows the operational pipeline. The middle one represents the Bayesian probability identity. The bottom one highlights the "naïve" decomposition — the joint likelihood is approximated as a product of univariate likelihoods.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (3 Marks)**
*State Bayes' theorem and explain the meaning of each term. How is it used in the Naïve Bayes classifier?*

**Model Answer:**
Bayes' theorem states:
$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$
Where $P(c)$ is the prior probability of class $c$, $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$ is the likelihood of the feature vector given class $c$, $P(\mathbf{x})$ is the evidence, and $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ is the posterior. In Naïve Bayes, we use this theorem combined with the conditional independence assumption to compute the posterior for each class and select the class with the highest posterior.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (3 Marks)**
*What is the "zero-frequency problem" in Naïve Bayes? How is it handled using Laplace smoothing?*

**Model Answer:**
The zero-frequency problem occurs when a categorical feature value never appears with a certain class in the training set, yielding $P(x_i \mid c) = 0$. This zeros out the entire product of likelihoods, making the posterior zero for that class regardless of other evidence. Laplace (add-1) smoothing addresses this by adding 1 to each count:
$$P(x_i = v \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i = v, c) + 1}{\text{count}(c) + \vert V_i \vert}$$
This ensures no probability is ever exactly zero, leading to more robust estimates.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Consider the Play Tennis training dataset given in Section 3.2 of these notes.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the Naïve Bayes classification rule from Bayes' theorem, clearly stating the conditional independence assumption.*

**Model Solution:**

The Naïve Bayes classifier assigns an instance $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ to the class that maximizes the posterior:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$$

Applying Bayes' theorem:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of $c$, we drop it:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$$

Expanding the joint likelihood using the chain rule:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid c, x_1) \cdot P(x_3 \mid c, x_1, x_2) \cdots P(x_n \mid c, x_1, \dots, x_{n-1})$$

The **conditional independence assumption** states that each feature is independent of others given the class:

$$P(x_i \mid c, x_1, \dots, x_{i-1}, x_{i+1}, \dots, x_n) = P(x_i \mid c)$$

Therefore, the joint likelihood reduces to a product:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

Substituting back, we obtain the Naïve Bayes decision rule:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c) \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** *For the test instance $\mathbf{x} = (\text{Sunny, Cool, High, Strong})$, classify whether tennis will be played using the Naïve Bayes classifier. Show all computations.*

**Model Solution:**

From the training data, we have the following probabilities:

**Priors:**
- $P(\text{Yes}) = 9/14$
- $P(\text{No}) = 5/14$

**Likelihoods:**

| **Feature** | **Value** | **P(Value $\mid$ Yes)** | **P(Value $\mid$ No)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Outlook | Sunny | $2/9$ | $3/5$ |
| Temperature | Cool | $3/9$ | $1/5$ |
| Humidity | High | $3/9$ | $4/5$ |
| Wind | Strong | $3/9$ | $3/5$ |

**Posterior for "Yes":**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}) &\propto P(\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) \\
&= \frac{9}{14} \cdot \frac{2}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \cdot \frac{3}{9} \\
&= \frac{9 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3}{14 \cdot 9 \cdot 9 \cdot 9 \cdot 9} = \frac{486}{91854} \\
&\approx 0.00529
\end{aligned}$$

**Posterior for "No":**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) &\propto P(\text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) \\
&= \frac{5}{14} \cdot \frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{3}{5} \\
&= \frac{5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1 \cdot 4 \cdot 3}{14 \cdot 5 \cdot 5 \cdot 5 \cdot 5} = \frac{180}{8750} \\
&\approx 0.02057
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison:** $0.02057 > 0.00529$

**Decision:** $P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) > P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x})$, so the classifier predicts **Play Tennis = No**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the three main variants of the Naïve Bayes classifier with suitable examples. State the formula used in each case.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Multinomial Naïve Bayes:**
Used for discrete count data (e.g., word frequencies in text classification). The likelihood of a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$ is:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \frac{(\sum_i x_i)!}{\prod_i x_i!} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)^{x_i}$$

Example: Document classification where features are word counts.

**2. Bernoulli Naïve Bayes:**
Used for binary/boolean features (e.g., word presence/absence in a document). The likelihood is:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)^{x_i} \cdot (1 - P(x_i \mid c))^{1 - x_i}$$

where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

Example: Spam detection where the model checks whether each word appears in the email.

**3. Gaussian Naïve Bayes:**
Used for continuous features. The likelihood of each feature is assumed to follow a Gaussian (normal) distribution conditioned on the class:

$$P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_c^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_c)^2}{2\sigma_c^2}\right)$$

where $\mu_c$ and $\sigma_c^2$ are the mean and variance of feature $x_i$ for class $c$, estimated from the training data.

Example: Iris dataset classification, medical diagnosis from continuous lab values.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *What is the zero-frequency problem? Explain Laplace smoothing with the following contingency table and compute the smoothed likelihoods:*

| **Outlook** | **Yes** | **No** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Sunny   | 2 | 3 |
| Overcast| 4 | **0** |
| Rain    | 3 | 2 |

**Model Solution:**

The zero-frequency problem arises when a feature-category combination has zero occurrences in the training set for a particular class. In the table, $P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = 0/5 = 0$, which would nullify the entire posterior when classifying an "Overcast" day as "No". Laplace (add-1) smoothing redistributes probability mass:

$$P(x_i = v \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i = v, c) + 1}{\text{count}(c) + \vert V_i \vert}$$

Here, $\vert V_{\text{Outlook}} \vert = 3$ and $\text{count}(\text{No}) = 5$.

**Smoothed Likelihoods:**

$$P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3 + 1}{5 + 3} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

$$P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{0 + 1}{5 + 3} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

$$P(\text{Rain} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{2 + 1}{5 + 3} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

For "Yes" ($\text{count}(\text{Yes}) = 9$):

$$P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2 + 1}{9 + 3} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.25$$

$$P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{4 + 1}{9 + 3} = \frac{5}{12} \approx 0.417$$

$$P(\text{Rain} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3 + 1}{9 + 3} = \frac{4}{12} \approx 0.333$$

Notice that $P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No})$ is now a small non-zero value, eliminating the zero-frequency issue.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop $P(\mathbf{x})$:** Many students leave the evidence term in the final formula — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this.
> 2. **Confusing the direction of conditioning:** $P(x_i \mid c)$ (likelihood) and $P(c \mid x_i)$ (posterior) are NOT the same. Always show the Bayes' theorem application clearly.
> 3. **Numerical underflow:** Direct multiplication of small probabilities can underflow. Always mention the **log-sum trick** for high-dimensional inputs.
> 4. **Not stating the naïve assumption explicitly:** Examiners award marks specifically for stating "$P(x_i \mid c, x_j) = P(x_i \mid c)$" — do not skip it.
> 5. **Wrong smoothing denominator:** Use $\text{count}(c) + \vert V_i \vert$, NOT $\text{count}(c) + 1$.
> 6. **No internal choice in ESE:** KTU Part B always has an internal choice — write both Question A and Question B solutions in your answer script only if both options are presented; pick ONE for writing, but be ready to defend either.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Naïve Bayes = Bayes' Theorem + Conditional Independence Assumption.**
- The "naïve" assumption: $P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^n P(x_i \mid c)$.
- **Decision Rule:** $c^* = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_{i=1}^n P(x_i \mid c)$.
- **Three Variants:** Multinomial (counts), Bernoulli (binary), Gaussian (continuous).
- **Gaussian NB formula:** $P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_c^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_c)^2}{2\sigma_c^2}\right)$.
- **Zero-Frequency Problem:** Occurs when a feature-category combination is missing in training.
- **Laplace Smoothing formula:** $\frac{\text{count} + 1}{N + \vert V \vert}$ — note the denominator uses the vocabulary size.
- **Log-Sum Trick:** $\log P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto \log P(c) + \sum \log P(x_i \mid c)$ prevents underflow.
- **Practical Advantages:** Fast, low memory, handles high dimensions, robust to irrelevant features, works well with small data.
- **Practical Disadvantages:** Independence assumption rarely holds, zero-frequency issue, sensitive to how features are represented.
- **Estimator Parameters in Gaussian NB:** $\mu_c = \frac{1}{N_c}\sum_{j: c_j=c} x_{i,j}$ and $\sigma_c^2 = \frac{1}{N_c}\sum_{j: c_j=c}(x_{i,j} - \mu_c)^2$.
- **Key Exam Trick:** Always compute **both** posteriors (one for each class) — the ratio is what matters, not absolute values, because $P(\mathbf{x})$ is dropped.
- **Real-World Apps (must know for KTU viva/labs):** Spam filtering (Bernoulli), sentiment analysis (Multinomial), medical diagnosis (Gaussian).
- **Play Tennis Dataset** is the **most frequently asked KTU numerical problem** — memorize its 14 rows and probabilities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Trees - ID3, Generalisation and Overfitting - Idea of Overfitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decision Trees - ID3, Generalisation and Overfitting - Idea of Overfitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Decision Trees — The Foundational Concept

A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning algorithm used for both classification and regression tasks. It recursively partitions the input feature space into smaller, purer sub-regions by learning simple decision rules inferred from the data features. The final model is a tree-structured plan of conditional checks, where internal nodes represent tests on attributes, branches represent outcomes of those tests, and leaf nodes represent class labels (or continuous values for regression).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** Decision Trees belong to the family of *greedy, top-down, divide-and-conquer* inductive learning algorithms. The most fundamental representative is **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)**, proposed by **J. Ross Quinlan (1986)**, later refined into **C4.5** and **C5.0**.

### Formal Definition (Mitchell, 1997)

> A decision tree is a representation of a disjunction of conjunctions of constraints on the attribute values of instances. Each path from the tree root to a leaf corresponds to a conjunction of attribute tests, and the tree itself to the disjunction of these conjunctions.

## 1.2 ID3 — Iterative Dichotomiser 3

**ID3** is a classical greedy top-down algorithm that constructs a decision tree by selecting, at each node, the *single attribute* that provides the **maximum information gain** with respect to the target class. It uses the information-theoretic measure of **Entropy** as its impurity criterion. ID3 is the conceptual ancestor of all modern tree learners such as C4.5, CART, and the ensemble-based Random Forests and Gradient Boosted Trees.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Iterative Dichotomiser"?** The word *Dichotomiser* means "something that splits into two." ID3 iteratively dichotomises (splits) the dataset into smaller subsets based on the discriminating power of each attribute until the subsets are pure or no further informative split exists.

## 1.3 Generalisation

**Generalisation** is the ability of a learned hypothesis (decision tree, in our case) to correctly classify **unseen, novel instances** drawn from the same underlying distribution as the training data. A tree with strong generalisation captures the *underlying functional mapping* rather than memorising specific training points. Mathematically, a tree *T* generalises well if its expected prediction error on the true joint distribution $P(X, Y)$ is low:

$$E_{generalisation} = \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim P} \left[ \mathbf{1}\{ T(x) \neq y \} \right]$$

## 1.4 Overfitting — The Core Curse of Decision Trees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Mitchell, 1997):** *A hypothesis $h$ is said to **overfit** the training data if there exists some other hypothesis $h'$ such that $h$ has smaller error than $h'$ over the training instances, but $h'$ has smaller error than $h$ over the entire distribution of instances.*

In plain words: **Overfitting** is the phenomenon where a model learns the training data *too well* — including its noise, outliers, and idiosyncratic fluctuations — at the cost of poor performance on unseen test data. It is the classic manifestation of the **Bias-Variance Trade-off**.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rote Student vs. Wise Student"

> [!NOTE]
> **🎓 Real-World Analogy: Rote Student vs Wise Student**
>
> Imagine a classroom of two students preparing for a Machine Learning exam:
>
> 1. **Rote Student (Overfit Tree):** Memorises every single line of the textbook, including misprints, margin doodles, and coffee stains. On the textbook questions, scores **100/100**. But on the unseen semester exam, scores **40/100** because the questions are *slightly different* from the memorised ones.
>
> 2. **Wise Student (Well-Generalised Tree):** Reads the textbook, identifies the *core patterns* and *underlying principles*, and ignores noise. Scores **85/100** on the textbook and **82/100** on the unseen exam — the performance gap is minimal.
>
> The **Rote Student** has *memorised the training set* — classic **overfitting**. The **Wise Student** has *learned the concept* — classic **good generalisation**.

A decision tree can grow arbitrarily deep, carving out micro-regions for every single training point (Rote Student). To avoid this, we apply **pruning**, **early stopping**, or **regularisation** so the tree stays at the "Wise Student" level.

## 1.6 Why Overfitting is Inherently Linked to Decision Trees

Decision trees are **non-parametric** — their capacity (number of leaves, depth) grows with the data. Unlike linear models with a fixed hypothesis space, a decision tree can theoretically grow to have *one leaf per training instance*, perfectly classifying every training point but generalising terribly. This unbounded capacity makes overfitting the single most important practical concern when deploying tree-based learners in real engineering systems such as medical diagnosis, credit scoring, and predictive maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ID3 Algorithm — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The ID3 algorithm follows a strict, recursive, greedy procedure:

1. **Initialise:** Compute the entropy of the target attribute $S$ (the entire training set).
2. **Attribute Evaluation:** For every *unused* attribute $A$:
   - Compute the conditional entropy $H(S \mid A)$.
   - Compute the **Information Gain** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$.
3. **Best Split Selection:** Choose the attribute $A^*$ with the **highest information gain** as the root (or current internal) node.
4. **Partition:** Partition the dataset $S$ along the values of $A^*$, creating one child branch per distinct value of $A^*$.
5. **Recursion:** Recursively apply steps 1–4 to each child subset, using only the *remaining unused attributes*.
6. **Termination:** Stop when:
   - All instances in a subset belong to the same class (pure node → leaf), OR
   - No remaining attributes provide any information gain (zero-gain node → leaf with majority class), OR
   - The subset is empty (→ leaf with parent majority class).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greediness Caveat:** ID3 never backtracks. Once an attribute is chosen, the decision is *committed*. This makes ID3 fast but **non-optimal in the global sense** — it may get stuck in locally optimal splits.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — The Information-Theoretic Core

The ID3 algorithm is grounded in **Claude Shannon's Information Theory (1948)**. Three quantities are central:

### 2.2.1 Entropy of a Set $S$ (Shannon's Entropy)

Measures the **impurity** or **uncertainty** in a collection of examples. For a binary classification:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

- $c$ = number of distinct classes.
- $p_i$ = proportion of instances in $S$ belonging to class $i$.
- $H(S) = 0$ ⇒ Perfectly pure set (all one class).
- $H(S) = \log_2 c$ ⇒ Maximally impure (uniformly distributed classes).

### 2.2.2 Conditional Entropy $H(S \mid A)$

The expected entropy of $S$ after partitioning by attribute $A$:

$$H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot H(S_v)$$

where $S_v$ is the subset of $S$ for which attribute $A$ takes value $v$.

### 2.2.3 Information Gain $IG(S, A)$

The **reduction in entropy** achieved by partitioning $S$ using attribute $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

ID3 selects $A^* = \arg\max_{A} \, IG(S, A)$.

### 2.2.4 Split Information (used in C4.5 / Gain Ratio)

Normalises the Information Gain to penalise attributes with many distinct values:

$$SplitInfo(S, A) = -\sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$$

### 2.2.5 Gain Ratio (C4.5 Enhancement)

$$GR(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{SplitInfo(S, A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** While ID3 itself uses pure **Information Gain**, KTU examiners frequently ask students to *compare* ID3 with C4.5, hence Gain Ratio is also a high-yield formula.

## 2.3 Overfitting — Theoretical Decomposition

### 2.3.1 Bias-Variance Decomposition of Expected Error

For any learned model, the expected prediction error on a new instance $(x, y)$ can be decomposed as:

$$E\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^2 \right] = \underbrace{Bias^2\left[\hat{f}(x)\right]}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{Variance\left[\hat{f}(x)\right]}_{\text{sensitivity to training data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

- **Bias** = error from wrong assumptions in the learning algorithm. Underfitting = high bias.
- **Variance** = error from sensitivity to small fluctuations in the training set. Overfitting = high variance.
- **Irreducible noise** $\sigma^2$ = the Bayes error floor; cannot be reduced by any model.

### 2.3.2 Overfitting in Decision Trees — The Depth Curve

As tree depth $d$ increases:

| Tree Depth | Training Error | Test Error | Model Behaviour |
|------------|---------------|------------|-----------------|
| Very small (shallow) | High | High | **Underfitting** (High Bias) |
| Optimal depth $d^*$ | Low | Minimum | **Ideal Generalisation** |
| Very large (deep) | ≈ 0 | High | **Overfitting** (High Variance) |

## 2.4 The Two-Phase Defence Against Overfitting

### 2.4.1 Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping)

Stop growing the tree *during* construction when:
- Information gain falls below a threshold $\tau$.
- A node has fewer than $N_{min}$ instances.
- Tree depth exceeds $D_{max}$.

### 2.4.2 Post-Pruning (Reduced Error Pruning)

Grow the full tree, then *replace* subtrees with leaves if doing so reduces validation error. Variants include:
- **Reduced-Error Pruning (Quinlan):** Uses a held-out validation set.
- **Cost-Complexity Pruning (CART, Breiman 1984):** Minimises $|T| + \alpha \cdot (\text{misclassifications})$ for a complexity parameter $\alpha$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Property | Units |
|---|----------|---------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | Shannon Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $0 \le H \le \log_2 c$ | **bits** |
| 2 | Conditional Entropy | $H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Weighted average of child entropies | **bits** |
| 3 | Information Gain | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | Always non-negative | **bits** |
| 4 | Split Information | $SplitInfo(S, A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Intrinsic value of split | **bits** |
| 5 | Gain Ratio | $GR(S, A) = IG(S,A) / SplitInfo(S,A)$ | Penalty for many-valued attributes | **dimensionless** |
| 6 | Gini Impurity (CART) | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_i p_i^2$ | Alternative to entropy | **dimensionless** |
| 7 | Expected MSE | $E[(y-\hat f)^2] = Bias^2 + Var + \sigma^2$ | Bias-Variance decomposition | **error²** |
| 8 | Cost-Complexity (CART) | $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ | $R$ = error, $\vert T\vert$ = leaves | **error + penalty** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Decision Tree Application | Overfitting Risk |
|--------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Predicting disease from patient symptoms | **High** — small datasets, high stakes |
| **Credit Scoring** | Loan approval based on applicant features | **High** — regulatory compliance issues |
| **Predictive Maintenance** | Classifying machine failure modes | **Medium** — sensor noise is significant |
| **Customer Churn** | Telecom / SaaS retention modelling | **Medium** — concept drift over time |
| **Spam Filtering** | Email classification (text features) | **High** — millions of features |
| **Remote Sensing** | Land-cover classification from satellite images | **Very High** — high-dimensional pixel data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In all the above domains, an overfit tree is *worse than useless* — it gives the operator *false confidence*. A slightly less accurate but well-generalised tree is operationally far more valuable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — The "Play Tennis" Dataset (Mitchell, 1997)

This is the **single most important example** for KTU board examinations. The dataset has **14 instances** with 4 candidate attributes, and the target is `PlayTennis ∈ \{Yes, No\}`.

### 3.1.1 The Training Table

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | PlayTennis |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------------|
| D1  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak | No |
| D2  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No |
| D3  | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak | Yes |
| D4  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak | Yes |
| D5  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes |
| D6  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No |
| D7  | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes |
| D8  | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak | No |
| D9  | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes |
| D10 | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak | Yes |
| D11 | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes |
| D12 | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes |
| D13 | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak | Yes |
| D14 | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No |

**Class Distribution:** 9 "Yes" (play) and 5 "No" (do not play) ⇒ Total $N = 14$.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute the Base Entropy $H(S)$

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i = -p_{Yes} \log_2 p_{Yes} - p_{No} \log_2 p_{No}$$

Substitute $p_{Yes} = 9/14$ and $p_{No} = 5/14$:

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14}$$

Compute each term using a logarithm table or calculator:

$$\log_2 \frac{9}{14} = \log_2 0.6429 \approx -0.6374$$

$$\log_2 \frac{5}{14} = \log_2 0.3571 \approx -1.4854$$

Substitute back:

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14}(-0.6374) - \frac{5}{14}(-1.4854)$$

$$H(S) = (0.6429)(0.6374) + (0.3571)(1.4854)$$

$$H(S) = 0.4098 + 0.5305 = 0.9403 \text{ bits}$$

**Result:** $H(S) \approx \mathbf{0.940}$ bits.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute Information Gain for Each Attribute

We must compute $IG(S, A)$ for $A \in \{Outlook, Temperature, Humidity, Wind\}$.

#### (A) Information Gain of *Outlook*

Outlook has 3 values: *Sunny* (D1, D2, D8, D9, D11) → 5 days, *Overcast* (D3, D7, D12, D13) → 4 days, *Rain* (D4, D5, D6, D10, D14) → 5 days.

**Sunny branch:** 2 Yes (D9, D11), 3 No (D1, D2, D8):

$$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2 \frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2 \frac{3}{5}$$

$$= -\frac{2}{5}(-1.3219) - \frac{3}{5}(-0.7370) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Overcast branch:** 4 Yes, 0 No ⇒ **Pure** subset:

$$H(S_{Overcast}) = -\frac{4}{4}\log_2 1 - 0 = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**Rain branch:** 3 Yes (D4, D5, D10), 2 No (D6, D14):

$$H(S_{Rain}) = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2 \frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2 \frac{2}{5}$$

$$= -\frac{3}{5}(-0.7370) - \frac{2}{5}(-1.3219) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Conditional Entropy:**

$$H(S \mid Outlook) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710)$$

$$= \frac{10}{14}(0.9710) = 0.7143 \times 0.9710 = 0.6936 \text{ bits}$$

**Information Gain:**

$$IG(S, Outlook) = H(S) - H(S \mid Outlook) = 0.9403 - 0.6936 = 0.2467 \text{ bits}$$

#### (B) Information Gain of *Temperature*

Temperature has 3 values: *Hot* (D1, D2, D3, D13) → 4 days, *Mild* (D4, D8, D10, D11, D12, D14) → 6 days, *Cool* (D5, D6, D7, D9) → 4 days.

**Hot branch:** 2 Yes (D3, D13), 2 No (D1, D2):

$$H(S_{Hot}) = -\frac{2}{4}\log_2 \frac{2}{4} - \frac{2}{4}\log_2 \frac{2}{4} = 1 \text{ bit}$$

**Mild branch:** 4 Yes (D4, D10, D11, D12), 2 No (D8, D14):

$$H(S_{Mild}) = -\frac{4}{6}\log_2 \frac{4}{6} - \frac{2}{6}\log_2 \frac{2}{6} = 0.9183 \text{ bits}$$

**Cool branch:** 3 Yes (D5, D7, D9), 1 No (D6):

$$H(S_{Cool}) = -\frac{3}{4}\log_2 \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}\log_2 \frac{1}{4} = 0.8113 \text{ bits}$$

**Conditional Entropy:**

$$H(S \mid Temp) = \frac{4}{14}(1) + \frac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{14}(0.8113)$$

$$= 0.2857 + 0.3935 + 0.2318 = 0.9110 \text{ bits}$$

**Information Gain:**

$$IG(S, Temperature) = 0.9403 - 0.9110 = 0.0293 \text{ bits}$$

#### (C) Information Gain of *Humidity*

Humidity has 2 values: *High* (D1, D2, D3, D4, D8, D12, D14) → 7 days, *Normal* (D5, D6, D7, D9, D10, D11, D13) → 7 days.

**High branch:** 3 Yes (D3, D4, D12), 4 No (D1, D2, D8, D14):

$$H(S_{High}) = -\frac{3}{7}\log_2 \frac{3}{7} - \frac{4}{7}\log_2 \frac{4}{7} = 0.9852 \text{ bits}$$

**Normal branch:** 6 Yes (D5, D7, D9, D10, D11, D13), 1 No (D6):

$$H(S_{Normal}) = -\frac{6}{7}\log_2 \frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7}\log_2 \frac{1}{7} = 0.5917 \text{ bits}$$

**Conditional Entropy:**

$$H(S \mid Humidity) = \frac{7}{14}(0.9852) + \frac{7}{14}(0.5917)$$

$$= 0.5 \times (0.9852 + 0.5917) = 0.7885 \text{ bits}$$

**Information Gain:**

$$IG(S, Humidity) = 0.9403 - 0.7885 = 0.1518 \text{ bits}$$

#### (D) Information Gain of *Wind*

Wind has 2 values: *Weak* (D1, D3, D4, D5, D8, D9, D10, D13) → 8 days, *Strong* (D2, D6, D7, D11, D12, D14) → 6 days.

**Weak branch:** 6 Yes (D3, D4, D5, D9, D10, D13), 2 No (D1, D8):

$$H(S_{Weak}) = -\frac{6}{8}\log_2 \frac{6}{8} - \frac{2}{8}\log_2 \frac{2}{8} = 0.8113 \text{ bits}$$

**Strong branch:** 3 Yes (D7, D11, D12), 3 No (D2, D6, D14):

$$H(S_{Strong}) = -\frac{3}{6}\log_2 \frac{3}{6} - \frac{3}{6}\log_2 \frac{3}{6} = 1.0000 \text{ bit}$$

**Conditional Entropy:**

$$H(S \mid Wind) = \frac{8}{14}(0.8113) + \frac{6}{14}(1.0)$$

$$= 0.5714 \times 0.8113 + 0.4286 \times 1.0 = 0.4636 + 0.4286 = 0.8922 \text{ bits}$$

**Information Gain:**

$$IG(S, Wind) = 0.9403 - 0.8922 = 0.0481 \text{ bits}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Choose the Root Attribute

| Attribute | Information Gain (bits) | Rank |
|-----------|------------------------|------|
| **Outlook** | **0.247** | 🥇 Highest |
| Humidity | 0.152 | 🥈 |
| Wind | 0.048 | 🥉 |
| Temperature | 0.029 | 4th |

**Decision:** $A^* = Outlook$ becomes the root. Since the *Overcast* branch is pure (entropy = 0), it terminates as a "Yes" leaf. The algorithm then recurses on the *Sunny* and *Rain* subsets using the remaining three attributes (Temperature, Humidity, Wind).

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Recursion on Sunny Subset (Recap)

For the *Sunny* subset (5 days, 2 Yes / 3 No), recomputing IG for the three remaining attributes (omitting detailed arithmetic for brevity) yields **Humidity** as the best splitter, which cleanly separates the 2 Yes and 3 No. Similarly, the *Rain* subset (5 days, 3 Yes / 2 No) yields **Wind** as the best splitter. The final ID3 tree perfectly classifies all 14 training instances.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why ID3 Overfits

| Aspect | Mechanism of Overfitting in ID3 | Engineering Consequence |
|--------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Unbounded Growth** | ID3 grows until pure leaves — no depth limit | Tree memorises noise; test accuracy crashes |
| **Greedy Myopia** | Local IG maximisation, no global look-ahead | Sub-optimal early splits propagate down |
| **Discrete Splits** | One branch per discrete value | Splits on irrelevant features inflate depth |
| **No Continuous Handling** | Native ID3 cannot split continuous attributes natively | Forces crude thresholding in extended versions |
| **Sample-Size Fragility** | Small training sets yield high-variance trees | One mislabelled point may flip a split |
| **No Regularisation** | ID3 has no penalty on tree size | Pathological trees with hundreds of leaves |

## 3.3 Mathematical Justification for Overfitting — Learning Curve Argument

Let $T_d$ denote a decision tree of depth $d$ trained on $N$ samples. The number of leaves is bounded by $|T_d| \le 2^d$, and the **effective hypothesis space** grows exponentially. By the **PAC-learning generalisation bound** (Blumer, Ehrenfeucht, Haussler, Warmuth 1989):

$$P\left( \text{Error}_{true}(h) - \text{Error}_{train}(h) > \epsilon \right) \le |\mathcal{H}| \cdot e^{-2N\epsilon^2}$$

As $|\mathcal{H}|$ grows (deeper tree), the bound becomes *vacuous* unless $N$ grows proportionally. For a tree with $2^d$ leaves, we need $N \gg 2^d$ for tight bounds. Hence deep trees on small data **provably overfit** with high probability.

## 3.4 Pruning Math — Cost-Complexity Pruning (CART)

The CART cost-complexity criterion is:

$$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \cdot |T|$$

where $R(T)$ is the misclassification rate (or sum of squared residuals) and $|T|$ is the number of terminal leaves. As $\alpha$ increases from 0, the optimal subtree shrinks. The classic CART algorithm finds the *smallest* subtree satisfying $R_\alpha(T) \le R_\alpha(T_{max})$ for each $\alpha$, then selects the final $\alpha$ via cross-validation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ID3 Algorithm — Top-Down Greedy Construction Flow

The diagram below maps the **recursive control flow** of the ID3 algorithm, highlighting the *greedy attribute selection* and the *three stopping conditions*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["START ID3 S A_used"] --> A1["Compute H S over target Y"]
    A1 --> A2{"All instances<br/>same class?"}
    A2 -- "Yes" --> A3["Return leaf with<br/>class label"]
    A2 -- "No" --> A4{"A_used empty<br/>or no positive IG?"}
    A4 -- "Yes" --> A5["Return leaf with<br/>majority class"]
    A4 -- "No" --> A6["For each candidate<br/>attribute A in A_used"]
    A6 --> A7["Compute IG S A"]
    A7 --> A8["Select A_star = argmax IG"]
    A8 --> A9["Create internal node<br/>labelled A_star"]
    A9 --> A10["For each value v in Values A_star"]
    A10 --> A11["Build subset S_v of S"]
    A11 --> A12{"S_v empty?"}
    A12 -- "Yes" --> A13["Attach leaf with<br/>parent majority class"]
    A12 -- "No" --> A14["Recursive call<br/>ID3 S_v A_used minus A_star"]
    A14 --> A10
    A13 --> A10
    A10 -- "All values processed" --> A15["Return internal<br/>node A_star"]
    A3 --> A16["END"]
    A5 --> A16
    A15 --> A16
```

## 4.2 Generalisation vs Overfitting — The Learning Curve

The diagram below depicts the canonical **error-vs-complexity** relationship. Note the *crossover point* where training and test errors diverge — the onset of overfitting.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REGION_UNDERFIT["UNDERFITTING REGION"]
        UF1["High Bias"]
        UF2["Both errors high"]
        UF3["Model too simple"]
    end
    subgraph REGION_OPTIMAL["OPTIMAL GENERALISATION"]
        OPT1["Bias-Variance Balance"]
        OPT2["Minimum test error"]
        OPT3["Cross-validation sweet spot"]
    end
    subgraph REGION_OVERFIT["OVERFITTING REGION"]
        OF1["High Variance"]
        OF2["Train error near 0"]
        OF3["Test error rises sharply"]
    end
    UF_IN["Low Complexity"] --> UF1
    UF1 --> UF2
    UF2 --> UF3
    UF3 --> CROSS["Crossover Point"]
    CROSS --> OPT1
    OPT1 --> OPT2
    OPT2 --> OPT3
    OPT3 --> OF1
    OF1 --> OF2
    OF2 --> OF3
    OF3 --> HIGH_IN["High Complexity"]
    style REGION_UNDERFIT fill:#ffe4b5
    style REGION_OPTIMAL fill:#90ee90
    style REGION_OVERFIT fill:#ffb6c1
    style CROSS fill:#ffff00
```

## 4.3 Play-Tennis Decision Tree — Final ID3 Output

The final ID3 tree learned from the worked example is shown below in compact tree-notation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootN["Outlook"] --> oN1["Sunny"]
    rootN --> oN2["Overcast"]
    rootN --> oN3["Rain"]
    oN2 --> leafY1["Play = Yes"]
    oN1 --> hN1["Humidity"]
    hN1 --> hN2["High"]
    hN1 --> hN3["Normal"]
    hN2 --> leafN1["Play = No"]
    hN3 --> leafY2["Play = Yes"]
    oN3 --> wN1["Wind"]
    wN1 --> wN2["Strong"]
    wN1 --> wN3["Weak"]
    wN2 --> leafN2["Play = No"]
    wN3 --> leafY3["Play = Yes"]
    style leafY1 fill:#90ee90
    style leafY2 fill:#90ee90
    style leafY3 fill:#90ee90
    style leafN1 fill:#ffb6c1
    style leafN2 fill:#ffb6c1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the tree:** *Outlook = Overcast* ⇒ always play. *Outlook = Sunny* + *Humidity = High* ⇒ never play. *Outlook = Rain* + *Wind = Strong* ⇒ never play. All other combinations ⇒ play. This tree perfectly classifies the 14 training instances.

