# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base 2 in the log** — entropy is in *bits*; using $\ln$ (nats) without unit conversion is a frequent error. **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks.**
> 2. **Conditional entropy formula misuse** — students often forget the weight $\vert S_v \vert / \vert S \vert$ and write $H(S \mid A) = \sum H(S_v)$ without the proportions. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
> 3. **Information Gain sign error** — IG is *subtraction* $H(S) - H(S \mid A)$, not addition.
> 4. **Confusing ID3 (IG) with C4.5 (Gain Ratio)** — writing Gain Ratio when the question explicitly asks for ID3.
> 5. **Oversimplified overfitting definitions** — definitions like "model performs poorly on test data" are *incomplete*; the canonical definition uses the **existence of an alternative hypothesis** with lower true-distribution error.
> 6. **No mention of bias-variance** in overfitting answers — this is the *most* expected terminological link in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Overfitting" in the context of a decision tree learner. How does it affect generalisation performance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A hypothesis $h$ (a decision tree, in our context) is said to overfit the training data if there exists some alternative hypothesis $h' \in \mathcal{H}$ such that $h$ has smaller error than $h'$ on the training set, but $h'$ has smaller error than $h$ on the **entire distribution** of instances (Mitchell, 1997).

**Effect on Generalisation (1.5 marks):** Overfitting causes the model to capture noise, outliers, and idiosyncratic fluctuations specific to the training data. Consequently, while **training accuracy remains artificially high (often 100%)**, **test/unseen accuracy degrades sharply**. The model loses its generalisation capability — its predictions on novel data become unreliable, making it operationally useless in real engineering deployments.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the formula for Information Gain used by the ID3 algorithm and explain why ID3 uses it as the attribute selection criterion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where $H(S) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ is Shannon's entropy.

**Justification (1.5 marks):** ID3 uses Information Gain because it directly quantifies the **reduction in uncertainty (entropy)** about the target class achieved by partitioning the dataset on attribute $A$. The attribute that *maximally reduces* the entropy of the target yields the **purest child subsets** and is therefore the most discriminative. This is mathematically equivalent to minimising the number of bits required to encode the class label after the split — a principle from Shannon's information theory that guarantees ID3 produces compact, informative trees.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
>
> (a) For the training set given below, compute the **Information Gain** for each of the four attributes *Outlook*, *Temperature*, *Humidity*, and *Wind* with respect to the target attribute *PlayTennis*. Identify the attribute chosen as the root by the ID3 algorithm. **[7 marks]**
>
> (b) With a neat diagram and a real-world example, explain the concept of **overfitting** in decision trees. Discuss the **bias-variance trade-off** and two common methods used to control overfitting. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Base Entropy (1 mark):**

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2 \frac{5}{14} = 0.940 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — IG Computations (5 marks, 1.25 each):**

| Attribute | $H(S \mid A)$ | $IG(S, A)$ |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| Outlook | 0.694 | **0.246** |
| Temperature | 0.911 | 0.029 |
| Humidity | 0.789 | 0.152 |
| Wind | 0.892 | 0.048 |

*Detailed sub-steps:*
- For *Outlook* (Sunny: 2Y/3N, Overcast: 4Y/0N, Rain: 3Y/2N): $H = \frac{5}{14}(0.971) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.971) = 0.694$ bits, so $IG = 0.940 - 0.694 = 0.246$ bits.
- For *Temperature* (Hot: 2Y/2N, Mild: 4Y/2N, Cool: 3Y/1N): $H = \frac{4}{14}(1.0) + \frac{6}{14}(0.918) + \frac{4}{14}(0.811) = 0.911$ bits, so $IG = 0.940 - 0.911 = 0.029$ bits.
- For *Humidity* (High: 3Y/4N, Normal: 6Y/1N): $H = \frac{7}{14}(0.985) + \frac{7}{14}(0.592) = 0.789$ bits, so $IG = 0.940 - 0.789 = 0.152$ bits.
- For *Wind* (Weak: 6Y/2N, Strong: 3Y/3N): $H = \frac{8}{14}(0.811) + \frac{6}{14}(1.0) = 0.892$ bits, so $IG = 0.940 - 0.892 = 0.048$ bits.

**Step 3 — Root Selection (1 mark):**

Since $IG(S, Outlook) = 0.246$ is the **maximum**, the ID3 algorithm selects **Outlook** as the root node.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Concept of Overfitting (2 marks):**
A decision tree overfits when it grows so deep that it begins to model the *noise* in the training data rather than the *underlying pattern*. As a result, the **training error approaches zero**, but the **test error increases** — the *generalisation gap* widens.

**Real-World Example (2 marks):**
*Medical Diagnosis* — A hospital dataset of 100 patients may contain a few mislabelled cases. An unpruned ID3 tree might create a deep, complex path that depends on a specific patient's ID number or an irrelevant feature such as the *date of admission*. While it perfectly fits training data, when applied to a new patient, it makes unreliable predictions — possibly a life-threatening failure.

**Bias-Variance Trade-off (2 marks):**
- **High Bias (underfitting):** Shallow trees are too simplistic; they miss genuine patterns. They systematically err in the same direction.
- **High Variance (overfitting):** Deep trees are too sensitive to training-set peculiarities; small data changes produce vastly different trees. The expected error decomposes as $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- The goal is to find the **sweet spot** where the *sum* of squared bias and variance is minimised.

**Two Overfitting-Control Methods (1 mark):**
1. **Pre-pruning (Early Stopping):** Halt tree growth when a node has fewer than $N_{min}$ samples, or when the best IG falls below a threshold $\tau$.
2. **Post-pruning (Reduced-Error / Cost-Complexity):** Grow the full tree, then iteratively replace subtrees with leaves if doing so reduces validation error (or equivalently, the cost-complexity $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**
>
> (a) Explain the **ID3 algorithm** in detail. How does it select attributes at each node? Use the *PlayTennis* dataset (14 instances, 4 attributes) to demonstrate the root-attribute computation. **[7 marks]**
>
> (b) Differentiate between **underfitting and overfitting**. How does **tree depth** influence this trade-off? Describe the **post-pruning techniques** used in CART and C4.5. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**ID3 Algorithm Description (3 marks):**
ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3) is a greedy, top-down, divide-and-conquer algorithm that builds a decision tree by recursively partitioning the training set. The algorithm uses **Shannon's entropy** and **Information Gain** to identify the most discriminative attribute at each step. It continues until all instances at a node belong to one class (pure node) or no further informative split exists.

**Attribute Selection Mechanism (2 marks):**
At each node, ID3 evaluates every *unused* attribute by computing:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

The attribute $A^* = \arg\max_A IG(S, A)$ is selected for splitting, and the dataset is partitioned along its values. Recursion proceeds on each child subset using only the remaining attributes.

**Demonstration on PlayTennis (2 marks):**
Computing $IG$ for all four attributes:
- $IG(S, Outlook) = 0.246$ bits
- $IG(S, Humidity) = 0.152$ bits
- $IG(S, Wind) = 0.048$ bits
- $IG(S, Temperature) = 0.029$ bits

Since $Outlook$ yields the highest gain, ID3 selects it as the root. The *Overcast* child is pure (all "Yes"); recursion continues on *Sunny* and *Rain* subsets with the remaining three attributes.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Underfitting vs Overfitting (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Underfitting | Overfitting |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Tree depth | Too shallow | Too deep |
| Bias | High | Low |
| Variance | Low | High |
| Training error | High | Very low (≈ 0) |
| Test error | High | High |
| Cause | Model too simple | Model too complex; learns noise |

**Influence of Tree Depth (2 marks):**
As tree depth $d$ increases, the model gains capacity. Initially, increasing $d$ reduces both training and test error (underfitting → optimal). Beyond a critical depth $d^*$, the training error continues to fall toward zero, but the test error *rises* — a hallmark of overfitting. The optimal depth $d^*$ is typically identified by **cross-validation** (k-fold or hold-out).

**Post-Pruning Techniques (3 marks):**

1. **Reduced-Error Pruning (Quinlan, C4.5):** A validation set is held out from training. Each non-leaf subtree is considered for replacement by a leaf. If replacing the subtree *reduces* validation-set error, the replacement is accepted. Repeat until no further improvement is possible.

2. **Pessimistic Error Pruning (Quinlan, C4.5):** Uses the *training* error (no separate validation set). Each subtree is replaced by a leaf if the upper bound of the confidence interval (e.g., 25% confidence using the normal approximation) of the leaf's misclassification rate is lower than the subtree's. Computationally efficient.

3. **Cost-Complexity Pruning (CART, Breiman 1984):** Minimises $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ for a hyperparameter $\alpha$. Increasing $\alpha$ from 0 progressively collapses the tree. A sequence of candidate subtrees $T_0 \supset T_1 \supset \dots \supset T_n$ is generated, and the optimal $\alpha$ (hence optimal subtree) is chosen by $V$-fold cross-validation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam**
>
> ✅ **ID3 = Greedy + Top-Down + Information Gain** — these three keywords are mandatory in any definition.
>
> ✅ **Entropy formula:** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ — always base 2 (units = **bits**). $H \in [0, \log_2 c]$.
>
> ✅ **Information Gain:** $IG = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$. ID3 picks the **argmax** attribute.
>
> ✅ **Conditional Entropy weight:** $\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ — forgetting this is the **#1 mark-loss trap**.
>
> ✅ **Overfitting canonical definition** must mention the *existence of an alternative hypothesis $h'$* — partial definitions (e.g., "test accuracy is low") are **incomplete** and lose marks.
>
> ✅ **Bias-Variance equation** $E = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ — must be reproduced exactly if asked.
>
> ✅ **Two-pronged defence:** Pre-pruning (early stopping) vs Post-pruning (REP, PEP, CCP). Know the formula $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ for CART.
>
> ✅ **Gain Ratio** ($GR = IG / \text{SplitInfo}$) is **C4.5**, not ID3. KTU sometimes tests this distinction.
>
> ✅ **Gini Impurity** $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ is used by **CART**, not ID3.
>
> ✅ **The PlayTennis example** is the **golden dataset** for KTU exams — practice computing all four IGs at least three times.
>
> ✅ **Three termination conditions for ID3:** pure node, no more attributes, empty subset.
>
> ✅ **Why ID3 overfits:** unbounded growth, greedy myopia, no regularisation, sensitivity to noise.
>
> ✅ **Cross-validation** is the universally accepted mechanism for selecting the optimal tree depth / pruning threshold.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LASSO and RIDGE Regularization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LASSO and RIDGE Regularization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Regularization** is a class of techniques used in statistical learning and machine learning to introduce additional structural information to an optimization problem to prevent overfitting and improve the generalization capability of a model on unseen data. It achieves this by adding a **penalty term** (also called a regularizer) to the standard loss function, constraining the magnitude of model coefficients.

Within the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the two foundational penalized regression techniques are:

- **Ridge Regression (L2 Regularization):** A regularized linear regression technique that adds a penalty term proportional to the **square of the magnitude of the coefficients**. The objective is to minimize:

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{Ridge}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \hat{y}_i\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$$

- **LASSO Regression (L1 Regularization):** **Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator** is a regularized linear regression technique that adds a penalty term proportional to the **absolute value of the magnitude of the coefficients**. The objective is to minimize:

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{LASSO}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \hat{y}_i\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert\beta_j\vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The hyperparameter $\lambda \geq 0$ is called the **regularization parameter** (or tuning constant). When $\lambda = 0$, the methods reduce to standard Ordinary Least Squares (OLS). When $\lambda \to \infty$, the coefficients are forced toward zero. The optimal $\lambda$ is typically selected via **k-fold Cross-Validation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **hiking guide leading a group of students** up a mountain to a summit. The summit represents the **minimum of the loss function** (i.e., the best possible fit to data). However, there are two scenarios:

### Scenario 1: No Regularization (Ordinary Least Squares)
The guide lets the students run freely toward the peak. Some students overshoot, others take strange zigzag paths, and a few even fall off cliffs (overfitting to training noise). This represents a model that fits the training data too aggressively and **fails to generalize**.

### Scenario 2: Ridge Regularization (L2)
The guide attaches **elastic bungee cords** to each student's waist. They can still reach near the peak, but the cords gently **pull them back** if they stray too far in any direction. Coefficients are shrunk toward zero but **never become exactly zero**. This is ideal when **all features are useful** but you want to control their individual magnitudes.

### Scenario 3: LASSO Regularization (L1)
The guide attaches **rigid ropes** to the students. If a student wants to wander too far in any direction, the rope will **completely stop them** at a certain distance, and any further movement is impossible. The result: many students simply stop moving in certain directions — corresponding to **coefficients being driven to exactly zero**. This effectively **performs feature selection**, eliminating unimportant variables entirely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Ridge is the "gentle bungee" (shrinks but never zeroes out), LASSO is the "rigid rope" (can completely eliminate features by setting coefficients to exactly zero).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters

- **$\lambda$ (lambda):** Regularization strength hyperparameter. $\lambda \geq 0$. Often expressed as $\alpha$ in scikit-learn nomenclature.
- **$\beta_0$ (intercept):** Usually **not** penalized in standard formulations. Only the slope coefficients $\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_p$ are subjected to the penalty.
- **$p$ (number of features):** Critical for LASSO's behavior. LASSO can yield sparse solutions (at most $n$ non-zero coefficients for $n$ samples).
- **Degrees of freedom:** For Ridge, $\text{df}(\lambda) = \text{tr}\left[\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\right]$, which decreases monotonically from $p$ to $0$ as $\lambda$ increases.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Practice in KTU Labs:** Always **standardize** the predictors ($x_{ij} \leftarrow \frac{x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j}{s_{x_j}}$) before applying Ridge or LASSO. Without standardization, the penalty unfairly penalizes features that simply happen to have smaller natural scales.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

### Why is Regularization Needed?

When we have a linear regression model with many predictors relative to the number of observations, the matrix $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ becomes **non-invertible (singular)** or **ill-conditioned**, leading to:

1. **High Variance:** Small changes in training data cause large swings in estimated coefficients.
2. **Multicollinearity:** Correlated predictors produce unstable, inflated coefficient estimates.
3. **Overfitting:** The model memorizes noise in the training set rather than learning the underlying signal.

### The OLS Starting Point

For linear regression with $n$ observations and $p$ predictors, the OLS objective is:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2 = \lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} \rVert_2^2$$

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = 0$ gives the **Normal Equation**:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{OLS}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$

This requires $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ to be invertible, which fails when $p > n$ (the **$p > n$ problem**) or when predictors are linearly dependent.

### 2.1.1 Ridge Regression — The Operational Logic

**Step 1:** Add the L2 penalty term $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$ to the OLS objective.

**Step 2:** Minimize the penalized objective. The closed-form solution becomes:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{Ridge}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$

**Why this works:** The matrix $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I}$ is **always invertible** for $\lambda > 0$, because adding $\lambda$ to every diagonal element makes the matrix **positive definite**. This solves the $p > n$ problem elegantly.

**Step 3:** Interpret geometrically. Ridge imposes a **circular (L2-ball) constraint** on the coefficients: $\sum \beta_j^2 \leq t$. The OLS loss contours (ellipses) touch this ball, producing shrunken but non-zero coefficients.

### 2.1.2 LASSO Regression — The Operational Logic

**Step 1:** Add the L1 penalty term $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$ to the OLS objective.

**Step 2:** The objective is **non-differentiable** at zero (because of the absolute value), so there is no closed-form solution. Iterative algorithms like **Coordinate Descent** or **Least Angle Regression (LARS)** are used.

**Step 3:** Geometrically, LASSO imposes a **diamond-shaped (L1-ball) constraint**: $\sum \vert \beta_j \vert \leq t$. The sharp corners of the diamond make it highly likely that the OLS contour will intersect a corner, setting some coefficients to **exactly zero**.

**Why this works:** The geometry of the L1 constraint creates sparse solutions, performing **automatic feature selection**.

### 2.1.3 Elastic Net (Brief Mention)

Combines L1 and L2 penalties:

$$J_{\text{EN}}(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} \rVert_2^2 + \lambda_1 \sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert\beta_j\vert + \lambda_2 \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$$

It overcomes LASSO's limitation of selecting at most $n$ variables and handles grouped correlated variables better.

---

## 2.2 Bias-Variance Tradeoff

Regularization introduces a controlled amount of **bias** to drastically reduce **variance**:

| Aspect | OLS ($\lambda=0$) | Ridge ($\lambda$ small) | Ridge ($\lambda$ large) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bias | Low | Slightly Higher | High |
| Variance | High | Reduced | Very Low |
| Total Error | High (overfits) | **Minimum (sweet spot)** | High (underfits) |

The optimal $\lambda$ minimizes the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** on held-out validation data.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Ridge Objective | $J_R = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$ | L2-penalized SSE |
| LASSO Objective | $J_L = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert\beta_j\vert$ | L1-penalized SSE |
| Ridge Solution | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_R = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ | Closed-form unique solution |
| LASSO Soft Thresholding | $\hat{\beta}_j = S\left(\sum_i x_{ij} r_i, \frac{\lambda}{2}\right)$ | Coordinate descent update; $S(a,b) = \text{sign}(a)\max(\vert a \vert - b, 0)$ |
| Ridge Constraint Region | $\sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2 \leq t$ | L2-ball (sphere/hypersphere) |
| LASSO Constraint Region | $\sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert\beta_j\vert \leq t$ | L1-ball (diamond/octahedron) |
| Effective df (Ridge) | $\text{df}(\lambda) = \text{tr}\left[\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\right]$ | Saturated model df reduces from $p$ to $0$ |
| Effective df (LASSO) | $\text{df}(\lambda) = E[\text{number of non-zero } \beta_j]$ | Equals expected sparsity count |
| Standardization | $x_{ij}^{*} = \frac{x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_i (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)^2}}$ | Required preprocessing step |
| Bias-Variance MSE | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Decomposition of expected prediction error |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Bioinformatics & Genomics:** LASSO is used for gene selection — when there are thousands of genes (features) but only hundreds of samples, LASSO identifies the most relevant biomarkers.
- **Financial Econometrics:** Ridge is used in portfolio optimization and risk modeling where predictors (market indicators) are highly correlated.
- **Computer Vision:** Elastic Net is used in sparse coding and feature selection pipelines.
- **Signal Processing:** LASSO is used in compressed sensing to reconstruct sparse signals from limited measurements.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Ridge regression is used when sensor data is highly collinear and one needs stable predictive models.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ridge Regression Closed-Form Solution

### Setup
Let the model be $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$, where $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (p+1)}$ (with a column of ones for the intercept), and $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^{p+1}$. Assume the intercept $\beta_0$ is **not** penalized.

For the slope coefficients $\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_p$, the Ridge objective is:

$$J_R(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$$

### Step 1: Write in Matrix Form
Expanding the squared error:

$$J_R(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}) + \lambda \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{I}_p \boldsymbol{\beta}$$

where $\mathbf{I}_p$ is the $p \times p$ identity matrix (applied only to slopes).

### Step 2: Expand the Quadratic Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_R(\boldsymbol{\beta}) &= \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \lambda \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \boldsymbol{\beta} \\
&= \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross terms combine because $\mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is a scalar and equals its transpose $\boldsymbol{\beta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$.

### Step 3: Differentiate with Respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J_R}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} &= -2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Set the Gradient to Zero (First-Order Optimality Condition)

$$
\begin{aligned}
-2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\beta} &= 0 \\
(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\beta} &= \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} \\
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{Ridge}} &= (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical Ridge estimator**.

### Step 5: Existence and Uniqueness Proof

For any $\lambda > 0$, the matrix $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I}$ is **symmetric positive definite** because:

- $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ is symmetric positive semi-definite (eigenvalues $\geq 0$).
- Adding $\lambda \mathbf{I}$ shifts all eigenvalues by $+\lambda$, making them all strictly positive.
- A symmetric positive definite matrix is invertible.

Therefore, $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{Ridge}}$ exists and is **unique** for all $\lambda > 0$, even when $p > n$ (where OLS fails). This is a critical KTU high-yield point.

---

## 3.2 Orthogonalization Case: Closed-Form Shrinkage Factor

When $\mathbf{X}$ has been standardized and orthogonalized (i.e., $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I}$ after PCA), the Ridge solution simplifies beautifully.

### Step 1: Assume Orthonormal Predictors
In this case, the OLS estimate is $\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}} = \mathbf{x}_j^T \mathbf{y}$ for each $j$.

### Step 2: Substitute into the Ridge Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{Ridge}} &= \left[(\mathbf{I} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\right]_{jj} \cdot \mathbf{x}_j^T \mathbf{y} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + \lambda} \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpret

**Each Ridge coefficient is shrunk by a factor of $\frac{1}{1+\lambda}$ relative to OLS.**

- If $\lambda = 0$: $\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{Ridge}} = \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}}$ (no shrinkage).
- If $\lambda = 1$: coefficients are halved.
- As $\lambda \to \infty$: coefficients approach zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** This orthogonality case is the "Rosetta Stone" for understanding regularization geometrically and is a common exam question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the LASSO Coordinate Descent Update

### Step 1: The Non-Differentiability Issue

The LASSO objective is:

$$J_L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert\beta_j\vert$$

The function $\vert \beta_j \vert$ is **not differentiable at $\beta_j = 0$**, so setting the full gradient to zero is infeasible. Instead, **coordinate descent** iteratively optimizes one coefficient at a time.

### Step 2: Partial Residual Definition
Define the **partial residual** when updating $\beta_j$, with all other $\beta_k$ ($k \neq j$) held fixed:

$$r_i^{(j)} = y_i - \sum_{k \neq j} \beta_k x_{ik}$$

The objective for $\beta_j$ alone becomes:

$$J_L(\beta_j) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(r_i^{(j)} - \beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2 + \lambda \vert \beta_j \vert$$

### Step 3: Differentiate the Smooth Part

Ignoring the non-smooth $\lambda \vert \beta_j \vert$ term for the moment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_j}\left[\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(r_i^{(j)} - \beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2\right] &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}\left(r_i^{(j)} - \beta_j x_{ij}\right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} - \beta_j \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming **standardized predictors** ($\sum_i x_{ij}^2 = 1$):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_j}\left[\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(r_i^{(j)} - \beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} - \beta_j$$

### Step 4: Apply Subgradient Optimality Condition

For an optimum, we need the subgradient to contain zero. The subgradient of $\vert \beta_j \vert$ is:
- $-1$ if $\beta_j < 0$
- $[-1, +1]$ if $\beta_j = 0$
- $+1$ if $\beta_j > 0$

Setting the full subgradient to zero:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} - \beta_j + \lambda \cdot s_j = 0$$

where $s_j = \text{sign}(\beta_j)$ if $\beta_j \neq 0$, and $s_j \in [-1, 1]$ if $\beta_j = 0$.

### Step 5: Solve for $\beta_j$

**Case 1: $\beta_j > 0$.** Then $s_j = +1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} - \beta_j + \lambda &= 0 \\
\beta_j &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} - \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\beta_j > 0$, we need $\sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} > \lambda$, so:

$$\hat{\beta}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} - \lambda$$

**Case 2: $\beta_j < 0$.** Then $s_j = -1$:

$$\hat{\beta}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} + \lambda$$

**Case 3: $\beta_j = 0$.** This requires $\left\vert \sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} \right\vert \leq \lambda$.

### Step 6: Combine into the Soft-Thresholding Operator

The three cases unify into the **soft-thresholding operator**:

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_j = S\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)}, \; \frac{\lambda}{2}\right) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)}\right) \cdot \max\left(\left\vert \sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} \right\vert - \frac{\lambda}{2}, \; 0\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "soft" thresholding?** The inner statistic $\sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)}$ (which equals the OLS update) is shrunk toward zero by $\lambda/2$, and if its magnitude is less than $\lambda/2$, it is set to **exactly zero**. This is what gives LASSO its sparsity property.

---

## 3.4 Comparison: Ridge vs. LASSO Coefficients Path

### Derivation of the Coefficient Behavior

For orthonormal predictors:

- **Ridge:** $\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{Ridge}}(\lambda) = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}}}{1 + \lambda}$ — **never exactly zero**, decreases smoothly.
- **LASSO:** $\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{LASSO}}(\lambda) = \text{sign}(\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}}) \cdot \max\left(\left\vert \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}} \right\vert - \frac{\lambda}{2}, 0\right)$ — **exactly zero** when $\left\vert \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}} \right\vert < \lambda/2$.

### Numerical Demonstration

Suppose $p = 2$ and the OLS estimates (after orthonormalization) are $\hat{\beta}_1^{\text{OLS}} = 3.0$ and $\hat{\beta}_2^{\text{OLS}} = 0.4$. Let $\lambda = 1$.

**Ridge ($\lambda = 1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta}_1^{\text{Ridge}} &= \frac{3.0}{1 + 1} = 1.5 \\
\hat{\beta}_2^{\text{Ridge}} &= \frac{0.4}{1 + 1} = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

Both are non-zero.

**LASSO ($\lambda = 1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta}_1^{\text{LASSO}} &= \text{sign}(3.0) \cdot \max(\vert 3.0 \vert - 0.5, 0) = +1 \cdot 2.5 = 2.5 \\
\hat{\beta}_2^{\text{LASSO}} &= \text{sign}(0.4) \cdot \max(\vert 0.4 \vert - 0.5, 0) = +1 \cdot 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

LASSO eliminated $\beta_2$ entirely because its OLS estimate was below the threshold $\lambda/2 = 0.5$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** LASSO is more aggressive in zeroing out small coefficients, while Ridge keeps all features with diminished magnitudes. This is the **fundamental operational difference** that examiners love to test.

---

## 3.5 Bias-Variance Decomposition for Ridge (Analytical)

The expected prediction error at a new point $\mathbf{x}_0$ can be decomposed as:

$$\text{EPE}(\mathbf{x}_0) = \text{Var}(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}_0)) + [\text{Bias}(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}_0))]^2 + \sigma^2$$

For Ridge with orthonormal predictors:

- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{Ridge}}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{(1+\lambda)^2} \cdot 1$ (since $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}}) = \sigma^2$ under orthonormality).
- **Bias:** $\text{Bias}(\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{Ridge}}) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_j^{\text{Ridge}}] - \beta_j = \frac{\beta_j}{1+\lambda} - \beta_j = -\frac{\lambda \beta_j}{1+\lambda}$.

The squared bias is $\left(\frac{\lambda \beta_j}{1+\lambda}\right)^2$, which increases with $\lambda$. The variance decreases as $\frac{1}{(1+\lambda)^2}$. The trade-off yields a U-shaped total error curve with an optimal $\lambda^*$.

## 4.2 LASSO Coordinate Descent Algorithm Flow

The following diagram shows the iterative procedure by which LASSO solves the non-differentiable optimization problem by cycling through each coefficient and applying soft thresholding.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start: Initialize all beta_j = 0] --> S1[Standardize Predictors X and Center y]
    S1 --> S2[Set Convergence Tolerance epsilon]
    S2 --> S3[Loop Until Convergence]
    S3 --> S4[For each coefficient j from 1 to p]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Partial Residual r_i_minus_j]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Inner Product z_j = sum over i of x_ij times r_i_minus_j]
    S6 --> S7{Is absolute value of z_j less than lambda over 2?}
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Set beta_j_new = 0]
    S7 -->|No| S9[Set beta_j_new = sign of z_j times absolute z_j minus lambda over 2]
    S8 --> S10[Check Convergence: max absolute beta_j_new minus beta_j_old less than epsilon]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 -->|Not Converged| S3
    S10 -->|Converged| S11[Return Final Sparse Coefficient Vector]
    S11 --> S12[End]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Ridge Regression. Write down its objective function and explain the role of the tuning parameter $\lambda$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Ridge Regression is a regularized version of linear regression that adds an L2 penalty term — proportional to the square of the magnitudes of the coefficients — to the standard sum-of-squares loss function. It was proposed by Hoerl and Kennard (1970) to address multicollinearity and the $p > n$ problem.

**Objective Function (1 Mark):**

$$J_{\text{Ridge}}(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$$

**Role of $\lambda$ (1 Mark):** The non-negative tuning parameter $\lambda$ controls the strength of the penalty.
- When $\lambda = 0$: The method reduces to Ordinary Least Squares (OLS).
- When $\lambda \to \infty$: The coefficients are driven to zero, producing a null model.
- The optimal $\lambda$ is chosen via cross-validation to minimize prediction error on unseen data.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between L1 (LASSO) and L2 (Ridge) regularization with respect to their penalty terms and the nature of solutions they produce.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **L2 (Ridge)** | **L1 (LASSO)** |
|---|---|---|
| Penalty Term | $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ (squared magnitudes) | $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ (absolute magnitudes) |
| Geometric Shape | Spherical / circular constraint | Diamond-shaped constraint |
| Nature of Solution | All coefficients are shrunk but remain **non-zero** | Many coefficients become **exactly zero** — sparse |
| Computational Method | Closed-form solution via normal equation | Iterative (coordinate descent, LARS) |
| Feature Selection | Does not perform feature selection | Performs automatic feature selection |
| Best For | Multicollinearity, all features useful | High-dimensional data, sparse true model |

> [!NOTE]
> Both methods introduce bias to reduce variance, but L1 additionally produces interpretable sparse models.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive the closed-form solution for Ridge Regression coefficients. Show that the solution exists and is unique for any $\lambda > 0$, even when the number of predictors exceeds the number of observations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the geometric interpretation of LASSO regression. With a 2D diagram (verbal description), show why LASSO yields sparse solutions while Ridge does not. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Ridge Objective Function (1 Mark)**

$$J_R(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j^2$$

**Step 2: Matrix Formulation (1 Mark)**

$$J_R(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}) + \lambda \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \boldsymbol{\beta}$$

**Step 3: Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J_R}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} &= -2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + 2\lambda \boldsymbol{\beta} \\
&= -2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} + 2(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Set to Zero and Solve (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\beta} &= \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} \\
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{Ridge}} &= (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Existence and Uniqueness Proof (1 Mark)**

For any $\lambda > 0$, the matrix $(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})$ is symmetric positive definite. To see this, let $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ be any non-zero vector:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}^T(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v} &= \mathbf{v}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\mathbf{v} + \lambda \mathbf{v}^T\mathbf{v} \\
&= \lVert \mathbf{X}\mathbf{v} \rVert_2^2 + \lambda \lVert \mathbf{v} \rVert_2^2 > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the matrix is symmetric and positive definite, it is invertible. Therefore $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{Ridge}}$ exists and is unique, **regardless of the rank of $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$**. This is why Ridge handles $p > n$ where OLS fails.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Geometric Interpretation of LASSO vs Ridge (7 Marks):**

The regularized regression problem can be cast as a **constrained optimization**:

$$\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} \rVert_2^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad C(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \leq t$$

where $C(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2$ for Ridge and $C(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$ for LASSO.

**For Ridge (L2):** The constraint region is a **circle (in 2D) or hypersphere (in higher dimensions)**, with all points on the boundary having $\beta_1^2 + \beta_2^2 = t$. The OLS loss function forms concentric **elliptical contours** centered at the unconstrained OLS estimate. The optimum occurs where the smallest ellipse (best fit) just touches the circle. Because the circle has a **smooth, curved boundary** with no corners, the touching point is highly unlikely to lie exactly on a coordinate axis. Therefore, **both $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ are typically non-zero**.

**For LASSO (L1):** The constraint region is a **diamond (in 2D) or octahedron (in higher dimensions)** with vertices on the coordinate axes. The constraint is $\vert \beta_1 \vert + \vert \beta_2 \vert = t$, with corners at $(t, 0)$, $(-t, 0)$, $(0, t)$, $(0, -t)$. When the OLS elliptical contours expand, they will very often first touch the L1 constraint region at one of its **sharp corners**. At these corners, **one coefficient is exactly zero** while the other is non-zero. This geometric feature is precisely why LASSO produces **sparse solutions** — coefficients corresponding to unimportant predictors are set to zero.

**Visual Summary (2D Description):**

- **Ridge:** OLS ellipse tangent to a smooth circle → intersection has $\beta_1 \neq 0$ and $\beta_2 \neq 0$.
- **LASSO:** OLS ellipse tangent to a diamond → intersection likely at a corner → one coefficient is exactly zero.

This geometric insight explains why LASSO performs **automatic feature selection** while Ridge only shrinks coefficients.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**(a)** With appropriate derivations, explain how the LASSO regression problem is solved using coordinate descent and the **soft-thresholding operator**. Show that the soft-thresholded update produces sparse solutions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A dataset has $p = 5$ predictors that have been standardized and orthogonalized. The OLS estimates are $\hat{\beta}_1 = 2.5$, $\hat{\beta}_2 = 0.3$, $\hat{\beta}_3 = -1.8$, $\hat{\beta}_4 = 0.05$, $\hat{\beta}_5 = -0.1$. Compute the Ridge and LASSO coefficient estimates for $\lambda = 2$. Comment on the differences. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: LASSO Objective (1 Mark)**

$$J_L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert\beta_j\vert$$

The $\vert \beta_j \vert$ term is **not differentiable at $\beta_j = 0$**, so the standard gradient = 0 approach fails.

**Step 2: Coordinate Descent Setup (1 Mark)**

In coordinate descent, all coefficients except $\beta_j$ are held fixed. Define the partial residual:

$$r_i^{(j)} = y_i - \sum_{k \neq j} \beta_k x_{ik}$$

**Step 3: Single-Variable Subproblem (1 Mark)**

$$J_L(\beta_j) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(r_i^{(j)} - \beta_j x_{ij}\right)^2 + \lambda \vert \beta_j \vert$$

**Step 4: Subgradient Optimality (2 Marks)**

The subgradient of $J_L$ with respect to $\beta_j$ (with standardized predictors, $\sum_i x_{ij}^2 = 1$) is:

$$\partial_{\beta_j} J_L = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} + \beta_j + \lambda \cdot s_j = 0$$

where $s_j$ is the subgradient of $\vert \beta_j \vert$. Setting this to zero and applying case analysis:

- If $\beta_j > 0$: $\hat{\beta}_j = \sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} - \lambda$
- If $\beta_j < 0$: $\hat{\beta}_j = \sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} + \lambda$
- If $\beta_j = 0$: requires $\left\vert \sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} \right\vert \leq \lambda$

**Step 5: Soft-Thresholding Operator (2 Marks)**

Combining all cases:

$$\hat{\beta}_j = S\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} r_i^{(j)}, \frac{\lambda}{2}\right) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)}\right) \cdot \max\left(\left\vert \sum_i x_{ij} r_i^{(j)} \right\vert - \frac{\lambda}{2}, 0\right)$$

The function $S(a, b)$ is called the **soft-thresholding operator**. It produces sparsity because whenever the inner product's magnitude is less than the threshold $\lambda/2$, the output is **exactly zero**. This is the key mechanism that gives LASSO its feature-selection property.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Standardized and orthogonalized predictors, OLS estimates are:

$$\hat{\beta}_1^{\text{OLS}} = 2.5, \; \hat{\beta}_2^{\text{OLS}} = 0.3, \; \hat{\beta}_3^{\text{OLS}} = -1.8, \; \hat{\beta}_4^{\text{OLS}} = 0.05, \; \hat{\beta}_5^{\text{OLS}} = -0.1$$

**Ridge Calculation (using $\hat{\beta}_j^R = \hat{\beta}_j^{\text{OLS}} / (1 + \lambda)$ with $\lambda = 2$):** (3 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta}_1^R &= \frac{2.5}{1 + 2} = \frac{2.5}{3} \approx 0.833 \\
\hat{\beta}_2^R &= \frac{0.3}{3} = 0.100 \\
\hat{\beta}_3^R &= \frac{-1.8}{3} = -0.600 \\
\hat{\beta}_4^R &= \frac{0.05}{3} \approx 0.0167 \\
\hat{\beta}_5^R &= \frac{-0.1}{3} \approx -0.0333
\end{aligned}
$$

**All five Ridge coefficients are non-zero**, though greatly shrunk.

**LASSO Calculation (using soft-thresholding with $\lambda/2 = 1$):** (3 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\beta}_1^L &= \text{sign}(2.5) \cdot \max(\vert 2.5 \vert - 1, 0) = +1 \cdot 1.5 = 1.5 \\
\hat{\beta}_2^L &= \text{sign}(0.3) \cdot \max(\vert 0.3 \vert - 1, 0) = +1 \cdot 0 = 0 \\
\hat{\beta}_3^L &= \text{sign}(-1.8) \cdot \max(\vert -1.8 \vert - 1, 0) = -1 \cdot 0.8 = -0.8 \\
\hat{\beta}_4^L &= \text{sign}(0.05) \cdot \max(\vert 0.05 \vert - 1, 0) = +1 \cdot 0 = 0 \\
\hat{\beta}_5^L &= \text{sign}(-0.1) \cdot \max(\vert -0.1 \vert - 1, 0) = -1 \cdot 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**LASSO has set $\beta_2, \beta_4, \beta_5$ to exactly zero.**

**Comparison and Comments (1 Mark):**

| Coefficient | OLS | Ridge ($\lambda=2$) | LASSO ($\lambda=2$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\beta_1$ | 2.50 | 0.833 | 1.500 |
| $\beta_2$ | 0.30 | 0.100 | **0** |
| $\beta_3$ | -1.80 | -0.600 | -0.800 |
| $\beta_4$ | 0.05 | 0.017 | **0** |
| $\beta_5$ | -0.10 | -0.033 | **0** |

- **Ridge shrinks** all five coefficients by the same factor $\frac{1}{3}$ but **never zeroes them out**.
- **LASSO retains** only the predictors with strong signal ($\beta_1, \beta_3$) and **eliminates** the weak ones ($\beta_2, \beta_4, \beta_5$).
- This is the operational essence of LASSO performing **automatic feature selection**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize predictors** (1–2 mark loss): When asked to apply Ridge/LASSO on raw data, students often skip standardization. Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme specifically deduct marks for not mentioning the standardization prerequisite.
>
> 2. **Confusing the soft-thresholding constant** (1 mark loss): LASSO's threshold is $\lambda/2$ in some derivations and $\lambda$ in others, depending on whether the objective uses $\sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ or $\frac{1}{2} \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$. **Always** check the coefficient of the penalty in the question.
>
> 3. **Penalizing the intercept** (1 mark loss): The intercept $\beta_0$ should **never** be penalized. Forgetting this loses a mark.
>
> 4. **Saying Ridge "selects features"** (2 mark loss): Ridge **never** sets coefficients to exactly zero in finite samples. Only LASSO and Elastic Net perform feature selection.
>
> 5. **Drawing the geometry wrong** (2 mark loss): The L1 constraint is a **diamond** (corners on axes), and the L2 constraint is a **circle**. Drawing a square for L1 or oval for L2 is a common conceptual error.
>
> 6. **Mixing up closed-form vs iterative** (1 mark loss): Ridge has a **closed-form solution**. LASSO does **not** — it requires coordinate descent or LARS. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Ridge (L2) Objective:** $\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum \beta_j^2$
- **LASSO (L1) Objective:** $\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$
- **Ridge Solution:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ — always exists for $\lambda > 0$.
- **LASSO Solution:** No closed form; use coordinate descent with soft-thresholding $S(a, b) = \text{sign}(a) \cdot \max(\vert a \vert - b, 0)$.
- **Ridge with orthonormal $\mathbf{X}$:** Coefficients shrunk by factor $\frac{1}{1+\lambda}$.
- **LASSO with orthonormal $\mathbf{X}$:** Soft-threshold OLS coefficients by $\lambda/2$.
- **Sparsity:** LASSO produces sparse solutions (exact zeros); Ridge does not.
- **Geometric Insight:** L1 constraint = diamond (sharp corners → sparse); L2 constraint = circle (smooth → non-sparse).
- **Standardization is Mandatory:** Always standardize predictors before applying any penalty; otherwise, scale-dependent bias distorts coefficients.
- **Tuning $\lambda$:** Use **k-fold cross-validation** (typically $k = 5$ or $k = 10$).
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Increasing $\lambda$ increases bias, decreases variance; optimal $\lambda$ minimizes total prediction error.
- **Feature Selection:** LASSO is preferred for sparse, interpretable models; Ridge for multicollinear data.
- **Elastic Net:** Combines L1 and L2 penalties; best for grouped correlated variables.
- **Intercept is NOT penalized** — only the slope coefficients are subject to regularization.
- **Degrees of Freedom:** Ridge df = $\text{df}(\lambda)$ decreases continuously; LASSO df = number of non-zero coefficients.
- **Computational Cost:** Ridge is fast (closed form); LASSO is slower (iterative).

> **End of Module 2 Notes — LASSO and RIDGE Regularization**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Idea of Training, Testing, Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Idea of Training, Testing, Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Supervised Machine Learning**, the **Idea of Training, Testing, and Validation** refers to the disciplined, statistically rigorous procedure of partitioning an available dataset into three distinct, non-overlapping subsets, each serving a unique epistemological role in the model-building pipeline. This tripartite data-splitting protocol is the cornerstone of **Generalization Theory** — it ensures that a learned hypothesis performs well not merely on the data it has *seen* (memorization) but on *unseen* future instances drawn from the same underlying distribution (true learning).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The conceptual understanding of *Training, Testing, and Validation* sets the foundation for **Model Selection**, **Hyperparameter Tuning**, **Cross-Validation**, and the diagnosis of **Overfitting vs. Underfitting**. Mastery of this topic is a **mandatory prerequisite** for comprehending advanced topics such as Regularization, Ensemble Methods, and Neural Network Optimization.

### Formal Definitions of the Three Subsets

| Subset | Symbol | Role | Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Training Set** | $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ | Used to **fit** the model parameters $\theta$ by minimizing the empirical loss | During model training |
| **Validation Set** | $\mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}$ | Used to **tune hyperparameters**, perform model selection, and detect overfitting | During model development |
| **Test Set** | $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$ | Used to estimate the **true generalization error** of the final, fully-tuned model | Once, at the very end |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Form of Generalization:** A model $\hat{f}$ is said to *generalize* if its expected risk $\mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P_{\text{data}}}[\mathcal{L}(\hat{f}(x), y)]$ is close to its empirical risk $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}(\hat{f}(x_i), y_i)$. The Test Set provides an unbiased Monte-Carlo estimate of this expected risk.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Student Examination" Framework

To demystify these abstract concepts, let us map them onto a tangible real-world scenario every KTU student is intimately familiar with: **preparing for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE)**.

> [!TIP]
> **🍎 The Student Analogy**

> **Imagine you are a B.Tech student preparing for the KTU Machine Learning End Semester Exam.**

### 🎓 Training Set = Your Textbook and Lecture Notes
When your professor teaches you the concepts of Linear Regression, Decision Trees, and Gradient Descent, you are *absorbing patterns*, *learning formulas*, and *solving worked-out examples*. This is the **Training Phase**. Your "model" (your brain) is adjusting its internal parameters (neural weights) by repeatedly studying the same set of examples. Just as a model minimizes its loss function on training data, you minimize your "confusion" by re-reading chapters and practicing derivations.

### 📝 Validation Set = University Model Exam / Series Test
A few weeks before the actual university exam, the college conducts a **Model Examination** (Series Test 1 & 2). The questions here are *new* but drawn from the *same syllabus and difficulty distribution* as the real exam. You take this test, analyze your mistakes, identify weak areas (e.g., "I keep losing marks in Cross-Validation derivations"), and adjust your study strategy — perhaps allocating more hours to weak topics, trying a new mnemonic, or switching reference books. This is **Hyperparameter Tuning** and **Model Selection** — the phase where you iteratively refine your preparation approach based on feedback from a *proxy* exam.

### 🏆 Test Set = The Actual KTU University Exam
On the day of the KTU ESE, you sit for the *real* exam with paper code PCCST503. The questions are fresh, and you get **only one attempt**. Your grade here is the **unbiased estimate of your true competence**. Notice that you do NOT go back and "study" based on the actual exam paper — that would defeat the purpose. Similarly, the Test Set in ML is touched **exactly once**, after all training and validation are complete.

### 📊 The "Data Leakage" Parallel
Suppose a student memorizes the **exact model exam paper** and reproduces it verbatim in the university exam. They will score well, but this does NOT reflect true understanding. In ML, this catastrophic phenomenon is called **Data Leakage** — when information from the validation or test set inadvertently influences the training process, producing overly optimistic performance estimates that collapse in production.

### 🔄 Cross-Validation = "Multiple Series Tests with Averaging"
To reduce the variance of any single validation estimate, a student might take **5 different mock tests** (e.g., from different coaching institutes) and average their scores to obtain a more stable measure of preparedness. This is precisely the spirit of **k-Fold Cross-Validation**.

---

## 1.3 Why This Tripartite Splitting Is Non-Negotiable

A common student question: *"Why not just use all the data for training and report training accuracy?"*

The answer lies in the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** and the **Curse of Overfitting**:

- **Training Error** is monotonically non-increasing as model complexity grows. A 100-degree polynomial will perfectly fit every training point with **zero training error**, yet it will produce absurd predictions on new data.
- **Validation/Test Error** captures **generalization** — the true north star of machine learning.
- Without a held-out set, we have **no diagnostic tool** to detect overfitting, no mechanism to compare competing models, and no credible claim about real-world performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Reporting training accuracy as "model accuracy" in ESE answers. The examiner expects the **test/validation accuracy** as the credible performance metric. Always specify *which set* the reported accuracy refers to.

---

## 1.4 The Standard Data Partitioning Ratios

While the optimal split ratio depends on dataset size and problem context, the following conventions are widely accepted in industry and academia:

| Dataset Size (N) | Train | Validation | Test | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small (N < 1,000) | 60% | 20% | 20% | Standard holdout; use CV instead |
| Medium (1,000 ≤ N ≤ 100,000) | 70% | 15% | 15% | More data for training |
| Large (N > 100,000) | 80% | 10% | 10% | Test set size remains statistically meaningful |
| Very Large (N > 1,000,000) | 98% | 1% | 1% | Even 1% of 1M = 10,000 samples — sufficient |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 Examinations:** When asked to "suggest a suitable split for a dataset of size N," the answer must explicitly mention (a) the percentages, (b) the rationale tied to N, and (c) the need for **stratified sampling** in classification tasks to preserve class proportions.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Three Phases of the ML Pipeline

### 🔹 Phase 1: The Training Phase

**Objective:** Learn the mapping $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ by minimizing an empirical risk function.

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Initialize** model parameters $\theta$ (e.g., randomly or via He/Xavier initialization for neural networks).
2. **Iterate** over mini-batches of $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ for $E$ epochs.
3. **Forward Pass:** Compute predictions $\hat{y}_i = f_\theta(x_i)$ for each training sample.
4. **Loss Computation:** Evaluate the loss function $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}_i, y_i)$ — e.g., **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** for regression or **Cross-Entropy Loss** for classification.
5. **Backward Pass (Gradient Computation):** Compute $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ via backpropagation.
6. **Parameter Update:** Apply the optimization rule (e.g., SGD: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$).
7. **Convergence Check:** Stop when validation loss plateaus (Early Stopping).

**The "Why" behind each step:**

- **Step 1:** Provides a starting point in the parameter space; bad initialization can lead to vanishing/exploding gradients.
- **Step 2:** Stochastic/mini-batch training introduces beneficial noise that helps escape local minima in non-convex landscapes.
- **Step 4:** Quantifies the *price* of wrong predictions; MSE penalizes large errors quadratically, while Cross-Entropy penalizes confident wrong predictions exponentially.
- **Step 7:** Prevents the model from "studying" the training set past the point of diminishing returns — a direct mechanism against overfitting.

**Mathematical Formulation of the Training Objective:**

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} \underbrace{\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} \vert} \sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(x_i),\, y_i\big)}_{\text{Empirical Risk on Training Set}} + \lambda \underbrace{\Omega(\theta)}_{\text{Regularizer (optional)}}$$

where $\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} \vert$ denotes the cardinality of the training set, and $\lambda$ controls regularization strength.

---

### 🔹 Phase 2: The Validation Phase

**Objective:** Estimate the generalization performance of *candidate models* and select the best hyperparameters $\alpha$.

**Operational Logic:**

1. After each training epoch (or after each hyperparameter configuration), **pause** and evaluate the current model on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}$.
2. **Compute validation metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score (classification); RMSE, MAE, $R^2$ (regression).
3. **Decision Points:**
   - If validation loss is *still decreasing* → continue training (model is still learning).
   - If validation loss *begins increasing* while training loss continues decreasing → **Early Stop** (overfitting detected).
   - Compare validation scores across different hyperparameter settings (e.g., learning rate $\eta \in \{0.1, 0.01, 0.001\}$) and **select the configuration with the lowest validation loss**.

**The "How" — Validation as Model Selection Engine:**

The validation set acts as a **proxy for the unknown true distribution $P_{\text{data}}$**. By holding out a portion of data, we simulate the "future" — the unseen data the model will encounter in production. The validation phase is where **all human-driven design decisions** (architecture choice, regularization strength, feature engineering choices) are made. Critically, the validation set is *consulted* repeatedly, which means it gradually becomes "polluted" with implicit information from past model versions — this is why a final, untouched test set is essential.

---

### 🔹 Phase 3: The Test Phase

**Objective:** Provide a **single, unbiased, final estimate** of the chosen model's generalization performance.

**Operational Logic:**

1. **Lock** the model: After validation-based model selection, no further parameter changes are allowed.
2. **Evaluate** $f_{\hat{\theta}_{\text{best}}}$ on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$ exactly once.
3. **Report** the test metrics as the authoritative performance claim.

**The "Why" — The Sanctity of the Test Set:**

- The test set simulates the **true deployment scenario** — a future, unseen data stream.
- It is **never used for any learning, tuning, or decision-making**.
- A single evaluation ensures **no leakage** from the test set into the model design.
- Published academic results and KTU project reports should always report **test-set metrics**, not training or validation metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The test set is analogous to the **Kerala University Final Exam** — a one-shot, unbiased assessment. Touching it during preparation (training/validation) invalidates the entire evaluation process.

---

## 2.2 Cross-Validation — A Statistical Refinement

When the dataset is too small to afford a clean three-way split, **Cross-Validation (CV)** provides a more robust performance estimate. The most common variant is **k-Fold Cross-Validation**.

### Algorithm: k-Fold Cross-Validation

1. **Shuffle** the dataset randomly.
2. **Split** the dataset into $k$ equal-sized, mutually exclusive folds $\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{F}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{F}_k$.
3. For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$:
   - Use $\mathcal{F}_i$ as the **validation fold**.
   - Use $\bigcup_{j \neq i} \mathcal{F}_j$ as the **training fold**.
   - Train the model and record the validation score $S_i$.
4. **Aggregate:** Compute the mean $\bar{S} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$ and the standard deviation $\sigma_S = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (S_i - \bar{S})^2}$.

**The "Why" behind k-Fold CV:**

- **Every data point is used for both training and validation** → no data is "wasted."
- The mean $\bar{S}$ is a **lower-variance** estimate of generalization performance than a single holdout split.
- The standard deviation $\sigma_S$ quantifies the **stability** of the model across different data partitions.

**Special Cases:**

- **$k = N$ (Leave-One-Out CV, LOOCV):** Maximum training data per fold, but computationally expensive and high variance in estimates.
- **$k = 5$ or $k = 10$:** Industry-standard choices — balance between bias and variance.
- **Stratified k-Fold:** Preserves the class distribution in each fold — *essential* for imbalanced classification.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Why $k=10$?", the answer is: it empirically provides a good trade-off between (a) computational cost, (b) bias of the estimate, and (c) variance of the estimate, as established by Kohavi's 1995 study.

---

## 2.3 The Bias-Variance Decomposition — The Theoretical Bedrock

The expected prediction error of any model can be decomposed into three irreducible components:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2\big[\hat{f}(x)\big]}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}\big[\hat{f}(x)\big]}_{\text{Sensitivity to Training Data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

| Component | Meaning | Diagnostic | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bias²** | Error from oversimplified assumptions; model misses relevant patterns | High training error + High test error (underfitting) | Increase model complexity, add features, reduce regularization |
| **Variance** | Error from sensitivity to small fluctuations in training data | Low training error + High test error (overfitting) | More data, regularization, dropout, ensemble methods |
| **$\sigma^2_\varepsilon$** | Intrinsic noise in the data generating process (Bayes error) | Cannot be reduced by any model | Improve data quality, better measurement instruments |

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation and Test Errors as Proxies:** Validation error and test error jointly serve as empirical proxies for the **Bias + Variance** components. A large gap between training and validation error indicates high variance (overfitting); both errors being large indicates high bias (underfitting).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a must-memorize reference for KTU ESE 2024.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Risk (Training Loss) | $\hat{R}_{\text{emp}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$ | Average loss over the training set |
| 2 | Expected Risk (True Generalization Error) | $R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P}[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)]$ | Loss over the true data distribution |
| 3 | MSE (Regression Loss) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error |
| 4 | Cross-Entropy (Classification Loss) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{ic} \log(\hat{p}_{ic})$ | Multi-class log loss |
| 5 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}$ | Classification accuracy |
| 6 | Precision | $\text{Prec} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | Positive predictive value |
| 7 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Rec} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | True positive rate |
| 8 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall |
| 9 | k-Fold CV Mean Score | $\bar{S}_{CV} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$ | Mean of $k$ validation scores |
| 10 | k-Fold CV Std Dev | $\sigma_{CV} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (S_i - \bar{S}_{CV})^2}$ | Bessel-corrected standard deviation |
| 11 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_\varepsilon$ | Fundamental decomposition of error |
| 12 | Data Split Ratios | Train : Val : Test $= 60:20:20$ (or $70:15:15$ or $80:10:10$) | Depends on dataset size |
| 13 | Stratified Sampling Constraint | $\frac{\vert \mathcal{F}_i^{(c)} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{F}_i \vert} \approx \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}^{(c)} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert}$ | Preserves class fraction in each fold |
| 14 | Holdout Test Estimate Variance | $\text{Var}(\hat{R}_{\text{test}}) \approx \frac{\sigma^2}{N_{\text{test}}}$ | Larger test set → lower variance estimate |
| 15 | Early Stopping Criterion | Stop when $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(t) > \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(t - \tau)$ for patience $\tau$ | Validation loss hasn't improved in $\tau$ epochs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Concepts:** In real-world ML systems deployed at companies like Google, Amazon, and Infosys (major KTU recruiters), these splitting and validation protocols are the **first line of defense** against model failures in production. A model with 99% training accuracy and 60% test accuracy is a deployment disaster — these protocols prevent such failures.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Bias-Variance Decomposition for Squared Error Loss

This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU ESE Module 2. We derive it from first principles with exhaustive algebraic steps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Let the true relationship be $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is zero-mean noise with $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2_\varepsilon$. Let $\hat{f}(x)$ be the model's prediction, which depends on the training set $\mathcal{D}$. We seek to decompose $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2]$.

### Step 1: Write the Squared Error

We begin with the squared difference between the true target and the model prediction:

$$(y - \hat{f}(x))^2$$

### Step 2: Add and Subtract the Expected Model Output

To introduce the concept of "bias," we insert the term $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)]$, denoted $\bar{f}(x)$ for brevity:

$$(y - \hat{f}(x))^2 = \big( (y - \bar{f}(x)) - (\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x)) \big)^2$$

### Step 3: Expand Using the Algebraic Identity $(a - b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$

With $a = y - \bar{f}(x)$ and $b = \hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x)$:

$$(y - \hat{f}(x))^2 = (y - \bar{f}(x))^2 - 2(y - \bar{f}(x))(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x)) + (\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2$$

### Step 4: Take Expectation Over the Training Set Distribution

Taking $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\cdot]$ on both sides, noting that $y$ is independent of $\mathcal{D}$ (since $y$ is a future, unseen label), so $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[y] = y$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(y - \bar{f}(x))^2] - 2\,\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(y - \bar{f}(x))(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x))] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2]$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Cross Term

In the middle term, $(y - \bar{f}(x))$ does not depend on $\mathcal{D}$ (it depends only on $x$ and the true $f$), so it can be pulled out of the expectation:

$$-2\,(y - \bar{f}(x)) \cdot \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x)]$$

But $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x)] = \bar{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x) = 0$ by definition of $\bar{f}(x)$.

Therefore, the **cross term vanishes entirely**:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(y - \bar{f}(x))^2] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2]$$

### Step 6: Decompose the First Term Further

Now we expand $(y - \bar{f}(x))^2$ by adding and subtracting $f(x)$ — the true function (without noise):

$$(y - \bar{f}(x))^2 = \big( (f(x) + \varepsilon - \bar{f}(x)) \big)^2 = \big( (f(x) - \bar{f}(x)) + \varepsilon \big)^2$$

Expanding:

$$(y - \bar{f}(x))^2 = (f(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2 + 2\varepsilon(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)) + \varepsilon^2$$

### Step 7: Take Expectation of the First Term

Taking $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, \varepsilon}$ (note: $f(x)$, $\bar{f}(x)$ are deterministic w.r.t. $\varepsilon$):

$$\mathbb{E}[(y - \bar{f}(x))^2] = (f(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2 + 2(f(x) - \bar{f}(x))\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2]$$

Since $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] = \text{Var}(\varepsilon) + (\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon])^2 = \sigma^2_\varepsilon$:

$$\mathbb{E}[(y - \bar{f}(x))^2] = (f(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2 + \sigma^2_\varepsilon$$

### Step 8: Assemble the Final Decomposition

Combining Steps 5 and 7:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \underbrace{(f(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2_\varepsilon}_{\text{Irreducible Error}}$$

This is the **Bias-Variance Decomposition** in its most general form.

### Step 9: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^2_\varepsilon}$$

where:

$$\text{Bias}(\hat{f}(x)) = f(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)] = f(x) - \bar{f}(x)$$

$$\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\big[(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2\big]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** Bias measures how far the *average* model prediction is from the *truth*. Variance measures how much the model prediction *fluctuates* across different training sets. The total error is the sum of these two model-dependent terms plus the irreducible noise $\sigma^2_\varepsilon$ which **no model can ever eliminate**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: k-Fold Cross-Validation Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A KTU student trains a Logistic Regression classifier for spam email detection. They perform **5-Fold Cross-Validation** and obtain the following fold-wise accuracies: $S_1 = 0.92$, $S_2 = 0.89$, $S_3 = 0.94$, $S_4 = 0.91$, $S_5 = 0.88$. Compute the mean CV score and the standard deviation. Also comment on model stability.

### Step 1: Compute the Mean CV Score

The mean is the arithmetic average:

$$\bar{S}_{CV} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i = \frac{0.92 + 0.89 + 0.94 + 0.91 + 0.88}{5}$$

Sum the fold accuracies:

$$0.92 + 0.89 + 0.94 + 0.91 + 0.88 = 4.54$$

Divide by $k = 5$:

$$\bar{S}_{CV} = \frac{4.54}{5} = 0.908$$

### Step 2: Compute the Deviations from the Mean

For each fold, compute $d_i = S_i - \bar{S}_{CV}$:

$$d_1 = 0.92 - 0.908 = +0.012$$

$$d_2 = 0.89 - 0.908 = -0.018$$

$$d_3 = 0.94 - 0.908 = +0.032$$

$$d_4 = 0.91 - 0.908 = +0.002$$

$$d_5 = 0.88 - 0.908 = -0.028$$

### Step 3: Square the Deviations

$$d_1^2 = (0.012)^2 = 0.000144$$

$$d_2^2 = (-0.018)^2 = 0.000324$$

$$d_3^2 = (0.032)^2 = 0.001024$$

$$d_4^2 = (0.002)^2 = 0.000004$$

$$d_5^2 = (-0.028)^2 = 0.000784$$

### Step 4: Sum the Squared Deviations

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} d_i^2 = 0.000144 + 0.000324 + 0.001024 + 0.000004 + 0.000784 = 0.002280$$

### Step 5: Compute the Variance (Bessel-Corrected, i.e., Sample Variance)

The sample variance uses denominator $(k-1)$ instead of $k$ to correct for the bias of using $\bar{S}$ (computed from the same data) as an estimate of $\mu$:

$$s^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} d_i^2}{k - 1} = \frac{0.002280}{5 - 1} = \frac{0.002280}{4} = 0.000570$$

### Step 6: Compute the Standard Deviation

$$s = \sqrt{s^2} = \sqrt{0.000570} \approx 0.02387$$

### Step 7: Final Result

$$\boxed{\bar{S}_{CV} = 0.908, \quad s \approx 0.0239 \quad \text{or} \quad 2.39\%}$$

**Interpretation for the KTU student:**

- The model achieves an **average accuracy of 90.8%** across 5 folds — a strong performance.
- The standard deviation of **2.39%** is relatively small, indicating that the model's performance is **stable** across different data partitions — a hallmark of a well-generalizing model.
- **Coefficient of Variation (CV):** $CV = s / \bar{S}_{CV} = 0.0239 / 0.908 \approx 0.0263$ or **2.63%**, which is low — confirming stability.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always report BOTH the mean and standard deviation of CV scores. Reporting only the mean hides critical information about model stability. Examiners specifically look for the $\pm$ notation (e.g., $90.8\% \pm 2.4\%$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Computing Generalization Gap (Overfitting Diagnosis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A deep neural network is trained on a 10,000-sample dataset (80% train, 10% val, 10% test). After 50 epochs, the student observes: Training Accuracy = 99.2%, Validation Accuracy = 71.5%, Test Accuracy = 70.8%. Diagnose the model and recommend a remedy.

### Step 1: Compute the Generalization Gap

The **Generalization Gap** is the difference between training and validation performance:

$$\text{Gap} = \text{Acc}_{\text{train}} - \text{Acc}_{\text{val}} = 99.2\% - 71.5\% = 27.7\%$$

### Step 2: Compute the Train-Test Gap

$$\text{Gap}_{\text{test}} = \text{Acc}_{\text{train}} - \text{Acc}_{\text{test}} = 99.2\% - 70.8\% = 28.4\%$$

### Step 3: Compute the Val-Test Gap (Consistency Check)

$$\text{Gap}_{\text{val-test}} = \text{Acc}_{\text{val}} - \text{Acc}_{\text{test}} = 71.5\% - 70.8\% = 0.7\%$$

### Step 4: Diagnosis

A **Generalization Gap of 27.7%** with high training accuracy and similar validation/test accuracy is a **textbook signature of severe overfitting** (high variance regime). The model has memorized the training set but fails to generalize.

### Step 5: Recommended Remedies

1. **Data Augmentation** — increase effective dataset size.
2. **Regularization** — apply L2 weight decay with $\lambda > 0$ or L1 regularization.
3. **Dropout** — randomly deactivate neurons during training (typical rate: 0.2–0.5).
4. **Early Stopping** — halt training when validation loss begins to rise.
5. **Reduce Model Capacity** — fewer layers/neurons.
6. **Cross-Validation** — for more robust hyperparameter selection.
7. **Ensemble Methods** — Bagging reduces variance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake in Diagnosis:** Students often confuse high bias with high variance. **Memorize these signatures:**
> - **Underfitting (High Bias):** Train Acc LOW + Test Acc LOW, Gap SMALL.
> - **Overfitting (High Variance):** Train Acc HIGH + Test Acc LOW, Gap LARGE.
> - **Good Fit:** Train Acc HIGH + Test Acc HIGH, Gap SMALL.

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart: The Complete Training-Validation-Testing Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the end-to-end workflow of the training-validation-testing protocol, including decision points and feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START: Raw Dataset D of size N]) --> splitNode{Choose Splitting Strategy}
    splitNode -- Small N --> holdoutNode[Holdout Split 60:20:20]
    splitNode -- Large N --> ratioNode[Ratio Split 80:10:10]
    splitNode -- Imbalanced Classes --> stratNode[Stratified k-Fold CV]
    holdoutNode --> trainSet[Training Set Dtrain]
    ratioNode --> trainSet
    stratNode --> trainSet
    trainSet --> trainLoop[Train Model with Gradient Descent]
    trainLoop --> epochCheck{After Each Epoch}
    epochCheck -- Continue --> trainLoop
    epochCheck -- Stop Signal --> valSet[Validation Set Dval]
    valSet --> metricNode[Compute Val Accuracy and Val Loss]
    metricNode --> decisionNode{Validation Diagnosis}
    decisionNode -- High Train Low Val --> overfit[OVERFITTING DETECTED: Apply Regularization, Dropout, Early Stop]
    decisionNode -- Low Train Low Val --> underfit[UNDERFITTING DETECTED: Increase Complexity, Add Features]
    decisionNode -- High Train High Val --> goodfit[GOOD FIT: Proceed to Test Evaluation]
    overfit --> tuneNode[Hyperparameter Tuning Loop]
    underfit --> tuneNode
    goodfit --> testSet[Test Set Dtest - UNTOUCHED]
    tuneNode --> valSet
    testSet --> finalMetric[Report Final Test Accuracy: 91.3 percent plus or minus 1.2]
    finalMetric --> endNode([END: Model Deployed to Production])
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Note the **feedback loop** from `overfit` and `underfit` back to `valSet` — this represents the **iterative** nature of model development. The `testSet` block has **no outgoing arrow** to any tuning node — this visually enforces the **one-shot, untouchable** nature of the test set.

## 4.4 Data Leakage Avoidance Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData[Raw Dataset D]
    rawData --> splitA[Step 1: Split into Dtrain, Dval, Dtest BEFORE any preprocessing]
    splitA --> dtrain[Dtrain: 70 percent]
    splitA --> dval[Dval: 15 percent]
    splitA --> dtest[Dtest: 15 percent LOCKED]
    dtrain --> fitScaler[Step 2a: Fit StandardScaler on Dtrain ONLY]
    fitScaler --> transformTrain[Step 2b: Transform Dtrain using fitted scaler]
    dval --> transformVal[Step 2c: Transform Dval using Dtrain fitted scaler]
    dtest --> transformTest[Step 2d: Transform Dtest using Dtrain fitted scaler]
    transformTrain --> trainModel[Step 3: Train Model on Transformed Dtrain]
    trainModel --> evalVal[Step 4: Evaluate on Transformed Dval for Hyperparameter Tuning]
    evalVal --> finalTune[Step 5: Retrain on Dtrain plus Dval with Best Hyperparameters]
    finalTune --> evalTest[Step 6: ONE-SHOT Evaluation on Transformed Dtest]
    evalTest --> reportAcc[Step 7: Report Final Test Accuracy]
    rawData -.->|WRONG: Leakage| leakage[Scaling fitted on FULL dataset before split: This causes test information to leak into training and inflates accuracy]
    leakage --> badResult[Overly Optimistic and Unreliable Test Accuracy]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Data Leakage — The Silent Killer:** A surprisingly common KTU project error is computing the mean/std for normalization using the **entire dataset** (including test) **before** splitting. This causes information from the test set to "leak" into the training process, producing artificially high test accuracy that does NOT generalize. Always fit preprocessors on the **training set only**.
