<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)** is a single scalar metric that quantifies the **overall discriminative ability** of a binary classifier across **all possible decision thresholds**. Formally, it is the area bounded by the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, the x-axis, and the vertical line at FPR = 1.

For a binary classifier that outputs a continuous score $s(x)$ for each input, the **ROC curve** is generated by plotting the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** as the classification threshold $\tau$ is swept from $+\infty$ down to $-\infty$. The AUC integrates this curve and is bounded in the closed interval $[0, 1]$.

The mathematically rigorous definition is given by:

$$
AUC \;=\; \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(t)) \, dt
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A more interpretable equivalent form is the **Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney (WMW) statistic**, which interprets AUC as the probability that a randomly drawn **positive** sample is ranked higher (i.e., assigned a larger score) than a randomly drawn **negative** sample.

$$
AUC \;=\; P\bigl(s(x^{+}) \;>\; s(x^{-})\bigr)
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Bouncer at a Club**
> Imagine a bouncer at an exclusive club. VIP guests (positives) and regular guests (negatives) arrive one by one, each with a "score" representing how fancy their outfit looks. The bouncer sets a threshold — only those above a certain "fanciness" get in. As the bouncer **lowers** the threshold, more VIPs and more regulars get in.
>
> - The **ROC curve** tracks how good the bouncer is at the *ranking* task — can he/she rank VIPs above regulars when money is on the line?
> - The **AUC** answers: *"If I pick one VIP and one regular at random, what is the probability the VIP is ranked higher?"*
> - **AUC = 1.0** → Perfect bouncer. Every VIP ranks above every regular.
> - **AUC = 0.5** → Random guessing. The bouncer is flipping a coin.
> - **AUC < 0.5** → Worse than random! The bouncer is consistently ranking regulars above VIPs (just invert the decision and you'd have a perfect classifier).

## 1.3 Key Boundary Values

| AUC Value | Interpretation | Symbol |
|-----------|----------------|--------|
| **1.0** | Perfect classifier | $\bigstar$ |
| **0.9 – 0.99** | Excellent discrimination | $\bigstar\bigstar$ |
| **0.7 – 0.89** | Good discrimination | $\bigstar$ |
| **0.5 – 0.69** | Poor / weak discrimination | $\dagger$ |
| **0.5** | Random guessing (no skill) | $\oslash$ |
| **< 0.5** | Worse than random (invert!) | $\times$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** An AUC of 0.5 does **not** mean the classifier is *useless in a specific business sense* — it means the classifier has **no ranking power** better than chance. Even at AUC = 0.5, the model may still be deployed with a calibrated threshold if the cost of false positives and false negatives are asymmetric.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks — TPR and FPR

For any classification threshold $\tau$, the predicted label is:

$$
\hat{y} \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s(x) \geq \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

From the resulting confusion matrix, the two rates that drive the ROC curve are:

$$
TPR(\tau) \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \;=\; \frac{TP}{P} \quad \text{(also called Recall or Sensitivity)}
$$

$$
FPR(\tau) \;=\; \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \;=\; \frac{FP}{N} \quad \text{(equals } 1 - \text{Specificity)}
$$

where $P$ is the total number of actual positives and $N$ is the total number of actual negatives in the dataset.

## 2.2 ROC Curve Construction Logic

1. Obtain predicted **scores** (not hard labels) from the classifier — e.g., posterior probabilities from Logistic Regression, decision values from SVM, leaf indices from Random Forests.
2. Sort all $m = P + N$ samples in **descending order** of their scores.
3. Start with $\tau = +\infty$ (every sample predicted negative). Plot the point $(0, 0)$.
4. For each sample in the sorted list, **decrement** $\tau$ until the next sample is included. Mark that sample as predicted positive and update the running TP and FP counts.
5. Plot the new point $(FPR, TPR)$.
6. After processing all $m$ samples, the curve ends at $(1, 1)$ (every sample predicted positive).
7. Connect the points in order to form the piecewise-linear ROC curve.

## 2.3 Properties of AUC

- **Threshold-invariant:** Unlike accuracy, precision, or F1-score, AUC is computed by considering *all* thresholds simultaneously. It rewards models that rank correctly, even if their absolute probability calibration is poor.
- **Scale-invariant:** AUC only depends on the *order* of scores, not on their absolute values. A Logistic Regression and an SVM producing identical rankings will have identical AUCs.
- **Discriminative, not generative:** AUC measures *separation* between classes, not the correctness of absolute probability estimates.
- **Equal-cost assumption:** AUC is most informative when misclassification costs are roughly equal for both classes and class distribution is not extremely skewed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** AUC is the **gold standard** in domains such as **medical diagnosis, credit scoring, fraud detection, and information retrieval (ranking)** — wherever a model must produce a *rank-order* of risk, not a hard yes/no prediction.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **complete set of equations** required to solve any AUC problem on the KTU 2024 ESE paper. Memorize these symbols — they appear in nearly every machine learning metrics question.

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition |
|:------:|---------|---------------------|
| $\text{AUC}$ | Area Under the ROC Curve | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR \, d(FPR)$ |
| $\text{AUC}_{\text{WMW}}$ | Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney form | $\dfrac{1}{P \cdot N} \sum\limits_{i: y_i = 1} \sum\limits_{j: y_j = 0} \mathbb{1}\!\left[s_i > s_j\right]$ |
| $\text{AUC}_{\text{trap}}$ | Trapezoidal numerical form | $\sum\limits_{k=1}^{K-1} \dfrac{(FPR_{k+1} - FPR_k)(TPR_k + TPR_{k+1})}{2}$ |
| $\text{TPR}$ | True Positive Rate (Recall) | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{TP}{P}$ |
| $\text{FPR}$ | False Positive Rate | $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = \dfrac{FP}{N}$ |
| $\text{TNR}$ | True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $1 - FPR$ |
| $\text{FNR}$ | False Negative Rate | $1 - TPR$ |
| $\text{Gini}$ | Gini Coefficient (related) | $2 \cdot \text{AUC} - 1$ |
| $\text{Youden}_J$ | Youden's J statistic | $\max_{\tau}\!\left[TPR(\tau) - FPR(\tau)\right]$ |
| $P$ | # Positives in dataset | $TP + FN$ |
| $N$ | # Negatives in dataset | $FP + TN$ |
| $m$ | Total samples | $P + N$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Youden's J statistic** is *not* an AUC alternative — it is the single best threshold $\tau^{\star}$ on a given ROC curve, given by $\tau^{\star} = \arg\max_{\tau} \left( TPR(\tau) - FPR(\tau) \right)$. It is the *operating point* on the curve, not the area under it.

## 2.5 The Diagonal Line — What it Means

The line $TPR = FPR$ on the ROC plot represents the **random classifier**. Any curve bowing toward the top-left corner is performing better than random. The geometric area between the ROC curve and the diagonal is called the **Gini Coefficient**, related to AUC by:

$$
\text{Gini} \;=\; 2 \cdot \text{AUC} - 1
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: WMW Form from First Principles

We start with the empirical (sample-based) definition. Given $P$ positive samples and $N$ negative samples with predicted scores $\{s_1^{+}, s_2^{+}, \ldots, s_P^{+}\}$ and $\{s_1^{-}, s_2^{-}, \ldots, s_N^{-}\}$, we want to estimate:

$$
\theta \;=\; P\!\left(s^{+} > s^{-}\right)
$$

The empirical estimate of this probability is the fraction of all $P \cdot N$ positive–negative pairs in which the positive sample receives a higher score. This is exactly the WMW U-statistic:

$$
\hat{\theta} \;=\; \frac{1}{P \cdot N} \sum_{i=1}^{P} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\!\left(s_i^{+} > s_j^{-}\right)
$$

The indicator function $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ returns $1$ when the condition holds and $0$ otherwise. This estimator is unbiased when the positive and negative score distributions are independent.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence of WMW and Trapezoidal AUC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation is a **high-yield proof** that KTU examiners may ask as a 7-mark sub-question. Be ready to reproduce the integration step.

Consider the empirical CDF of the positive scores, $F_{+}(s) = P(s^{+} \leq s)$, and the empirical CDF of the negative scores, $F_{-}(s) = P(s^{-} \leq s)$. Under the assumption of independence between positive and negative scores:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(s^{+} > s^{-}) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P(s^{-} < s) \cdot f_{+}(s) \, ds \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} F_{-}(s) \cdot f_{+}(s) \, ds
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a Riemann–Stieltjes integral. If we change the variable of integration to $t = F_{+}(s)$, then $dt = f_{+}(s) \, ds$ and $F_{-}(s) = F_{-}(F_{+}^{-1}(t))$. When the threshold moves, a small increase in TPR corresponds to a fraction of the positives being newly included, and the corresponding FPR is exactly the fraction of negatives that were also wrongly included. This gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AUC} &= \int_{0}^{1} F_{-}\!\left(F_{+}^{-1}(t)\right) dt \\
&= \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(t)) \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Geometrically, this integral is exactly the area under the ROC curve — which equals the probability of correct ranking, completing the equivalence.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Trapezoidal Rule

**Problem Statement:** A binary classifier produces the following probabilistic scores for $P = 5$ positive and $N = 5$ negative test samples.

| Sample | True Label | Score |
|:------:|:----------:|:-----:|
| 1 | Positive | 0.90 |
| 2 | Positive | 0.70 |
| 3 | Positive | 0.60 |
| 4 | Positive | 0.40 |
| 5 | Positive | 0.30 |
| 6 | Negative | 0.80 |
| 7 | Negative | 0.50 |
| 8 | Negative | 0.35 |
| 9 | Negative | 0.20 |
| 10 | Negative | 0.10 |

**Step 1 — Sort in descending order of score:**

$$
\text{pos}(0.9),\; \text{neg}(0.8),\; \text{pos}(0.7),\; \text{pos}(0.6),\; \text{neg}(0.5),\; \text{pos}(0.4),\; \text{neg}(0.35),\; \text{pos}(0.3),\; \text{neg}(0.2),\; \text{neg}(0.1)
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize counts and walk through sorted list:**

We start at $(FPR, TPR) = (0, 0)$. At each step, we mark the current sample as predicted positive and update the running TP and FP counts.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{After pos}(0.9): && TP = 1,\; FP = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 1/5 = 0.20,\;\; FPR = 0/5 = 0.00 \\[4pt]
&\text{After neg}(0.8): && TP = 1,\; FP = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 0.20,\;\; FPR = 1/5 = 0.20 \\[4pt]
&\text{After pos}(0.7): && TP = 2,\; FP = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 2/5 = 0.40,\;\; FPR = 0.20 \\[4pt]
&\text{After pos}(0.6): && TP = 3,\; FP = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 3/5 = 0.60,\;\; FPR = 0.20 \\[4pt]
&\text{After neg}(0.5): && TP = 3,\; FP = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 0.60,\;\; FPR = 2/5 = 0.40 \\[4pt]
&\text{After pos}(0.4): && TP = 4,\; FP = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 4/5 = 0.80,\;\; FPR = 0.40 \\[4pt]
&\text{After neg}(0.35): && TP = 4,\; FP = 3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 0.80,\;\; FPR = 3/5 = 0.60 \\[4pt]
&\text{After pos}(0.3): && TP = 5,\; FP = 3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 5/5 = 1.00,\;\; FPR = 0.60 \\[4pt]
&\text{After neg}(0.2): && TP = 5,\; FP = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 1.00,\;\; FPR = 4/5 = 0.80 \\[4pt]
&\text{After neg}(0.1): && TP = 5,\; FP = 5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; TPR = 1.00,\;\; FPR = 5/5 = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate the ROC points:**

| Step $k$ | Sample | TP | FP | TPR ($y_k$) | FPR ($x_k$) |
|:--------:|:------:|:-:|:--:|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| 0 | start | 0 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 1 | pos(0.90) | 1 | 0 | 0.20 | 0.00 |
| 2 | neg(0.80) | 1 | 1 | 0.20 | 0.20 |
| 3 | pos(0.70) | 2 | 1 | 0.40 | 0.20 |
| 4 | pos(0.60) | 3 | 1 | 0.60 | 0.20 |
| 5 | neg(0.50) | 3 | 2 | 0.60 | 0.40 |
| 6 | pos(0.40) | 4 | 2 | 0.80 | 0.40 |
| 7 | neg(0.35) | 4 | 3 | 0.80 | 0.60 |
| 8 | pos(0.30) | 5 | 3 | 1.00 | 0.60 |
| 9 | neg(0.20) | 5 | 4 | 1.00 | 0.80 |
| 10 | neg(0.10) | 5 | 5 | 1.00 | 1.00 |

**Step 4 — Apply the trapezoidal rule:**

For each consecutive pair $(x_k, y_k)$ and $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$, the trapezoidal area is:

$$
\Delta_k \;=\; \frac{(x_{k+1} - x_k)(y_k + y_{k+1})}{2}
$$

Substituting values for the **non-zero width** trapezoids (i.e., where $x_{k+1} \neq x_k$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{(1\to 2)} &= \frac{(0.20 - 0.00)(0.20 + 0.20)}{2} = 0.04 \\[4pt]
\Delta_{(4\to 5)} &= \frac{(0.40 - 0.20)(0.60 + 0.60)}{2} = 0.12 \\[4pt]
\Delta_{(6\to 7)} &= \frac{(0.60 - 0.40)(0.80 + 0.80)}{2} = 0.16 \\[4pt]
\Delta_{(8\to 9)} &= \frac{(0.80 - 0.60)(1.00 + 1.00)}{2} = 0.20 \\[4pt]
\Delta_{(9\to 10)} &= \frac{(1.00 - 0.80)(1.00 + 1.00)}{2} = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum all trapezoidal areas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AUC} &= 0.04 + 0.12 + 0.16 + 0.20 + 0.20 \\[4pt]
&= 0.72
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification via Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney:**

Counting positive–negative pairs where positive > negative:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Pair } (0.9, \cdot): 5 \text{ wins} \quad (0.7, \cdot): 4 \text{ wins} \quad (0.6, \cdot): 4 \text{ wins} \\
&(0.4, \cdot): 3 \text{ wins} \quad (0.3, \cdot): 2 \text{ wins} \\
&\text{Total wins} = 5 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 2 = 18 \\
&\text{AUC}_{\text{WMW}} = \frac{18}{5 \times 5} = \frac{18}{25} = 0.72
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods yield $\text{AUC} = 0.72$, confirming the calculation. This is a **good** classifier (above 0.7) but not perfect.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Metric

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is frequently tested in KTU Module-2 questions. KTU examiners reward students who can clearly articulate *why* AUC is preferred in specific scenarios.

| Scenario | Preferred Metric | Why AUC Falls Short / Shines |
|----------|------------------|------------------------------|
| Balanced classes, equal misclassification cost | **Accuracy or AUC** | Both are valid; AUC gives more info |
| Imbalanced classes (e.g., 1% fraud) | **AUC-PR (Average Precision)** | AUC can be overly optimistic on skewed data |
| Need a calibrated probability | **Log-loss, Brier Score** | AUC ignores calibration; only measures ranking |
| Binary ranking problem (medical risk) | **AUC** | Threshold-free ranking is exactly what AUC captures |
| Multiclass classification | **One-vs-Rest AUC (macro/micro)** | Naive AUC only works on binary; extensions required |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ROC Curve Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Classifier f of x] --> B[Compute scores s of x for all m samples]
    B --> C[Sort samples in descending order of score]
    C --> D[Initialize TP=0, FP=0, TPR=0, FPR=0]
    D --> E[Plot point of 0 comma 0 on ROC plane]
    E --> F[For each sample in sorted list]
    F --> G{Is the sample a positive?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Increment TP counter]
    G -- No --> I[Increment FP counter]
    H --> J[Compute TPR = TP over P]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute FPR = FP over N]
    K --> L[Plot point of FPR comma TPR on ROC plane]
    L --> M{More samples remaining?}
    M -- Yes --> F
    M -- No --> N[End: Curve reaches point of 1 comma 1]
    N --> O[Apply Trapezoidal Rule under the curve]
    O --> P[Output AUC scalar value]
    P --> Q[Compare with AUC equals 0.5 baseline]
    Q -- Above 0.5 --> R[Better than random]
    Q -- Below 0.5 --> S[Invert predictions to improve]
    Q -- Exactly 0.5 --> T[No discriminative power]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Dual Interpretation of AUC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AUC Scalar] --> B[Geometric Interpretation]
    A --> C[Probabilistic Interpretation]
    B --> D[Area under the ROC curve in unit square]
    D --> E[Threshold-invariant summary of classifier]
    C --> F[Wilcoxon Mann Whitney statistic]
    F --> G[Probability that a random positive ranks above a random negative]
    G --> H[Discriminative power of the score function]
    E --> I[Useful for ranking tasks]
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Threshold-Sweep Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Threshold tau equals plus infinity] --> P2[Threshold tau equals 0.9]
    P2 --> P3[Threshold tau equals 0.8]
    P3 --> P4[Threshold tau equals 0.7]
    P4 --> P5[Threshold tau equals 0.6]
    P5 --> P6[Threshold tau equals 0.5]
    P6 --> P7[Threshold tau equals 0.4]
    P7 --> P8[Threshold tau equals 0.3]
    P8 --> P9[Threshold tau equals 0.2]
    P9 --> P10[Threshold tau equals 0.1]
    P10 --> P11[Threshold tau equals minus infinity]
    P1 --> Q1[Point of 0 comma 0]
    P2 --> Q2[Point of 0.00 comma 0.20]
    P3 --> Q3[Point of 0.20 comma 0.20]
    P4 --> Q4[Point of 0.20 comma 0.40]
    P5 --> Q5[Point of 0.20 comma 0.60]
    P6 --> Q6[Point of 0.40 comma 0.60]
    P7 --> Q7[Point of 0.40 comma 0.80]
    P8 --> Q8[Point of 0.60 comma 0.80]
    P9 --> Q9[Point of 0.60 comma 1.00]
    P10 --> Q10[Point of 0.80 comma 1.00]
    P11 --> Q11[Point of 1.00 comma 1.00]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above maps the **threshold sweep** from $+\infty$ down to $-\infty$ to the discrete ROC points computed in Section 3.3. Each "stair" in the curve corresponds to one sample in the sorted score list — vertical jumps occur when a positive sample is included (TPR rises), and horizontal jumps occur when a negative sample is included (FPR rises). The AUC is the area of the resulting shape.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions mirror the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Each Part-A question carries 3 marks; each Part-B sub-part carries 7 marks, totaling 14 marks. The question is designed per KTU Module Internal Choice — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`
**Define the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) for a binary classifier. What is the significance of AUC = 0.5 and AUC = 1.0?**

**Model Answer:**

The Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) is a scalar performance metric that measures the **overall discriminative ability** of a binary classifier across all possible decision thresholds. It is computed as the area of the region bounded by the ROC curve (plot of TPR vs FPR) and the axes of the unit square.

$$
\text{AUC} \;=\; \int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR} \, d(\text{FPR})
$$

**Significance of boundary values:**

- **AUC = 1.0** indicates a **perfect classifier** — every positive sample receives a strictly higher score than every negative sample, so the ROC curve passes through the top-left corner $(0, 1)$.
- **AUC = 0.5** indicates a **random classifier** — the model has no discriminative power beyond chance, and the ROC curve coincides with the diagonal $TPR = FPR$.
- **AUC < 0.5** indicates the classifier is **worse than random**; this typically signals an inverted score function or a buggy implementation, and the predictions can be flipped to obtain a useful model.

Mathematically, AUC also equals the probability $P(s^{+} > s^{-})$, i.e., the chance that a randomly chosen positive sample is ranked above a randomly chosen negative sample.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`
**Explain why AUC is considered a "threshold-invariant" metric. How does this property make it superior to plain accuracy in certain scenarios?**

**Model Answer:**

A metric is **threshold-invariant** if its value does not depend on the specific cut-off value chosen to convert soft scores into hard class labels. AUC is threshold-invariant because it is computed by integrating the ROC curve, which is itself constructed by **sweeping the threshold** from $+\infty$ down to $-\infty$ and recording $(FPR, TPR)$ at every operating point. Thus, AUC is a property of the **ranking** induced by the classifier, not of any single decision rule.

**Why this is superior to accuracy:**

Accuracy $= \frac{TP + TN}{P + N}$ depends entirely on a single chosen threshold $\tau$. If the threshold is poorly tuned — or if class priors shift between training and deployment — accuracy collapses, but AUC remains stable. For example, in a **fraud detection** scenario where only 0.1% of transactions are fraudulent, a model that always predicts "not fraud" achieves 99.9% accuracy yet is useless. AUC, in contrast, would correctly reflect that the model has no rank-ordering power. AUC is therefore the **gold standard for ranking-quality evaluation** in medical risk scoring, credit risk modelling, and information retrieval.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney (WMW) statistic interpretation of AUC. Show that the WMW statistic and the integral form of AUC are mathematically equivalent.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the empirical ranking probability.** Let $S^{+} = \{s_1^{+}, s_2^{+}, \ldots, s_P^{+}\}$ denote the scores of the $P$ positive samples and $S^{-} = \{s_1^{-}, s_2^{-}, \ldots, s_N^{-}\}$ denote the scores of the $N$ negative samples. Define:

$$
\hat{\theta} \;=\; P(s^{+} > s^{-}) \;=\; \frac{1}{P \cdot N} \sum_{i=1}^{P} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\!\left(s_i^{+} > s_j^{-}\right)
$$

This is the **Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney U-statistic**. It counts the fraction of all $P \cdot N$ positive–negative pairs in which the positive sample is ranked above the negative sample.

**Step 2 — Express as an integral over score values.** Assuming the positive and negative score distributions have CDFs $F_{+}$ and $F_{-}$ and densities $f_{+}$ and $f_{-}$ respectively, and that they are independent, we can write:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(s^{+} > s^{-}) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mathbb{1}(u > v) \, f_{+}(u) f_{-}(v) \, du \, dv \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[\int_{v}^{+\infty} f_{+}(u) \, du \right] f_{-}(v) \, dv \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} (1 - F_{+}(v)) \, f_{-}(v) \, dv
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Change of variable to ROC space.** Let $t = 1 - F_{+}(v)$ and $dt = -f_{+}(v) \, dv$ (this re-parameterization maps score thresholds to TPR). Equivalently, the lower limit $v \to -\infty$ maps to $t = 1$, and the upper limit $v \to +\infty$ maps to $t = 0$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(s^{+} > s^{-}) &= \int_{1}^{0} F_{-}\!\left(F_{+}^{-1}(1 - t)\right) (-dt) \\
&= \int_{0}^{1} F_{-}\!\left(F_{+}^{-1}(1 - t)\right) dt
\end{aligned}
$$

But the inner expression is exactly $TPR$ as a function of $FPR$ — because $1 - F_{+}(v)$ is the fraction of positives exceeding threshold $v$ (i.e., $TPR$), and $1 - F_{-}(v)$ is the fraction of negatives exceeding the same threshold (i.e., $1 - FPR$). Therefore:

$$
P(s^{+} > s^{-}) \;=\; \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(t)) \, dt \;=\; \text{AUC}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** The WMW statistic is the discrete (sample) estimator, and the ROC-integral is the continuous (population) form. They converge in the limit of large samples, hence the two definitions of AUC are mathematically equivalent.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A binary classifier is evaluated on a test set with 5 positive and 5 negative samples. The predicted scores are: Positives $\to$ {0.90, 0.70, 0.60, 0.40, 0.30}, Negatives $\to$ {0.80, 0.50, 0.35, 0.20, 0.10}. Compute the AUC using the trapezoidal rule and verify with the WMW statistic.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sort in descending order.** The combined sorted list with labels is:

$$
\text{pos}(0.9),\; \text{neg}(0.8),\; \text{pos}(0.7),\; \text{pos}(0.6),\; \text{neg}(0.5),\; \text{pos}(0.4),\; \text{neg}(0.35),\; \text{pos}(0.3),\; \text{neg}(0.2),\; \text{neg}(0.1)
$$

**Step 2 — Walk through and tabulate TPR/FPR.** With $P = 5$ and $N = 5$:

| Step | Sample | TP | FP | TPR | FPR |
|:----:|:------:|:-:|:--:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | start | 0 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 1 | pos(0.90) | 1 | 0 | 0.20 | 0.00 |
| 2 | neg(0.80) | 1 | 1 | 0.20 | 0.20 |
| 3 | pos(0.70) | 2 | 1 | 0.40 | 0.20 |
| 4 | pos(0.60) | 3 | 1 | 0.60 | 0.20 |
| 5 | neg(0.50) | 3 | 2 | 0.60 | 0.40 |
| 6 | pos(0.40) | 4 | 2 | 0.80 | 0.40 |
| 7 | neg(0.35) | 4 | 3 | 0.80 | 0.60 |
| 8 | pos(0.30) | 5 | 3 | 1.00 | 0.60 |
| 9 | neg(0.20) | 5 | 4 | 1.00 | 0.80 |
| 10 | neg(0.10) | 5 | 5 | 1.00 | 1.00 |

**Step 3 — Apply trapezoidal rule.** For each segment with non-zero FPR width:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{1\to 2} &= \frac{(0.20)(0.20 + 0.20)}{2} = 0.04 \\
\Delta_{4\to 5} &= \frac{(0.20)(0.60 + 0.60)}{2} = 0.12 \\
\Delta_{6\to 7} &= \frac{(0.20)(0.80 + 0.80)}{2} = 0.16 \\
\Delta_{8\to 9} &= \frac{(0.20)(1.00 + 1.00)}{2} = 0.20 \\
\Delta_{9\to 10} &= \frac{(0.20)(1.00 + 1.00)}{2} = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
\text{AUC} \;=\; 0.04 + 0.12 + 0.16 + 0.20 + 0.20 \;=\; 0.72
$$

**Step 4 — Verify with WMW.** Count the number of positive–negative pairs where the positive score exceeds the negative score:

- $0.90$ beats all 5 negatives: **5** wins
- $0.70$ beats 4 of 5 negatives (loses to 0.80): **4** wins
- $0.60$ beats 4 of 5 negatives (loses to 0.80): **4** wins
- $0.40$ beats 3 of 5 negatives (loses to 0.80, 0.50): **3** wins
- $0.30$ beats 2 of 5 negatives (loses to 0.80, 0.50, 0.35): **2** wins

$$
\text{Total wins} = 5 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 2 = 18, \quad \text{AUC}_{\text{WMW}} = \frac{18}{5 \times 5} = 0.72
$$

Both methods agree: $\boxed{\text{AUC} = 0.72}$, indicating a **good** classifier (above 0.7 threshold) but with room for improvement.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the construction of the ROC curve with reference to TPR and FPR. Discuss why AUC is threshold-independent and how this property is exploited in cost-sensitive applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**ROC Curve Construction:**

The Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve is constructed by plotting the **True Positive Rate (Recall)** on the y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (1 − Specificity)** on the x-axis. The curve is parameterized by a decision threshold $\tau$ applied to the classifier's continuous score $s(x)$:

$$
\hat{y}(x; \tau) \;=\; \mathbb{1}\!\left(s(x) \geq \tau\right)
$$

As $\tau$ is varied from $+\infty$ (no sample predicted positive) down to $-\infty$ (all samples predicted positive), the operating point $(FPR(\tau), TPR(\tau))$ traces out the ROC curve from $(0, 0)$ to $(1, 1)$.

**Threshold Independence of AUC:**

Unlike accuracy, precision, or recall — which are *point estimates* corresponding to one specific $\tau$ — AUC is computed by **integrating** the ROC curve over all possible $\tau$:

$$
\text{AUC} \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} TPR(\tau) \cdot \left|\frac{d \, FPR(\tau)}{d\tau}\right| d\tau
$$

Hence AUC depends only on the **ranking order** of scores, not on where the threshold is placed. This means:

- AUC is **invariant** to monotonic transformations of the score (e.g., applying a sigmoid or logit).
- A model with AUC = 0.85 will retain this value even if business constraints force a threshold change tomorrow.
- Calibration drift in production does not affect AUC, only the chosen operating point on the curve.

**Exploitation in Cost-Sensitive Applications:**

In real-world deployment, the optimal threshold depends on the **cost ratio** $c = \frac{C_{FN}}{C_{FP}}$ between false negatives and false positives. AUC allows the data scientist to **defer threshold selection** until business costs are known. For example:

- In **medical screening**, a low threshold is chosen to maximize recall, even at the cost of many false positives — the operating point is in the high-TPR region of the ROC curve.
- In **spam filtering**, a high threshold is chosen to avoid blocking legitimate emails — the operating point is in the low-FPR region.

AUC summarizes the *ceiling* of performance available at all thresholds, enabling **post-hoc threshold tuning** without retraining the model.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast AUC with accuracy. Under what conditions is AUC preferred, and what are its limitations? Discuss with an example.**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Accuracy | AUC |
|--------|----------|-----|
| Depends on threshold? | **Yes** — single operating point | **No** — integrates over all thresholds |
| Insensitive to class prior shift? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Requires calibrated probabilities? | No (only hard labels needed) | No (only ranking needed) |
| Range | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Meaningful for ranking? | No | **Yes** |
| Penalizes "always predict majority" baseline? | Yes (it shows low accuracy if minority is important) | Yes (AUC = 0.5 indicates no ranking) |
| Sensitive to imbalanced data? | **Highly** — accuracy paradox | Less so for ranking quality |

**When AUC is Preferred:**

1. **Class Imbalance:** In a credit card fraud dataset with 1% fraud rate, a model predicting "not fraud" everywhere has 99% accuracy but AUC = 0.5. AUC reveals that the model has no ranking power.

2. **Ranking Tasks:** In medical diagnosis or information retrieval, the goal is to rank patients/customers by risk, not to make binary decisions. AUC directly measures ranking quality via the WMW statistic.

3. **Cost-Sensitive Applications:** When downstream decision thresholds may change based on business needs, AUC provides a stable, threshold-free summary.

**Limitations of AUC:**

1. **Optimism on Imbalanced Data:** AUC aggregates over regions of the ROC curve that may be operationally irrelevant (e.g., very high FPR regions). For highly skewed data, **Average Precision (AUC-PR)** is preferred.

2. **Equal-Weight Treatment of Errors:** AUC weights FPR and TPR equally. If misclassification costs are asymmetric, AUC is not a complete summary — partial AUC (e.g., focusing on low-FPR region) may be more appropriate.

3. **Not a Proper Scoring Rule:** AUC does not measure the *quality* of probability estimates, only the *ranking*. A model that outputs badly calibrated probabilities can still have AUC = 1.0.

4. **Binary-Only:** Naive AUC only works for binary classification. Multiclass extension requires One-vs-Rest or One-vs-One strategies.

**Illustrative Example:**

Consider two classifiers on a 100-sample dataset with 5% positive class:

- **Classifier A:** Always predicts "negative". Accuracy = 95%, AUC = 0.50
- **Classifier B:** Outputs well-calibrated probabilities, perfectly ranks the 5 positives above the 95 negatives. Accuracy = 92% (at default threshold 0.5), AUC = 1.00

Although A has higher accuracy, B is vastly more useful in practice because it can **rank** customers by fraud risk. AUC correctly identifies B as the superior model.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in AUC Questions (Marks Lost per KTU Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Confusing ROC with Precision–Recall (PR) curve.** A PR curve plots Recall vs Precision; an ROC curve plots TPR vs FPR. Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks** immediately. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to sort by score, not by label.** Many students sort positives first and negatives second. The correct procedure is a **single descending sort by score** with labels carried along. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Using $P$ instead of $TP$ in TPR formula.** TPR is $\frac{TP}{P}$, not $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$. Conflating this with precision is a frequent error. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting the start point $(0, 0)$ and end point $(1, 1)$** of the ROC curve. Both must be plotted for correct trapezoidal area computation. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Computing trapezoidal area as $(x_{k+1} - x_k) \cdot y_k$ instead of $\frac{(x_{k+1} - x_k)(y_k + y_{k+1})}{2}$**. The former is a rectangle; the latter is a trapezoid. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 6. **Stating "AUC measures accuracy"** — this is fundamentally wrong. AUC measures **ranking quality**, not classification accuracy at a single threshold. (Lose 1 mark)
> 7. **Saying AUC = 0.5 means the classifier is useless** — technically it means "no better than random in *ranking*." The classifier may still be useful in some business contexts. (Lose 1 mark for imprecision)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Area Under Curve (AUC)**
>
> - **Definition:** AUC = area under the ROC curve in the unit square; bounded in $[0, 1]$.
> - **ROC Curve:** Plot of $TPR$ (y-axis) vs $FPR$ (x-axis) as threshold $\tau$ is swept.
> - **Boundary:** $AUC = 1$ perfect; $AUC = 0.5$ random; $AUC = 0$ inverted perfect.
> - **Probabilistic Form (WMW):** $AUC = P(s^{+} > s^{-})$ — probability of correct ranking.
> - **Integral Form:** $AUC = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR^{-1}(t)) \, dt$.
> - **Trapezoidal Numerical Form:** $AUC = \sum_k \frac{(x_{k+1} - x_k)(y_k + y_{k+1})}{2}$.
> - **Threshold-invariant:** Depends only on score ranking, not on chosen $\tau$.
> - **Scale-invariant:** Monotonic score transformations (sigmoid, logit) leave AUC unchanged.
> - **Formulas:** $TPR = \frac{TP}{P}$, $FPR = \frac{FP}{N} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$.
> - **Construction Procedure:** Sort all $m$ samples by descending score → walk through, update running TP/FP → plot $(FPR, TPR)$ → connect with trapezoids.
> - **Gini Coefficient:** $Gini = 2 \cdot AUC - 1$ (area between ROC curve and diagonal).
> - **Youden's J:** Best threshold $\tau^{\star} = \arg\max_\tau (TPR - FPR)$ — gives the *operating point*, NOT the area.
> - **Preferred over accuracy** when: (i) class imbalance exists, (ii) ranking is the goal, (iii) threshold may change in deployment.
> - **Limitations:** Optimistic on highly imbalanced data (use AUC-PR instead); not a proper scoring rule; binary-only.
> - **High-Yield Number to Remember:** For the worked example, $AUC = 0.72$.
> - **Real-World Domains:** Medical diagnosis, credit scoring, fraud detection, search engine ranking, anomaly detection.
> - **Multiclass Extension:** Use One-vs-Rest (OvR) AUC, often averaged as macro or micro.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression - Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), R Squared/Coefficient of Determination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regression - Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), R Squared/Coefficient of Determination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Regression Evaluation Metrics

In **Supervised Machine Learning**, once a regression model is trained, we must quantify **how wrong** its predictions are compared to the actual ground-truth values. The quantitative tools used to measure this deviation are called **Regression Performance Metrics**. The three foundational metrics mandated by the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCST503 – Machine Learning, Module 2)** are:

1. **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**
2. **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)**
3. **R-Squared / Coefficient of Determination (R²)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**  
> Students must be able to *derive, calculate, and interpret* MAE, RMSE, and R² for any given regression problem. Examiner questions typically test formula derivation, numerical computation on small datasets, and the *qualitative interpretation* of the resulting values.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Archery Target"

Imagine an archer shooting arrows at a target board. The **bullseye (center)** represents the *true actual value*, and the **arrows stuck on the board** represent the *predicted values* shot by our regression model.

| Archery Concept | Regression Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Distance of each arrow from the bullseye | Error (residual) per data point |
| Average distance of arrows from the center | **MAE** — average magnitude of error |
| Distance of the *worst* arrow that pulls the average | **RMSE** — penalizes large errors more |
| How much the spread of arrows is *explained* by the archer's skill (vs. random scatter) | **R²** — proportion of variance explained |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** MAE and RMSE are **error metrics** (lower is better), while **R²** is an **explanatory metric** (higher is better, capped at 1.0 for a perfect model).

---

## 1.3 Formal Definitions

### Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
**MAE** is the arithmetic mean of the absolute differences between the predicted values $\hat{y}_i$ and the actual values $y_i$. It belongs to the **L1-norm family** of loss functions and is **robust to outliers**.

### Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
**RMSE** is the square root of the mean of the squared differences between predicted and actual values. It belongs to the **L2-norm family** of loss functions and **penalizes larger errors disproportionately** because of the squaring operation.

### R-Squared (Coefficient of Determination)
**R²** is a **normalized, scale-free** metric that represents the proportion of the variance in the dependent variable $y$ that is *explained* (or predictable) from the independent variables $x$. It is bounded between $-\infty$ and $1.0$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Each Metric

### 2.1.1 Mean Absolute Error (MAE) — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** For every data point $i$, compute the **residual** $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$.
- **Step 2:** Take the **absolute value** $\vert e_i \vert$ to remove the sign (errors above and below the true value should both count as positive mistakes).
- **Step 3:** **Sum** all absolute residuals: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$.
- **Step 4:** **Divide** by $n$ to obtain the average magnitude of error.
- **Why?** It gives an intuitive, interpretable measure in the **same physical units** as the target variable.

### 2.1.2 Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Compute the residual $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$.
- **Step 2:** **Square** each residual: $e_i^2$. This step is critical because:
  - It eliminates negative signs.
  - It **amplifies** large errors (a residual of 10 contributes 100, while a residual of 2 contributes only 4).
- **Step 3:** Compute the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**: $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum e_i^2$.
- **Step 4:** Take the **square root** of MSE to return to the original units of $y$.
- **Why RMSE and not just MSE?** MSE is in *squared units* (e.g., dollars², meters²), making it hard to interpret. The square root restores interpretability.

### 2.1.3 R-Squared (Coefficient of Determination) — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Compute the **Total Sum of Squares (SS_tot)** — the variance of $y$ around its own mean $\bar{y}$:
  
$$SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2$$

- **Step 2:** Compute the **Residual Sum of Squares (SS_res)** — the unexplained variance left over after applying the model:

$$SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

- **Step 3:** Form the **ratio** $\frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ — what fraction of variance the model **failed** to explain.
- **Step 4:** Subtract from 1: $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ — the fraction of variance **successfully explained**.
- **Why?** It provides a normalized (0 to 1) score that is **independent of the scale of $y$**, allowing comparison across different datasets.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Units | Range | Sensitivity to Outliers |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MAE** | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Same as $y$ | $[0, \infty)$ | **Low** (Robust) |
| **MSE** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Squared units of $y$ | $[0, \infty)$ | **High** |
| **RMSE** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Same as $y$ | $[0, \infty)$ | **High** |
| **R²** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Dimensionless | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Moderate |
| **Adjusted R²** | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1}$ | Dimensionless | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - $R^2 = 1$ → Model explains **100%** of variance (perfect fit).
> - $R^2 = 0$ → Model is **no better** than predicting the mean $\bar{y}$ for every point.
> - $R^2 < 0$ → Model is **worse** than the mean baseline (severely overfitting or wrong model).
> - $RMSE \geq MAE$ always (by Jensen's inequality), with equality only when all residuals are equal in magnitude.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MAE** is widely used in **forecasting weather, demand planning, and finance** where interpretability and resistance to outliers (e.g., one black-swan event) are critical.
- **RMSE** is the **default metric in scikit-learn for regression** and is heavily used in **signal processing, control systems, and physics-based modeling** where large errors are unacceptable.
- **R²** is the **standard metric reported in academic research papers** and in **regression-based statistical software (SPSS, R, SAS)** to compare competing models on the same dataset.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Numerical Demonstration

**Consider a small regression dataset with 5 observations** (a common KTU 2-mark computation problem):

| $i$ | $y_i$ (Actual) | $\hat{y}_i$ (Predicted) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 2.8 |
| 2 | 5 | 4.6 |
| 3 | 2 | 2.5 |
| 4 | 7 | 6.8 |
| 5 | 9 | 8.2 |

---

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Compute the Residuals

The residual for the $i^{th}$ observation is defined as:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$$

Applying this to each row:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= 3 - 2.8 = +0.2 \\
e_2 &= 5 - 4.6 = +0.4 \\
e_3 &= 2 - 2.5 = -0.5 \\
e_4 &= 7 - 6.8 = +0.2 \\
e_5 &= 9 - 8.2 = +0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of consistency:** A perfect model would give $\sum e_i = 0$. Here, $\sum e_i = 0.2 + 0.4 - 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.8 = 1.1 \neq 0$ — the model is biased, which is acceptable for a pedagogical example.

---

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Calculate MAE

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MAE} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \left( \vert 0.2 \vert + \vert 0.4 \vert + \vert -0.5 \vert + \vert 0.2 \vert + \vert 0.8 \vert \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \left( 0.2 + 0.4 + 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.8 \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \times 2.1 \\
&= \boxed{0.42}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, our model's predictions deviate from the true value by **0.42 units** of $y$.

---

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Calculate MSE

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSE} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \left( 0.2^2 + 0.4^2 + (-0.5)^2 + 0.2^2 + 0.8^2 \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \left( 0.04 + 0.16 + 0.25 + 0.04 + 0.64 \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{5} \times 1.13 \\
&= \boxed{0.226}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Calculate RMSE

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RMSE} &= \sqrt{\text{MSE}} \\
&= \sqrt{0.226} \\
&= \boxed{0.4754 \; (\text{approx.})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The RMSE ($0.4754$) is **greater than** MAE ($0.42$), confirming the rule that RMSE $\geq$ MAE. The slight difference indicates the presence of the moderate outlier error of 0.8 in row 5, which the squaring operation amplified.

---

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Calculate R²

**Sub-step 5a:** Compute the mean of the actual values.

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{1}{5}(3 + 5 + 2 + 7 + 9) = \frac{26}{5} = 5.2
$$

**Sub-step 5b:** Compute the **Total Sum of Squares** $SS_{tot}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{tot} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2 \\
&= (3-5.2)^2 + (5-5.2)^2 + (2-5.2)^2 + (7-5.2)^2 + (9-5.2)^2 \\
&= (-2.2)^2 + (-0.2)^2 + (-3.2)^2 + (1.8)^2 + (3.8)^2 \\
&= 4.84 + 0.04 + 10.24 + 3.24 + 14.44 \\
&= \boxed{32.80}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-step 5c:** Compute the **Residual Sum of Squares** $SS_{res}$ (note: this is simply $n \times MSE$).

$$
SS_{res} = 5 \times 0.226 = 1.13
$$

**Sub-step 5d:** Compute R².

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 &= 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1.13}{32.80} \\
&= 1 - 0.03445 \\
&= \boxed{0.9656}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Our model explains approximately **96.56%** of the variance in the target variable. This is an **excellent fit**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MAE vs RMSE vs R²

| Property | MAE | RMSE | R² |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Error metric | Error metric | Explanatory metric |
| **Mathematical Family** | L1-Norm | L2-Norm | Ratio-based |
| **Optimal Value** | $0$ | $0$ | $1$ |
| **Bounded** | No (unbounded above) | No (unbounded above) | Yes (mostly $[0,1]$, can be negative) |
| **Scale-dependent?** | Yes | Yes | No (dimensionless) |
| **Outlier Sensitivity** | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Differentiability at zero** | Not differentiable | Differentiable | Differentiable |
| **Best Use Case** | Robust reporting, presence of outliers | Default loss, penalize big errors | Cross-dataset comparison |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Comparison:** "Why is RMSE always $\geq$ MAE?" — Answer: By the **Jensen's Inequality** for convex functions, $\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[X^2]} \geq \mathbb{E}[\vert X \vert]$, which directly yields $RMSE \geq MAE$ with equality only when all residuals are equal in magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Computational Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end computational pipeline** for computing MAE, RMSE, and R² from raw residuals:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: y_actual and y_predicted Arrays] --> B[Stage 1: Compute Residuals e_i = y_i - y_hat_i]
    B --> C{Stage 2: Branch into Three Parallel Metrics}
    C --> D[MAE Branch: Take Absolute Value and Mean]
    C --> E[RMSE Branch: Square, Mean, then Square Root]
    C --> F[R2 Branch: Compute SS_tot and SS_res]
    D --> G[Output MAE]
    E --> H[Output RMSE]
    F --> I[Output R2]
    G --> J[Stage 3: Aggregate Results into Performance Report]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Decision Block: Model Acceptable?]
    K -->|Yes| L[Deploy Model]
    K -->|No| M[Retrain with Different Hyperparameters]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style C fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style J fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style K fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy of Metrics

This diagram illustrates **how the three metrics relate to each other hierarchically**, with MSE acting as the bridge between RMSE and R²:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Residuals e_i] --> B[Absolute Value L1]
    A --> C[Squared Value L2]
    B --> D[MAE]
    C --> E[MSE]
    E --> F[RMSE]
    E --> G[SS_res]
    H[Mean of y] --> I[SS_tot]
    G --> J[R2]
    I --> J

    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style D fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style F fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style J fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Metric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Model Evaluation Required] --> B{Is the dataset prone to outliers?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use MAE: Robust to outliers]
    B -->|No| D{Is scale-free comparison required?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use R2: Normalized 0 to 1]
    D -->|No| F[Use RMSE: Penalizes large errors]
    C --> G[Report MAE and R2 together]
    F --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H[End: Performance Documented]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style H fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Recommendation:** In KTU lab examinations and project work, it is good practice to **report all three metrics together** — MAE for interpretability, RMSE for sensitivity analysis, and R² for cross-dataset benchmarking.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between MAE and RMSE. Why is RMSE always greater than or equal to MAE?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | MAE | RMSE |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ |
| **Norm Type** | L1 | L2 |
| **Outlier Sensitivity** | Low | High |

**Mathematical Justification:**  
By the **Jensen's Inequality** applied to the convex square-root function, the square root of the mean of squares is always $\geq$ the mean of the absolute values. Formally, for any set of residuals $\{e_i\}$:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum e_i^2} \geq \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_i \vert \quad \Rightarrow \quad RMSE \geq MAE
$$

Equality holds **only when all residuals are equal in magnitude**, which is rare in practice. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**
**Q: Explain the Coefficient of Determination (R²). What does a value of R² = 0.85 signify?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Coefficient of Determination (R²)** is defined as:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

- It quantifies the **proportion of variance** in the dependent variable $y$ that is predictable from the independent variables.
- It is **dimensionless** and bounded in $[-\infty, 1]$.
- **R² = 0.85** means the model successfully explains **85% of the total variance** in $y$, while the remaining 15% is unexplained (residual error or noise). **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE). For the following regression predictions, compute both values and comment on which metric is more sensitive to the outlier.

| $i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 11 |
| 2 | 15 | 14 |
| 3 | 20 | 18 |
| 4 | 25 | 27 |
| 5 | 30 | 29 |
| 6 | 35 | 90 *(outlier prediction)* |

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same dataset (excluding the outlier row), compute the **Adjusted R²** and explain why it is preferred over plain R² when comparing models with different numbers of features.

---

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — MAE and RMSE Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$:

$$
e_1 = -1, \quad e_2 = +1, \quad e_3 = +2, \quad e_4 = -2, \quad e_5 = +1, \quad e_6 = -55
$$

**Step 2: MAE Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MAE} &= \frac{1}{6}(\vert -1 \vert + \vert 1 \vert + \vert 2 \vert + \vert -2 \vert + \vert 1 \vert + \vert -55 \vert) \\
&= \frac{1}{6}(1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 55) \\
&= \frac{62}{6} \\
&= 10.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: RMSE Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RMSE} &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{6}(1 + 1 + 4 + 4 + 1 + 3025)} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{3036}{6}} \\
&= \sqrt{506} \\
&\approx 22.49
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comparative Comment (2 Marks within the 7):**

RMSE (22.49) is **drastically larger** than MAE (10.33) due to the outlier residual of $-55$. Squaring $(-55)^2 = 3025$ dominates the sum. Hence, **RMSE is far more sensitive to outliers**, while MAE remains relatively robust. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Adjusted R² Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Use the first 5 rows (excluding the outlier).
- $n = 5$, $k = 1$ (one feature), residuals: $\{-1, +1, +2, -2, +1\}$.
- $\bar{y} = \frac{10+15+20+25+30}{5} = 20$

**Step 2:** $SS_{res}$:

$$
SS_{res} = 1 + 1 + 4 + 4 + 1 = 11
$$

**Step 3:** $SS_{tot}$:

$$
SS_{tot} = (10-20)^2 + (15-20)^2 + (20-20)^2 + (25-20)^2 + (30-20)^2 = 100+25+0+25+100 = 250
$$

**Step 4:** Plain R²:

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{11}{250} = 1 - 0.044 = 0.956
$$

**Step 5:** Adjusted R²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2_{adj} &= 1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1} \\
&= 1 - \frac{(1-0.956)(5-1)}{5-1-1} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.044 \times 4}{3} \\
&= 1 - 0.0587 \\
&\approx 0.9413
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Adjusted R² is preferred (2 Marks within 7):**  
Plain R² **always increases** (or stays the same) when you add more features, even if they are irrelevant. Adjusted R² **penalizes unnecessary features** by accounting for the number of predictors $k$. Hence, when comparing models with different feature counts, Adjusted R² is the fair metric. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between R² and RMSE for a linear regression model. Show that $R^2 = 1 - \frac{n \cdot \text{RMSE}^2}{SS_{tot}}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A real estate dataset has actual house prices (in lakhs ₹) = [45, 52, 48, 60, 55, 70, 65] and predicted prices = [47, 50, 49, 58, 57, 68, 67]. Compute MAE, RMSE, and R², and provide a holistic model assessment in 3-4 lines.

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Starting Point:** Definitions

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} \quad \text{where} \quad SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
$$

**Step 1:** Recall the definition of MSE:

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \frac{SS_{res}}{n}
$$

**Step 2:** Recall the definition of RMSE:

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{RMSE}^2 = \text{MSE} = \frac{SS_{res}}{n}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange for $SS_{res}$:

$$
SS_{res} = n \cdot \text{RMSE}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the R² formula:

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{n \cdot \text{RMSE}^2}{SS_{tot}}
$$

**Hence Proved.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Compute residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$:

$$
-2, \; +2, \; -1, \; +2, \; -2, \; +2, \; -2
$$

**Step 2: MAE**

$$
\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{7}(2+2+1+2+2+2+2) = \frac{13}{7} \approx 1.857
$$

**Step 3: RMSE**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RMSE} &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{7}(4+4+1+4+4+4+4)} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{25}{7}} \\
&= \sqrt{3.5714} \\
&\approx 1.890
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: R²**

- $\bar{y} = \frac{45+52+48+60+55+70+65}{7} = \frac{395}{7} \approx 56.43$
- $SS_{tot} = (45-56.43)^2 + (52-56.43)^2 + \cdots + (65-56.43)^2$
- $SS_{tot} = 130.74 + 19.59 + 71.16 + 12.74 + 2.04 + 184.14 + 73.44 \approx 493.85$

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{25}{493.85} = 1 - 0.0506 \approx 0.949
$$

**Holistic Assessment (2 Marks within 7):**  
The model shows an **MAE of ₹1.86 lakhs** and **RMSE of ₹1.89 lakhs**, both very close (indicating no severe outliers). With **R² ≈ 0.949**, the model explains **94.9% of the variance** in house prices, indicating an **excellent fit** suitable for deployment. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square root in RMSE** — Many students report MSE instead of RMSE. **Valuation: -1 to -2 marks.**
> 2. **Using $n$ in the denominator instead of $n-1$ in $SS_{tot}$** — For sample variance, use $n-1$, but for population-based R² (as KTU typically tests), use $n$. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Confusing Adjusted R² with plain R²** — Adjusted R² uses the formula $1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1}$ with $k$ = number of predictors. Forgetting $k$ leads to wrong answers.
> 4. **Not specifying units in MAE/RMSE** — Always state "in lakhs ₹" or "in units of $y$" for full credit.
> 5. **Stating R² > 1** — Mathematically impossible. If you compute a value > 1, you have made a sign error in the numerator.
> 6. **Reporting only one metric** — KTU module questions often ask to "comment on the model." Always discuss MAE, RMSE, and R² together for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Master Before the Exam:**

- ☐ **MAE** = Average of $\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ → **Robust, L1, linear penalty**.
- ☐ **MSE** = Average of $(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ → Squared units, differentiable.
- ☐ **RMSE** = $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ → **Sensitive to outliers, L2, same units as $y$**.
- ☐ **R²** = $1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ → Dimensionless, range $(-\infty, 1]$, **higher is better**.
- ☐ **$R^2 = 1$** → Perfect prediction; **$R^2 = 0$** → No better than mean; **$R^2 < 0$** → Worse than mean.
- ☐ **Always $RMSE \geq MAE$** (Jensen's Inequality).
- ☐ **Adjusted R²** penalizes extra features and is preferred for model comparison.
- ☐ **$SS_{res} = n \cdot \text{MSE} = n \cdot \text{RMSE}^2$** — useful identity for derivations.
- ☐ Report all three metrics together in lab reports and viva for full credit.
- ☐ For KTU 14-mark questions, expect a 7-mark **derivation** + 7-mark **numerical computation** split.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SVM - Linear SVM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SVM - Linear SVM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linear Support Vector Machine (Linear SVM)** is a supervised machine learning algorithm used for binary classification tasks. It is a linear classifier that finds the **optimal separating hyperplane** in an N-dimensional feature space (where N is the number of features) which maximizes the **margin** (distance) between the two classes. The decision boundary is defined by the equation:

$$w \cdot x + b = 0$$

The classifier is called "linear" because the decision boundary is a straight line in 2D, a plane in 3D, or a hyperplane in higher dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Linear SVM is a maximum-margin classifier that identifies the hyperplane which separates the data points of two different classes with the largest possible margin, using only the most informative training points called **support vectors**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Hyperplane** | A decision boundary separating data points in N-dimensional space |
| **Support Vectors** | The data points closest to the hyperplane that influence its position |
| **Margin** | The perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the nearest support vector |
| **Hard Margin** | Strict separation with no misclassifications allowed (linearly separable data) |
| **Soft Margin** | Allows some misclassifications via slack variables (non-separable data) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Widest Highway**
> Imagine two types of vehicles — **red cars** and **blue cars** — parked on a road. You want to draw a line (in 2D) or a wall (in 3D) to separate them. A naive approach might place the wall anywhere between the two groups. However, the **widest highway principle** states that you should place the wall such that the road on either side of the wall is as **wide as possible**. The cars closest to the wall (on either side) are your **support vectors**, and the width of the road is your **margin**. The wider the margin, the better the classifier generalizes to new, unseen vehicles.

### 1.4 Why Linear SVM Matters in KTU Context

Linear SVM is foundational because:
- It introduces the concept of **geometric margin** in ML.
- It is computationally efficient for high-dimensional sparse data (e.g., text classification).
- It is the gateway to understanding **kernelized SVM** and **non-linear classification**.
- The optimization formulation (primal and dual) is a classic constrained optimization problem tested in KTU examinations.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linear SVM is widely used in **spam email detection**, **sentiment analysis**, **document categorization**, **bioinformatics (cancer classification)**, and **image recognition** where the data is approximately linearly separable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundation: The Separating Hyperplane

Given a training dataset with $n$ samples:
$$D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$$

where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the feature vector and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ is the class label (binary), Linear SVM seeks a hyperplane parameterized by a weight vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and a bias term $b \in \mathbb{R}$ such that:

$$w \cdot x_i + b \geq +1 \quad \text{for } y_i = +1$$

$$w \cdot x_i + b \leq -1 \quad \text{for } y_i = -1$$

These two conditions can be compactly written as:

$$y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$

### 2.2 The Margin and Its Geometric Meaning

The margin is the **perpendicular distance** between the two parallel supporting hyperplanes:
- $w \cdot x + b = +1$ (positive class boundary)
- $w \cdot x + b = -1$ (negative class boundary)

The total margin width is:
$$\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$$

where $\|w\|$ is the **Euclidean norm** of the weight vector.

### 2.3 The Optimization Problem (Primal Form)

**Hard Margin Linear SVM (Linearly Separable Case):**

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2$$

subject to:
$$y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Soft Margin Linear SVM (Non-Separable Case):**

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$$

subject to:
$$y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

where $\xi_i$ are **slack variables** and $C > 0$ is the **regularization parameter** controlling the trade-off between margin maximization and classification error.

### 2.4 The Hinge Loss Formulation

Linear SVM can also be expressed as an **unconstrained optimization** problem using the **hinge loss function**:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \max(0, 1 - y_i(w \cdot x_i + b))$$

The hinge loss for a single sample is:
$$L_{\text{hinge}}(y, f(x)) = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot f(x))$$

where $f(x) = w \cdot x + b$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description / Condition** |
|-----------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Hyperplane Equation | $w \cdot x + b = 0$ | The decision boundary |
| Classification Rule | $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$ | Predicts class label |
| Margin Width | $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$ | Total width of margin between two classes |
| Distance from Point to Hyperplane | $\frac{\|w \cdot x_i + b\|}{\|w\|}$ | Perpendicular distance |
| Hard Margin Objective | $\min \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2$ | Subject to $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$ |
| Soft Margin Objective | $\min \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ | With slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ |
| Hinge Loss | $\max(0, 1 - y_i f(x_i))$ | Loss for misclassification and margin violation |
| Support Vector Condition | $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$ | Points exactly on the margin boundary |
| Regularization Parameter | $C > 0$ | Trades off margin width vs. misclassification |
| Lagrangian Dual Objective | $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$ | With $\alpha_i \geq 0, \sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Minimizing $\|w\|^2$ is equivalent to **maximizing the margin** $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$. This is a foundational concept tested repeatedly in KTU question papers.

### 2.6 Why Minimize ||w||² ?

The relationship between minimizing $\|w\|^2$ and maximizing the margin is mathematically elegant:

- A larger margin → smaller $\|w\|$ → smaller objective $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$.
- A smaller margin → larger $\|w\|$ → larger objective.

Therefore, the SVM optimization is intrinsically a **margin maximization** problem.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Application:** In **text classification** (e.g., spam detection), each email is represented as a high-dimensional sparse vector (with thousands of features representing word frequencies). Linear SVM is the go-to algorithm because it scales efficiently to high-dimensional data and produces robust decision boundaries even with limited training samples.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Distance from a Point to a Hyperplane

**Setup:** Consider a hyperplane $H: w \cdot x + b = 0$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$. Let $x_0$ be any point on the hyperplane, and let $p$ be an arbitrary point in space. We want to find the perpendicular distance from $p$ to $H$.

**Step 1:** The vector $w$ is normal to the hyperplane (perpendicular to it).

**Step 2:** The vector from $x_0$ to $p$ is $p - x_0$.

**Step 3:** The projection of $(p - x_0)$ onto the normal direction $w$ gives the perpendicular distance:

$$d = \frac{|(p - x_0) \cdot w|}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 4:** Since $x_0$ lies on the hyperplane, $w \cdot x_0 + b = 0$, so $w \cdot x_0 = -b$.

**Step 5:** Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{|(p - x_0) \cdot w|}{\|w\|} \\
  &= \frac{|p \cdot w - x_0 \cdot w|}{\|w\|} \\
  &= \frac{|p \cdot w - (-b)|}{\|w\|} \\
  &= \frac{|w \cdot p + b|}{\|w\|}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$d = \frac{|w \cdot p + b|}{\|w\|}$$

This is the **perpendicular distance** from point $p$ to the hyperplane $w \cdot x + b = 0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Width of the Margin

**Setup:** The two canonical hyperplanes bounding the margin are:
- $H_1: w \cdot x + b = +1$ (touching the positive class support vectors)
- $H_2: w \cdot x + b = -1$ (touching the negative class support vectors)

**Step 1:** Pick a point $x^+$ on $H_1$ (positive class support vector) and a point $x^-$ on $H_2$ (negative class support vector). Both are on the same line perpendicular to the hyperplane.

**Step 2:** The distance from $x^+$ to the central hyperplane $w \cdot x + b = 0$ is:

$$d^+ = \frac{|w \cdot x^+ + b|}{\|w\|} = \frac{+1}{\|w\|} = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 3:** The distance from $x^-$ to the central hyperplane is:

$$d^- = \frac{|w \cdot x^- + b|}{\|w\|} = \frac{|-1|}{\|w\|} = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 4:** The total margin width is the sum of these two distances:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= d^+ + d^- \\
              &= \frac{1}{\|w\|} + \frac{1}{\|w\|} \\
              &= \frac{2}{\|w\|}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** To maximize the margin $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$, we must minimize $\|w\|$, which is the geometric motivation behind minimizing $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Lagrangian Dual Form of Linear SVM

**Step 1:** Start with the primal problem:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$$

**Step 2:** Construct the Lagrangian with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1]$$

**Step 3:** Take the partial derivative with respect to $w$ and set to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} &= w - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \\
\end{aligned}$$

This gives:
$$w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

**Step 4:** Take the partial derivative with respect to $b$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

This gives:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $w = \sum \alpha_i y_i x_i$ back into the Lagrangian:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L} &= \frac{1}{2} \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i x_i\right) \cdot \left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_j y_j x_j\right) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \left[y_i \left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_j y_j x_j \cdot x_i + b\right) - 1\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify using $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

**Step 7:** The dual problem is therefore:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KKT Condition Insight:** At the optimal solution, $\alpha_i > 0$ only for **support vectors** (points lying on the margin). For all other points, $\alpha_i = 0$. This sparsity is why SVM is memory-efficient.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Margin Calculation

**Problem:** Given a linear SVM with weight vector $w = (2, 3)$ and bias $b = -5$, calculate the margin width.

**Step 1:** Calculate $\|w\|$:

$$\|w\| = \sqrt{2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$$

**Step 2:** Apply the margin formula:

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\|w\|} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}} \approx 0.555$$

**Step 3:** Decision boundary equation: $2x_1 + 3x_2 - 5 = 0$, or equivalently $x_2 = \frac{5 - 2x_1}{3}$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table

| **Aspect** | **Hard Margin SVM** | **Soft Margin SVM** |
|------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Data Assumption | Linearly separable | May have overlaps |
| Slack Variables | Not used | $\xi_i \geq 0$ used |
| Objective | $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ | $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ |
| Constraints | $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$ | $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ |
| Robustness | Fails on noisy data | Handles outliers gracefully |
| KTU Preference | Less common in questions | Frequently tested |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Linear SVM Conceptual Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the operational flow of a Linear SVM classifier, from input data to prediction:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Dataset X with Labels y] --> B[Feature Representation in R-d]
    B --> C[Initialize Weight Vector w and Bias b]
    C --> D[Define Margin Constraints y_i w x_i + b >= 1]
    D --> E[Optimization Solver Quadratic Programming]
    E --> F[Obtain Optimal w and b]
    F --> G[Identify Support Vectors]
    G --> H[Construct Decision Hyperplane w x + b = 0]
    H --> I[New Test Point x_test]
    I --> J[Compute f x_test = w x_test + b]
    J --> K{Sign of f x_test}
    K -->|+1| L[Predict Class Positive]
    K -->|-1| M[Predict Class Negative]
```

### 4.2 Linear SVM Geometric Layout

This diagram illustrates the geometric relationships between the central hyperplane, margin boundaries, and support vectors:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP1[Positive Class Region]
        SV1[Support Vector x_plus on margin]
        P1[Other Positive Points]
    end
    subgraph MM[Margin Strip of width 2 divided by norm of w]
        H1[Boundary H1: w x + b = +1]
        HP[Central Hyperplane H0: w x + b = 0]
        H2[Boundary H2: w x + b = -1]
    end
    subgraph SP2[Negative Class Region]
        SV2[Support Vector x_minus on margin]
        P2[Other Negative Points]
    end
    SV1 --> H1
    H1 --> HP
    HP --> H2
    H2 --> SV2
```

### 4.3 Linear SVM Optimization Workflow

The complete optimization pipeline for solving the Linear SVM problem:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Labeled Training Data] --> B[Formulate Primal Problem]
    B --> C{Is Data Linearly Separable?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Hard Margin Formulation]
    C -->|No| E[Soft Margin Formulation with Slack Variables]
    D --> F[Construct Lagrangian Function]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply KKT Conditions]
    G --> H[Derive Dual Optimization Problem]
    H --> I[Solve Dual using Quadratic Programming]
    I --> J[Recover Primal Variables w and b]
    J --> K[Output Final Linear SVM Classifier]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Since Linear SVM operates in an abstract geometric space, the mermaid diagrams above are functional representations of the algorithmic pipeline rather than literal geometric renderings. The conceptual meaning — input data, hyperplane construction, margin identification, and prediction — is fully captured.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a Support Vector in the context of Linear SVM. Why are support vectors critical to the model's decision boundary?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **support vector** is a data point from the training set that lies exactly on the margin boundary (i.e., $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$) or violates the margin. These points are the closest to the decision hyperplane and are the only training samples that influence the position and orientation of the separating hyperplane. **(1 mark)**

If all other training points were removed (keeping the support vectors), the optimal hyperplane would remain unchanged. **(1 mark)**

This makes support vectors critical because they define the maximum margin and are the "support" structure of the classifier. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the regularization parameter $C$ in soft margin SVM? How does varying $C$ affect the model?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The regularization parameter $C > 0$ controls the trade-off between **margin maximization** and **misclassification penalty** in soft margin SVM. **(1 mark)**

- **Large $C$:** The model heavily penalizes misclassifications, leading to a **narrower margin** with fewer training errors. Risk of overfitting.
- **Small $C$:** The model tolerates more misclassifications in favor of a **wider margin**. Risk of underfitting. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Linear SVM**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to write the constraint $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$ in the primal formulation. KTU examiners specifically look for this.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the margin formula $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$ with the distance formula $\frac{|w \cdot x + b|}{\|w\|}$. These are different.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not mentioning that **only support vectors** have non-zero $\alpha_i$ in the dual formulation.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Skipping the geometric interpretation of why we minimize $\|w\|^2$ (i.e., margin maximization).
> - **Mark Loss Estimate:** 2 to 3 marks lost per oversight on a 14-mark question.

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) Formulate the optimization problem for the Linear SVM in both its hard margin and soft margin forms. Clearly explain each term in the objective function and the constraints.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Hard Margin Linear SVM (Linearly Separable Data):**

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2$$

subject to:
$$y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Explanation of Terms:**
- $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$: This is the objective function to be minimized. Minimizing $\|w\|^2$ is equivalent to **maximizing the margin** $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$. The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience for cleaner differentiation. **(2 marks)**
- $w$: The weight vector (normal to the hyperplane).
- $b$: The bias term (offsets the hyperplane from the origin).
- $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$: The constraint enforcing that every training point is correctly classified with a margin of at least 1. **(1 mark)**

**Soft Margin Linear SVM (Non-Separable Data):**

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$$

subject to:
$$y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

**Explanation of Terms:**
- $\xi_i$ (slack variables): Measure the degree of margin violation for each training point. If $\xi_i = 0$, the point is correctly classified on the margin; if $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$, the point is inside the margin; if $\xi_i > 1$, the point is misclassified. **(2 marks)**
- $C > 0$: Regularization parameter that controls the penalty for misclassifications. **(1 mark)**
- The constraint $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ allows margin violations. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Derive the dual optimization problem of the Linear SVM using Lagrangian multipliers. State the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions and explain the role of support vectors in the dual formulation.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Start with the primal hard margin problem and form the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1]$$

with $\alpha_i \geq 0$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $w$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \implies w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $b$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Substitute $w$ back into the Lagrangian and simplify to get the dual:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5:** State the KKT conditions:
- Primal feasibility: $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$
- Dual feasibility: $\alpha_i \geq 0$
- Complementary slackness: $\alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1] = 0$
- Stationarity: $w = \sum \alpha_i y_i x_i$, $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 6:** Role of support vectors: From complementary slackness, $\alpha_i > 0$ only when $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$, i.e., the point lies on the margin. For all non-support vectors, $\alpha_i = 0$. Hence, $w$ is determined solely by the support vectors. **(1 mark)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**(a) Explain the geometric intuition behind the Linear SVM. Why is the margin equal to $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$? Show the derivation clearly.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Geometric Intuition:** Linear SVM aims to find a hyperplane that **maximally separates** the two classes. The margin is the "street" between the two parallel boundary lines touching the closest points of each class. **(1 mark)**

The two canonical hyperplanes are:
- $H_1: w \cdot x + b = +1$ (passes through positive support vectors)
- $H_2: w \cdot x + b = -1$ (passes through negative support vectors)

The central decision boundary is:
- $H_0: w \cdot x + b = 0$

**(1 mark)**

**Derivation of Margin:**

Using the distance formula from a point to a hyperplane:

$$d = \frac{|w \cdot x + b|}{\|w\|}$$

Distance from a positive support vector $x^+$ to $H_0$:

$$d^+ = \frac{|w \cdot x^+ + b|}{\|w\|} = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$

Distance from a negative support vector $x^-$ to $H_0$:

$$d^- = \frac{|w \cdot x^- + b|}{\|w\|} = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$

Total margin:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= d^+ + d^- \\
&= \frac{1}{\|w\|} + \frac{1}{\|w\|} \\
&= \frac{2}{\|w\|}
\end{aligned}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Why Maximize Margin?** A larger margin provides better **generalization** to unseen data. It reduces the model's sensitivity to small perturbations in input features (lower variance). **(1 mark)**

**Connection to Optimization:** Maximizing $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\|w\|^2$, which is the SVM objective. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Discuss the Hinge Loss formulation of Linear SVM. How does it relate to the primal optimization problem? Explain the role of the parameter $C$ in this formulation.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Hinge Loss Definition:** The hinge loss for a single training sample is defined as:

$$L_{\text{hinge}}(y_i, f(x_i)) = \max(0, 1 - y_i \cdot f(x_i))$$
