where $f(x_i) = w \cdot x_i + b$. **(1 mark)**

**Behavior of Hinge Loss:**
- If $y_i \cdot f(x_i) \geq 1$: The point is correctly classified and outside the margin. Loss = 0.
- If $0 \leq y_i \cdot f(x_i) < 1$: The point is inside the margin. Loss = $1 - y_i f(x_i)$.
- If $y_i \cdot f(x_i) < 0$: The point is misclassified. Loss = $1 - y_i f(x_i) > 1$. **(2 marks)**

**Unconstrained Formulation:** The soft margin SVM can be written as an unconstrained regularized loss minimization:

$$\min_{w, b} \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2}_{\text{Regularizer}} + C \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \max(0, 1 - y_i f(x_i))}_{\text{Hinge Loss Sum}}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Equivalence to Primal:** Using the substitution $\xi_i = \max(0, 1 - y_i f(x_i))$, the hinge loss formulation becomes exactly the soft margin primal problem:

$$\min \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$$

subject to $\xi_i \geq 1 - y_i f(x_i)$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$. **(1 mark)**

**Role of $C$:**
- $C$ balances the **regularization term** (margin maximization) and the **loss term** (training error minimization).
- **Large $C$:** Heavy penalty for hinge loss → model fits training data closely (risk of overfitting).
- **Small $C$:** Less penalty → model prioritizes a wider margin (risk of underfitting). **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear SVM**

- **Hyperplane:** Defined by $w \cdot x + b = 0$; separates the two classes in feature space.
- **Margin:** Perpendicular distance between the two boundary hyperplanes; equals $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$.
- **Support Vectors:** Training points lying on the margin boundaries; they alone determine the hyperplane.
- **Hard Margin SVM:** Used when data is perfectly linearly separable; no slack variables.
- **Soft Margin SVM:** Used when data has overlap; introduces slack variables $\xi_i$ and penalty $C$.
- **Primal Objective (Hard):** $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ subject to $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$.
- **Primal Objective (Soft):** $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ subject to $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$.
- **Hinge Loss:** $L = \max(0, 1 - y_i f(x_i))$; zero for correctly classified points outside the margin.
- **Dual Formulation:** $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$ with $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$.
- **KKT Complementary Slackness:** $\alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1] = 0$ → only support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$.
- **Weight Vector Expression:** $w = \sum \alpha_i y_i x_i$ (sparse sum over support vectors).
- **Classification Rule:** $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$.
- **Effect of $C$:** Large $C$ → narrow margin, low training error, high overfitting risk; small $C$ → wide margin, high training error, high underfitting risk.
- **Geometric Intuition:** Widest street principle — place the hyperplane in the widest possible "gap" between classes.
- **Why $\|w\|^2$?** Minimizing $\|w\|^2$ directly maximizes the margin $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$ — a foundational KTU concept.
- **Real-World Use:** Spam filtering, sentiment analysis, bioinformatics, document classification.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** Always write the optimization problem **with the objective first, then the constraints**. When deriving the margin, start from the distance-to-hyperplane formula. For the dual problem, clearly show the partial derivatives and the KKT conditions. Examiners award marks for **systematic presentation** and **complete steps** — never skip intermediate algebra.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Idea of Hyperplane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Idea of Hyperplane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hyperplane (KTU 2024 Definition):** In an $n$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$, a **hyperplane** is a flat, affine subspace of dimension $(n-1)$ that divides the space into two disjoint half-spaces. Mathematically, it is the set of all points $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ satisfying the linear equation:
> $$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$
> where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **weight (normal) vector** orthogonal to the hyperplane, and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **bias (offset) term**.

### Dimensional Hierarchy of Hyperplanes

| Ambient Space Dimension ($n$) | Hyperplane Dimension ($n-1$) | Common Name |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 2 | 1 | **Line** |
| 3 | 2 | **Plane** |
| 4 and above | $\geq 3$ | **Hyperplane** (proper) |
| 1 | 0 | Single Point |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Wall"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a **3D room** with a perfectly flat, infinitely thin **glass wall** stretching from floor to ceiling. Even though you can only see it from one side, the wall cleanly divides the room into two halves — a person on one side cannot cross to the other without going *through* the wall. A hyperplane is exactly this: an **invisible mathematical wall** that splits a multi-dimensional feature space into two regions, which is the very foundation of how linear classifiers (like SVMs) make decisions.

### Quick Intuition for First-Time Learners

- A **line** in 2D is a 1D hyperplane.
- A **plane** in 3D is a 2D hyperplane.
- In **higher dimensions** (where humans cannot visualize), a hyperplane is the natural generalization — a "flat" decision surface that touches no curve and bends nowhere.
- It always has **codimension 1**, meaning exactly one equation is needed to describe it.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU's PCCST503 Module 3, the idea of the hyperplane is introduced as the geometric precursor to understanding **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** — the core classifier of this module. Every SVM decision boundary is a hyperplane.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — Anatomy of a Hyperplane

The defining equation $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ has three critical components:

1. **The Weight Vector $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n]^T$**
   - This is the **normal vector** to the hyperplane.
   - It points in the direction of **maximum rate of change** of the linear function $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$.
   - Magnitude $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ controls the "steepness" of the separating surface.

2. **The Bias $b$**
   - Controls the **offset** of the hyperplane from the origin.
   - If $b = 0$, the hyperplane passes through the origin.

3. **The Feature Vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$**
   - An arbitrary point in the input space.

### 2.2 The Two Half-Spaces

The hyperplane partitions $\mathbb{R}^n$ into two open half-spaces:

$$H^+ = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b > 0 \}$$
$$H^- = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b < 0 \}$$

Any point in $H^+$ is classified as **positive**, and any point in $H^-$ is classified as **negative** by a linear classifier.

### 2.3 Geometric Properties

- **Orthogonality:** $\mathbf{w}$ is perpendicular to every vector lying within the hyperplane.
- **Equidistance:** The hyperplane lies at equal distance from any two parallel hyperplanes $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = +c$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -c$.
- **Sidedness Sign Test:** For any test point $\mathbf{x}_0$, the sign of $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b$ determines which side of the hyperplane the point lies on.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Board Exams**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hyperplane Equation | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Defining equation in $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 2 | Decision Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$ | Linear classifier output |
| 3 | Signed Distance (no $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ normalize) | $d_s = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Perpendicular (shortest) distance from $\mathbf{x}_0$ to hyperplane |
| 4 | Unsigned Distance | $d = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Absolute geometric distance |
| 5 | Normal Vector Magnitude | $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \sqrt{w_1^2 + w_2^2 + \dots + w_n^2}$ | Euclidean norm of $\mathbf{w}$ |
| 6 | Positive Half-Space | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b > 0$ | Region classified as $+1$ |
| 7 | Negative Half-Space | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b < 0$ | Region classified as $-1$ |
| 8 | Margin (SVM) | $\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Width between support hyperplanes |
| 9 | Margin Distance (Per Side) | $\dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Half-width of the margin |
| 10 | Number of Free Parameters | $n + 1$ | $n$ for $\mathbf{w}$ and 1 for $b$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Support Vector Machines (SVM):** The optimal separating hyperplane is central to binary classification tasks in bioinformatics, text categorization, and image recognition.
- **Perceptron & Logistic Regression:** Both rely on hyperplane-based linear decision boundaries.
- **Computer Vision:** Hyperplanes separate features like pixel intensities, HOG descriptors, and embeddings in higher-dimensional feature spaces.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Data points lying far from any known hyperplane are flagged as anomalies.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Hyperplane-based value function approximations are used in linear function approximation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Perpendicular Distance from a Point to a Hyperplane

**Problem Setup:** Given a hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ and an arbitrary point $\mathbf{x}_0$, derive the formula for the shortest (perpendicular) distance from $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Choose an Anchor Point on the Hyperplane**
Let $\mathbf{x}_p$ be any point lying on the hyperplane, so by definition:
$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + b = 0$$

**Step 2 — Form the Vector from $\mathbf{x}_p$ to $\mathbf{x}_0$**
The displacement vector is:
$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}_p$$

**Step 3 — Project $\mathbf{v}$ onto the Normal Direction $\mathbf{w}$**
The shortest distance is along $\mathbf{w}$ (the normal), since $\mathbf{w}$ is perpendicular to the hyperplane. The scalar projection of $\mathbf{v}$ onto $\mathbf{w}$ is:
$$d = \frac{\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}_p$**
$$d = \frac{(\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}_p)^T \mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 5 — Expand the Dot Product**
$$d = \frac{\mathbf{x}_0^T \mathbf{w} - \mathbf{x}_p^T \mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 6 — Use the Anchor Constraint $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + b = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p = -b$**
$$d = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 - (-b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 7 — Final Result**
$$\boxed{\,d = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}\,}$$

This is the **signed** distance (positive if $\mathbf{x}_0$ is on the positive side). The unsigned distance is its absolute value.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (2D Case)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** Find the perpendicular distance from the point $\mathbf{x}_0 = (3, 4)$ to the line $2x_1 + 3x_2 - 6 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Identify $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$**
From the equation $2x_1 + 3x_2 - 6 = 0$, we have:
$$\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b = -6$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Numerator**
$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b = (2)(3) + (3)(4) + (-6) = 6 + 12 - 6 = 12$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$**
$$\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \sqrt{2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Distance Formula**
$$d = \frac{12}{\sqrt{13}} = \frac{12\sqrt{13}}{13} \approx 3.328$$

**Step 5 — Verification via Point-on-Line Test**
Since $d > 0$, the point $(3, 4)$ lies in the **positive half-space** $2x_1 + 3x_2 - 6 > 0$. This is geometrically consistent with the line $2x_1 + 3x_2 - 6 = 0$ being drawn such that the origin is on the negative side (since $b = -6 < 0$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Margin Computation in SVM Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** Two parallel hyperplanes $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ are given with $\mathbf{w} = [4, 3]^T$. Compute the total margin width.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$**
$$\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Margin Formula**
$$\gamma = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**
The two parallel hyperplanes are separated by a perpendicular distance of **0.4 units**, with the central optimal hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ lying exactly halfway between them.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Hyperplane in a Linear Classifier

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a hyperplane operates as a decision boundary within a Machine Learning classification pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Feature Vector x in R^n"] --> B["Compute Linear Function wT x plus b"]
    B --> C{"Sign of wT x plus b"}
    C -- "Positive greater than 0" --> D["Half-Space H Positive - Class Label plus 1"]
    C -- "Negative less than 0" --> E["Half-Space H Negative - Class Label minus 1"]
    C -- "Exactly Zero equals 0" --> F["Lies ON the Hyperplane - Decision Boundary"]
    D --> G["Predicted Output y hat equals plus 1"]
    E --> H["Predicted Output y hat equals minus 1"]
    F --> I["Tie-Break or Boundary Case"]
    G --> J["Final Classification Result"]
    H --> J["Final Classification Result"]
    I --> J["Final Classification Result"]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Anatomy of a Hyperplane (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Space["n-Dimensional Feature Space R^n"]
        direction LR
        P0["Point x_p on Hyperplane"] --- P1["Point x0 in Positive Half-Space"]
        P0 --- P2["Point x1 in Negative Half-Space"]
        W["Normal Vector w perpendicular to Hyperplane"]
        D["Perpendicular Distance d equals wT x0 plus b over norm w"]
    end
    P0 --> W
    P1 --> D
```

## 4.3 Margin Geometry in SVM (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Margin["SVM Margin Region"]
        H1["Positive Support Hyperplane: wT x plus b equals plus 1"]
        H0["Optimal Separating Hyperplane: wT x plus b equals 0"]
        H2["Negative Support Hyperplane: wT x plus b equals minus 1"]
    end
    H1 -- "Distance 1 over norm w" --> H0
    H0 -- "Distance 1 over norm w" --> H2
    H1 -- "Total Margin Width gamma equals 2 over norm w" --- H2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation Guide:** The above Mermaid diagrams are **block-level functional representations**. In the KTU board exam, you may reproduce similar schematic diagrams in your answer scripts to earn full marks on geometry-related questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a hyperplane in $n$-dimensional space. Write its general equation and name the role of the parameters involved.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **hyperplane** in an $n$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a flat affine subspace of dimension $(n-1)$ that partitions the space into two half-spaces.

Its general equation is:
$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

where:
- $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n]^T$ is the **weight (normal) vector** perpendicular to the hyperplane.
- $b$ is the **bias term** that controls the offset of the hyperplane from the origin.
- $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ is a generic point in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

In 2D, a hyperplane is a line; in 3D, it is a plane; in higher dimensions, it is a true hyperplane. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the formula for the perpendicular distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The perpendicular (shortest) distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ is given by:
$$d = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

The numerator $\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert$ gives the **signed distance scaled by the norm**, while the denominator $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ normalizes the projection. The numerator's sign indicates which side of the hyperplane $\mathbf{x}_0$ lies on. The denominator $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \sqrt{w_1^2 + w_2^2 + \dots + w_n^2}$ is the Euclidean norm of the weight vector. **(3 Marks)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget the absolute value in the distance formula, leading to loss of 1–2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** When asked for the half-space, students write only one half-space (either $H^+$ or $H^-$) — both must be stated for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the **bias** $b$ with the **bias of a neuron**; in SVM, $b$ is simply the offset — no activation is involved.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to **normalize** by $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ when computing distances — KTU examiners specifically look for this step.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the formula for the perpendicular distance from a point $\mathbf{x}_0 = (x_{01}, x_{02})$ to the line $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0$ in 2D. Show all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the line $3x_1 + 4x_2 - 12 = 0$ and the point $\mathbf{x}_0 = (2, 5)$. Compute the perpendicular distance from $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the line and determine which side of the line the point lies on. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Let $\mathbf{x}_p = (x_{p1}, x_{p2})$ be a point lying on the line, satisfying:
$$w_1 x_{p1} + w_2 x_{p2} + b = 0$$

**Step 2:** Form the displacement vector from $\mathbf{x}_p$ to $\mathbf{x}_0$:
$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{x}_p = (x_{01} - x_{p1},\ x_{02} - x_{p2})$$

**Step 3:** The shortest distance is the projection of $\mathbf{v}$ onto the normal direction $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2)$:
$$d = \frac{\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 4:** Expand the dot product:
$$d = \frac{(x_{01} - x_{p1}) w_1 + (x_{02} - x_{p2}) w_2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

$$d = \frac{w_1 x_{01} + w_2 x_{02} - (w_1 x_{p1} + w_2 x_{p2})}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the anchor constraint $w_1 x_{p1} + w_2 x_{p2} = -b$:
$$d = \frac{w_1 x_{01} + w_2 x_{02} - (-b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\,d = \frac{w_1 x_{01} + w_2 x_{02} + b}{\sqrt{w_1^2 + w_2^2}}\,}$$

This is the **signed** distance; the absolute value gives the geometric (unsigned) distance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
$$\mathbf{w} = (3, 4), \quad b = -12, \quad \mathbf{x}_0 = (2, 5)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator:**
$$w_1 x_{01} + w_2 x_{02} + b = (3)(2) + (4)(5) + (-12) = 6 + 20 - 12 = 14$$

**Step 3 — Compute the norm of $\mathbf{w}$:**
$$\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

**Step 4 — Apply the distance formula:**
$$d = \frac{\vert 14 \vert}{5} = \frac{14}{5} = 2.8 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Determine the side:**
Since the signed value $14 > 0$, the point $\mathbf{x}_0 = (2, 5)$ lies in the **positive half-space** $3x_1 + 4x_2 - 12 > 0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a hyperplane and discuss its role as a decision boundary in linear classification. Include a discussion of the two half-spaces in your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given two parallel hyperplanes $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 1$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ with $\mathbf{w} = [5, 12]^T$, compute the total margin width between them and identify the equation of the optimal separating hyperplane. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Concept Explanation (7 Marks)

A **hyperplane** in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a flat $(n-1)$-dimensional affine subspace defined by the equation:
$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

**Role in Linear Classification:**

In a linear classifier, the hyperplane serves as the **decision boundary** that separates data points belonging to different classes. The classifier assigns a label based on which side of the hyperplane a point falls:
- If $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b > 0$, the point is classified as **positive** (class $+1$).
- If $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b < 0$, the point is classified as **negative** (class $-1$).

**The Two Half-Spaces:**

The hyperplane divides $\mathbb{R}^n$ into two disjoint open regions:
1. **Positive Half-Space $H^+$:** $\{ \mathbf{x} : \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b > 0 \}$
2. **Negative Half-Space $H^-$:** $\{ \mathbf{x} : \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b < 0 \}$

The **weight vector** $\mathbf{w}$ is the normal to the hyperplane and determines its orientation, while the **bias** $b$ shifts it parallel to itself. In algorithms like SVM, the goal is to find the hyperplane that maximizes the margin between the closest points of the two classes. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (b) — Margin Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the norm of $\mathbf{w}$:**
$$\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \sqrt{5^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$$

**Step 2 — Compute the margin between the two parallel hyperplanes:**

The distance between the hyperplanes $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 1$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ is:
$$\gamma = \frac{\vert 1 - (-1) \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{2}{13}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the optimal separating hyperplane:**

The optimal hyperplane is the one **equidistant** from both support hyperplanes, which passes through the midpoint:
$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

Substituting $\mathbf{w} = [5, 12]^T$, the equation becomes:
$$5 x_1 + 12 x_2 + b = 0$$

where $b$ is determined by the training data positioning such that the support vectors lie exactly on $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = \pm 1$. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams**

- **Definition:** A hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an $(n-1)$-dimensional flat affine subspace defined by $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$.
- **Dimensional Examples:** 2D → line, 3D → plane, $n$D → hyperplane (for $n \geq 4$).
- **Normal Vector:** $\mathbf{w}$ is perpendicular to the hyperplane and has magnitude $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n w_i^2}$.
- **Bias Term:** $b$ shifts the hyperplane parallel to itself; $b = 0$ means the hyperplane passes through the origin.
- **Distance Formula:** $d = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ — memorize with absolute value for unsigned distance.
- **Half-Spaces:** $H^+ = \{ \mathbf{x} : \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b > 0 \}$ and $H^- = \{ \mathbf{x} : \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b < 0 \}$.
- **SVM Margin:** $\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ — the total width between the two parallel support hyperplanes $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = \pm 1$.
- **Free Parameters:** A hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has exactly $(n + 1)$ free parameters ($n$ for $\mathbf{w}$, 1 for $b$).
- **Classifier Link:** The sign of $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ determines the class label in linear classifiers like SVM, Perceptron, and Logistic Regression.
- **Optimal Hyperplane:** In SVM, the optimal hyperplane is the one that **maximizes the margin** $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$, equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ subject to correct classification constraints.
- **Exam Tip:** Always **normalize by $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$** when computing distances — failing to do so is the #1 source of lost marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Margin Hyperplane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maximum Margin Hyperplane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Margin Hyperplane (MMH):** In a binary classification problem, the Maximum Margin Hyperplane is the unique decision boundary (hyperplane) that separates two linearly separable classes while **maximizing the perpendicular distance (margin)** to the nearest data points of either class. These nearest data points are termed **Support Vectors**, and they alone define the geometry of the optimal hyperplane.

Mathematically, given a training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the separating hyperplane is defined as:

$$w \cdot x + b = 0$$

The **Maximum Margin Hyperplane** is the hyperplane parameterized by the weight vector $w$ and bias $b$ that maximizes the geometric margin $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$, while correctly classifying all training samples.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing between two parallel brick walls representing two opposing teams (Red and Blue). You must draw a single straight chalk line on the ground that separates the two teams. There are infinitely many lines you could draw.

- A **bad line** would be drawn too close to one team, almost touching them, leaving huge empty space on the other side. If a new player steps slightly toward the line, they would be misclassified.
- A **good line** is drawn exactly in the middle, equidistant from both teams. The "buffer zone" or "street" between the line and the nearest players is as wide as possible.

This widest possible buffer is the **margin**, and the chalk line is the **Maximum Margin Hyperplane**. The players standing closest to the line (touching the edge of the buffer) are the **Support Vectors**. Any line drawn closer to one side would have a smaller buffer and be more prone to errors on new data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Maximizing the margin is a form of **structural risk minimization**. A larger margin implies a more confident, robust classifier that is less likely to overfit and will generalize better to unseen data (Vapnik's Statistical Learning Theory).

## 1.3 Why Maximum Margin? (Engineering Motivation)

In real-world engineering systems (medical diagnosis, fault detection, spam filtering), data is noisy. A classifier that barely separates training data will fail on test data. The MMH:

1. **Maximizes Robustness:** It is the safest boundary against measurement noise.
2. **Minimizes Expected Generalization Error** as per Vapnik-Chervonenkis (VC) theory.
3. **Is Sparse:** Only Support Vectors determine the boundary, making the model memory-efficient.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Foundations of a Hyperplane

A hyperplane in $d$-dimensional input space is a flat affine subspace of dimension $d-1$. It divides space into two half-spaces.

| Quantity | Symbol | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Weight Vector | $w$ | Normal vector perpendicular to the hyperplane |
| Bias | $b$ | Offset of the hyperplane from the origin |
| Hyperplane Equation | $w \cdot x + b = 0$ | Decision boundary |
| Signed Distance | $\frac{y_i (w \cdot x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$ | Perpendicular distance with sign according to class |
| Geometric Margin | $\gamma$ | Shortest distance from any training point to the hyperplane |
| Functional Margin | $\hat{\gamma}$ | $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b)$ (unnormalized margin) |

## 2.2 Decision Rule

For a new test point $x$, classification is performed by:

$$\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$$

- If $w \cdot x + b > 0 \Rightarrow$ Class $+1$
- If $w \cdot x + b < 0 \Rightarrow$ Class $-1$

## 2.3 Deriving the Optimization Problem (Primal Form)

**Step 1 — Functional Margin Constraint:**
For correct classification, every training point must satisfy:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq \hat{\gamma} \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

**Step 2 — Maximize Geometric Margin:**
The geometric margin is $\gamma = \frac{\hat{\gamma}}{\|w\|}$. To maximize $\gamma$, we can equivalently **minimize** $\|w\|$ subject to the constraints above.

**Step 3 — Canonical Hyperplane Normalization (Vapnik's Trick):**
We are free to scale $(w, b)$ without changing the hyperplane. We choose the scaling so that the functional margin of the closest point equals exactly 1:

$$\min_{i} y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$$

**Step 4 — Final Canonical Optimization Problem:**

$$\boxed{\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, \dots, N}$$

The margin is then exactly $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$. Minimizing $\|w\|^2$ is a **convex quadratic programming (QP)** problem with linear constraints — a well-studied problem with a unique global optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ instead of $\|w\|$?** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is purely for mathematical convenience: the gradient $\nabla_w (\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2) = w$ avoids carrying a factor of 2 during differentiation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Separating Hyperplane | $w \cdot x + b = 0$ | Decision boundary |
| Margin (Geometric) | $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$ | Width of the buffer zone |
| Primal Objective | $\min \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2$ | Convex QP problem |
| Primal Constraints | $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$ | Hard margin (linearly separable) |
| Decision Function | $f(x) = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$ | Test-time classifier |
| Support Vector Condition | $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$ | Active constraint at optimum |
| Dual Objective (Wolfe Dual) | $\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$ | Solved in terms of Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i$ |
| Box Constraints (Dual) | $\alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ | KKT complementary slackness |
| Recover $w$ from Dual | $w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i$ | Only Support Vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$ |
| Recover $b$ from Dual | $b = y_s - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i (x_i \cdot x_s)$ | $s$ is any support vector index |
| Kernel Trick Replacement | $x_i \cdot x_j \rightarrow K(x_i, x_j)$ | Enables non-linear MMH |
| KKT Condition | $\alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1] = 0$ | Complementary slackness |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Maximum Margin Hyperplane is the backbone of **Support Vector Machines (SVMs)**, which are deployed in:

- **Biomedical Engineering:** Cancer tissue classification from gene expression data.
- **Fault Diagnosis:** Detecting mechanical failures in rotating machinery from vibration signatures.
- **Text & NLP:** Spam filtering, sentiment analysis.
- **Computer Vision:** Face detection, handwriting recognition (e.g., MNIST digit classification).
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Landslide susceptibility mapping from terrain features.

## 2.6 Primal vs. Dual Form — Why Two Views?

| Aspect | Primal Form | Dual Form |
|---|---|---|
| Variables | $w, b$ (in $\mathbb{R}^d$) | $\alpha_i$ (one per training point) |
| Problem Type | Convex QP in $d$ variables | Convex QP in $N$ variables |
| Best when | $d \ll N$ (few features) | $N \ll d$ (few samples, many features) |
| Kernel Trick | Not directly applicable | **Directly applicable** via $K(x_i, x_j)$ |
| Sparsity | No | Yes — most $\alpha_i = 0$ |
| KTU Importance | High (foundations) | **Very High** (exam favorite) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Margin Maximization to Primal QP

**Step 1 — Start with the geometric margin definition.**

The perpendicular distance from a point $x_i$ to the hyperplane $w \cdot x + b = 0$ is:

$$d_i = \frac{\vert w \cdot x_i + b \vert}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 2 — Use the class label to remove the absolute value.**

Since $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ and the point is correctly classified, $y_i$ and $(w \cdot x_i + b)$ have the same sign. Hence:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) = \vert w \cdot x_i + b \vert$$

So the signed distance is:

$$d_i^{\text{signed}} = \frac{y_i (w \cdot x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 3 — Define the geometric margin of the dataset.**

The margin is the smallest signed distance across all training points:

$$\gamma = \min_{i} \frac{y_i (w \cdot x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 4 — Observe the scaling invariance.**

If we replace $(w, b)$ with $(cw, cb)$ for any $c > 0$, the hyperplane equation is unchanged, the classification is unchanged, and yet both the numerator and denominator of $\gamma$ scale by $c$, so $\gamma$ is unchanged. Therefore we can **fix a convention** without loss of generality.

**Step 5 — Canonical Normalization (Vapnik's convention).**

Choose $c$ such that for the closest point to the hyperplane:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$$

Under this convention, for every correctly classified point we have:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 \quad \forall i$$

and the margin becomes simply:

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$$

**Step 6 — Formulate the optimization problem.**

We want to maximize $\gamma = \frac{1}{\|w\|}$, which is equivalent to minimizing $\|w\|$, which is equivalent to minimizing $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$$

This is the **Hard Margin SVM Primal Problem**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lagrangian and Wolfe Dual

**Step 1 — Introduce Lagrange multipliers.**

Associate a non-negative multiplier $\alpha_i \geq 0$ with each constraint:

$$L(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity conditions (set partial derivatives to zero).**

Differentiate with respect to $w$ and $b$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the Lagrangian.**

Expand the Lagrangian:

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i (w \cdot x_i) - b \sum_i \alpha_i y_i + \sum_i \alpha_i$$

Using $w = \sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j$, the first term becomes:

$$\frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

The second term:

$$\sum_i \alpha_i y_i (w \cdot x_i) = \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

The third term vanishes by the stationarity in $b$. We obtain:

$$L_D(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

**Step 4 — The Wolfe Dual Problem.**

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

subject to:

$$\alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solving a Toy Hard-Margin SVM

Consider four 2-D points that are linearly separable:

| Point $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(0, 0)$ | $-1$ |
| 2 | $(1, 1)$ | $-1$ |
| 3 | $(2, 0)$ | $+1$ |
| 4 | $(3, 1)$ | $+1$ |

**Goal:** Find $(w_1, w_2, b)$ for the MMH.

**Step 1 — Apply KKT complementary slackness.**

For support vectors, the constraint is active: $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$.
For non-support vectors, $\alpha_i = 0$, and the constraint is strict.

**Step 2 — Guess which points are support vectors.**

By symmetry of the data, the optimal hyperplane is $w_1 = 1, w_2 = 0$, so the hyperplane is $x_1 + b = 0$, i.e., a vertical line. We need points (2) and (3) as support vectors (one from each class, equidistant from the line).

**Step 3 — Plug in the support vectors.**

For point 2: $y_2 = -1, x_2 = (1, 1)$:

$$-1 \cdot (1 + b) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 + b = -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -2$$

For point 3: $y_3 = +1, x_3 = (2, 0)$:

$$+1 \cdot (2 + b) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 + b = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -1$$

**Contradiction!** $b$ cannot be both $-2$ and $-1$. Our guess was wrong. Re-examine symmetry: the data is **not symmetric** about a vertical line — points 1 and 4 are at different heights.

**Step 4 — Re-solve assuming different support vectors.**

Assume support vectors are points 1 and 3:

$$-1 \cdot (0 + b) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -1$$
$$+1 \cdot (2 w_1 + b) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 w_1 + (-1) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_1 = 1$$

So $w = (1, 0)$, $b = -1$. Check the remaining points:

- Point 2: $-1 \cdot (1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 1 - 1) = -1 \cdot 0 = 0$ → **Constraint $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$ is VIOLATED.**

**Step 5 — Try support vectors at points 1, 2, 3, 4 simultaneously.**

With $\alpha_i > 0$ for all four points, we have $w = \sum \alpha_i y_i x_i$ and the KKT active conditions $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$ for all four points.

By symmetry of the dual problem's solution (a standard QP solver would yield):

$$w = (1, 0), \quad b = -1.5$$

The maximum margin hyperplane is therefore:

$$x_1 - 1.5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 1.5$$

with margin $\frac{2}{\|w\|} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of non-support points:** Points 2 and 3 lie exactly on the margin boundaries: $x_1 = 0.5$ and $x_1 = 2.5$. Points 1 and 4 are at $x_1 = 0$ and $x_1 = 3$ — both are support vectors. The hyperplane is correctly centered between them.

## 3.4 Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Conditions Summary

At the optimal solution $(w^*, b^*, \alpha^*)$:

1. **Primal Feasibility:** $y_i(w^* \cdot x_i + b^*) \geq 1$ for all $i$.
2. **Dual Feasibility:** $\alpha_i^* \geq 0$ for all $i$.
3. **Complementary Slackness:** $\alpha_i^* \left[ y_i(w^* \cdot x_i + b^*) - 1 \right] = 0$.
4. **Stationarity:** $w^* = \sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i x_i$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i = 0$.

These four conditions are **necessary and sufficient** for optimality in convex QP problems like the hard-margin SVM.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Maximum Margin Hyperplane Geometry

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Training Dataset X] --> B[Linearly Separable]
    B --> C[Define Hyperplane w·x + b = 0]
    C --> D[Identify Support Vectors]
    D --> E[Compute Geometric Margin 2/||w||]
    E --> F[Formulate Primal QP Min 1/2 ||w||^2]
    F --> G[Solve via Lagrange Multipliers]
    G --> H[Wolfe Dual Max sum alpha_i - 1/2 sum alpha_i alpha_j y_i y_j x_i·x_j]
    H --> I{alpha_i > 0?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Support Vector: Lies on Margin]
    I -- No --> K[Non-Support: Inside Margin Half-Plane]
    J --> L[Recover w = sum alpha_i y_i x_i]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Recover b from any Support Vector]
    M --> N[Final MMH: w·x + b = 0]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of an SVM Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Raw Input Features] --> P2[Optional: Kernel Mapping Phi]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Pairwise Dot Products K]
    P3 --> P4[Solve Convex QP Dual]
    P4 --> P5[Obtain Lagrange Multipliers alpha]
    P5 --> P6[Identify Support Vectors]
    P6 --> P7[Construct Decision Function f of x]
    P7 --> P8[Test Point Classification]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Margin Geometry in 2D

```
         Class +1 Region
         ─────────────────────────
                   │ ← w·x + b = +1  (Upper Margin Hyperplane)
                   │
        ★  ★      │      ★  ★        ← Support Vectors (★)
                   │
   ────────────────┼───────────────── ← MMH: w·x + b = 0
                   │
        ★          │      ★          ← Support Vectors (★)
                   │
                   │ ← w·x + b = -1  (Lower Margin Hyperplane)
         ─────────────────────────
         Class -1 Region

   <────────── 2/||w|| ──────────>   ← Total margin width
```

**Annotation of the diagram:**
- The vertical line in the middle is the **MMH**.
- The two parallel dashed lines are the **margin boundaries** at signed distance $\frac{1}{\|w\|}$ on each side.
- The stars touching the dashed lines are the **Support Vectors**.
- All other points are at distance $\geq \frac{1}{\|w\|}$ from the MMH.
- The **margin width** is the distance between the two dashed lines, equal to $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Primal–Dual Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    PRIM[Primal: Min 1/2 ||w||^2] -- "Lagrangian L" --> DUAL[Dual: Max L_D of alpha]
    PRIM -.->|"w* = sum alpha_i y_i x_i"| WREC[w Recovery]
    DUAL -.->|"alpha_i > 0 indicates SV"| SVID[Support Vector Identification]
    WREC --> MMHFIN[Final MMH: w·x + b = 0]
    SVID --> MMHFIN
    DUAL -- "Box constraints: alpha_i >= 0" --> KKT[KKT Complementary Slackness]
    KKT --> MMHFIN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask students to show the **flow from primal formulation to dual solution** and to identify which $\alpha_i$ are non-zero. Mastery of this pipeline is essential for any 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term **Maximum Margin Hyperplane** in the context of Support Vector Machines. Why is it preferred over other separating hyperplanes?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Maximum Margin Hyperplane is the decision boundary in a binary classification problem that separates the two classes while **maximizing the perpendicular distance (margin)** to the nearest training points of either class. These closest points are called **Support Vectors**.

It is preferred because:
- **(1 mark)** It is the most robust separator against noise and perturbations in test data.
- **(1 mark)** It minimizes the upper bound on generalization error (Vapnik's principle).
- **(1 mark)** It is unique and is determined only by support vectors, making the model sparse and efficient.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State and explain the **primal optimization problem** for finding the Maximum Margin Hyperplane for linearly separable data.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The primal optimization problem is:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

- **(1 mark)** Objective $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ minimizes the norm of the weight vector, which **maximizes the margin** $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$.
- **(1 mark)** The constraint ensures that every training point is correctly classified with a functional margin of at least 1.
- **(1 mark)** This is a convex QP, guaranteeing a unique global optimum.

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Answer the following:*

**(a)** Derive the **primal optimization problem** for the Maximum Margin Hyperplane from first principles, clearly stating the role of the canonical hyperplane normalization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Formulate the **Wolfe dual problem** using Lagrange multipliers, and explain how the **support vectors** are identified from the dual solution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

1. **(1 mark)** Define the separating hyperplane: $w \cdot x + b = 0$ and decision function $f(x) = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$.
2. **(1 mark)** Express the signed distance from a point $x_i$ to the hyperplane: $d_i = \frac{y_i(w \cdot x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$.
3. **(1 mark)** Define the geometric margin of the dataset: $\gamma = \min_i d_i$.
4. **(1 mark)** State the **maximization problem**: $\max_{w, b} \gamma$.
5. **(1 mark)** Apply **canonical normalization**: Choose scaling such that $\min_i y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$, which gives the constraint $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$.
6. **(1 mark)** Under this scaling, the margin becomes $\frac{1}{\|w\|}$, so maximizing it is equivalent to minimizing $\|w\|$.
7. **(1 mark)** Final primal formulation: $\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ subject to $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

1. **(1 mark)** Define the Lagrangian:
$$L(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$
with $\alpha_i \geq 0$.

2. **(1 mark)** Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = 0$ to obtain $w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i$.

3. **(1 mark)** Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = 0$ to obtain $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

4. **(1 mark)** Substitute back into $L$ to eliminate $w$ and $b$, yielding the dual:
$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

5. **(1 mark)** Write the dual constraints: $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

6. **(1 mark)** State the KKT complementary slackness: $\alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1] = 0$, from which $\alpha_i > 0$ iff $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$.

7. **(1 mark)** Conclude: **Support vectors** are those training points with $\alpha_i > 0$, which lie exactly on the margin hyperplanes $w \cdot x + b = \pm 1$. All other points have $\alpha_i = 0$ and do not affect the decision boundary.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Answer the following:*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Support Vectors** in SVM. With a neat diagram, illustrate the geometry of the Maximum Margin Hyperplane and clearly mark the support vectors, margin hyperplanes, and the decision boundary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following 2-D training data: $x_1 = (1, 1), y_1 = +1$; $x_2 = (1, -1), y_2 = +1$; $x_3 = (-1, -1), y_3 = -1$; $x_4 = (-1, 1), y_4 = -1$. Find the Maximum Margin Hyperplane by solving the dual problem. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

1. **(2 marks)** **Support Vectors:** The training samples that lie closest to the decision boundary and satisfy $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$. They are the only points with $\alpha_i > 0$ in the dual solution and the only points that determine the hyperplane's position.

2. **(5 marks)** **Diagram Description:**

```
                y2
                │
              1 ┤  ● x1 (+1)         ● x4 (-1)
                │
                │      ╱─────────────╲
                │     ╱  Margin Zone  ╲
                │    ╱                 ╲
              0 ┤───┼───────────────────┼──── x1
                │    ╲                 ╱
                │     ╲               ╱
                │      ╲─────────────╱
             -1 ┤  ● x2 (+1)         ● x3 (-1)
                │
```

The center vertical line is the MMH. The dashed lines on either side are the margin hyperplanes. Points touching the dashed lines are the **support vectors** (in this symmetric case, $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$).

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** By symmetry, the MMH must pass through the origin. Thus $b = 0$, and we need only solve for $w = (w_1, w_2)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** All four points are equidistant from the origin and by symmetry are all support vectors. The KKT conditions give:

$$y_i(w \cdot x_i) = 1 \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Write the four equations:
- $w_1 + w_2 = 1$
- $w_1 - w_2 = 1$
- $-w_1 - w_2 = 1$
- $-w_1 + w_2 = 1$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Add the first and fourth: $2 w_2 = 2 \Rightarrow w_2 = 0$. Add the first and second: $2 w_1 = 2 \Rightarrow w_1 = 1$. The third equation becomes $-1 - 0 = -1 \neq 1$ — contradiction!

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The correct interpretation: $y_i(w \cdot x_i) = 1$ means $w \cdot x_i = y_i$. So:
- $w_1 + w_2 = +1$
- $w_1 - w_2 = +1$
- $-w_1 - w_2 = -1$
- $-w_1 + w_2 = -1$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Solving: From eq.1 and eq.2, $2 w_2 = 0 \Rightarrow w_2 = 0$ and $w_1 = 1$. Eq.3: $-1 - 0 = -1$ ✓. Eq.4: $-1 + 0 = -1$ ✓.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final MMH:

$$w_1 x_1 + 0 \cdot x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 0$$

with margin $\frac{2}{\|w\|} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Constraints (most common — loses 1-2 marks):** Students frequently write $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$ but plug in values without ensuring the **sign of $y_i$** is applied to both terms. Always multiply the *entire bracket* by $y_i$ before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting KKT complementary slackness (loses 2-3 marks):** When asked to "identify support vectors," many students say "the points closest to the hyperplane" without invoking the KKT condition $\alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1] = 0$. Examiners expect the explicit KKT reference.
> 3. **Confusing functional and geometric margin (loses 1 mark):** Functional margin $\hat{\gamma} = y_i(w \cdot x_i + b)$ is **not** scale-invariant; geometric margin $\gamma = \frac{\hat{\gamma}}{\|w\|}$ **is**. The two differ by a factor of $\|w\|$. Many 3-mark questions are failed due to this confusion.
> 4. **Skipping the canonical normalization step (loses 2 marks):** Going directly from $\max \gamma$ to the QP without explaining Vapnik's scaling trick loses credit in long-answer questions.
> 5. **Writing dual objective with sign errors (loses 2 marks):** The dual is a **maximization** of $\sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$. Students often flip the sign or write the **minimization** form. Memorize the sign pattern carefully.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maximum Margin Hyperplane**

- **Core Idea:** The MMH is the hyperplane that maximizes the perpendicular distance to the closest training points from either class. **(1 mark)**
- **Hyperplane Equation:** $w \cdot x + b = 0$ with decision function $f(x) = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$. **(1 mark)**
- **Geometric Margin:** $\gamma = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$ under canonical normalization. **(1 mark)**
- **Primal Form (Hard Margin):** $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ subject to $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$. **(2 marks)**
- **Why $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$:** Mathematical convenience (clean gradient = $w$) and convexity guarantee. **(1 mark)**
- **Support Vectors:** Training points with $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$ — they touch the margin hyperplanes. **(1 mark)**
- **Lagrangian:** $L = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 - \sum_i \alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1]$ with $\alpha_i \geq 0$. **(2 marks)**
- **Stationarity:** $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$. **(1 mark)**
- **Wolfe Dual:** $\max_\alpha \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$ with $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$. **(2 marks)**
- **KKT Complementary Slackness:** $\alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) - 1] = 0$. This is the bridge identifying which $\alpha_i$ (and hence which $x_i$) are support vectors. **(1 mark)**
- **Recovery Formulas:** $w^* = \sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i x_i$ and $b^* = y_s - \sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i (x_i \cdot x_s)$ for any support vector $s$. **(1 mark)**
- **Sparsity:** Only support vectors ($\alpha_i > 0$) contribute to $w$. This is what makes SVMs memory-efficient. **(1 mark)**
- **Linearly Separable Assumption:** The hard-margin formulation requires it. Soft-margin (with slack variables $\xi_i$) is the extension for non-separable data. **(1 mark)**
- **Kernel Trick Entry Point:** Replacing $x_i \cdot x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$ in the dual enables non-linear MMH in the original space. **(1 mark)**
- **Why MMH Over Other Hyperplanes:** Robustness to noise, lower VC dimension, better generalization bound (Vapnik). **(1 mark)**

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Maximum Margin Hyperplane**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-linear SVM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-linear SVM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Linear Support Vector Machine (SVM) Definition**
> A **Non-Linear SVM** is a supervised classification algorithm that extends the capabilities of the standard linear SVM by employing **kernel functions** to implicitly map input data, which is not linearly separable in its original space, into a higher-dimensional **feature space**, where a linear separating hyperplane can be constructed.

### Why Non-Linear SVM is Necessary
Standard linear SVMs can only draw a **straight line (2D)** or a **flat hyperplane (nD)** to separate classes. Real-world datasets (e.g., XOR problem, image recognition, medical diagnosis) are rarely linearly separable. A linear boundary fails catastrophically in such cases.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Marble Lift" Intuition**
> Imagine you have two types of marbles — red and blue — scattered on a 2D table such that no straight stick can separate them. You are stuck! Now, imagine you have magical powers to **lift the marbles into 3D air** along a third axis (height) based on some rule. Suddenly, a **flat cardboard sheet** can perfectly slice through the air and separate the red from the blue marbles.
> - **Lifting the marbles** = Mapping data $\mathbf{x}$ to a higher-dimensional space $\phi(\mathbf{x})$.
> - **Flat cardboard sheet** = Linear hyperplane in the high-dimensional space.
> - **Kernel trick** = Performing the lift *mathematically* without actually computing the coordinates in the high-dimensional space (because that can be computationally impossible).

### Key Terminology
- **Feature Space ($\mathcal{F}$):** The high-dimensional space where data becomes linearly separable.
- **Feature Map ($\phi : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{F}$):** The function that transforms input vectors.
- **Kernel Function ($K$):** A function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle$ that computes inner products in feature space directly from the original space.
- **Slack Variable ($\xi_i$):** Allows misclassification for non-separable data (Soft Margin).
- **Penalty Parameter ($C$):** Controls the trade-off between margin width and classification error.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Pipeline of Non-Linear SVM
1. **Receive Data:** Input pairs $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ where $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$.
2. **Choose a Kernel:** Select $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ appropriate for the data distribution.
3. **Solve the Optimization:** Maximize the dual objective to find the optimal Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i$.
4. **Identify Support Vectors:** Data points where $\alpha_i > 0$ lie closest to the decision boundary.
5. **Construct the Decision Function:** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$.
6. **Predict Class:** $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(f(\mathbf{x}))$.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step
- **Why a kernel?** Direct computation of $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ for polynomial features of degree $d$ results in $\binom{n+d}{d}$ dimensions — often infeasible (e.g., $d=10, n=100 \Rightarrow$ billions of features).
- **Why slack variables?** Real data has noise and overlapping classes. A perfectly hard margin would overfit or have no solution.
- **Why the dual form?** It is the *only* form in which the kernel trick can be applied, because it depends solely on inner products of training examples.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations**

| Symbol / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linear Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z}$ | Default; equivalent to standard linear SVM. |
| Polynomial Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + r)^d$ | Captures polynomial feature interactions; degree $d$, coef0 $r$. |
| RBF (Gaussian) Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp(-\gamma \, \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \vert\vert^2)$ | Most popular; maps to infinite-dimensional space. |
| Sigmoid Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \tanh(\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + r)$ | Originates from neural networks; not always a valid kernel. |
| Primal Objective (Soft Margin) | $\min_{\mathbf{w},b,\xi} \frac{1}{2} \vert\vert \mathbf{w} \vert\vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$ | Minimizes margin norm and misclassification penalty. |
| Primal Constraint | $y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$ | Ensures correct classification within margin tolerance. |
| Dual Objective (Maximization) | $\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | The form where the **kernel trick** is applied. |
| Box Constraint (Dual) | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | Bounds Lagrange multipliers. |
| Equality Constraint (Dual) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$ | Ensures class balance in support vectors. |
| Decision Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$ | Used to classify new test points. |
| Margin Width | $\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\vert\vert \mathbf{w} \vert\vert}$ | Geometric distance between support hyperplanes. |
| Gaussian $\gamma$ Relation | $\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}$ | Higher $\gamma$ $\Rightarrow$ narrower Gaussian $\Rightarrow$ more complex boundary. |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Bioinformatics:** Cancer classification from gene expression data (RBF kernel).
- **Computer Vision:** Face detection, handwriting recognition (RBF / Polynomial).
- **Text Mining:** Spam filtering, sentiment classification (Linear kernel).
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Soil classification and landslide prediction (Polynomial / RBF).
- **Medical Diagnosis:** ECG, MRI, and disease prediction where classes are non-linearly entangled.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Primal Soft-Margin SVM to Dual Form

**Step 1: Primal Optimization Problem (Soft Margin)**
We start with the primal formulation of the non-linear soft-margin SVM.

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \quad \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i
$$

$$
\text{subject to: } y_i \left( \mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b \right) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, n
$$

This says: keep the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ small (large margin) and the total slack $\sum \xi_i$ small (few errors), penalized by $C$.

**Step 2: Construct the Lagrangian**
Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for the margin constraints and $\mu_i \geq 0$ for the non-negativity of slacks.

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_i \xi_i
$$

**Step 3: Stationarity Conditions (Setting Partial Derivatives to Zero)**

Take partial derivatives w.r.t. the primal variables $\mathbf{w}$, $b$, and $\xi_i$, and set them to zero.

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C
$$

**Step 4: Substitute Back into the Lagrangian**
Substitute the stationarity relations back into $\mathcal{L}$. The $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w}$ and $\xi_i$ terms get eliminated, leaving only $\alpha$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_D(\alpha) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

We replaced the inner product $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ with the kernel $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$. This substitution is the **Kernel Trick** — it allows us to operate in the high-dimensional feature space **without ever computing $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly**.

**Step 5: Final Dual Problem**

$$
\max_{\alpha} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

$$
\text{subject to: } \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: RBF Kernel Expanded in Infinite Dimensions

The RBF kernel is a Taylor-series expansion in disguise. We start with the definition and use the identity $\exp(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^k}{k!}$.

**Step 1: Rewrite the RBF Kernel**
Given $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^2)$, expand the squared norm.

$$
\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^2 = \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 + \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2 - 2 \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z}
$$

So,

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\left( -\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 \right) \exp\left( -\gamma \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2 \right) \exp\left( 2\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} \right)
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Taylor Series to the Inner Product Term**

$$
\exp(2\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(2\gamma)^k}{k!} (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z})^k
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Back**

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(2\gamma)^k}{k!} \left[ \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2) \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2) \right] (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z})^k
$$

This shows RBF is equivalent to an **infinite-dimensional polynomial expansion**, justifying its immense expressive power.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Polynomial Kernel (Degree 2, 2D Input)

Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)$ and $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2)$. Direct feature map of degree 2 is:

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1^2, \sqrt{2} x_1 x_2, x_2^2, \sqrt{2} x_1, \sqrt{2} x_2, 1)
$$

**Step 1: Compute $\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z})$**

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z}) = x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 z_1 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2 + 2 x_1 z_1 + 2 x_2 z_2 + 1
$$

**Step 2: Recognize the Pattern**

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z}) = (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2 + 1)^2 = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + 1)^2
$$

**Step 3: Generalize**
For degree $d$ and offset $r$, $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + r)^d$. We never need to compute the $\binom{n+d}{d}$-dimensional $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Non-Linear SVM Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Training Data x_i, y_i] --> B[Choose Kernel Function K]
    B --> C[Compute Kernel Matrix K_ij]
    C --> D[Solve Dual Optimization Problem]
    D --> E[Identify Support Vectors alpha_i greater than 0]
    E --> F[Compute Bias Term b]
    F --> G[Construct Decision Function f of x]
    G --> H[New Test Point x_test]
    H --> I[Apply f of x_test to get Class Label]
```

### 4.2 Implicit Feature Mapping via Kernel Trick

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Space X - 2D] -- Feature Map phi --> B[High-Dim Feature Space F - InfinteD]
    A -- Kernel Function K --> C[Inner Product in F - Computed in X]
    B --> D[Linear Hyperplane in F]
    C --> E[Non-Linear Boundary in X]
    D --> E
```

### 4.3 Margin and Support Vector Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputSpace
        A1[Class +1 Region]
        A2[Margin Boundary 1]
        A3[Support Vectors alpha_i greater than 0]
        A4[Decision Boundary]
        A5[Margin Boundary 2]
        A6[Class -1 Region]
    end
    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A3 --- A4
    A4 --- A5
    A5 --- A6
```

### 4.4 Kernel Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Kernel] --> B{N is large, n features greater than m samples}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Linear Kernel]
    B -- No --> D{N is small, features non-linear}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use RBF Kernel Default]
    D -- No --> F{Need explicit feature interactions}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Polynomial Kernel]
    F -- No --> H[Use Sigmoid Kernel Rare]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define the Kernel Trick in SVM. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**Model Answer:**
The **kernel trick** is a mathematical technique in Support Vector Machines that allows the algorithm to operate in a high-dimensional feature space *without explicitly computing the coordinates* of the data in that space. Instead of computing the costly feature map $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ and then taking inner products $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$, we define a **kernel function** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ that returns the inner product directly from the original input vectors.

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

This makes non-linear classification computationally feasible, as $K$ is often cheap to evaluate even when $\phi$ would map to an infinite-dimensional space (as in the RBF kernel). It is applied in the dual formulation of the SVM optimization problem.

**Keywords for 3 marks:** Kernel function, inner product, feature space, computational efficiency.

---

#### Question 2: List any four commonly used kernel functions in SVM with their formulas. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Model Answer:**
The four commonly used kernel functions in SVM are:

1. **Linear Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z}$
2. **Polynomial Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + r)^d$
3. **Radial Basis Function (RBF) / Gaussian Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^2)$
4. **Sigmoid Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \tanh(\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + r)$

Where $\gamma$ is the kernel coefficient, $r$ is the independent term, and $d$ is the polynomial degree.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing the primal formulation when the question asks for the *dual* — both are scored separately. Always read the verb in the question.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to substitute $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ in the final dual expression. This loses **2 marks** in derivation questions.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing $\gamma$ in the RBF kernel with $\gamma$ in the polynomial kernel — they share the symbol but have different roles and ranges. Always define them.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting the constraint $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ in the dual problem statement.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing decision function using $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}$ instead of kernel form $\sum \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x})$ — for non-linear SVM, only the kernel form is valid.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]

**(a) Explain the concept of non-linear classification using SVM with a neat diagram. Discuss how the kernel trick enables this. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Non-linear classification using SVM addresses the limitation of linear SVM, which can only separate classes using a straight hyperplane. In real-world problems, data often requires curved or irregular decision boundaries (e.g., concentric circles, XOR patterns).

**Working Principle:**
1. The input data $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is mapped to a higher-dimensional feature space $\mathcal{F}$ using a feature map $\phi : \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathcal{F}$.
2. In $\mathcal{F}$, a linear hyperplane is constructed that separates the classes.
3. When this hyperplane is projected back to the original input space, it appears as a non-linear boundary.

**Why Kernel Trick is Needed:**
Direct computation of $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ for high-degree polynomials leads to a feature space of dimension $\binom{n+d}{d}$, which becomes computationally infeasible for moderate $n$ and $d$. The kernel trick circumvents this by defining:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

This way, we evaluate the kernel in the original low-dimensional space, but it behaves *as if* we performed the inner product in the high-dimensional space.

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
Original 2D Space (Non-Linear Boundary)        High-Dim Feature Space (Linear Boundary)
   + + *  *  *                                        .  +    +
      *   +   *                              +   . /          |
   +    *    *                              . /  HYPERPLANE    |
      *  +    *                              /                 +
   *    +   *                               +   *    *    *
        +   *                                  *   *  *
   Legend: + = Class +1, * = Class -1
```

**Conclusion:** The kernel trick makes non-linear SVM both **expressive** and **computationally tractable**, enabling applications in image, text, and biological data.

---

**(b) Derive the dual optimization problem for the soft-margin non-linear SVM starting from the primal formulation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Primal Problem**
$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i
$$
subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$.

**Step 2: Lagrangian**
$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum \xi_i - \sum \alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b) - 1 + \xi_i] - \sum \mu_i \xi_i
$$

**Step 3: Stationarity (KKT Conditions)**
- $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$
- $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \Rightarrow \sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$
- $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \Rightarrow C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \Rightarrow 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$

**Step 4: Substitute Back**

Substitute $\mathbf{w}$ from Step 3 into $\mathcal{L}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_D &= \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} - \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + \sum \alpha_i \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum \alpha_i y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) \right)^\top \left( \sum \alpha_j y_j \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \right) - \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) + \sum \alpha_i \\
&= \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final Dual**

$$
\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)
$$

subject to: $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ and $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

The substitution of $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ for the inner product is the **kernel trick applied**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model — Alternative]

**(a) Compare the Linear, Polynomial, and RBF kernels in SVM. Discuss a suitable application scenario for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Aspect | Linear Kernel | Polynomial Kernel | RBF (Gaussian) Kernel |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Formula** | $K = \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z}$ | $K = (\gamma \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + r)^d$ | $K = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^2)$ |
| **Feature Space** | Original space | Degree-$d$ polynomial expansion | Infinite-dimensional |
| **Hyperparameters** | None | $\gamma, r, d$ | $\gamma$ |
| **Computational Cost** | Lowest | Moderate (degree-dependent) | Higher (exp) |
| **Boundary Type** | Linear | Polynomial curves | Highly flexible, smooth |
| **Risk of Overfitting** | Lowest | High at large $d$ | Highest if $\gamma$ is large |
| **Suitable For** | Text classification, large sparse data | Image processing, moderate non-linearity | Most general tabular data, non-linear patterns |
| **Sample Size Suitability** | Very large $n$ | Medium $n$ | Small to medium $n$ |

**Application Scenarios:**
- **Linear:** Spam detection (high-dimensional sparse text data, $n \gg m$).
- **Polynomial:** Handwritten digit recognition (e.g., MNIST) where pixel interactions matter.
- **RBF:** Bioinformatics (gene expression, cancer classification) with complex, non-linear class boundaries.

---

**(b) Explain the RBF (Gaussian) kernel in detail. Show that it corresponds to an infinite-dimensional feature map. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel**, also called the **Gaussian kernel**, is defined as:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\left( -\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^2 \right), \quad \gamma > 0$$

**Step 1: Expand the Squared Norm**

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\left( -\gamma \left[ \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 + \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2 - 2\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} \right] \right)
$$

**Step 2: Factor the Exponential**

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\left( -\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 \right) \exp\left( -\gamma \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2 \right) \exp\left( 2\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} \right)
$$

**Step 3: Apply Taylor Expansion to the Inner-Product Term**

Using $\exp(u) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{u^k}{k!}$:

$$
\exp\left( 2\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} \right) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(2\gamma)^k}{k!} (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z})^k
$$

**Step 4: Combine**

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(2\gamma)^k}{k!} \left[ \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2) \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2) \right] (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z})^k
$$

This expansion is equivalent to an **infinite-dimensional inner product**:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z}), \quad \text{where } \phi(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^{\infty}
$$

**Step 5: Properties of RBF Kernel**
- It is a **valid Mercer kernel** (positive semi-definite).
- It is **shift-invariant:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = K(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z}, 0)$.
- It is **localized:** $K \to 0$ as $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert \to \infty$.
- The parameter $\gamma$ (or bandwidth $\sigma$) controls smoothness: large $\gamma$ $\Rightarrow$ narrow kernel $\Rightarrow$ complex boundary; small $\gamma$ $\Rightarrow$ wide kernel $\Rightarrow$ smoother boundary.

**Conclusion:** The RBF kernel implicitly maps data to an infinite-dimensional space, providing immense flexibility to model complex non-linear patterns — yet it requires only $\mathcal{O}(d)$ operations to evaluate, making it both powerful and efficient.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Non-linear SVM** handles data that is **not linearly separable** by using kernels to project data into a higher-dimensional feature space.
- The **kernel trick** computes inner products in the high-dimensional space *implicitly*, avoiding the cost of explicit feature mapping.
- **Mercer's Theorem** governs valid kernels: $K$ must be symmetric and positive semi-definite.
- The **dual formulation** is mandatory for non-linear SVM; the primal cannot use kernels directly.
- **Decision function** in non-linear SVM: $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$, not $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}$.
- **Soft margin parameter $C$:** large $C$ $\Rightarrow$ low bias / high variance; small $C$ $\Rightarrow$ high bias / low variance.
- **RBF $\gamma$:** large $\gamma$ $\Rightarrow$ complex boundary / risk of overfitting; small $\gamma$ $\Rightarrow$ smoother boundary / underfitting.
- **Polynomial degree $d$:** higher $d$ $\Rightarrow$ more flexibility but $\binom{n+d}{d}$ feature explosion — use the kernel form.
- **Support vectors** are the only training points that influence the decision boundary; all others have $\alpha_i = 0$.
- **Most common kernel in practice:** RBF (default in scikit-learn's `SVC`).
- **Sigmoid kernel** is *not* always a valid Mercer kernel — use with caution.
- The kernel matrix $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is **positive semi-definite** and of size $n \times n$.
- **Bias term $b$** is computed as: $b = y_i - \sum \alpha_j y_j K(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i)$ for any support vector with $0 < \alpha_i < C$.
- The **margin** in feature space is $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$, but this is computed indirectly via $\alpha$ in the dual.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kernels for Learning Non-linear Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kernels for Learning Non-linear Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of supervised learning, many real-world datasets exhibit **non-linear decision boundaries** that cannot be separated by a simple hyperplane in the original input space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$. The **Kernel Method** is a mathematical technique that enables linear learning algorithms (such as Support Vector Machines, Perceptrons, Ridge Regression, and PCA) to learn **non-linear functions** by implicitly mapping the original input data into a higher-dimensional (often infinite-dimensional) feature space $\mathcal{F}$, where a linear separator exists.

Formally, given an input space $\mathcal{X}$, we define a feature map $\phi : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$, where $\mathcal{F}$ is a Hilbert space. The corresponding **kernel function** is defined as:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}), \phi(\mathbf{z}) \rangle_{\mathcal{F}}$$

The **Kernel Trick** refers to the computational shortcut of evaluating $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ directly in the input space using a kernel function, **without ever explicitly computing** the coordinates of $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ in the high-dimensional feature space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A kernel is a function that corresponds to an inner product in some expanded feature space. The kernel trick allows us to operate in the high-dimensional feature space using only the original low-dimensional inputs, provided the kernel satisfies **Mercer's Condition**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Shadow on the Wall"**
>
> Imagine you have a 3D sculpture of a tangled wire (the data) sitting on a table. Looking at its **shadow on the 2D wall** (the original input space), the shadow looks like a hopelessly mixed, inseparable blob. You cannot draw a straight line to classify the shadow.
>
