### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the back propagation algorithm in detail. State and derive the four fundamental equations used in the algorithm.

**(b) [7 Marks)** For a 2-2-1 feedforward network with sigmoid activation, the following values are given:
- Input: $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 0$; Target: $y = 1$
- Hidden layer weights: $w_{11} = 0.5, w_{12} = 0.4, w_{21} = 0.3, w_{22} = 0.6$
- Hidden biases: $b_1 = 0.1, b_2 = 0.2$
- Output layer weights: $v_1 = 0.7, v_2 = 0.8$; Output bias: $b_3 = 0.1$
- Learning rate: $\eta = 0.5$

Compute the output of the network and update all weights using one iteration of back propagation.

#### **Model Solution for B-A (a)**

**Step 1: Define the architecture and notation.**

Let the network have:
- Input layer with 2 neurons: $x_1, x_2$
- Hidden layer (layer 2) with 2 neurons using sigmoid activation $\sigma$
- Output layer (layer 3) with 1 neuron using sigmoid activation
- Loss: $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}(y - a^{(3)})^2$

**Step 2: Present the four fundamental equations.**

**Equation 1 — Output Error:**

$$
\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{a^{(L)}} \mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)}) = (a^{(L)} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})
$$

**Derivation:** Starting from $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_j (y_j - a_j^{(L)})^2$ and applying the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_j^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_j^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_j^{(L)}}{\partial z_j^{(L)}} = -(y_j - a_j^{(L)}) \cdot a_j^{(L)}(1 - a_j^{(L)})
$$

which gives the boxed equation.

**Equation 2 — Backprop Equation for Hidden Layers:**

$$
\delta^{(l)} = \bigl((W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)}\bigr) \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})
$$

**Derivation:** Routing the derivative through the next layer's net inputs using the chain rule:

$$
\delta_j^{(l)} = \sum_k \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k^{(l+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_k^{(l+1)}}{\partial z_j^{(l)}} = \sigma'(z_j^{(l)}) \sum_k w_{kj}^{(l+1)} \delta_k^{(l+1)}
$$

**Equation 3 — Weight Gradient:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T
$$

**Derivation:** Since $z_j^{(l)} = \sum_k w_{jk}^{(l)} a_k^{(l-1)} + b_j^{(l)}$, we have $\partial z_j^{(l)}/\partial w_{jk}^{(l)} = a_k^{(l-1)}$, and so $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial w_{jk}^{(l)} = \delta_j^{(l)} a_k^{(l-1)}$.

**Equation 4 — Bias Gradient:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}
$$

**Derivation:** Since $\partial z_j^{(l)} / \partial b_j^{(l)} = 1$, the bias gradient equals $\delta_j^{(l)}$.

**Step 3: Discuss the algorithm in words.** The forward pass propagates signals; the backward pass propagates errors. Weights and biases are updated as $W := W - \eta \cdot \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W$.

#### **Model Solution for B-A (b)**

**Step 1: Forward pass — compute hidden net inputs.**

$$
z_1^{(2)} = w_{11} x_1 + w_{12} x_2 + b_1 = 0.5(1) + 0.4(0) + 0.1 = 0.6
$$

$$
z_2^{(2)} = w_{21} x_1 + w_{22} x_2 + b_2 = 0.3(1) + 0.6(0) + 0.2 = 0.5
$$

**Step 2: Hidden activations.**

$$
a_1^{(2)} = \sigma(0.6) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.6}} = \frac{1}{1.5488} \approx 0.6457
$$

$$
a_2^{(2)} = \sigma(0.5) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.5}} = \frac{1}{1.6065} \approx 0.6225
$$

**Step 3: Output net input and activation.**

$$
z^{(3)} = v_1 a_1^{(2)} + v_2 a_2^{(2)} + b_3 = 0.7(0.6457) + 0.8(0.6225) + 0.1
$$

$$
z^{(3)} = 0.4520 + 0.4980 + 0.1000 = 1.0500
$$

$$
a^{(3)} = \sigma(1.05) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-1.05}} = \frac{1}{2.8577} \approx 0.3499
$$

**Step 4: Compute loss and output error.**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.3499)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.6501)^2 \approx 0.2113
$$

$$
\delta^{(3)} = (a^{(3)} - y) \cdot a^{(3)}(1 - a^{(3)}) = (0.3499 - 1)(0.3499)(0.6501)
$$

$$
\delta^{(3)} = (-0.6501)(0.2274) \approx -0.1479
$$

**Step 5: Hidden layer errors.**

$$
\delta_1^{(2)} = v_1 \cdot \delta^{(3)} \cdot a_1^{(2)}(1 - a_1^{(2)}) = 0.7 \cdot (-0.1479) \cdot (0.6457)(0.3543)
$$

$$
\delta_1^{(2)} = (-0.1035)(0.2288) \approx -0.02368
$$

$$
\delta_2^{(2)} = v_2 \cdot \delta^{(3)} \cdot a_2^{(2)}(1 - a_2^{(2)}) = 0.8 \cdot (-0.1479) \cdot (0.6225)(0.3775)
$$

$$
\delta_2^{(2)} = (-0.1183)(0.2350) \approx -0.02780
$$

**Step 6: Update output weights and bias.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial v_1} = \delta^{(3)} a_1^{(2)} = -0.1479 \cdot 0.6457 \approx -0.0955
$$

$$
v_1^{\text{new}} = 0.7 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.0955) = 0.7 + 0.0478 = 0.7478
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial v_2} = \delta^{(3)} a_2^{(2)} = -0.1479 \cdot 0.6225 \approx -0.0921
$$

$$
v_2^{\text{new}} = 0.8 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.0921) = 0.8 + 0.0461 = 0.8461
$$

$$
b_3^{\text{new}} = 0.1 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.1479) = 0.1 + 0.0740 = 0.1740
$$

**Step 7: Update hidden weights and biases.**

$$
w_{11}^{\text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.5 \cdot (\delta_1^{(2)} \cdot x_1) = 0.5 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.02368 \cdot 1) = 0.5 + 0.01184 = 0.51184
$$

$$
w_{12}^{\text{new}} = 0.4 - 0.5 \cdot (\delta_1^{(2)} \cdot x_2) = 0.4 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.02368 \cdot 0) = 0.4
$$

$$
w_{21}^{\text{new}} = 0.3 - 0.5 \cdot (\delta_2^{(2)} \cdot x_1) = 0.3 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.02780) = 0.3 + 0.01390 = 0.31390
$$

$$
w_{22}^{\text{new}} = 0.6 - 0.5 \cdot (\delta_2^{(2)} \cdot x_2) = 0.6 - 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0.6
$$

$$
b_1^{\text{new}} = 0.1 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.02368) = 0.1 + 0.01184 = 0.11184
$$

$$
b_2^{\text{new}} = 0.2 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.02780) = 0.2 + 0.01390 = 0.21390
$$

**Final Updated Parameters Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Old | New |
|---|---|---|
| $v_1$ | 0.7 | **0.7478** |
| $v_2$ | 0.8 | **0.8461** |
| $b_3$ | 0.1 | **0.1740** |
| $w_{11}$ | 0.5 | **0.51184** |
| $w_{12}$ | 0.4 | **0.4000** |
| $w_{21}$ | 0.3 | **0.31390** |
| $w_{22}$ | 0.6 | **0.6000** |
| $b_1$ | 0.1 | **0.11184** |
| $b_2$ | 0.2 | **0.21390** |

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the activation function in a neural network. Compare sigmoid, tanh, and ReLU activation functions with respect to their derivatives and suitability in back propagation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Vanishing Gradient Problem** in deep networks. Explain how it affects back propagation and suggest two techniques to mitigate it.

#### **Model Solution for B-B (a)**

**Step 1: Role of Activation Function.**

An activation function introduces **non-linearity** into the neural network. Without it, the network — regardless of depth — would collapse to a *single linear transformation* of the input, and could not learn complex patterns (e.g., XOR). During back propagation, the activation derivative $\sigma'(z)$ is multiplied with the error signal, directly controlling *how much* each neuron learns.

**Step 2: Comparison Table.**

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\sigma(z) = \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\tanh(z) = \tfrac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$ | $\max(0, z)$ |
| Output range | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Derivative | $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | $0$ if $z<0$, else $1$ |
| Max derivative | $\mathbf{0.25}$ at $z=0$ | $\mathbf{1}$ at $z=0$ | $\mathbf{1}$ for $z > 0$ |
| Zero-centered? | No (positive outputs) | Yes | No |
| Vanishing gradient? | Yes (severe) | Yes (moderate) | No (for $z>0$) |
| Computational cost | Expensive (exponential) | Expensive | Cheap (just a max) |

**Step 3: Suitability for Back Propagation.**

- **Sigmoid** is rarely used in hidden layers of deep networks today because its maximum derivative is only $0.25$. When this is multiplied across many layers, gradients shrink exponentially, causing the **vanishing gradient problem**.
- **Tanh** is preferred over sigmoid for hidden layers because it is zero-centered, leading to faster convergence. However, it still saturates for large $|z|$.
- **ReLU** is the **default choice** for hidden layers in modern deep networks because its derivative is exactly $1$ for positive inputs, allowing gradients to flow unimpeded during back propagation.

#### **Model Solution for B-B (b)**

**Step 1: Definition of the Vanishing Gradient Problem.**

The **Vanishing Gradient Problem** refers to the phenomenon in which the gradients of the loss with respect to the weights in *early* (i.e., input-side) layers of a deep network become **exponentially small** during back propagation. As a result, the weights in these layers update negligibly, and the network effectively *fails to learn* the lower-level features.

**Step 2: Mathematical Cause.**

From the back propagation equation:

$$
\delta^{(l)} = \bigl((W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)}\bigr) \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})
$$

If the activation function (e.g., sigmoid) has derivative $\sigma'(z) \le 0.25$, and the weights are initialized smaller than 1, then each layer multiplies the gradient by a factor $< 1$. Across $L$ layers, the gradient decays roughly as $(0.25 \cdot \text{weight})^L \to 0$ as $L$ grows.

**Step 3: Consequences.**

- Early layers learn very slowly (or not at all).
- Training stagnates; the loss plateaus at a high value.
- The network behaves as if only the last few layers are being trained.

**Step 4: Mitigation Techniques.**

1. **Use ReLU (or Leaky ReLU) Activation:** ReLU has derivative $1$ for $z > 0$, so the gradient does not shrink. Leaky ReLU additionally allows a small gradient for $z < 0$, avoiding the "dying ReLU" issue.
2. **Proper Weight Initialization:** Schemes such as **He Initialization** (variance $= 2/n_{in}$ for ReLU) or **Xavier/Glorot Initialization** (variance $= 1/n_{in}$ for tanh) keep the variance of activations and gradients stable across layers.
3. *(Bonus, optional)* **Batch Normalization:** Normalizes activations within each mini-batch, keeping them in a regime where derivatives are not too small.
4. *(Bonus, optional)* **Residual Connections (ResNets):** Provide skip connections that allow gradients to bypass layers, preventing shrinkage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Vanishing vs. Exploding Gradient:** Students often mix up these two problems. *Vanishing* = gradients shrink to zero (sigmoid, deep nets). *Exploding* = gradients grow to infinity (improper init, RNNs). State explicitly which is which.
> 2. **Forgetting the Chain Rule in Derivations:** Marks for the four fundamental equations are awarded only if you *apply* the chain rule step-by-step. Writing the final equation without derivation gets at most half credit.
> 3. **Sign Errors in the Output Delta:** It is $\delta^{(L)} = (a^{(L)} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$, NOT $(y - a^{(L)})$. Note that for cross-entropy loss the form is different — always re-derive based on the loss function given.
> 4. **Confusing Layer Indexing:** KTU uses 1-indexed layers (input = 1). Hidden layer after input is 2. Output is $L$. Do not mix with 0-indexed Python conventions.
> 5. **Skipping Numerical Steps:** In Part B numerical questions, examiners expect every intermediate arithmetic step. A direct jump to the final weights = **0 marks** for computation, even if the final answer is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this section to your study wall before the exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** Back Propagation = forward pass + backward pass + gradient descent.
- [x] **Purpose:** Compute gradients of loss w.r.t. weights efficiently in $O(W)$ time.
- [x] **Mathematical Engine:** Chain rule of calculus.
- [x] **Output Delta:** $\delta^{(L)} = (a^{(L)} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$ — for MSE loss.
- [x] **Hidden Delta:** $\delta^{(l)} = ((W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)}) \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$.
- [x] **Weight Gradient:** $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W^{(l)} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$.
- [x] **Bias Gradient:** $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial b^{(l)} = \delta^{(l)}$.
- [x] **Update Rule:** $W := W - \eta \cdot \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W$; $b := b - \eta \cdot \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial b$.
- [x] **Learning Rate:** Too small = slow; too large = oscillation/divergence; sweet spot $\approx 0.01$ to $0.1$.
- [x] **Activation Choices:** Sigmoid (max derivative $0.25$, vanishing), tanh (max derivative $1$, still saturates), ReLU (derivative $1$ for $z>0$, default for deep nets).
- [x] **Sigmoid Derivative Trick:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ — use the *activation value*, not the net input.
- [x] **Initialization:** Never use zeros (causes symmetry). Use small random values.
- [x] **Vanishing Gradient:** Mitigated by ReLU + He initialization + BatchNorm.
- [x] **Exploding Gradient:** Mitigated by gradient clipping.
- [x] **Notation Convention:** Input = layer 1, Hidden = layer 2, $\ldots$, Output = layer $L$. Inputs are *activations* of layer 1.
- [x] **Exam Trick:** Always show the *forward pass outputs* before the backward pass — partial credit even if backprop calculation is incomplete.
- [x] **Common Loss Function:** MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification.
- [x] **Computational Cost:** One forward + one backward per example = $O(W)$ time. Total epoch cost = $O(m \cdot W)$ for $m$ examples.
- [x] **Historical Note:** Back Propagation was popularized by Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986) — the foundational paper of modern deep learning. *(KTU sometimes asks this as a 1-mark extra.)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning Clustering - Similarity Measures, Hierarchical Clustering - Agglomerative Clustering, Partitional Clustering, K-Means Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning Clustering - Similarity Measures, Hierarchical Clustering - Agglomerative Clustering, Partitional Clustering, K-Means Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unsupervised Learning — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (PCCST503 - Module 4)**
> **Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of machine learning in which a model is trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ consisting of input feature vectors **without any corresponding target labels**. The objective is to discover hidden structure, intrinsic patterns, or meaningful organization (such as clusters, low-dimensional manifolds, or density regions) directly from the input space $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$.

Unlike **supervised learning**, there is no explicit signal $y_i$ telling the algorithm the "correct answer." The algorithm must instead formulate its own notion of what constitutes a useful grouping, usually by optimizing an internal criterion such as **intra-cluster compactness** and **inter-cluster separation**.

**Clustering** is the most prominent sub-task of unsupervised learning. Formally:

> [!NOTE]
> **Clustering (KTU Board Definition)**
> Given $N$ data points in a $d$-dimensional feature space, clustering seeks to partition the data into $K$ groups (clusters) $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$ such that:
> 1. $C_i \neq \emptyset$ for all $i$,
> 2. $\bigcup_{i=1}^{K} C_i = \mathcal{D}$,
> 3. $C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$.
> The aim is to maximize the similarity of points within a cluster while maximizing the dissimilarity between points in different clusters.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Without a Catalogue"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder)**
> Imagine you walk into a brand-new library that has **thousands of books dumped in a single pile** — there is no catalogue, no Dewey Decimal System, and no librarian. You are asked to organize the books sensibly.
>
> You will naturally:
> 1. Pick up two books that *look* similar (say, both have blue covers and are thin) and place them together.
> 2. Continue building small piles based on what "feels" similar — weight, color, size, topic on the spine.
> 3. Eventually you have several piles, each more cohesive than the random starting pile.
>
> **You are performing clustering.** You had no labels telling you "this is a Science pile." You discovered the groups yourself, using an internal notion of similarity.
>
> - **Similarity Measures** = your rule for "look similar" (e.g., both blue, both thin, both heavy).
> - **Partitional Clustering (K-Means)** = deciding *upfront* "I want exactly 3 piles" and then re-sorting books until each pile is tight.
> - **Hierarchical Clustering (Agglomerative)** = starting with each book as its own pile, then *greedily* merging the two most similar piles until you have just a few big ones — producing a **tree of merges** (a dendrogram).

---

## 1.3 The Central Role of Similarity Measures

The entire edifice of clustering rests on one primitive operation: **measuring how similar (or dissimilar) two points are**.

- A **similarity measure** $s(x_i, x_j)$ returns a *larger* value when points are alike.
- A **distance (dissimilarity) measure** $d(x_i, x_j)$ returns a *smaller* value when points are alike.

These two are often interchangeable because for many metrics, $d = 1 - s$ or $d = \sqrt{1 - s}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In a Board Exam, the choice of similarity measure is *not* arbitrary. Euclidean distance works for **continuous, isotropic** features; Manhattan distance is robust to **outliers**; Cosine similarity is the standard for **text/document/vector embeddings**; Jaccard is used for **binary/categorical sets**.

---

## 1.4 The Two Grand Families of Clustering

| Family | Strategy | Output | Representative Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hierarchical** | Build a tree of nested clusters (bottom-up or top-down) | Dendrogram + flat cut | **Agglomerative** (AGNES), Divisive (DIANA) |
| **Partitional** | Directly partition the data into $K$ groups | A single set of $K$ clusters | **K-Means**, K-Medoids, DBSCAN (density-based variant) |

The rest of these notes will dissect similarity measures first, then **Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering**, and finally **K-Means Partitional Clustering** — the three most heavily tested topics in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Similarity & Distance Measures — The Foundation Layer

A **distance metric** $d: X \times X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ on a feature space $X$ is a function that, for all $x, y, z \in X$, satisfies the four metric axioms:

1. **Non-negativity:** $d(x, y) \geq 0$
2. **Identity of indiscernibles:** $d(x, y) = 0 \iff x = y$
3. **Symmetry:** $d(x, y) = d(y, x)$
4. **Triangle inequality:** $d(x, z) \leq d(x, y) + d(y, z)$

### A. Minkowski Family (Lₚ Norms) — The General Form

For two vectors $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_d)$ and $y = (y_1, \ldots, y_d)$, the **Minkowski distance of order $p$** is:

$$
d_p(x, y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}
$$

- $p = 1 \Rightarrow$ **Manhattan (City-Block / L₁) distance**
- $p = 2 \Rightarrow$ **Euclidean (L₂) distance** — the default choice in K-Means
- $p \to \infty \Rightarrow$ **Chebyshev (L∞) distance** = $\max_i \vert x_i - y_i \vert$

### B. Euclidean Distance (L₂) — The Workhorse

$$
d_E(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - y_i)^2}
$$

- Geometric interpretation: the straight-line distance between two points in $\mathbb{R}^d$.
- **Sensitivity:** dominated by dimensions with the largest scale. **Therefore, standardisation (z-score) is mandatory before clustering when features have different units.**

### C. Manhattan Distance (L₁)

$$
d_{Man}(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert
$$

- Travel distance on a grid; less sensitive to outliers than L₂.

### D. Cosine Similarity & Distance

For non-zero vectors, **cosine similarity** measures the angle between them:

$$
s_{\cos}(x, y) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \, \|y\|} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i^2} \, \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} y_i^2}}
$$

The corresponding **cosine distance** is $d_{\cos} = 1 - s_{\cos}$, with range $[0, 2]$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why cosine for text?** Two documents of different lengths but identical topic distribution have **cosine similarity = 1** but **Euclidean distance $\gg 0$**. Cosine ignores magnitude, captures orientation — perfect for TF-IDF vectors.

### E. Jaccard Similarity & Distance (Binary / Set Data)

For two sets $A$ and $B$:

$$
s_J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}, \qquad d_J(A, B) = 1 - s_J(A, B)
$$

> **Engineering Utility:** Used in market-basket analysis, plagiarism detection, and binary attribute clustering.

---

## 2.2 Hierarchical Clustering — The Theory

Hierarchical clustering produces a **nested sequence of partitions** represented as a tree called a **dendrogram**. There are two strategies:

- **Agglomerative (bottom-up):** Start with $N$ singleton clusters. At each step, merge the two *closest* clusters. Stop when one cluster remains or a stopping criterion is met.
- **Divisive (top-down):** Start with one cluster containing all points. Recursively split. (Rarely used in practice; $O(2^N)$ complexity.)

### Agglomerative Algorithm (AGNES) — Step Logic

1. Initialize: each point $x_i$ is its own cluster $C_i = \{x_i\}$. Total clusters = $N$.
2. Compute the $N \times N$ **proximity matrix** $D^{(0)} = [d(x_i, x_j)]$.
3. **Repeat** until $K$ clusters remain (or one cluster):
   a. Find the pair $(C_a, C_b)$ with the **smallest** inter-cluster distance.
   b. Merge them: $C_{ab} = C_a \cup C_b$.
   c. Update the proximity matrix by computing the distance from $C_{ab}$ to every other cluster $C_k$ using a **linkage rule**.
4. Output the dendrogram and (optionally) cut it at a chosen height to obtain a flat clustering.

### Linkage Rules (How to Measure Cluster-to-Cluster Distance)

Let $d(x, y)$ be the point-wise distance. For clusters $C_a$ and $C_b$:

| Linkage | Formula | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Single (MIN)** | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Tends to produce **chaining** — long, straggly clusters. Sensitive to noise. |
| **Complete (MAX)** | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Produces **compact, equal-diameter** clusters. Tolerant, less chaining. |
| **Average (UPGMA)** | $d(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Compromise between single and complete. Most commonly used. |
| **Ward (Minimum Variance)** | Merges the pair that causes the **smallest increase in total within-cluster SSE** | Excellent for spherical, equal-size clusters. Default in many packages. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Complexity of Agglomerative Clustering**
> - Naïve: $O(N^3)$ (recomputing the full matrix each step).
> - With priority queue: $O(N^2 \log N)$.
> - **Space:** $O(N^2)$ to store the proximity matrix.
> - **Implication:** Agglomerative is **not scalable** to $N > 10{,}000$. For big data, K-Means or BIRCH is preferred.

---

## 2.3 Partitional Clustering & K-Means — The Theory

Partitional clustering directly produces a **single flat partition** of $K$ disjoint clusters. The canonical algorithm is **K-Means** (Lloyd, 1957).

### K-Means Objective Function

Given data $\{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$ and a chosen $K$, K-Means minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** — also called the *inertia* or *distortion*:

$$
J(\{C_1, \ldots, C_K\}, \{\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_K\}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|_2^2
$$

where $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ is the **centroid** (mean vector) of cluster $C_k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the centroid is the mean (intuition):** For a fixed cluster assignment, the WCSS is minimized with respect to $\mu_k$ by setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} = 0$, which yields the arithmetic mean. K-Means alternates between (a) fixing centroids and assigning points, and (b) fixing assignments and recomputing centroids — this is **coordinate descent** on $J$.

---

## 2.4 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minkowski distance | $d_p(x,y) = \left( \sum_{i} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | $p=1$ Manh., $p=2$ Eucl. |
| 2 | Euclidean | $d_E = \sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Default in K-Means |
| 3 | Manhattan | $d_{Man} = \sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 4 | Cosine similarity | $s = \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \, \|y\|}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$; used for text |
| 5 | Cosine distance | $1 - s_{\cos}$ | Used by `sklearn` for text clustering |
| 6 | Jaccard | $s_J = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | For binary/set data |
| 7 | Single linkage | $\min d(x,y)$ | Chaining tendency |
| 8 | Complete linkage | $\max d(x,y)$ | Compact clusters |
| 9 | Average linkage | mean of all pairs | Compromise |
| 10 | K-Means objective | $J = \sum_k \sum_{x \in C_k} \|x - \mu_k\|^2$ | Minimized iteratively |
| 11 | Centroid update | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x \in C_k} x$ | Arithmetic mean of cluster |
| 12 | Agglomerative complexity | $O(N^2 \log N)$ time, $O(N^2)$ space | Not scalable |
| 13 | K-Means complexity | $O(N \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ per iter. | $I$ = # iterations |
| 14 | Elbow rule | Plot WCSS vs $K$; pick the "elbow" | Heuristic for choosing $K$ |
| 15 | Silhouette score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$; higher is better |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Customer Segmentation** in CRM: K-Means on RFM (Recency-Frequency-Monetary) features.
- **Image Compression / Vector Quantization:** K-Means on pixel colours reduces 16.7M colours to $K$ representative colours.
- **Document / News Group Organization:** Cosine + K-Means or Agglomerative on TF-IDF vectors.
- **Anomaly Detection:** K-Means residuals; points far from any centroid are anomalies.
- **Bioinformatics:** Hierarchical clustering of gene-expression data → heatmaps; Ward linkage is standard.
- **Telecom / Network Engineering:** Clustering cell-tower traffic for capacity planning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Centroid is the Arithmetic Mean

We fix the cluster assignment $C_k$ and minimize $J$ with respect to $\mu_k$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k)^\top (x_i - \mu_k) = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0 \implies \vert C_k \vert \, \mu_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i \implies \mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i
$$

This is exactly the sample mean. **Hence, the K-Means centroid is provably the within-cluster mean.**

---

## 3.2 Lloyd's K-Means Algorithm — Exhaustive Step List

**Input:** Data $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$, number of clusters $K$, distance metric $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ (default Euclidean), max iterations $T$, tolerance $\epsilon$.

**Output:** Centroids $\{\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_K\}$ and assignment $z_i \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$ for each $x_i$.

1. **Initialize** centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$.
   - **Forgy:** randomly pick $K$ data points.
   - **Random Partition:** randomly assign each point to a cluster, then take the mean.
   - **K-Means++:** probabilistic seeding that spreads initial centroids apart — gives $O(\log K)$-competitive solution.
2. **Repeat for $t = 0, 1, \ldots, T$ :**
   a. **Assignment step:** For each $x_i$, assign to the nearest centroid:
   $$z_i^{(t+1)} = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}} \| x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \|_2^2$$
   b. **Update step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points assigned to it:
   $$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t+1)} \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k^{(t+1)}} x_i$$
   c. **Convergence check:** If $\sum_k \| \mu_k^{(t+1)} - \mu_k^{(t)} \|_2^2 < \epsilon$, break.
3. **Return** the final centroids and assignments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Guarantee (Board Favourite)**
> K-Means always converges in a **finite number of steps** because:
> - $J$ is bounded below by $0$.
> - Both the assignment and update steps **never increase** $J$.
> - The number of possible partitions is finite ($\le K^N$).
> However, K-Means converges to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum — hence the importance of multiple restarts and K-Means++ initialization.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means on a 2-D Toy Dataset

**Dataset (5 points, $K=2$):**
$$
A = (2, 10), \quad B = (2, 5), \quad C = (8, 4), \quad D = (5, 8), \quad E = (7, 5)
$$

**Initial centroids (Forgy):** $\mu_1^{(0)} = A = (2, 10)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = B = (2, 5)$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment Step.** Compute squared Euclidean distances:

| Point | $\|x - \mu_1\|^2$ | $\|x - \mu_2\|^2$ | Assigned to |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A=(2,10)$ | $0$ | $25$ | $\mu_1$ |
| $B=(2,5)$ | $25$ | $0$ | $\mu_2$ |
| $C=(8,4)$ | $36+16=52$ | $36+1=37$ | $\mu_2$ |
| $D=(5,8)$ | $9+4=13$ | $9+9=18$ | $\mu_1$ |
| $E=(7,5)$ | $25+25=50$ | $25+0=25$ | $\mu_2$ |

- Cluster 1 = $\{A, D\}$; Cluster 2 = $\{B, C, E\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Update Step.**

$$
\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{A + D}{2} = \left( \frac{2+5}{2}, \frac{10+8}{2} \right) = (3.5, 9)
$$

$$
\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{B + C + E}{3} = \left( \frac{2+8+7}{3}, \frac{5+4+5}{3} \right) = \left(\frac{17}{3}, \frac{14}{3}\right) \approx (5.67, 4.67)
$$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment Step.**

| Point | $\|x - \mu_1^{(1)}\|^2$ | $\|x - \mu_2^{(1)}\|^2$ | Assigned to |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A=(2,10)$ | $2.25+1=3.25$ | $(2-5.67)^2+(10-4.67)^2 \approx 13.47+28.41=41.88$ | $\mu_1$ |
| $B=(2,5)$ | $2.25+16=18.25$ | $13.47+0.11=13.58$ | $\mu_1$ (changed!) |
| $C=(8,4)$ | $20.25+25=45.25$ | $5.43+0.45=5.88$ | $\mu_2$ |
| $D=(5,8)$ | $2.25+1=3.25$ | $0.45+11.09=11.54$ | $\mu_1$ |
| $E=(7,5)$ | $12.25+16=28.25$ | $1.77+0.11=1.88$ | $\mu_2$ |

- New Cluster 1 = $\{A, B, D\}$; Cluster 2 = $\{C, E\}$.

**Iteration 2 — Update Step.**

$$
\mu_1^{(2)} = \frac{A + B + D}{3} = \left( \frac{2+2+5}{3}, \frac{10+5+8}{3} \right) = (3, 7.67)
$$

$$
\mu_2^{(2)} = \frac{C + E}{2} = \left( \frac{8+7}{2}, \frac{4+5}{2} \right) = (7.5, 4.5)
$$

**Iteration 3 — Assignment Step.** Point $B=(2,5)$:

$$
\|B - \mu_1^{(2)}\|^2 = (2-3)^2 + (5-7.67)^2 = 1 + 7.13 = 8.13
$$

$$
\|B - \mu_2^{(2)}\|^2 = (2-7.5)^2 + (5-4.5)^2 = 30.25 + 0.25 = 30.50
$$

$B$ stays with $\mu_1$. All assignments remain unchanged from Iteration 2. **Converged.**

**Final WCSS (verify):**
$$
J = (8.13 + 3.25 + 3.25) + (5.88 + 1.88) \approx 22.39
$$

(where the first three are A, B, D's residuals; the latter two are C, E's residuals.)

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Agglomerative Clustering with Single Linkage

**Dataset (4 points in 2D):**
$$
P_1 = (1, 1), \quad P_2 = (1, 2), \quad P_3 = (5, 4), \quad P_4 = (6, 5)
$$

**Step 1: Pairwise Euclidean distance matrix** $D^{(0)}$:

|   | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $P_3$ | $P_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | $0$ | $1.00$ | $5.00$ | $6.40$ |
| $P_2$ | $1.00$ | $0$ | $4.47$ | $5.83$ |
| $P_3$ | $5.00$ | $4.47$ | $0$ | $1.41$ |
| $P_4$ | $6.40$ | $5.83$ | $1.41$ | $0$ |

(Specifically: $d(P_1,P_3) = \sqrt{16+9}=5$, $d(P_1,P_4) = \sqrt{25+16}=\sqrt{41}\approx 6.40$, $d(P_2,P_3) = \sqrt{16+4}=\sqrt{20}\approx 4.47$, $d(P_2,P_4) = \sqrt{25+9}=\sqrt{34}\approx 5.83$, $d(P_3,P_4) = \sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2}\approx 1.41$.)

**Step 2: First merge.** Minimum off-diagonal = $1.00$ (between $P_1$ and $P_2$). **Merge $P_1$ and $P_2 \Rightarrow C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}$.**

**Step 3: Update matrix using SINGLE linkage** $d(C_1, C_3) = \min(d(P_1,P_3), d(P_2,P_3)) = \min(5.00, 4.47) = 4.47$; $d(C_1, C_4) = \min(6.40, 5.83) = 5.83$.

|   | $C_1$ | $P_3$ | $P_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $0$ | $4.47$ | $5.83$ |
| $P_3$ | $4.47$ | $0$ | $1.41$ |
| $P_4$ | $5.83$ | $1.41$ | $0$ |

**Step 4: Second merge.** Minimum = $1.41$ (between $P_3$ and $P_4$). **Merge $P_3$ and $P_4 \Rightarrow C_2 = \{P_3, P_4\}$.**

**Step 5: Third merge.** Only $C_1$ and $C_2$ remain. $d(C_1, C_2) = 4.47$ (or whichever pair — both methods give the same value here).

**Dendrogram structure (heights at which merges occur):**

- $P_1, P_2$ merged at height $1.00$
- $P_3, P_4$ merged at height $1.41$
- $\{P_1, P_2\}$ and $\{P_3, P_4\}$ merged at height $4.47$

If we cut the dendrogram at height $3.0$, we obtain **two clusters**: $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}$ and $C_2 = \{P_3, P_4\}$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU Board Perspective

| Aspect | K-Means (Partitional) | Agglomerative (Hierarchical) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of clusters $K$ | **Must be specified a priori** | Determined by cutting the dendrogram |
| Cluster shape | **Spherical, equal variance** (Euclidean) | Flexible; depends on linkage |
| Scalability | $O(N K I d)$ — very scalable | $O(N^2 \log N)$ or worse — not scalable |
| Sensitivity to initialization | High (use **K-Means++**) | Deterministic (no init issue) |
| Sensitivity to outliers | High (centroid is dragged) | Lower (especially with complete linkage) |
| Result reproducibility | Depends on random init | Fully reproducible |
| Output | Flat partition | Dendrogram (full merge history) |
| Best use case | Large $N$, known $K$, spherical clusters | Small $N$, unknown $K$, need for hierarchy |

---

## 3.6 Choosing the Number of Clusters $K$ (Board Favourite)

Two well-known heuristics:

### A. Elbow Method
Plot $J(K)$ (WCSS) vs $K$. The "elbow" — the point where adding more clusters yields diminishing reduction in WCSS — is the chosen $K$.

### B. Silhouette Score

For a single point $x_i$ with cluster $C_a$:
- $a(i)$ = mean distance from $x_i$ to all other points in $C_a$ (cohesion).
- $b(i)$ = min over all other clusters $C_b$ of the mean distance from $x_i$ to points in $C_b$ (separation).

$$
s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{ a(i), \, b(i) \}}
$$

Range is $[-1, 1]$. Higher is better. Pick $K$ that maximizes the **average silhouette** over all points.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.2 K-Means Algorithm Flow (Lloyd's Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Input Data X and K] --> S2[Step 2 Initialize K Centroids mu1 to muK]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Assignment Step<br/>Assign each xi to nearest centroid]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Update Step<br/>Recompute mu k as mean of cluster Ck]
    S4 --> S5{Step 5 Converged<br/>or Max Iterations}
    S5 -->|No| S3
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Step 6 Output Final Clusters and Centroids]
```

---

## 4.3 Agglomerative Clustering Bottom Up Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start N Singleton Clusters] --> A1[Compute N by N Distance Matrix]
    A1 --> A2{Find Pair of Clusters<br/>with Minimum Distance}
    A2 --> A3[Merge the Two Closest Clusters]
    A3 --> A4[Update Distance Matrix<br/>using Linkage Rule]
    A4 --> A5{Stopping Criterion<br/>K Clusters or 1 Cluster}
    A5 -->|Not Met| A2
    A5 -->|Met| A6[Cut Dendrogram at Chosen Height<br/>to get Flat Clusters]
```

    end

        AV1[Mean of all pairwise distances] --> AVM[Compromise between single and complete]
    end

    end
```

    Q2 -->|Small N below 10k| Q5{Need hierarchy?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Use Agglomerative Ward]
    Q5 -->|No| Q7[Use K Means]
    Q3 --> Q6
    Q4 --> END1[Run K Means and evaluate]
    Q6 --> END2[Cut dendrogram at chosen K]
    Q7 --> END1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Style — Module 4 — Short Definitions**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
Define **clustering** in machine learning. Differentiate between **supervised** and **unsupervised** learning with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Clustering** is an unsupervised learning technique that groups similar data points into clusters based on intrinsic similarity, such that intra-cluster similarity is high and inter-cluster similarity is low. *(1 mark)*

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| Labels | Yes $(x_i, y_i)$ | No, only $x_i$ |
| Goal | Learn $f: X \to Y$ | Discover hidden structure |
| Example | Email spam classification | Customer segmentation via K-Means |

*(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, S3 Model]**
List any **three distance metrics** used in clustering. State the **Minkowski distance** formula and identify which value of $p$ gives **Euclidean** and **Manhattan** distances.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three distance metrics are: (1) **Euclidean distance** $\sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$, (2) **Manhattan distance** $\sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert$, (3) **Minkowski distance** $\left( \sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$. *(1 mark)*

**Minkowski formula:** $d_p(x,y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ *(1 mark)*

- $p = 2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean
- $p = 1 \Rightarrow$ Manhattan *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers **one** of the two alternatives.

---

### ✅ Question A — 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** in detail. State its objective function, outline the steps of **Lloyd's algorithm**, and discuss its **convergence properties** and **limitations**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following 2-D data points. Apply **K-Means clustering with $K = 2$**, taking $P_1$ and $P_3$ as initial centroids. Show **two complete iterations** and report the **final cluster assignments** and **centroids**. *(7 marks)*

**Data points:**
$$
P_1 = (2, 2), \quad P_2 = (3, 4), \quad P_3 = (5, 6), \quad P_4 = (6, 3), \quad P_5 = (8, 5)
$$

---

#### Model Solution (Question A)

**(a) K-Means Algorithm — Detailed Explanation** *(7 marks)*

**1. Objective Function** *(1.5 marks)*
K-Means partitions $N$ data points into $K$ disjoint clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**:
$$
J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|_2^2
$$
where $\mu_k$ is the centroid of cluster $C_k$.

**2. Lloyd's Algorithm Steps** *(3 marks)*
1. **Initialize** $K$ centroids (Forgy / Random Partition / K-Means++).
2. **Assignment step:** for each $x_i$, set $z_i = \arg\min_{k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$.
3. **Update step:** recompute $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$.
4. **Repeat** 2–3 until convergence (centroids no longer move or $J$ stops decreasing).

**3. Convergence** *(1 mark)*
- $J$ is monotonically non-increasing across iterations; bounded below by $0$.
- Hence K-Means converges in a **finite** number of steps to a **local minimum** of $J$ (not necessarily the global minimum).

**4. Limitations** *(1.5 marks)*
- Requires $K$ to be specified a priori.
- Sensitive to initial centroid placement (hence K-Means++).
- Assumes spherical, equal-variance clusters; fails for non-convex shapes.
- Sensitive to outliers (centroid dragged); sensitive to feature scaling.
- Local optima issue.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

**Initial centroids:** $\mu_1^{(0)} = P_1 = (2, 2)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = P_3 = (5, 6)$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment.** Compute squared Euclidean distances:

| Point | $\|x - \mu_1\|^2$ | $\|x - \mu_2\|^2$ | Assignment |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1=(2,2)$ | $0$ | $9+16=25$ | $\mu_1$ |
| $P_2=(3,4)$ | $1+4=5$ | $4+4=8$ | $\mu_1$ |
| $P_3=(5,6)$ | $9+16=25$ | $0$ | $\mu_2$ |
| $P_4=(6,3)$ | $16+1=17$ | $1+9=10$ | $\mu_2$ |
| $P_5=(8,5)$ | $36+9=45$ | $9+1=10$ | $\mu_2$ |

- $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}$, $C_2 = \{P_3, P_4, P_5\}$. *(2 marks)*

**Iteration 1 — Update.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2} = \left( \frac{2+3}{2}, \frac{2+4}{2} \right) = (2.5, 3)
$$

$$
\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{P_3 + P_4 + P_5}{3} = \left( \frac{5+6+8}{3}, \frac{6+3+5}{3} \right) = \left(\frac{19}{3}, \frac{14}{3}\right) \approx (6.33, 4.67)
$$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment.**

| Point | $\|x - \mu_1^{(1)}\|^2$ | $\|x - \mu_2^{(1)}\|^2$ | Assignment |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1=(2,2)$ | $0.25+1=1.25$ | $(2-6.33)^2+(2-4.67)^2 \approx 18.75+7.13=25.88$ | $\mu_1$ |
| $P_2=(3,4)$ | $0.25+1=1.25$ | $(3-6.33)^2+(4-4.67)^2 \approx 11.09+0.45=11.54$ | $\mu_1$ |
| $P_3=(5,6)$ | $6.25+9=15.25$ | $(5-6.33)^2+(6-4.67)^2 \approx 1.77+1.77=3.54$ | $\mu_2$ |
| $P_4=(6,3)$ | $12.25+0=12.25$ | $(6-6.33)^2+(3-4.67)^2 \approx 0.11+2.79=2.90$ | $\mu_2$ |
| $P_5=(8,5)$ | $30.25+4=34.25$ | $(8-6.33)^2+(5-4.67)^2 \approx 2.79+0.11=2.90$ | $\mu_2$ |

- **No change in assignments.** Algorithm has converged. *(1.5 marks)*

**Final Result:**

- $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2\}$ with centroid $\mu_1^* = (2.5, 3)$
- $C_2 = \{P_3, P_4, P_5\}$ with centroid $\mu_2^* \approx (6.33, 4.67)$ *(2 marks)*

**WCSS at convergence (verification):**
$$
J = 1.25 + 1.25 + 3.54 + 2.90 + 2.90 = 11.84
$$

---

### ✅ Question B — 14 Marks (CHOICE 2)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **hierarchical clustering**. With a neat sketch of a **dendrogram**, describe **agglomerative clustering** and discuss the various **linkage methods** (single, complete, average) with their properties. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the following **5 data points** in 1-D, perform **agglomerative clustering using single linkage**. Draw the resulting **dendrogram** and identify clusters if the cut is made at height $h = 3$. *(7 marks)*

**Data:** $x_1 = 1, \; x_2 = 2, \; x_3 = 6, \; x_4 = 8, \; x_5 = 9$.

---

#### Model Solution (Question B)

**(a) Hierarchical Clustering** *(7 marks)*

**Definition** *(1 mark)*: Hierarchical clustering builds a **nested tree of clusters** (a dendrogram) without requiring $K$ to be specified upfront. Two strategies exist:
- **Agglomerative** (bottom-up): start with $N$ singletons, merge closest pairs.
- **Divisive** (top-down): start with one cluster, recursively split.

**Agglomerative Algorithm** *(1.5 marks)*:
1. Begin with $N$ singleton clusters.
2. Compute the $N \times N$ pairwise distance matrix.
3. Repeat: merge the **two closest clusters**, update the distance matrix using a **linkage rule**.
4. Stop when one cluster remains or desired $K$ is reached.

**Linkage Methods** *(3.5 marks)*:

| Linkage | Definition | Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Single (MIN)** | $d_{\min}(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Tends to **chain**; elongated clusters; sensitive to noise |
| **Complete (MAX)** | $d_{\max}(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Produces **compact** clusters; outlier-resistant |
| **Average (UPGMA)** | $d_{avg}(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert} \sum d(x,y)$ | **Compromise** between single & complete; widely used |

**Dendrogram Sketch** *(1 mark)*: A tree where each merge is drawn as a horizontal line whose vertical position equals the inter-cluster distance at which the merge occurred. Cutting horizontally at any height yields a flat partition.

---

**(b) Agglomerative Clustering with Single Linkage** *(7 marks)*

**Data:** $1, 2, 6, 8, 9$ (1-D, so Euclidean distance is just absolute difference).

**Step 1: Pairwise Distance Matrix** *(1.5 marks)*

|   | 1 | 2 | 6 | 8 | 9 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 5 | 7 | 8 |
| 2 |   | 0 | 4 | 6 | 7 |
| 6 |   |   | 0 | 2 | 3 |
| 8 |   |   |   | 0 | 1 |
| 9 |   |   |   |   | 0 |

**Step 2: Iterative Merging** *(3 marks)*

| Iteration | Smallest distance | Pair merged | New cluster | Updated distances (single linkage) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d=1$ | $\{1, 2\}$ | $C_{12} = \{1, 2\}$ | $d(C_{12}, 6) = \min(5,4) = 4$; $d(C_{12}, 8) = \min(7,6)=6$; $d(C_{12}, 9)=\min(8,7)=7$ |
| 2 | $d=1$ | $\{8, 9\}$ | $C_{89} = \{8, 9\}$ | $d(C_{89}, 6) = \min(2,3) = 2$; $d(C_{89}, C_{12}) = \min(7,6,8,7)=6$ |
| 3 | $d=2$ | $\{6, C_{89}\}$ | $C_{689} = \{6, 8, 9\}$ | $d(C_{689}, C_{12}) = \min(4,5,6,7,7,8) = 4$ |
| 4 | $d=4$ | All merged | $C_{all}$ | Stop |

**Step 3: Dendrogram** *(1.5 marks)*

Merge heights: $1 \leftrightarrow 2$ at $h=1$; $8 \leftrightarrow 9$ at $h=1$; $\{8,9\}$ with $6$ at $h=2$; $\{6,8,9\}$ with $\{1,2\}$ at $h=4$.

```
        h=4
   ┌────────────┐
   │            │
   │       ┌────┴───┐
 h=2      │        │
   │   ┌──┴──┐     │
   │   │     │     │
 h=1  6    8   9    1   2
```

**Step 4: Cutting at $h=3$** *(1 mark)*

The horizontal cut at $h=3$ intersects the dendrogram between the merge at $h=2$ and the merge at $h=4$. Therefore we obtain **two clusters:**

- **Cluster 1:** $\{1, 2\}$
- **Cluster 2:** $\{6, 8, 9\}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Distance matrix typo:** Students often misread the absolute difference in 1-D as some squared value. Use $\vert x_i - x_j \vert$ directly.
> 2. **Single-linkage update:** After a merge, recompute by taking the **MINIMUM** of pairwise distances — students frequently use maximum (which is complete linkage).
> 3. **Skipping the dendrogram:** A numerical answer without the dendrogram sketch loses 1–1.5 marks in KTU ESE.
> 4. **Confusing agglomerative with K-Means:** Agglomerative is **deterministic** (no random init); K-Means is **not**.
> 5. **For K-Means numericals:** Forgetting to show the **centroid update formula** explicitly costs 1 mark. Always show $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum x_i$.
> 6. **K-Means convergence:** Many students write "converges to global minimum" — **wrong**, it converges to a **local** minimum. This is a 1-mark trap in Part A definitions.
> 7. **Choosing $K$:** Students forget that K-Means requires $K$ to be specified, but Agglomerative allows choosing $K$ *after* by cutting the dendrogram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [x] **Unsupervised learning** = learning from inputs only, no labels; clustering is the flagship task.
- [x] **Similarity measures** are the heart of clustering: Euclidean (L₂) is default for continuous data, **Cosine** for text/TF-IDF vectors, Jaccard for sets/binary data.
- [x] **Minkowski distance** generalises Euclidean ($p=2$) and Manhattan ($p=1$).
- [x] **K-Means** is **partitional**, requires $K$ upfront, minimises WCSS, uses **centroid = arithmetic mean**, and converges in finite steps to a **local** minimum.
- [x] **K-Means++** initialization is the practical standard to avoid poor local minima.
- [x] **K-Means complexity** $O(N K I d)$ — highly scalable; suitable for large datasets.
- [x] **K-Means limitations:** spherical-cluster assumption, sensitive to outliers and feature scaling, requires $K$ a priori.
- [x] **Hierarchical clustering** produces a **dendrogram**; cut at a chosen height to get $K$ flat clusters.
- [x] **Agglomerative** is **bottom-up**; **Divisive** is top-down. Agglomerative is the practical choice.
- [x] **Single linkage (MIN)** — chaining tendency; **Complete linkage (MAX)** — compact; **Average linkage** — balanced; **Ward's** — minimum WCSS increase.
- [x] Agglomerative complexity is $O(N^2 \log N)$ — **not scalable** to very large $N$.
- [x] **Elbow method** (plot WCSS vs $K$) and **Silhouette score** (range $[-1, 1]$) are the two standard heuristics to choose $K$.
- [x] For a KTU numerical, always show: (1) initial centroids, (2) distance table, (3) assignment, (4) centroid update formula, (5) convergence check.
- [x] For an agglomerative numerical, always: (1) compute initial distance matrix, (2) show every merge + height, (3) draw the dendrogram, (4) state the clusters at the chosen cut.
- [x] Remember: **Agglomerative is deterministic**; **K-Means is not** (multiple restarts recommended).
- [x] **Standardise features** (z-score) before K-Means to prevent high-magnitude features from dominating the Euclidean distance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis, Multidimensional Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis, Multidimensional Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Dimensionality Reduction?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *Dimensionality Reduction* is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a meaningful low-dimensional representation (typically 2D or 3D) that retains the intrinsic structure, variance, and statistical properties of the original data as much as possible.

In Machine Learning, when datasets have hundreds or thousands of features (e.g., gene expression data, image pixels), we face the **"Curse of Dimensionality"** — models become slow, overfit easily, and become hard to visualize. Dimensionality reduction solves this by either:
- **Feature Selection** (picking a subset of original features), or
- **Feature Extraction** (creating new, compact features) — *this is what PCA and MDS do.*

### 1.2 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PCA (Pearson, 1901):** A linear, orthogonal transformation that projects the original $n$-dimensional data onto a new coordinate system such that the greatest variance lies along the first principal component (PC1), the second greatest variance along PC2 (orthogonal to PC1), and so on.

Mathematically, PCA finds an orthonormal basis $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ such that the projected data $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}$ maximizes the retained variance, where $k \ll n$.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Shadow" Analogy):**
Imagine a 3D statue placed in front of a wall with a light source. The **shadow** cast on the wall is a 2D representation of the 3D object. By choosing the right angle of the light, we can capture the **maximum information** (the maximum variance/spread) in the shadow. That optimal "light angle" is exactly what PCA computes — it finds the **best 2D plane** to "flatten" high-dimensional data while losing the least amount of information.

### 1.3 Multidimensional Scaling (MDS) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **MDS (Torgerson, 1952):** A non-linear (in the geometric sense) dimensionality reduction technique that finds a low-dimensional embedding of data points such that the **pairwise distances (or dissimilarities)** between points in the low-dimensional space match as closely as possible to the distances in the original high-dimensional space.

Unlike PCA, which preserves **variance/covariance structure**, MDS preserves **inter-point distances**.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Map Maker" Analogy):**
Suppose you have the **air-distance** between every pair of 10 cities (a 10×10 distance matrix). MDS is the "map maker" who, given only this matrix, can reconstruct a 2D map placing the cities in the correct relative positions — even though no GPS coordinates were given! It works purely from "how far apart" things are.

### 1.4 Key Comparison: PCA vs MDS

| Property | PCA | MDS |
|----------|-----|-----|
| **Preserves** | Maximum variance (global structure) | Pairwise distances (local + global) |
| **Input** | Raw feature matrix $\mathbf{X}$ | Distance/Dissimilarity matrix $\mathbf{D}$ |
| **Mathematics** | Eigendecomposition of covariance matrix | Eigendecomposition of double-centered $\mathbf{B}$ matrix |
| **Linearity** | Linear projection | Linear embedding (Classical MDS) / Non-linear (Non-metric MDS) |
| **Use case** | Feature compression, whitening, noise removal | Visualization, perceptual mapping, psychology, GIS |

### 1.5 Notation Conventions Used Throughout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times n}$ — Data matrix (N samples, n features)
> - $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — i-th sample row vector
> - $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — Mean vector
> - $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — Covariance matrix
> - $\mathbf{D} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$ — Distance (dissimilarity) matrix
> - $\mathbf{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$ — Double-centered inner-product matrix
> - $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ — Projection (loading) matrix
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PCA — The Conceptual "Why"

PCA solves a single core problem: **"Find a direction $\mathbf{w}$ (unit vector) along which the projected data has maximum variance."**

### Step 2.1.1 — Data Centering
Before anything, subtract the mean so that the data is centered at the origin:

$$\mathbf{x}_i^{\text{centered}} = \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}, \quad \text{where} \quad \boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i$$

### Step 2.1.2 — Variance Maximization Objective
The variance of the data projected onto direction $\mathbf{w}$ (with $\vert\vert \mathbf{w} \vert\vert = 1$) is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$$

We wish to **maximize** $J(\mathbf{w})$ subject to $\vert\vert \mathbf{w} \vert\vert^2 = 1$.

### Step 2.1.3 — Lagrangian Formulation
Using a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1)$$

Setting $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$ yields the celebrated **eigen-equation**:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is the **eigenvector** of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ corresponding to its **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda$.

### Step 2.1.4 — Geometric Intuition
- Eigenvectors of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ point along the **principal axes** of the data ellipsoid.
- Eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ measure the **variance** along each corresponding axis.
- The principal components are the projections $\mathbf{y}_i = \mathbf{W}^{\top}(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})$.

## 2.2 MDS — The Conceptual "Why"

MDS solves: **"Given only pairwise distances $d_{ij}$, find coordinates $\mathbf{y}_i \in \mathbb{R}^k$ such that $\vert\vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \vert\vert \approx d_{ij}$."**

### Step 2.2.1 — Two Flavors of MDS
- **Classical (Metric) MDS** — Assumes the dissimilarities are true Euclidean distances; recovers coordinates exactly via eigendecomposition of the **double-centered matrix** $\mathbf{B}$.
- **Non-metric MDS** — Only assumes the *ordering* of distances matters (monotonic relationship); minimizes a **stress function** via iterative optimization (e.g., SMACOF algorithm).

### Step 2.2.2 — Stress Function
For a candidate configuration $\mathbf{Y}$:

$$\text{Stress}(\mathbf{Y}) = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i<j} \left( \vert\vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \vert\vert - d_{ij} \right)^2}{\sum_{i<j} \vert\vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \vert\vert^2}}$$

The goal is to find $\mathbf{Y}$ that **minimizes** Stress.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board Exam Lifesaver)

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Mean vector | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{x}_i$ | Sample mean of features |
| 2 | Covariance matrix | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{N-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$ | $\mathbf{X}_c$ is mean-centered |
| 3 | PCA eigen-equation | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w}$ | Core PCA condition |
| 4 | Explained variance ratio | $\text{EVR}_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_j}$ | % variance captured by PC $i$ |
| 5 | Cumulative variance | $\text{Cumulative}(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_j}$ | Used to pick $k$ (often $\geq 0.95$) |
| 6 | Reconstruction error | $J_{\text{recon}} = \dfrac{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i \vert\vert^2}{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i \vert\vert^2}$ | Should be minimized |
| 7 | Squared Euclidean distance | $d_{ij}^2 = \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \vert\vert^2$ | Input to MDS |
| 8 | Centering matrix | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I} - \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^{\top}$ | Used for double-centering |
| 9 | Double-centered matrix | $\mathbf{B} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{D}^{(2)}\mathbf{H}$ | Core MDS matrix |
| 10 | MDS eigen-equation | $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{v}_i = \mu_i \mathbf{v}_i$ | Eigendecomposition of $\mathbf{B}$ |
| 11 | MDS embedding | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{V}_k \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$ | Final low-D coordinates |
| 12 | Kruskal Stress-1 | $\text{Stress}_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i<j}(\hat{d}_{ij} - d_{ij})^2}{\sum_{i<j} d_{ij}^2}}$ | Goodness of fit (MDS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **PCA** is heavily used in face recognition (Eigenfaces), image compression, genomics (microarray data), and as a **preprocessing step** to decorrelate features before regression/neural networks.
> - **MDS** is the workhorse in **psychometrics** (mapping customer preferences), **bioinformatics** (protein structure), **GIS/cartography**, and **market segmentation**.

## 2.4 Standardization — A Critical Pre-Step

If features have **different units/scales** (e.g., height in cm vs. salary in ₹), PCA will be dominated by the largest-scaled feature. **Standardize** each feature:

$$z_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$$

After standardization, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ becomes the **correlation matrix** $\mathbf{R}$.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Board Pitfall: Students often forget to standardize, which leads to nonsensical PCs dominated by scale. Always state: "We standardize when features are on different scales."

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of PCA (Lagrangian Method)

### Step 1: Formulate the Variance Objective
Let $\mathbf{X}_c \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times n}$ be the **mean-centered** data matrix (each column has zero mean). The sample covariance matrix is:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{N-1} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$$

For a unit projection vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (with $\vert\vert \mathbf{w} \vert\vert = 1$), the projected scalar values are $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^N$. The variance of these projections is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{N-1} \mathbf{z}^{\top} \mathbf{z} = \frac{1}{N-1} \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$$

### Step 2: Constrained Optimization
We want to **maximize** $J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ subject to the constraint $g(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1 = 0$.

The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1)$$

### Step 3: Take the Gradient
Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0$$

This simplifies to:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal direction $\mathbf{w}$ is an **eigenvector** of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.

### Step 4: Determine Which Eigenvector
Pre-multiply both sides by $\mathbf{w}^{\top}$:

$$\mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} = \lambda$$

Since we want to maximize $J(\mathbf{w}) = \lambda$, we must choose the **eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue**.

### Step 5: Generalization to $k$ Components
The top-$k$ eigenvectors $\mathbf{w}_1, \mathbf{w}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{w}_k$ (sorted by descending eigenvalues) form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$. The reduced data is:

$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times k}$$

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation of Classical MDS

### Step 1: Start with Squared Distances
The squared Euclidean distance between samples $i$ and $j$:

$$d_{ij}^{2} = \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \vert\vert^{2} = \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + \mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j - 2\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$$

Let $b_{ij} = \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$ (the inner-product matrix $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^{\top}$). Then:

$$d_{ij}^{2} = b_{ii} + b_{jj} - 2b_{ij}$$

### Step 2: Double-Center the Distance Matrix
Compute row, column, and grand means of $\mathbf{D}^{(2)} = [d_{ij}^{2}]$:

$$d_{i\cdot}^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} d_{ij}^{2}, \quad d_{\cdot j}^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_{ij}^{2}, \quad d_{\cdot\cdot}^{2} = \frac{1}{N^{2}}\sum_{i,j} d_{ij}^{2}$$

The double-centered matrix is:

$$b_{ij} = -\frac{1}{2}\left(d_{ij}^{2} - d_{i\cdot}^{2} - d_{\cdot j}^{2} + d_{\cdot\cdot}^{2}\right)$$

**Equivalent matrix form** using the centering matrix $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I} - \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^{\top}$:

$$\mathbf{B} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{D}^{(2)}\mathbf{H}$$

### Step 3: Eigendecomposition
Since $\mathbf{B}$ is symmetric, it can be decomposed as:

$$\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{V}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_N)$ contains the eigenvalues (sorted descending), and $\mathbf{V}$ contains the eigenvectors.

### Step 4: Extract the Embedding
To keep $k$ dimensions, take the top-$k$ eigenvalues and eigenvectors:

$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{V}_k \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_k^{1/2} = \text{diag}(\sqrt{\mu_1}, \sqrt{\mu_2}, \ldots, \sqrt{\mu_k})$. The columns of $\mathbf{Y}$ are the $k$-dimensional coordinates of each sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** If the original distances were *exactly* Euclidean in some $k$-dimensional space and $N > k$, then only the top $k$ eigenvalues will be positive; the rest will be zero (or negligible). This also tells us the **intrinsic dimensionality** of the data.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Given:** Data matrix $\mathbf{X}$ with $N = 4$ samples in 2D, and we want to reduce to $k = 1$:

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Sub-step 3.3.1 — Compute Mean
$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left[2.5, \; 3.5\right]$$

### Sub-step 3.3.2 — Center the Data
$$\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -1.5 \\ -0.5 & -0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 1.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Sub-step 3.3.3 — Compute Covariance Matrix
$$\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 5 \\ 5 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 5 \\ 5 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.667 & 1.667 \\ 1.667 & 1.667 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Sub-step 3.3.4 — Solve Eigen-equation
$\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$(1.667-\lambda)^2 - 1.667^2 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 - 3.333\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda(\lambda - 3.333) = 0$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 3.333, \; \lambda_2 = 0$.

### Sub-step 3.3.5 — Eigenvectors
For $\lambda_1 = 3.333$: $(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} - 3.333 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{w} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 1\end{bmatrix}$.

For $\lambda_2 = 0$: $\mathbf{w}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix}1 \\ -1\end{bmatrix}$.

### Sub-step 3.3.6 — Project to 1D
$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -1.5 \\ -0.5 & -0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 1.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 \\ 0.707 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.121 \\ -0.707 \\ 0.707 \\ 2.121 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Variance retained:** $\dfrac{3.333}{3.333 + 0} = 100\%$. Perfect reduction — the data is already 1-dimensional (a perfect line).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: PCA Algorithm Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Data Matrix X] --> B[Step 1: Compute Mean Vector mu]
    B --> C[Step 2: Center the Data Xc = X - mu]
    C --> D{Features on Different Scales?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Step 2a: Standardize Xc]
    D -- No --> F[Step 3: Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step 4: Eigendecomposition Sigma W = lambda W]
    G --> H[Step 5: Sort Eigenvalues in Descending Order]
    H --> I[Step 6: Select Top k Eigenvectors forming Wk]
    I --> J[Step 7: Project Data Y = Xc times Wk]
    J --> K[Output: Reduced Data Y in R^k]
    K --> L[Compute Explained Variance Ratio for Validation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Classical MDS Algorithm Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Distance Matrix D] --> B[Step 1: Compute Squared Distance Matrix D2]
    B --> C[Step 2: Apply Double Centering H = I - 1/N times 11 transpose]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute B = -1/2 times H times D2 times H]
    D --> E[Step 4: Eigendecomposition B = V Lambda V transpose]
    E --> F[Step 5: Sort Eigenvalues Descending]
    F --> G{Number of Positive Eigenvalues?}
    G -- k found --> H[Step 6: Take Top k Eigenvectors Vk and Eigenvalues Lambdak]
    G -- All positive --> H
    H --> I[Step 7: Compute Embedding Y = Vk times Lambdak raised to 1/2]
    I --> J[Output: Low-D Coordinate Matrix Y in R^k]
    J --> K[Optional: Compute Kruskal Stress for Goodness of Fit]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: PCA vs MDS — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [Input Layer]
    A1[High-D Feature Matrix X]:::input
    A2[Pairwise Distance Matrix D]:::input
    end

    subgraph TRANSFORM [Transformation Engine]
    B1[Covariance Sigma]:::pca
    B2[Double-Centered B Matrix]:::mds
    C1[Eigendecomposition of Sigma]:::pca
    C2[Eigendecomposition of B]:::mds
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT [Output Embedding]
    D1[Principal Components Y]:::pca
    D2[Distance-Preserving Coords Y]:::mds
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> D1
    A2 --> B2 --> C2 --> D2

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pca fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mds fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px
```
