# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Principal Component Analysis (PCA). What is the role of eigenvectors and eigenvalues in PCA?**

**Model Answer:**
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a linear dimensionality reduction technique that transforms correlated high-dimensional variables into a smaller set of uncorrelated variables called **principal components**, ordered by the amount of variance they capture.

**Role of eigenvalues/eigenvectors:**
- The covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is decomposed as $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$.
- **Eigenvectors** $\mathbf{w}_i$ define the **directions** (axes) of the new principal component space — they are the *loadings*.
- **Eigenvalues** $\lambda_i$ measure the **variance** of the data along their corresponding eigenvectors. Larger $\lambda_i$ = more important principal component.
- The $i$-th principal component explains a fraction $\lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$ of the total variance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is Multidimensional Scaling (MDS)? Differentiate between classical (metric) MDS and non-metric MDS.**

**Model Answer:**
**MDS** is a dimensionality reduction technique that finds a low-dimensional representation of data such that the pairwise distances in the original high-dimensional space are preserved as much as possible in the low-dimensional embedding.

**Difference between classical and non-metric MDS:**

| Aspect | Classical (Metric) MDS | Non-Metric MDS |
|--------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Input** | Quantitative distances | Ranked/ordinal dissimilarities |
| **Preserves** | Actual distance values | Only the *order* (rank) of distances |
| **Method** | Closed-form eigendecomposition | Iterative optimization of Stress function |
| **Algorithm** | Torgerson / SMACOF | SMACOF with isotonic regression |
| **Use case** | Continuous metric data | Perceptual, preference data |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — PCA Focus** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the mathematical formulation of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) using the **Lagrangian method**. Clearly state the objective function, constraint, and final eigen-equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following 2D dataset with 5 points. Compute the principal components, project the data onto PC1, and find the percentage of variance explained. **(7 marks)**

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 3 \\ 5 & 4 \\ 6 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Objective:** Find unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes the variance of projections: 

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$$

**Constraint:** $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} = 1$.

**Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1)$$

**Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w}}$$

This shows that $\mathbf{w}$ must be an eigenvector of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. To maximize $J$, we pick the eigenvector with the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_{\max}$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Mean:** $\boldsymbol{\mu} = [4, 3]$.

**Step 2 — Centered data:**

$$\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -2 \\ -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Covariance:**

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 2.5 \\ 2.5 & 2.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Eigendecomposition:** Eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 5, \lambda_2 = 0$. Eigenvectors:

$$\mathbf{w}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{w}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Project onto PC1:**

$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -2.828 \\ -1.414 \\ 0 \\ 1.414 \\ 2.828 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Variance explained:**

$$\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{5}{5 + 0} = 100\%$$

**Conclusion:** All variance is captured by PC1; the data is intrinsically 1-dimensional.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — MDS Focus** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Classical Multidimensional Scaling algorithm** step by step. Clearly derive the double-centering transformation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the squared Euclidean distance matrix $\mathbf{D}^{(2)}$ for 4 points below, apply Classical MDS to obtain a 2D embedding. Show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

$$\mathbf{D}^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 & 9 & 8 \\ 4 & 0 & 1 & 4 \\ 9 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 8 & 4 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Classical MDS algorithm (Torgerson, 1952) reconstructs coordinates from a distance matrix.

**Step 1:** Given $\mathbf{D}^{(2)} = [d_{ij}^2]$, compute row, column, and grand means.

**Step 2:** Apply **double centering**:

$$b_{ij} = -\frac{1}{2}\left(d_{ij}^{2} - d_{i\cdot}^{2} - d_{\cdot j}^{2} + d_{\cdot\cdot}^{2}\right)$$

Equivalently, using the centering matrix $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I} - \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^{\top}$:

$$\mathbf{B} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{D}^{(2)}\mathbf{H}$$

**Step 3:** Eigendecompose: $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{V}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$.

**Step 4:** Extract top-$k$ eigenvalues/vectors. Embedding:

$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{V}_k \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$$

The number of strictly positive eigenvalues indicates the **intrinsic dimensionality** of the data.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Row means:** $[5.25, 2.25, 2.75, 3.25]$.

**Step 2 — Column means:** $[5.25, 2.25, 2.75, 3.25]$. (Note: symmetric matrix gives equal row/column means).

**Step 3 — Grand mean:** $d_{\cdot\cdot}^{2} = 3.375$.

**Step 4 — Compute $b_{ij}$ using the formula** (e.g., $b_{12} = -0.5 \cdot (4 - 5.25 - 2.25 + 3.375) = -0.5 \cdot (-0.125) = 0.0625$). Continuing for all entries:

$$\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.125 & 0.0625 & -1.6875 & -0.9375 \\ 0.0625 & 0.125 & 0.6875 & 0.125 \\ -1.6875 & 0.6875 & 1.125 & -0.4375 \\ -0.9375 & 0.125 & -0.4375 & 0.4375 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Eigendecomposition of $\mathbf{B}$** yields top eigenvalues (approximately): $\mu_1 \approx 3.25, \mu_2 \approx 1.10$, with $\mu_3, \mu_4 \approx 0$.

**Step 6 — 2D Embedding:** $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{V}_2 \cdot \text{diag}(\sqrt{3.25}, \sqrt{1.10}) \approx \mathbf{V}_2 \cdot \text{diag}(1.803, 1.049)$.

The resulting 4×2 matrix $\mathbf{Y}$ gives the 2D coordinates of the four points.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $-\frac{1}{2}$ factor** in the double-centering formula — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Not sorting eigenvalues** in descending order before picking top-$k$ — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing PCA with MDS inputs** — PCA takes raw data, MDS takes a distance matrix. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this conceptual error.
> 4. **Skipping the square root** $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$ in MDS — yields wrong embedding coordinates, costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in MDS (e.g., "we assume Euclidean distances") — loses 1 mark for lack of rigor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points (Memorize Before Exam):**

- **PCA = Variance maximizer**; **MDS = Distance preserver**.
- PCA's core equation: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w}$. **Always** standardize features on different scales.
- The **top-k eigenvectors** of the covariance matrix form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W}$.
- **Explained Variance Ratio** = $\lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$ — pick $k$ such that cumulative ratio $\geq 0.95$.
- MDS input is a **dissimilarity matrix** $\mathbf{D}$, NOT raw features.
- The **double-centering** step: $\mathbf{B} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{D}^{(2)}\mathbf{H}$ is the heart of Classical MDS.
- Number of **strictly positive eigenvalues** of $\mathbf{B}$ = intrinsic dimensionality of data.
- **MDS embedding formula:** $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{V}_k \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$ (note the **square root**).
- **Kruskal Stress-1** quantifies MDS goodness of fit: $< 0.05$ excellent, $< 0.10$ good, $< 0.20$ fair, $> 0.20$ poor.
- **PCA is linear** and assumes Gaussian-like distribution; **Non-metric MDS** handles non-linear perceptual data.
- PCA is **not rotation-invariant** w.r.t. raw features (but is w.r.t. centered data); MDS is **distance-invariant** w.r.t. translation/rotation of the original space.
- **Limitations:** PCA is sensitive to outliers; MDS can suffer from local minima (in non-metric form).
- **Real-world anchors:** Eigenfaces (PCA), customer-preference maps (MDS), gene expression analysis (both), recommender systems (MDS for similarity visualization).
- **Computational tip:** For large $N$, prefer using **SVD** on $\mathbf{X}_c$ directly: $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$ — the principal directions are the columns of $\mathbf{V}$ — avoids forming the covariance matrix (more numerically stable).
- **Examiner keywords to use in answers:** "orthogonal transformation," "eigendecomposition," "explained variance ratio," "intrinsic dimensionality," "double-centering," "stress minimization," "isotonic regression" (for non-metric MDS).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensemble Methods - Bagging, Boosting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ensemble Methods - Bagging, Boosting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ensemble Methods — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ensemble Learning** is a machine learning paradigm where multiple individual learners (called **base learners** or **weak learners**) are strategically combined to form a single, more powerful **strong learner** that achieves significantly better predictive performance than any constituent model acting alone.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, ensemble methods are positioned as a meta-learning strategy grounded in the **Condorcet Jury Theorem** and the **bias-variance decomposition** of expected prediction error. The central thesis is that a diversified committee of imperfect predictors can be aggregated to approximate the true decision boundary with arbitrarily low error, provided:

1. Each base learner has an error rate **better than random guessing** (i.e., $\varepsilon < 0.5$ for binary classification).
2. The base learners make **statistically independent** (or weakly correlated) errors.

The expected error of an ensemble of $B$ independent classifiers, each with error $\varepsilon$, obeys the binomial aggregation law:

$$P_{\text{ensemble}} = \sum_{k=\lceil B/2 \rceil}^{B} \binom{B}{k} \varepsilon^{k} (1-\varepsilon)^{B-k}$$

This probability decreases **exponentially** with $B$ when $\varepsilon < 0.5$.

---

## 1.2 Bagging — Bootstrap Aggregating

> [!NOTE]
> **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)** is a parallel ensemble technique introduced by *Leo Breiman (1996)* that trains $B$ base learners on $B$ different bootstrap samples drawn **with replacement** from the original training set, and combines their predictions via **majority voting** (classification) or **averaging** (regression).

**Key KTU terminology to remember verbatim:**
- **Bootstrap Sample** $D_b$ : a sample of size $N$ drawn uniformly with replacement from original dataset $D$ of size $N$.
- **Out-of-Bag (OOB) Samples** : the $\approx 36.8\%$ of original instances not selected in a given bootstrap.
- **Aggregation Rule** : $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \dfrac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b(x)$ (regression) or mode of votes (classification).

### Real-World Analogy — The "Medical Jury" 🩺

Imagine a hospital wants to diagnose a rare disease. Instead of relying on **one doctor**, they convene a panel of **50 doctors**, each given a **slightly different set of patient history cards** (bootstrap samples). Each doctor independently forms a diagnosis. The final diagnosis is the **majority verdict**. Individual doctors may occasionally be wrong, but a wise crowd of diverse, independent opinions dramatically reduces diagnostic error. This is exactly the spirit of **Bagging**.

---

## 1.3 Boosting — Sequential Error Correction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boosting** is a sequential ensemble technique that trains base learners **one after another**, where each successive learner focuses **disproportionately on the instances misclassified by previous learners**. Predictions are combined via a **weighted majority vote** where stronger learners receive higher voting weights.

The seminal algorithm, **AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting)** by *Freund & Schapire (1997)*, is a KTU syllabus cornerstone.

**Key KTU terminology:**
- **Weak Learner** $h_t(x)$ : any classifier performing slightly better than random ($\varepsilon_t < 0.5$).
- **Instance Weight** $w_i^{(t)}$ : emphasis placed on training sample $i$ at round $t$.
- **Learner Weight** $\alpha_t$ : voting power assigned to learner $h_t$.
- **Strong Classifier** $H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \, h_t(x)\right)$.

### Real-World Analogy — The "Iterative Study Group" 📚

Consider a group of 10 students preparing for an exam. They have only one tutor at a time. In **Round 1**, all topics are equally important. After the test, they identify which students struggled with which topics. In **Round 2**, the tutor **spends more time on the weakest topics** for those particular students. After each round, the weakest areas are re-prioritized. Eventually, the group's collective understanding converges to mastery. This is the essence of **Boosting** — adaptive re-weighting of "hard" examples.

---

## 1.4 The Bias-Variance Motivation

> [!NOTE]
> The expected prediction error decomposes as:
> $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Variance}}_{\text{sensitivity to data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$
> **Bagging** primarily attacks the **Variance** term, while **Boosting** primarily attacks the **Bias** term.

| Aspect | Bagging | Boosting |
|---|---|---|
| Training Style | **Parallel** (independent) | **Sequential** (dependent) |
| Main Target | Variance Reduction | Bias Reduction |
| Sample Weighting | Uniform (bootstrap) | Adaptive (re-weighted) |
| Learner Weighting | Uniform ($1/B$) | Adaptive ($\alpha_t$) |
| Risk of Overfitting | Low | Moderate to High |
| Sensitive to Noise | Robust | Sensitive |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bias-Variance Decomposition — The Theoretical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 expects you to **derive** the bias-variance decomposition from first principles. This is a high-yield 7-mark question territory.

Let $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon$ is zero-mean noise with variance $\sigma^2$. The expected squared error of an estimator $\hat{f}(x)$ at point $x$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]$$

Expanding and separating deterministic from stochastic components, we obtain the canonical decomposition whose three terms carry the following operational meanings:

| Term | Symbol | Meaning | Attacked by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bias²** | $\left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\right)^2$ | How far the average prediction is from the truth | **Boosting** |
| **Variance** | $\mathbb{E}\!\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]$ | How much $\hat{f}$ fluctuates across training sets | **Bagging** |
| **Noise** | $\sigma^2$ | Irreducible randomness in $y$ | **Cannot be removed** |

---

## 2.2 Bagging — Mathematical Mechanics

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Given** : Original training set $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
2. **Generate** : $B$ bootstrap samples $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_B$, each of size $N$, drawn **with replacement** from $D$.
3. **Train** : Base learner $h_b$ on $D_b$ independently for each $b \in \{1, \dots, B\}$.
4. **Aggregate** : Combine via averaging (regression) or majority voting (classification).
5. **Predict** : Output the aggregated result on test data.

### Why Bagging Reduces Variance

If the $B$ base learners are **independent** with each having variance $\sigma^2$, the variance of the averaged predictor is reduced by a factor of $B$:

$$\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \frac{1}{B^2} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \text{Var}(\hat{f}_b) = \frac{\sigma^2}{B}$$

In practice, base learners are **not perfectly independent** (they share data structure), so the realistic reduction is:

$$\text{Var}\!\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \rho \, \sigma^2 + \frac{1 - \rho}{B} \, \sigma^2$$

where $\rho$ is the **pairwise correlation** between learners. **Random Forest** explicitly minimizes $\rho$ by randomizing feature selection at each split.

---

## 2.3 AdaBoost — Mathematical Mechanics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **AdaBoost weight update equations** and the **derivation of $\alpha_t$**. Memorize these cold.

### Algorithm Steps (Exhaustive)

1. **Initialize** uniform weights : $w_i^{(1)} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ for all $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$.
2. **For** $t = 1$ to $T$ :
   - Train weak learner $h_t(x)$ on the weighted dataset.
   - Compute the **weighted error** :
     
     $$\varepsilon_t = \sum_{i: \, h_t(x_i) \neq y_i} w_i^{(t)}$$
   - Compute the **learner weight** :
     
     $$\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)$$
   - Update **instance weights** for the next round :
     
     $$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\alpha_t \, y_i \, h_t(x_i)\right)$$
   - **Normalize** so that $\sum_i w_i^{(t+1)} = 1$.
3. **Output** : Strong classifier
   
   $$H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \, h_t(x)\right)$$

### Interpretation of $\alpha_t$

- If $\varepsilon_t \to 0$ (very accurate learner), $\alpha_t \to +\infty$ → high voting power.
- If $\varepsilon_t \to 0.5$ (random learner), $\alpha_t \to 0$ → no voting power.
- If $\varepsilon_t > 0.5$, $\alpha_t$ becomes **negative** (learner is worse than random; AdaBoost would abort).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Summary

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **single-page revision anchor**. Print this and pin it to your wall.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ensemble expected error | $P_{\text{ens}} = \sum_{k=\lceil B/2\rceil}^{B} \binom{B}{k}\varepsilon^k(1-\varepsilon)^{B-k}$ | Requires **independent** learners |
| 2 | Bias² + Variance + Noise | $\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var} + \sigma^2$ | KTU core decomposition |
| 3 | Bagging predictor (regression) | $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{f}_b(x)$ | Mean over $B$ learners |
| 4 | Bagging variance reduction | $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho\sigma^2 + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{B}$ | $\rho$ = pairwise correlation |
| 5 | OOB fraction | $P(\text{not chosen}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N \approx \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | **36.8%** OOB always |
| 6 | AdaBoost weighted error | $\varepsilon_t = \sum_{i:\,h_t(x_i)\neq y_i} w_i^{(t)}$ | Weights must sum to 1 |
| 7 | AdaBoost learner weight | $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)$ | **Sign function** of confidence |
| 8 | AdaBoost instance weight update | $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$ | $\exp(- \alpha_t)$ if correct, $\exp(+\alpha_t)$ if wrong |
| 9 | AdaBoost final classifier | $H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x)\right)$ | Weighted majority vote |
| 10 | Gradient Boosting pseudo-residual | $r_{im} = -\!\left[\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right]_{F=F_{m-1}}$ | Negative gradient of loss $L$ |
| 11 | Gradient Boosting update | $F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu \cdot h_m(x)$ | $\nu$ = learning rate (shrinkage) |
| 12 | Margin of AdaBoost | $\text{margin}(x,y) = \frac{y \sum_t \alpha_t h_t(x)}{\sum_t \alpha_t}$ | AdaBoost **maximizes margin** |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world deployment domains of Ensemble Methods:**

- **Banking & Credit Scoring** : XGBoost dominates fraud detection and loan default prediction due to its bias-reduction power.
- **Medical Diagnosis** : Bagging-based Random Forests are preferred for genomic and imaging data due to robustness against noise.
- **NLP** : Boosting is used in early spam filters; deep ensembles in modern transformers.
- **Recommender Systems** : Netflix, Amazon use ensemble hybrids.
- **Autonomous Vehicles** : Sensor fusion ensembles reduce variance in perception.
- **Cybersecurity** : AdaBoost variants for malware classification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bias-Variance Decomposition

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most asked KTU derivation** for ensemble-related questions. Practice until you can write it from memory in under 4 minutes.

**Setup :** Let $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, with $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2$. Let $\hat{f}(x)$ be an estimator trained on a random sample.

**Step 1 :** Write the expected squared error at point $x$.

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]$$

**Step 2 :** Expand the square. Let $A = f(x) - \hat{f}(x)$ for compactness. Then:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(\varepsilon + A)^2\right] = \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] + 2\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon]\mathbb{E}[A] + \mathbb{E}[A^2]$$

**Step 3 :** Use the noise properties. $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$, so the cross term vanishes.

$$= \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] + \mathbb{E}[A^2] = \sigma^2 + \mathbb{E}\!\left[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]$$

**Step 4 :** Add and subtract $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ inside $A$, i.e., $A = (f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]) + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x))$.

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[A^2\right] = (f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2 + 2(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]]}_{= \, 0} + \mathbb{E}\!\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]$$

**Step 5 :** The middle cross term vanishes by definition of variance.

$$= \underbrace{(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\!\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]}_{\text{Variance}}$$

**Step 6 :** Assemble the canonical result.

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{systematic}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{fluctuation}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible}}}$$

**Interpretation for Ensembles :**
- **Bagging** keeps Bias nearly constant but reduces Variance (averaging cancels out fluctuations).
- **Boosting** keeps Variance bounded but reduces Bias (sequential learners progressively approximate the true function $f$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AdaBoost's $\alpha_t$ from Exponential Loss Minimization

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation shows **why** $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\tfrac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)$ — it is not a magic formula but an **optimality condition**.

**Step 1 :** Define the **exponential loss** of the ensemble at round $T$:

$$L_T = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\left(-y_i \, F_T(x_i)\right)$$

where $F_T(x) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x)$ is the combined (real-valued) output.

**Step 2 :** At round $t$, we have $F_t = F_{t-1} + \alpha_t h_t$. We want to choose $\alpha_t$ and $h_t$ to minimize $L_t$.

$$L_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\left(-y_i (F_{t-1}(x_i) + \alpha_t h_t(x_i))\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)} \exp\!\left(-\alpha_t \, y_i \, h_t(x_i)\right)$$

where $w_i^{(t)} = \exp(-y_i F_{t-1}(x_i))$ are constants w.r.t. $\alpha_t$.

**Step 3 :** Split the sum over correctly classified (C) and misclassified (M) indices.

$$L_t = e^{-\alpha_t}\sum_{i \in C} w_i^{(t)} + e^{+\alpha_t}\sum_{i \in M} w_i^{(t)}$$

Define $W_C = \sum_{i \in C} w_i^{(t)}$ and $W_M = \sum_{i \in M} w_i^{(t)}$. Note that $W_C + W_M = 1$ (after normalization), and $\varepsilon_t = W_M$.

**Step 4 :** Differentiate $L_t$ with respect to $\alpha_t$ and set to zero.

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \alpha_t} = -e^{-\alpha_t} W_C + e^{+\alpha_t} W_M = 0$$

$$e^{+\alpha_t} W_M = e^{-\alpha_t} W_C \implies e^{2\alpha_t} = \frac{W_C}{W_M} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}$$

**Step 5 :** Solve for $\alpha_t$.

$$\boxed{\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)}$$

**Step 6 :** Substitute back to get the weight update rule. After minimizing, the optimal weights for the next round are:

$$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$$

which gives a multiplicative factor of $e^{-\alpha_t}$ for correctly classified samples and $e^{+\alpha_t}$ for misclassified samples — exactly the re-weighting rule of AdaBoost.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bagging's Out-of-Bag (OOB) Fraction

> [!NOTE]
> KTU loves the magic number **$1/e \approx 36.8\%$**. Derive it.

**Setup :** Dataset of size $N$. A bootstrap sample also has size $N$ drawn with replacement.

**Step 1 :** The probability that a specific instance $i$ is **not** selected in a single draw is $1 - \frac{1}{N}$.

**Step 2 :** Since there are $N$ independent draws, the probability that instance $i$ is **never** selected is:

$$P(\text{OOB for } i) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N$$

**Step 3 :** Take the limit as $N \to \infty$. Use the standard limit $\lim_{N \to \infty}(1 + a/N)^N = e^a$.

$$P(\text{OOB for } i) = \lim_{N \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N = e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679$$

**Step 4 :** Conclude that approximately **36.8%** of original samples are OOB for any given bootstrap, providing a free validation set.

$$\boxed{P(\text{OOB}) = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368}$$

**Engineering Use :** OOB samples provide an unbiased estimate of generalization error **without** needing a separate validation set — a major practical advantage of Bagging.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exam-day summary table** for 7-mark comparison questions.

| Dimension | Bagging | Boosting |
|---|---|---|
| **Year / Originators** | 1996, Breiman | 1990s; AdaBoost 1997, Freund & Schapire |
| **Training Topology** | Parallel (independent) | Sequential (each depends on previous) |
| **Sampling Strategy** | Bootstrap (with replacement) | Re-weighting of original dataset |
| **Weight on Samples** | Uniform within each bootstrap | Adaptive: $w_i^{(t)}$ updated each round |
| **Weight on Learners** | Equal ($1/B$ each) | Adaptive: $\alpha_t$ from weighted error |
| **Variance Behavior** | **Decreases** significantly | May increase slightly |
| **Bias Behavior** | Roughly unchanged | **Decreases** significantly |
| **Best Base Learner** | High-variance (e.g., unpruned decision tree) | Low-variance weak learner (e.g., decision stump) |
| **Effect of Noisy Data** | Robust (variance averaging) | Sensitive (over-focuses on noise) |
| **Risk of Overfitting** | Low | Higher (especially with deep base learners) |
| **Computational Cost** | Easily parallelizable | Inherently sequential |
| **Famous Implementations** | Random Forest, Extra Trees | AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, LightGBM |
| **Mathematical Core** | Variance reduction via averaging | Sequential minimization of exponential loss |
| **Margin Interpretation** | Not naturally margin-based | Schapire et al. proved margin maximization |
| **Stopping Criterion** | Fixed $B$ or OOB error plateau | Fixed $T$, or validation error early-stop |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bagging — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    data1["Original Dataset D of size N"]
    bootA["Bootstrap Sample D1 of size N"]
    bootB["Bootstrap Sample D2 of size N"]
    bootC["Bootstrap Sample DB of size N"]
    trainA["Train Base Learner h1"]
    trainB["Train Base Learner h2"]
    trainC["Train Base Learner hB"]
    predA["Prediction h1 x"]
    predB["Prediction h2 x"]
    predC["Prediction hB x"]
    agg["Aggregator Average or Majority Vote"]
    out["Final Prediction f bag x"]

    data1 --> bootA
    data1 --> bootB
    data1 --> bootC
    bootA --> trainA
    bootB --> trainB
    bootC --> trainC
    trainA --> predA
    trainB --> predB
    trainC --> predC
    predA --> agg
    predB --> agg
    predC --> agg
    agg --> out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram :** The vertical "trunk" of bagging is the original dataset. The three "branches" represent three parallel bootstrap samples (note: in practice, $B$ can be 100–1000). Each branch trains a learner independently. The "Aggregator" node fuses all predictions into one final output.

---

## 4.2 AdaBoost — Sequential Re-weighting Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initW["Initialize Weights w1 to 1 by N for all i"]
    trainT["Train Weak Learner ht on Weighted Data"]
    compE["Compute Weighted Error epsilon t"]
    compA["Compute Learner Weight alpha t = 0.5 ln of 1 minus epsilon t over epsilon t"]
    updateW["Update Instance Weights wi new equals wi times exp of minus alpha t yi ht of xi"]
    normW["Normalize Weights to Sum to 1"]
    checkT["Check t less than T"]
    outputH["Output Strong Classifier H x equals sign of sum of alpha t h t of x"]

    initW --> trainT
    trainT --> compE
    compE --> compA
    compA --> updateW
    updateW --> normW
    normW --> checkT
    checkT -->|Yes| trainT
    checkT -->|No| outputH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram :** The cycle from `trainT` to `checkT` represents the sequential nature of boosting. The instance weights $w_i$ are the "feedback signal" passed from round $t$ to round $t+1$. Misclassified samples get **boosted** weights, forcing the next learner to focus on them.

---

## 4.3 Bagging vs. Boosting — Side-by-Side Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BAG["BAGGING Parallel"]
        direction TB
        bD["Dataset D"]
        bS1["Sample D1"]
        bS2["Sample D2"]
        bS3["Sample DB"]
        bH1["Learner h1"]
        bH2["Learner h2"]
        bH3["Learner hB"]
        bA["Aggregator Average or Vote"]
        bD --> bS1 --> bH1 --> bA
        bD --> bS2 --> bH2 --> bA
        bD --> bS3 --> bH3 --> bA
    end

    subgraph BOOST["BOOSTING Sequential"]
        direction TB
        sD["Dataset D with Weights w"]
        sH1["Learner h1"]
        sE1["Error epsilon 1 and Alpha 1"]
        sU1["Update Weights w to w new"]
        sH2["Learner h2 with new weights"]
        sE2["Error epsilon 2 and Alpha 2"]
        sU2["Update Weights w to w new2"]
        sH3["Learner hT with final weights"]
        sF["Weighted Vote Sum of alpha t h t x"]
        sD --> sH1 --> sE1 --> sU1 --> sH2 --> sE2 --> sU2 --> sH3 --> sF
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical visual contrast :** The Bagging subgraph has **no internal arrows between learners** (they are independent), whereas the Boosting subgraph has a **chained arrow** from each learner's error computation into the next learner's input. This single visual difference captures the entire conceptual distinction.

---

## 4.4 Bias-Variance Tradeoff under Ensembling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["High Bias Low Variance Unstable High Variance"] --> B["Ensemble Reduces Variance Bagging"]
    A --> C["Ensemble Reduces Bias Boosting"]
    B --> D["Low Bias Low Variance Sweet Spot"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Strong Generalization"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram :** The starting state is a single high-bias or high-variance model. Bagging moves the operating point leftward (lower variance), while Boosting moves it upward (lower bias). The goal is the bottom-left quadrant: low bias AND low variance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Ensemble Learning. With a neat diagram, explain how the bias-variance decomposition motivates the use of ensemble methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ensemble learning combines multiple base learners to produce a single strong learner with better generalization. The expected prediction error decomposes as:

$$\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$$

Bagging reduces **Variance** by averaging independent predictors; Boosting reduces **Bias** by sequentially correcting errors. The diagram (draw flowchart showing input → multiple learners → aggregator → output) makes the parallel/sequential nature explicit. (3 marks: 1 def + 1 equation + 1 motivation)

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Out-of-Bag (OOB) estimate in Bagging? Prove that approximately 36.8% of original samples are OOB for any bootstrap.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

OOB samples are instances not selected in a bootstrap. The probability that a specific sample is never selected in $N$ draws is:

$$P(\text{OOB}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$$

(3 marks: 1 OOB definition + 1 derivation + 1 final value)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the bias-variance decomposition of expected squared error and explain how it justifies the design of Bagging and Boosting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the AdaBoost algorithm with the mathematical formulation of $\alpha_t$ and the instance weight update rule. Show how the algorithm minimizes the exponential loss function. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Bias-Variance Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 [1 mark] :** Define the setup. Let $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ with $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2$.

**Step 2 [2 marks] :** Write and expand the expected squared error:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] + 2\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon]\mathbb{E}[f(x) - \hat{f}(x)] + \mathbb{E}[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2]$$

Since $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$, the cross term vanishes:

$$= \sigma^2 + \mathbb{E}\!\left[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]$$

**Step 3 [2 marks] :** Add and subtract $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ inside the bracket, then apply the variance identity $\mathbb{E}[Z^2] = \text{Var}(Z) + (\mathbb{E}[Z])^2$ with $Z = f(x) - \hat{f}(x)$:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\!\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]}_{\text{Variance}}$$

**Step 4 [1 mark] :** Assemble the canonical decomposition:

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y-\hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2}$$

**Step 5 [1 mark] :** Connect to ensembles. Bagging targets **Variance** (averaging independent fluctuations); Boosting targets **Bias** (sequential approximation of $f$). 

---

### Part (b) — AdaBoost Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 [1 mark] :** State the exponential loss objective:

$$L_T = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\left(-y_i F_T(x_i)\right), \quad F_T(x) = \sum_{t=1}^{T}\alpha_t h_t(x)$$

**Step 2 [1 mark] :** At round $t$, with $F_t = F_{t-1} + \alpha_t h_t$, isolate the dependence on $\alpha_t$ and $h_t$:

$$L_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \underbrace{\exp(-y_i F_{t-1}(x_i))}_{w_i^{(t)}} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)) = e^{-\alpha_t}W_C + e^{+\alpha_t}W_M$$

where $W_C$ = sum of weights of correctly classified, $W_M$ = sum of misclassified = $\varepsilon_t$.

**Step 3 [2 marks] :** Differentiate w.r.t. $\alpha_t$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \alpha_t} = -e^{-\alpha_t}W_C + e^{+\alpha_t}W_M = 0 \implies e^{2\alpha_t} = \frac{W_C}{W_M} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)}$$

**Step 4 [2 marks] :** State the instance weight update rule and its interpretation:

$$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$$

Misclassified samples ($y_i h_t(x_i) = -1$) get their weights multiplied by $e^{+\alpha_t} > 1$ (boosted), while correctly classified samples get multiplied by $e^{-\alpha_t} < 1$ (de-emphasized). Weights are then normalized.

**Step 5 [1 mark] :** State the final strong classifier:

$$H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x)\right)$$

This is a **weighted majority vote** where each weak learner contributes proportionally to its accuracy.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the Bagging algorithm in detail. Derive the OOB fraction and explain how Random Forest extends Bagging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast Bagging and Boosting along 6 key dimensions. Which is more suitable for noisy data and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Bagging Algorithm (7 marks)

**Step 1 [2 marks] :** Describe the Bagging algorithm with bootstrap mechanism:
- Given dataset $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
- For $b = 1$ to $B$: draw bootstrap sample $D_b$ of size $N$ **with replacement** from $D$.
- Train base learner $h_b$ on $D_b$.
- Aggregate: $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_b \hat{f}_b(x)$ (regression) or majority vote (classification).

**Step 2 [2 marks] :** Derive the OOB fraction:
- Probability that sample $i$ is **not** selected in a single draw: $1 - \frac{1}{N}$.
- Probability of never being selected in $N$ draws: $\left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N$.
- Taking $N \to \infty$: $\lim_{N\to\infty}(1 - 1/N)^N = e^{-1} = 1/e \approx 0.368$.

**Step 3 [1 mark] :** Explain OOB's utility: provides free validation set for hyperparameter tuning and unbiased error estimate.

**Step 4 [2 marks] :** Random Forest extension:
- Uses decision trees as base learners.
- At each split, considers only a random subset of features (typically $\sqrt{d}$ for classification, $d/3$ for regression) to **decorrelate** the trees.
- Reduces the pairwise correlation $\rho$ in the variance formula $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho\sigma^2 + (1-\rho)\sigma^2/B$, thereby maximizing variance reduction.

---

### Part (b) — Comparison and Noise Robustness (7 marks)

| # | Dimension | Bagging | Boosting |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Training topology | Parallel | Sequential |
| 2 | Target | Variance reduction | Bias reduction |
| 3 | Sample weighting | Uniform | Adaptive |
| 4 | Learner weighting | Uniform $1/B$ | Adaptive $\alpha_t$ |
| 5 | Overfitting risk | Low | Higher |
| 6 | Noise robustness | **High** | Low |

(7 marks: 1 mark for 6 dimensions + 1 mark for conclusion)

**Conclusion on Noisy Data (1 mark):** **Bagging is more suitable for noisy data.** Boosting aggressively re-weights misclassified samples; noisy/outlier points are frequently misclassified and thus receive exponentially growing weights, causing the ensemble to overfit to noise. Bagging, by averaging over many bootstrap samples, dilutes the influence of any individual noisy point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization step** in AdaBoost: after updating $w_i$, you **must** divide by $\sum_i w_i$ so weights sum to 1. Examiners explicitly test this. **[−1 mark]**
> 2. **Confusing the sign convention** in the weight update: many students write $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp(\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$ (with the **wrong sign**). The correct formula has $-\alpha_t$ inside the exponent. **[−2 marks if wrong]**
> 3. **Stating the OOB fraction as exactly 0.5** (this is a common confusion with the median). The correct value is $\mathbf{1/e \approx 0.368}$. **[−1 mark]**
> 4. **Omitting the irreducibility justification** for noise $\sigma^2$: examiners expect you to mention that noise comes from the data-generating process itself, not the model. **[−1 mark]**
> 5. **Calling Bagging "Boosting" or vice versa** in a comparison question: this is an instant 0 on that dimension. Use a clear table.
> 6. **Writing the AdaBoost final classifier as a simple average** instead of $\text{sign}(\sum \alpha_t h_t(x))$: the sign function and weighted sum are both essential. **[−1 mark]**
> 7. **Forgetting to mention** that Bagging assumes base learners are **independent** for the variance-reduction math to hold perfectly. In practice, learners are correlated, hence Random Forest's feature randomization.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Pin This List.**

- ✅ **Ensemble = multiple base learners → one strong learner.** Foundation for both bagging and boosting.
- ✅ **Bias-Variance decomposition** : $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$. Always write the full equation.
- ✅ **Bagging = parallel + bootstrap + uniform weighting + variance reduction.**
- ✅ **Boosting = sequential + re-weighting + adaptive $\alpha_t$ + bias reduction.**
- ✅ **OOB fraction = $1/e \approx 36.8\%$** — derived from $\lim_{N\to\infty}(1-1/N)^N$.
- ✅ **Bagging variance** : $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho\sigma^2 + (1-\rho)\sigma^2/B$. Lower $\rho$ ⇒ better reduction.
- ✅ **AdaBoost weighted error** : $\varepsilon_t = \sum_{i: h_t \neq y_i} w_i^{(t)}$. Always **normalized**.
- ✅ **AdaBoost $\alpha_t$** : $\frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_t}\right)$. Derived from exponential loss minimization.
- ✅ **AdaBoost weight update** : $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)}\exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$. **Minus sign is critical.**
- ✅ **Final classifier** : $H(x) = \text{sign}(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x))$.
- ✅ **Gradient Boosting** : generalizes AdaBoost to arbitrary differentiable loss via negative gradient (pseudo-residual).
- ✅ **XGBoost / LightGBM / CatBoost** : modern, regularized, production-grade gradient boosting.
- ✅ **Random Forest** : bagging + random feature subset at each split = decorrelated trees.
- ✅ **Bagging is robust to noise; Boosting is sensitive to noise and outliers.**
- ✅ **Bagging base learners should be high-variance, low-bias** (e.g., deep trees); **Boosting base learners should be low-variance, weak** (e.g., decision stumps).
- ✅ **Schapire's theorem** : AdaBoost maximizes the margin on the training set, connecting it to support vector machines.
- ✅ **Practical tip** : For $B$ in bagging, $B = 100$–$500$ usually suffices; for boosting, monitor validation error to set $T$ and use early stopping.
- ✅ **Computational note** : Bagging is embarrassingly parallel (Map-Reduce friendly); Boosting cannot be parallelized across rounds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resampling Methods - Bootstrapping, Cross Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Resampling Methods - Bootstrapping, Cross Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Resampling Methods — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resampling Methods** are a class of statistical procedures that draw repeated samples from an *observed dataset* (instead of the true population) in order to quantify the **uncertainty**, **variance**, **bias**, and **generalization performance** of a learning algorithm. Under the KTU 2024 syllabus, the two canonical resampling methods studied are **Bootstrapping** (used to estimate the sampling distribution of an estimator) and **Cross-Validation** (used to estimate the **test error rate** / generalization error of a fitted model).

In supervised learning, when training a model we are forced to work with a *single* finite sample drawn from the population. The unknown true error $Err_{\mathcal{D}} = \mathbb{E}[L(Y, f(X))]$ and the unknown training error $Err_{\tau} = \mathbb{E}[L(Y, \hat{f}(X)) \mid \tau]$ can only be *estimated*. Resampling methods replace the unavailable population with the empirical dataset and use the *variation across many re-subsamples* to mimic the variation that would be seen across many fresh samples from the population.

The two techniques serve different but complementary purposes:

| Method | Primary Purpose | Typical Use Case in ML |
|---|---|---|
| **Bootstrapping** | Estimate the *sampling distribution* of an estimator (variance, bias, CI) | Assessing model coefficient stability, ensemble methods (Bagging), standard error of any statistic |
| **Cross-Validation** | Estimate the *generalization/test error* of a learning algorithm | Model selection, hyperparameter tuning, comparing classifiers/regressors |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ticket Jar" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Lottery Ticket Jar:** Imagine a glass jar containing $n$ lottery tickets, each representing one observation in your dataset. The true population is an *infinite warehouse* of tickets, but you only have the jar in hand.

- **Bootstrapping** is like *reaching into the jar with replacement*, pulling out $n$ tickets, recording the average prize, putting them back, shaking, and repeating $B$ times. Because of replacement, the same ticket may appear multiple times in one draw. The *spread* of the $B$ averages tells you how much the *estimate itself* would vary if you had drawn a different jar from the warehouse.
- **Cross-Validation** is like *splitting the jar* into $K$ smaller jars, training a model on $K-1$ jars and testing on the held-out one, rotating the hold-out each time. The *average test performance* across the $K$ rotations gives a fair estimate of how the model would perform on a *new* jar (i.e., a fresh test set).

This dual view — **bootstrapping probes the stability of the statistic**, while **cross-validation probes the honesty of the model** — is the conceptual heart of Module 4.

---

## 1.3 Why These Methods Matter for KTU 2024 Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework expects a student of `PCCST503` to demonstrate the Course Outcomes (CO) linked to **model selection, performance estimation, and uncertainty quantification**. Bootstrapping and Cross-Validation are the standard *practical* and *theoretical* tools that operationalize these COs in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

- **CO Mapped:** *Estimate the generalization performance of a learning model using resampling techniques.*
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply / Analyze (the *Apply* level is tested in Part A, *Analyze* in Part B).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bootstrapping — Operational Logic

**The Bootstrap Procedure** (Bradley Efron, 1979) replaces theoretical derivations of estimator variability with brute-force computation. Given a dataset

$$D = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$$

and an estimator $\hat{\theta} = s(D)$ (e.g., the sample mean, sample median, or a regression coefficient vector), the steps are:

1. **Treat the empirical distribution $\hat{F}$ as the population.** $\hat{F}$ assigns probability $1/n$ to each observed point.
2. **Draw a bootstrap sample** $D^{*b} = \{x_1^{*b}, x_2^{*b}, \dots, x_n^{*b}\}$ of size $n$ from $D$ *with replacement* (each $x_i^{*b} \sim \hat{F}$ uniformly).
3. **Compute the bootstrap statistic** $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = s(D^{*b})$.
4. **Repeat steps 2–3 for $b = 1, 2, \dots, B$**, where $B$ is large (typically $B \in [1000, 10000]$).
5. **Aggregate** the $\hat{\theta}^{*b}$ to obtain:
   - Bootstrap estimate of the standard error
   - Bootstrap bias
   - Bootstrap confidence intervals

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** The *plug-in principle*: the empirical CDF $\hat{F}_n$ is the *non-parametric maximum likelihood estimator* of the true CDF $F$. When you treat $\hat{F}$ as the truth, the sampling distribution of $\hat{\theta}$ under $F$ is approximated by its sampling distribution under $\hat{F}$ — which is exactly the bootstrap distribution.

### 2.1.1 Bootstrap Estimate of Standard Error

The bootstrap standard error of the estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is the standard deviation of the $B$ bootstrap replicates:

$$SE_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^2}$$

where

$$\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{\theta}^{*b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $B-1$ divisor (instead of $B$) gives an *unbiased* estimator of the variance — a detail KTU examiners frequently probe.

### 2.1.2 Bootstrap Bias Estimation

The bias of an estimator is the difference between its expected value and the true parameter. The bootstrap estimate of bias is:

$$\widehat{\text{bias}}_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \hat{\theta}$$

If $\widehat{\text{bias}}_{B}$ is large (e.g., for the sample median or 25th percentile), one may subtract it from the original estimate to produce a *bias-corrected* estimator:

$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{bc}} = \hat{\theta} - \widehat{\text{bias}}_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = 2\hat{\theta} - \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{\theta}^{*b}$$

### 2.1.3 Bootstrap Confidence Intervals

The **Percentile Method** is the simplest:

$$CI_{1-\alpha}^{\text{percentile}} = \left[\hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)},\ \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)}\right]$$

where $\hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)}$ is the empirical $\alpha/2$ quantile of the bootstrap replicates.

The **Pivotal (Basic) Method** inverts around the original estimate:

$$CI_{1-\alpha}^{\text{pivotal}} = \left[2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)},\ 2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)}\right]$$

The **Normal Approximation Method** uses:

$$CI_{1-\alpha}^{\text{normal}} = \left[\hat{\theta} \pm z_{1-\alpha/2} \cdot SE_{B}(\hat{\theta})\right]$$

The **BCa (Bias-Corrected and Accelerated) Method** extends the percentile method by adjusting for both bias and skewness — the most accurate in practice.

---

## 2.2 Cross-Validation — Operational Logic

**Cross-Validation (CV)** partitions the training data into $K$ disjoint folds, trains on $K-1$ folds and validates on the held-out fold, then averages the held-out errors. The primary goal is to estimate $Err_{\mathcal{D}} = \mathbb{E}[L(Y, f(X))]$ without leaking test data.

### 2.2.1 The K-Fold CV Algorithm

1. **Split** the indexed training set $\mathcal{T} = \{(x_1,y_1), \dots, (x_n,y_n)\}$ randomly into $K$ *approximately equal-sized* folds $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_K$ such that $F_i \cap F_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$.
2. **For** $k = 1, 2, \dots, K$:
   - Fit the model $\hat{f}^{-k}$ on $\mathcal{T} \setminus F_k$ (the $K-1$ folds excluding $F_k$).
   - Compute held-out predictions $\hat{y}_i^{-k} = \hat{f}^{-k}(x_i)$ for $i \in F_k$.
   - Compute the validation loss on fold $k$:
   
   $$\widehat{Err}_k = \frac{1}{\vert F_k \vert}\sum_{i \in F_k} L\!\left(y_i, \hat{f}^{-k}(x_i)\right)$$

3. **The K-fold CV estimate** of the test error is:

$$CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \widehat{Err}_k$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Loss Functions Used in the KTU Syllabus:**
> - Regression: $L(y, \hat{y}) = (y - \hat{y})^2$ (squared error)
> - Classification: $L(y, \hat{y}) = \mathbb{1}\{y \neq \hat{y}\}$ (misclassification rate)

### 2.2.2 Special Case — Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)

When $K = n$, each fold consists of a single observation. The LOOCV estimate is:

$$CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} L\!\left(y_i, \hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i)\right)$$

where $\hat{f}^{(-i)}$ is the model fit on the $n-1$ observations *excluding* $(x_i, y_i)$.

**Computational shortcut for least-squares regression:**

For linear models fit by least squares, the LOOCV error has the closed-form expression:

$$CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2$$

where $h_{ii}$ is the $i$-th diagonal element of the **hat matrix** $H = X(X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}$. This avoids $n$ refits.

### 2.2.3 The Validation Set Approach

A single random split into training (≈ 70%) and validation (≈ 30%) sets. The validation error is taken as an estimate of the test error. **Disadvantages:** high variance (depends on the split), wastes data (model trained on a subset).

### 2.2.4 Bias-Variance Trade-Off in Choosing K

- **Small K (e.g., K = 2)**: Each training fold is large → low bias. But high variance in the CV estimate (only 2 estimates averaged).
- **Large K (e.g., K = n, LOOCV)**: Low variance (n estimates), but training sets are *highly correlated* (each differs by only 1 point) → can produce a slightly *upward-biased* CV estimate (since each training set is slightly smaller than the full $n$).
- **Standard recommendation:** $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ balances bias and variance.

---

## 2.3 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table. KTU ESE questions on this module routinely test one of these formulas directly.**

| Concept | Formula | Notation / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Bootstrap sample size | $n$ | Same as original dataset |
| Bootstrap replicate | $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = s(D^{*b})$ | $b = 1, \dots, B$ |
| Bootstrap mean of replicates | $\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{\theta}^{*b}$ | Used in SE, bias |
| **Bootstrap SE** | $SE_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^2}$ | Unbiased variance estimate |
| **Bootstrap Bias** | $\widehat{\text{bias}}_{B} = \bar{\theta}^{*} - \hat{\theta}$ | Signed difference |
| Bias-corrected estimator | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{bc}} = 2\hat{\theta} - \bar{\theta}^{*}$ | Removes estimated bias |
| Percentile CI | $\left[\hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)},\ \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)}\right]$ | Empirical quantiles |
| Pivotal CI | $\left[2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)},\ 2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)}\right]$ | Reflection of percentile |
| K-Fold CV error | $CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\widehat{Err}_k$ | Average over K folds |
| LOOCV error | $CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2$ | Linear-model closed form |
| Fold MSE | $\widehat{Err}_k = \frac{1}{\vert F_k \vert}\sum_{i \in F_k}\left(y_i - \hat{f}^{-k}(x_i)\right)^2$ | Regression version |
| Hat matrix | $H = X(X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}$ | $h_{ii}$ = leverage of $i$-th obs. |
| Probability a specific obs. is *not* in bootstrap sample | $P(i \notin D^{*b}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$ | $\approx 0.3679$ for large $n$ |
| Expected unique elements in bootstrap sample | $n\left(1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}\right)$ | $\approx 0.6321 \cdot n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The number $0.6321$ is the famous *0.632 rule* — approximately **63.21%** of the original observations appear (at least once) in a bootstrap sample, and about **36.79%** are left out. This connects bootstrap to the *.632 bootstrap* cross-validation estimator.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnostics:** Bootstrap CIs for sensitivity/specificity estimates from a finite patient cohort.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Bootstrap SE of the Weibull shape parameter for failure-time modeling.
- **NLP/Speech:** CV-based hyperparameter tuning for SVM regularization in text classification.
- **Computer Vision:** Bootstrap to assess variability of test accuracy across model retrainings.
- **Financial Engineering:** Bootstrap to construct prediction intervals for VaR (Value-at-Risk) without distributional assumptions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bootstrap Standard Error from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** We observe $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n \stackrel{i.i.d.}{\sim} F$ with unknown true mean $\mu$ and true variance $\sigma^2$. We use the sample mean $\hat{\mu} = \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ as our estimator. Goal: estimate $SE(\hat{\mu}) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ *without* knowing $\sigma$.

### Step 1 — Write the true standard error

The exact standard error of the sample mean under the true distribution $F$ is:

$$SE_F(\hat{\mu}) = \sqrt{\text{Var}_F(\hat{\mu})} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2}{n}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

The problem: $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(X_i)$ is unknown, and $F$ is unknown.

### Step 2 — Replace $F$ with $\hat{F}$ (the bootstrap idea)

Define the empirical distribution $\hat{F}$ that assigns probability $1/n$ to each $X_i$:

$$\hat{F}(x) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\{X_i \le x\}$$

Under $\hat{F}$, the variance of a single draw is the sample variance $S^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2$.

### Step 3 — Express the bootstrap standard error

Because a bootstrap sample $D^{*b}$ of size $n$ is an i.i.d. sample from $\hat{F}$, the bootstrap *mean* $\bar{X}^{*b}$ has the bootstrap standard error:

$$SE_{\hat{F}}(\bar{X}^{*}) = \sqrt{\frac{S^2}{n}} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$$

This is the **classical sample standard error** — recovered exactly by the bootstrap. So bootstrapping the mean gives back the textbook formula, validating the procedure.

### Step 4 — Generalize to an arbitrary statistic $\hat{\theta} = s(X_1, \dots, X_n)$

For a non-trivial statistic (e.g., the median, the 90th percentile, a regression coefficient), no closed-form standard error exists. The bootstrap proceeds by simulation:

For each $b = 1, 2, \dots, B$:
- Draw $D^{*b} = \{X_1^{*b}, \dots, X_n^{*b}\}$ i.i.d. from $\hat{F}$ (i.e., resample $D$ with replacement).
- Compute $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = s(D^{*b})$.

Aggregate via the *empirical* second central moment:

$$SE_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^2}$$

### Step 5 — Justification of the $B-1$ divisor

Let $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{B} = \frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^2$. Compute its expectation under the bootstrap distribution $\hat{F}$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\hat{F}}\!\left[\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{B}\right] = \mathbb{E}_{\hat{F}}\!\left[\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^2\right]$$

Substitute $\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{\theta}^{*b}$:

$$= \frac{1}{B-1}\,\mathbb{E}_{\hat{F}}\!\left[\sum_{b=1}^{B}(\hat{\theta}^{*b})^{2} - B(\bar{\theta}^{*})^{2}\right]$$

Now, since $\mathbb{E}_{\hat{F}}[\hat{\theta}^{*b}] = \mu_{\hat{\theta}}$ and $\text{Var}_{\hat{F}}(\hat{\theta}^{*b}) = \sigma^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}$ (constants w.r.t. $b$):

$$\mathbb{E}_{\hat{F}}\!\left[\sum_{b=1}^{B}(\hat{\theta}^{*b})^{2}\right] = B\!\left(\sigma^{2}_{\hat{\theta}} + \mu^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}\right)$$

And $\mathbb{E}_{\hat{F}}\!\left[(\bar{\theta}^{*})^{2}\right] = \frac{\sigma^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}}{B} + \mu^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}$, so:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\hat{F}}\!\left[\sum_{b=1}^{B}(\hat{\theta}^{*b})^{2} - B(\bar{\theta}^{*})^{2}\right] = B\!\left(\sigma^{2}_{\hat{\theta}} + \mu^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}\right) - B\!\left(\frac{\sigma^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}}{B} + \mu^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}\right) = (B-1)\sigma^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\hat{F}}\!\left[\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{B}\right] = \sigma^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $B-1$ divisor makes $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{B}$ an **unbiased estimator** of the true bootstrap variance $\sigma^{2}_{\hat{\theta}}$. The $B$ divisor would understate the variance by a factor of $B/(B-1)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bias of the Sample Median (Bootstrap Rescue)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** For a Normal population $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the sample median $\hat{\theta} = \text{med}(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ is a biased estimator of $\mu$. Bootstrap can estimate this bias without closed-form derivations.

### Step 1 — The (known) bias for the median of a Normal

It is a known theoretical result that for a Normal population the *expected* sample median satisfies:

$$\mathbb{E}[\text{med}(X_1, \dots, X_n)] = \mu$$

That is, for symmetric distributions the median is *unbiased*. The interesting case is the **trimmed mean** or the **sample 25th percentile**, where the bias is non-trivial.

### Step 2 — Bootstrap bias estimate for a non-trivial statistic

Take $D = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ and the estimator $\hat{\theta} = Q_{0.25}(D)$ (25th percentile). Compute the original estimate $\hat{\theta}$.

For $b = 1, \dots, B$:
- Resample $D^{*b}$ with replacement.
- Compute $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = Q_{0.25}(D^{*b})$.

Aggregate:

$$\widehat{\text{bias}}_{B} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \hat{\theta}$$

### Step 3 — Worked numerical demonstration

Let $D = \{2.1,\ 3.4,\ 5.6,\ 7.2,\ 8.9\}$ (a small sample, $n = 5$).

**Original statistic:** $\hat{\theta} = Q_{0.25} = 2.1$ (the smallest value, since $0.25 \cdot 5 = 1.25$ ⇒ the 1.25th ordered value).

**Simulated bootstrap estimates** (draw 5 with replacement, take Q_0.25 each time):

| $b$ | Bootstrap sample $D^{*b}$ | $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = Q_{0.25}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\{2.1, 5.6, 3.4, 2.1, 7.2\}$ | 2.1 |
| 2 | $\{3.4, 8.9, 5.6, 7.2, 7.2\}$ | 3.4 |
| 3 | $\{2.1, 2.1, 2.1, 3.4, 5.6\}$ | 2.1 |
| 4 | $\{7.2, 8.9, 5.6, 5.6, 3.4\}$ | 3.4 |
| 5 | $\{8.9, 2.1, 7.2, 8.9, 5.6\}$ | 2.1 |

Compute the mean: $\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{1}{5}(2.1 + 3.4 + 2.1 + 3.4 + 2.1) = \frac{13.1}{5} = 2.62$.

**Bootstrap bias estimate:**

$$\widehat{\text{bias}}_{B} = 2.62 - 2.1 = 0.52$$

> The bootstrap tells us the 25th percentile estimator is biased upward by approximately **0.52** in this sample. The bias-corrected estimate is $\hat{\theta}_{\text{bc}} = 2.1 - 0.52 = 1.58$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Point:** Bias correction *subtracts* the bootstrap bias from the original estimate, not adds. Many students make sign errors here.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — K-Fold Cross-Validation: Algebra of the Held-Out Error

### Step 1 — Define the empirical loss

Suppose we have a regression task with squared-error loss. For a model $\hat{f}^{-k}$ trained on $\mathcal{T} \setminus F_k$:

$$\widehat{Err}_k = \frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{i \in F_k}\left(y_i - \hat{f}^{-k}(x_i)\right)^2, \qquad n_k = \vert F_k \vert$$

### Step 2 — Combine across folds

$$CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \widehat{Err}_k = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{i \in F_k}\left(y_i - \hat{f}^{-k}(x_i)\right)^2$$

### Step 3 — Equal fold-size simplification

If $n_k = n/K$ (balanced folds), then:

$$CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{K}{n}\sum_{i \in F_k}\left(y_i - \hat{f}^{-k}(x_i)\right)^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{i \in F_k}\left(y_i - \hat{f}^{-k}(x_i)\right)^2$$

> Since the $F_k$ partition the training set, this is equivalent to:

$$CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \hat{f}^{-k(i)}(x_i)\right)^2$$

where $k(i)$ is the fold containing observation $i$.

### Step 4 — LOOCV closed form for linear least squares

For a linear model $\hat{y} = X\hat{\beta}$ with $\hat{\beta} = (X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}y$ and the hat matrix $H = X(X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}$:

$$\hat{y}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} h_{ij}\,y_j$$

The model refit *without* observation $i$ has a well-known closed-form prediction (a result of the Sherman–Morrison identity applied to $(X^{\top}X)^{-1}$):

$$\hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i) = \hat{y}_i + \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}} \cdot (1 - 1) \quad \text{(this is incorrect — let me redo it)}$$

**Correct derivation.** The prediction at $x_i$ from the model fit without $(x_i, y_i)$ is:

$$\hat{y}_{i}^{(-i)} = x_i^{\top}\hat{\beta}^{(-i)} = \frac{\hat{y}_i - h_{ii} y_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

Proof sketch: The leave-one-out residual is:

$$y_i - \hat{y}_i^{(-i)} = \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

Squaring and summing gives the LOOCV MSE in a single matrix computation:

$$CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2$$

This is the famous *PRESS* (Prediction Sum of Squares) statistic — derived in full in standard regression texts and frequently quoted in KTU Module 4 questions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Probability of Exclusion in a Bootstrap Sample

A common exam question: *What fraction of the original observations does NOT appear in a bootstrap sample of size $n$?*

For a single observation $X_i$, the probability it is **not** selected in a given draw is $(1 - 1/n)$. Across $n$ independent draws:

$$P(X_i \notin D^{*b}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$$

Expected fraction of excluded observations:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

The expected number of *distinct* observations in a bootstrap sample is therefore:

$$n\left(1 - e^{-1}\right) \approx 0.6321 \cdot n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **0.632 rule** connects bootstrap to cross-validation: the *.632 bootstrap* uses this $0.632$ weighting to combine the bootstrap training error and the bootstrap leave-one-out error into a low-bias, low-variance generalization estimate.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bootstrap vs. Cross-Validation

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important comparison** for the KTU Module 4 ESE. Memorize it.

| Dimension | **Bootstrapping** | **K-Fold Cross-Validation** |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling scheme | With replacement, size $n$ | Without replacement, disjoint folds |
| Primary goal | Sampling distribution of a statistic | Test / generalization error of a model |
| Output | SE, bias, CI of estimator | Estimate of $Err_{\mathcal{D}}$ |
| Variance of the *estimate* | Low (averages $B$ replicates) | Higher (only $K$ estimates) |
| Bias | Can estimate and correct | Slight upward bias (model trained on $n(K-1)/K$ points) |
| Computational cost | $B$ resamples + $B$ model fits | $K$ model fits (typically $K \ll B$) |
| Used in model selection | Rarely | Standard (GridSearchCV, etc.) |
| Used in ensemble methods | Yes (Bagging = bootstrap aggregating) | No |
| Disjoint folds required? | No (replacement) | Yes (partition) |
| Typical hyperparameter $B$ or $K$ | $B \in [1000, 10000]$ | $K \in [5, 10]$ |
| KTU-exam weight | ~ 6 marks (Part A + part of Part B) | ~ 8 marks (Part A + main Part B) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bootstrap Procedure — Block-Level Flow Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the *resampling loop* of a bootstrap procedure. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Original Dataset D of size n] --> B1[Fix estimator function s]
    B1 --> C1[For b equals 1 to B]
    C1 --> D1[Draw bootstrap sample D star b of size n with replacement]
    D1 --> E1[Compute bootstrap statistic theta star b equals s of D star b]
    E1 --> F1[Store theta star b in replicates array]
    F1 --> G1{b equals B}
    G1 -->|No| C1
    G1 -->|Yes| H1[Compute mean theta bar star]
    H1 --> I1[Compute Bootstrap SE using B minus 1 divisor]
    H1 --> J1[Compute Bootstrap Bias equals theta bar star minus theta hat]
    I1 --> K1[Output Standard Error and Confidence Interval]
    J1 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Optionally compute bias corrected estimator]
```

## 4.2 K-Fold Cross-Validation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Training Set T of size n] --> Q1[Choose number of folds K equals 5 or 10]
    Q1 --> R1[Randomly partition T into F1 to FK]
    R1 --> S1[For k equals 1 to K]
    S1 --> T1[Fit model f hat minus k on T minus Fk]
    T1 --> U1[Predict on held out fold Fk]
    U1 --> V1[Compute validation loss MSE k or Error k on Fk]
    V1 --> W1[Store MSE k in array]
    W1 --> X1{k equals K}
    X1 -->|No| S1
    X1 -->|Yes| Y1[Compute CV error equals 1 by K times sum of MSE k]
    Y1 --> Z1[Report CV error as estimate of test error]
    Y1 --> AA1[Use CV error for model selection and hyperparameter tuning]
```

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparison — Data Partitioning Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BOOT[BOOTSTRAP]
        BO1[Original sample of size n] --> BO2[Resample n points with replacement]
        BO2 --> BO3[Some points appear twice, some are missing]
        BO3 --> BO4[Compute statistic and repeat B times]
    end

    subgraph CV[K FOLD CV]
        CV1[Original sample of size n] --> CV2[Split into K disjoint folds]
        CV2 --> CV3[Train on K minus 1 folds, test on 1 fold]
        CV3 --> CV4[Rotate held out fold K times]
        CV4 --> CV5[Average the K held out errors]
    end
```

## 4.4 The 0.632 Bootstrap Connection Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Draw bootstrap sample of size n] --> R2[About 63.21 percent unique original points]
    R2 --> R3[About 36.79 percent out of bag points]
    R3 --> R4[Predict on out of bag points]
    R4 --> R5[Compute out of bag error]
    R5 --> R6[Form 0.632 estimator equals 0.368 times train error plus 0.632 times OOB error]
    R6 --> R7[Use as cross validation like generalization estimate]
```

## 4.5 LOOCV Shortcut Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1{Need LOOCV error?} --> ST2{Is the model a linear least squares model?}
    ST2 -->|Yes| ST3[Use closed form CV equals 1 by n times sum of yi minus yhat i divided by 1 minus hii squared]
    ST3 --> ST4[Single matrix inverse gives hat matrix H]
    ST4 --> ST5[Compute leverage hii from diagonal of H]
    ST5 --> ST6[Report PRESS statistic]
    ST2 -->|No| ST7[Refit model n times excluding one observation each time]
    ST7 --> ST8[Average the n held out errors]
```
