# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define bootstrapping. List the steps involved in computing the bootstrap estimate of the standard error of an estimator. Mention the role of the number of bootstrap samples $B$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** *Bootstrapping* is a non-parametric resampling method introduced by Bradley Efron (1979) in which repeated samples are drawn *with replacement* from the observed dataset, treating the empirical distribution as a proxy for the unknown population distribution. It is used to quantify the sampling variability of an estimator without making strong distributional assumptions.

> **Steps to compute bootstrap SE (1.5 marks):**
> 1. Given data $D = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ and estimator $\hat{\theta} = s(D)$, draw $B$ bootstrap samples $D^{*1}, D^{*2}, \dots, D^{*B}$, each of size $n$, with replacement from $D$.
> 2. For each $b$, compute the bootstrap statistic $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = s(D^{*b})$.
> 3. Compute the bootstrap mean: $\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{\theta}^{*b}$.
> 4. Compute the bootstrap standard error: $SE_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^{2}}$.

> **Role of $B$ (0.5 mark):** As $B \to \infty$, the bootstrap SE converges to the true standard error. In practice, $B \in [1000, 10000]$ is recommended — at least $B = 1000$ for stable SE estimates, $B = 10000$ for accurate confidence-interval tail quantiles.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is K-Fold Cross-Validation? Explain the bias-variance trade-off in choosing the number of folds $K$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** *K-Fold Cross-Validation* is a resampling technique that partitions the training set into $K$ disjoint folds, trains the model on $K-1$ folds, and tests it on the held-out fold. The procedure is repeated for each fold, and the $K$ held-out error estimates are averaged to produce the cross-validation estimate $CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\widehat{Err}_k$.

> **Bias-Variance Trade-off (2 marks):**
> - **Bias decreases as $K$ increases.** With $K$ large (e.g., $K = n$ in LOOCV), each training fold is almost the entire dataset, so the bias of the CV estimate relative to the true test error is small.
> - **Variance of the CV estimate decreases as $K$ increases.** LOOCV averages $n$ estimates, reducing variance.
> - **However**, the *training folds* in LOOCV are *highly correlated* (each differs by only one observation), so the *model variance* can remain substantial and LOOCV can produce a *slightly upward-biased* generalization error estimate.
> - **Standard recommendation:** $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ — balances training-set size, bias, and computational cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Part B Module-Internal Choice.* Students answer ONE of two alternatives. Each alternative is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below, **OR-Question A** and **OR-Question B** are the two alternative choices.

---

### OR-Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the bootstrap method for estimating the standard error and the bias of an estimator. Use the dataset $D = \{4, 8, 9, 11, 15\}$ and the estimator $\hat{\theta} =$ sample median. Take $B = 5$ bootstrap samples (use a fixed set of resamples provided) and compute the bootstrap SE and bias.**

**Provided bootstrap samples:**

| $b$ | $D^{*b}$ (resample) |
|---|---|
| 1 | $\{4, 8, 9, 9, 15\}$ |
| 2 | $\{8, 9, 11, 11, 15\}$ |
| 3 | $\{4, 4, 8, 11, 15\}$ |
| 4 | $\{4, 8, 11, 15, 15\}$ |
| 5 | $\{8, 9, 9, 11, 15\}$ |

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 (0.5 mark) — Compute the original statistic.**
> Sort $D$: $\{4, 8, 9, 11, 15\}$. For $n = 5$, the sample median is the 3rd order statistic:
> $\hat{\theta} = 9$.

> **Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute bootstrap statistics.**
> - $b = 1$: $\{4, 8, 9, 9, 15\}$ → median = 9
> - $b = 2$: $\{8, 9, 11, 11, 15\}$ → median = 11
> - $b = 3$: $\{4, 4, 8, 11, 15\}$ → median = 8
> - $b = 4$: $\{4, 8, 11, 15, 15\}$ → median = 11
> - $b = 5$: $\{8, 9, 9, 11, 15\}$ → median = 9
>
> Hence $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = \{9, 11, 8, 11, 9\}$.

> **Step 3 (1 mark) — Bootstrap mean of replicates.**
> $$\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{9 + 11 + 8 + 11 + 9}{5} = \frac{48}{5} = 9.6$$

> **Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Bootstrap standard error.**
> $$SE_{B}(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^{2}}$$
> Squared deviations:
> - $(9 - 9.6)^2 = 0.36$
> - $(11 - 9.6)^2 = 1.96$
> - $(8 - 9.6)^2 = 2.56$
> - $(11 - 9.6)^2 = 1.96$
> - $(9 - 9.6)^2 = 0.36$
>
> Sum = $0.36 + 1.96 + 2.56 + 1.96 + 0.36 = 7.20$.
> $$SE_{B} = \sqrt{\frac{7.20}{4}} = \sqrt{1.80} \approx 1.342$$

> **Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Bootstrap bias estimate.**
> $$\widehat{\text{bias}}_{B} = \bar{\theta}^{*} - \hat{\theta} = 9.6 - 9.0 = 0.6$$
> The bootstrap estimates that the sample median is biased *upward* by 0.6 in this dataset. The bias-corrected estimator is:
> $$\hat{\theta}_{\text{bc}} = 2\hat{\theta} - \bar{\theta}^{*} = 2(9) - 9.6 = 8.4$$

> **Step 6 (0.5 mark) — Conclusion.** The bootstrap SE of the sample median for this dataset is approximately **1.342**, and the bootstrap bias is **+0.6**, suggesting the bias-corrected median of 8.4 may be a more accurate point estimate of the population center.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of the 0.632 bootstrap estimator in assessing the generalization error of a learning algorithm. Derive the expected fraction of unique observations in a bootstrap sample.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 (2 marks) — The need for a generalization error estimate.**
> Standard training error $\overline{err} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}L(y_i, \hat{f}(x_i))$ is *optimistic* — the model has seen these data. Cross-validation (CV) provides a better estimate but at $K$-fold × training cost. The 0.632 bootstrap provides an *alternative* using bootstrap resampling.

> **Step 2 (2.5 marks) — Derivation of the unique-observation fraction.**
> Let $D^{*b}$ be a bootstrap sample of size $n$ drawn with replacement from $D$. For a specific observation $X_i$, the probability it is *not* selected in one draw is $(1 - 1/n)$. Across $n$ independent draws:
> $$P(X_i \notin D^{*b}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$$
>
> The expected number of *distinct* original observations that appear at least once in $D^{*b}$ is:
> $$\mathbb{E}[\vert D^{*b} \cap D \vert] = n \cdot \left[1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}\right]$$
>
> Expected fraction:
> $$\lim_{n \to \infty}\left[1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}\right] = 1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.6321$$
>
> Hence the **0.632 rule**: approximately 63.21% of original observations are *in* the bootstrap sample (in-bag), and 36.79% are *out-of-bag* (OOB).

> **Step 3 (2.5 marks) — The 0.632 bootstrap estimator.**
> Define:
> - $\overline{err}_{\text{in-bag}}$ = average training error on in-bag observations.
> - $\overline{err}_{\text{OOB}}$ = average error on the out-of-bag observations (genuinely unseen during training).
>
> The **0.632 estimator** is:
> $$\widehat{Err}_{0.632} = 0.368 \cdot \overline{err}_{\text{in-bag}} + 0.632 \cdot \overline{err}_{\text{OOB}}$$
>
> The weights (0.368 = 1 - 0.632, 0.632) balance:
> - The *optimistic in-bag error* (underestimates generalization error)
> - The *pessimistic OOB error* (overestimates due to smaller training set)
>
> The 0.632 estimator is a *convex combination* that typically has lower bias and lower variance than either component alone, and is widely used in ensemble methods (e.g., Random Forest's OOB error is a special case).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often quote the *limiting* value 0.632 without showing the derivation $\lim_{n\to\infty}(1 - 1/n)^{n} = e^{-1}$. For full marks you MUST show the limit calculation explicitly.

---

### OR-Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain K-Fold Cross-Validation with a suitable diagram. For a dataset of $n = 20$ observations split into $K = 4$ folds of size 5 each, the following hold-out errors are obtained: $E_1 = 0.18$, $E_2 = 0.22$, $E_3 = 0.20$, $E_4 = 0.16$. Compute the 4-fold CV error and construct an approximate 95% confidence interval for the test error using the standard error of the CV estimate.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 (1.5 marks) — K-Fold CV definition with diagram description.**
> K-Fold CV partitions the training set of $n$ observations into $K$ disjoint folds $F_1, \dots, F_K$. For each $k = 1, \dots, K$:
> - Train model $\hat{f}^{-k}$ on $\mathcal{T} \setminus F_k$.
> - Compute $\widehat{Err}_k$ on $F_k$.
>
> The K-Fold CV estimate is the average of these $K$ errors:
> $$CV_{(K)} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\widehat{Err}_k$$
>
> **Diagrammatic representation (text):**
> For $K = 4$, $n = 20$, each fold has $n/K = 5$ observations:
>
> | Iteration | Train on | Test on | Hold-out error |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | $k=1$ | $F_2 \cup F_3 \cup F_4$ (15 obs.) | $F_1$ (5 obs.) | $E_1 = 0.18$ |
> | $k=2$ | $F_1 \cup F_3 \cup F_4$ (15 obs.) | $F_2$ (5 obs.) | $E_2 = 0.22$ |
> | $k=3$ | $F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_4$ (15 obs.) | $F_3$ (5 obs.) | $E_3 = 0.20$ |
> | $k=4$ | $F_1 \cup F_2 \cup F_3$ (15 obs.) | $F_4$ (5 obs.) | $E_4 = 0.16$ |

> **Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Compute the 4-fold CV error.**
> $$CV_{(4)} = \frac{1}{4}(0.18 + 0.22 + 0.20 + 0.16) = \frac{0.76}{4} = 0.19$$

> **Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute the standard error of the CV estimate.**
> Sample variance of the four errors:
> $$\sigma_{E}^{2} = \frac{1}{K-1}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(E_k - \overline{E})^{2} = \frac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{4}(E_k - 0.19)^{2}$$
> - $(0.18 - 0.19)^2 = 0.0001$
> - $(0.22 - 0.19)^2 = 0.0009$
> - $(0.20 - 0.19)^2 = 0.0001$
> - $(0.16 - 0.19)^2 = 0.0009$
>
> Sum = $0.0020$, so $\sigma_{E}^{2} = 0.0020/3 \approx 0.000667$.
> $$\sigma_{E} = \sqrt{0.000667} \approx 0.02582$$
>
> Standard error of the *mean* of the four $E_k$:
> $$SE_{CV} = \frac{\sigma_{E}}{\sqrt{K}} = \frac{0.02582}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{0.02582}{2} \approx 0.01291$$

> **Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Construct the 95% confidence interval.**
> Using the $z_{0.975} \approx 1.96$ approximation:
> $$CI_{95\%} = CV_{(4)} \pm 1.96 \cdot SE_{CV} = 0.19 \pm 1.96 \times 0.01291 \approx 0.19 \pm 0.0253$$
> $$CI_{95\%} \approx [0.1647,\ 0.2153]$$
>
> **Conclusion:** With 95% confidence, the true test error lies in the interval **[0.165, 0.215]**.

> **Step 5 (0.5 mark) — Caveat.** The fold errors are not strictly independent (training sets overlap), so the standard error computed this way is an *approximation* — the CI is therefore approximate.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare the Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV) and the K-Fold CV approaches. For a least-squares linear regression, derive the closed-form LOOCV formula involving the hat matrix diagonal elements $h_{ii}$.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 (2.5 marks) — Comparison: LOOCV vs. K-Fold CV.**
>
> | Aspect | LOOCV ($K = n$) | K-Fold CV ($K = 5$ or $10$) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Number of fits | $n$ | $K$ |
> | Training-set size per fit | $n - 1$ | $n(K-1)/K$ |
> | Bias of test-error estimate | Slightly upward (training set is $\approx 1$ point smaller) | Slightly upward but usually acceptable |
> | Variance of test-error estimate | **High** (training folds are highly correlated) | **Lower** (training folds are more independent) |
> | Computational cost | High ($n$ fits) | Low ($K$ fits, $K \ll n$) |
> | Closed-form available? | Yes (linear models only) | No general closed form |
> | When to prefer | Small $n$, expensive models, theoretical analysis | General-purpose, large $n$ |

> **Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Hat matrix definition.**
> For linear regression $\hat{y} = X\hat{\beta}$ with $\hat{\beta} = (X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}y$:
> $$\hat{y} = X(X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top} y = Hy$$
> where $H = X(X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}$ is the **hat matrix** (symmetric, idempotent: $H^{2} = H$). The diagonal element $h_{ii}$ is the *leverage* of observation $i$, satisfying $0 \le h_{ii} \le 1$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_{ii} = p + 1$ (where $p$ is the number of predictors, including intercept).

> **Step 3 (2.5 marks) — Derivation of LOOCV closed form.**
> The leave-one-out prediction at $x_i$ (model fit without observation $i$) can be shown to be:
> $$\hat{y}_i^{(-i)} = \frac{\hat{y}_i - h_{ii} y_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$
>
> Hence the LOOCV residual:
> $$y_i - \hat{y}_i^{(-i)} = y_i - \frac{\hat{y}_i - h_{ii} y_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = \frac{y_i(1 - h_{ii}) - \hat{y}_i + h_{ii} y_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$
>
> Squaring and averaging over all $i$:
> $$CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^{2}$$
>
> where $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ is the ordinary residual. This is the **PRESS** (Prediction Sum of Squares) statistic. It can be computed in closed form from a *single* model fit (no $n$ refits required), giving LOOCV a major computational advantage for linear models.

> **Step 4 (0.5 mark) — High-leverage caveat.** Observations with $h_{ii} \approx 1$ produce very large LOOCV residuals and dominate the PRESS statistic — this is why LOOCV is *sensitive to high-leverage points*, a fact KTU examiners occasionally test.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in bootstrap bias** — Students often write $\hat{\theta} - \bar{\theta}^{*}$ instead of $\bar{\theta}^{*} - \hat{\theta}$. The convention in Efron's framework is bias = bootstrap mean minus original estimate.
> 2. **Confusing $B$ with $K$** — Bootstrap uses $B$ (resamples), CV uses $K$ (folds). Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **LOOCV formula applied to non-linear models** — The hat-matrix shortcut is *only* valid for linear least-squares. For logistic regression, SVM, neural networks, the full refit is required.
> 4. **Forgetting $B-1$ divisor** — Use $B-1$ (not $B$) for the unbiased variance estimator of bootstrap replicates.
> 5. **The 0.632 rule is a LIMIT, not a finite-$n$ value** — Always derive the limit explicitly: $\lim_{n\to\infty}(1-1/n)^n = e^{-1}$.
> 6. **K-Fold CV is *not* independent across folds** — The standard-error computation treats the $K$ fold errors as if they were independent, which is an *approximation*. State this caveat in long-answer solutions to gain full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4:**

- ✅ **Resampling methods** use the observed dataset as a proxy for the population to estimate estimator variability and generalization performance.
- ✅ **Bootstrapping** = sample *with replacement* of size $n$, repeat $B$ times, aggregate.
- ✅ **Cross-Validation** = partition into $K$ disjoint folds, train on $K-1$, test on 1, average.
- ✅ The bootstrap **standard error** uses the $B-1$ divisor for *unbiased* variance estimation.
- ✅ The bootstrap **bias** is $\bar{\theta}^{*} - \hat{\theta}$ (signed difference: bootstrap mean minus original estimate).
- ✅ The **bias-corrected** estimator is $\hat{\theta}_{\text{bc}} = 2\hat{\theta} - \bar{\theta}^{*}$.
- ✅ The **Percentile CI** uses empirical quantiles of $\hat{\theta}^{*b}$.
- ✅ The **Pivotal CI** is $2\hat{\theta}$ minus the percentile endpoints.
- ✅ **K-Fold CV** error = simple mean of $K$ held-out fold errors.
- ✅ **LOOCV** = K = n; can have a closed-form for linear models: PRESS = $\frac{1}{n}\sum e_i^{2}/(1-h_{ii})^{2}$.
- ✅ The **0.632 rule** states that approximately **63.21%** of original observations are *in-bag* and **36.79%** are *out-of-bag* in a bootstrap sample, with the limit $(1-1/n)^{n} \to e^{-1}$.
- ✅ The **0.632 bootstrap estimator** is a convex combination: $0.368 \cdot \overline{err}_{\text{in-bag}} + 0.632 \cdot \overline{err}_{\text{OOB}}$.
- ✅ **Bagging** (Bootstrap Aggregating) is the canonical application of bootstrapping in ensemble learning.
- ✅ **Standard choice for K**: $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ — balances bias, variance, and computational cost.
- ✅ **LOOCV is low-variance but slightly biased upward** (training set is $n-1$, not $n$).
- ✅ **5-Fold or 10-Fold CV** is the de-facto industry standard for model selection in scikit-learn, R, and statistical practice.
- ✅ **Validation set approach** is the simplest but most wasteful and most variable alternative — generally avoided when $K$-fold is feasible.
- ✅ **Bootstrap** is preferred when the goal is inference about an *estimator* (SE, bias, CI).
- ✅ **Cross-Validation** is preferred when the goal is *model assessment* or *model selection*.
- ✅ The KTU 2024 Module 4 question bank almost always tests: (i) the bootstrap SE formula, (ii) the K-fold CV algorithm, (iii) bias-variance trade-off in $K$, and (iv) the 0.632 rule derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Aspects - Bias-Variance Tradeoff
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST503 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Practical Aspects - Bias-Variance Tradeoff

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In supervised machine learning, the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** is the foundational decomposition of a model's expected prediction error into three irreducible components: the squared **bias** of the learner, the **variance** of the learner, and the **irreducible noise** of the data. It formally explains *why* no single model can simultaneously minimize training error and generalize perfectly to unseen data.

Let $\mathcal{D}$ denote the training dataset sampled from an unknown joint distribution $P(X,Y)$, and let $\hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x)$ be the model learned from $\mathcal{D}$. The expected squared prediction error at input $x$ is decomposed as:

$$E_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x)\right)^2\right] = \underbrace{\left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x)]\right)^2}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x)\right]}_{\text{Estimation Error}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The bias-variance tradeoff is the *practical lens* through which every model selection decision (regularization strength, tree depth, neural network width, number of epochs) must be justified in the End-Semester Examination.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Dart Board of Machine Learning

Imagine you are a darts player trying to hit the bullseye (the *true function* $f(x)$).

- **High Bias, Low Variance** (e.g., always throws 5 cm to the left of the bullseye, but consistently): The player is *systematically off-target*. The aim is wrong. This corresponds to **underfitting** — a model that is too simple (e.g., fitting a straight line to curved data).
- **Low Bias, High Variance** (e.g., on average hits the bullseye, but throws scatter wildly across the board): The player can sometimes hit the target, but is *inconsistent*. This corresponds to **overfitting** — a model that memorizes the training data (e.g., a deep tree with `max_depth=50`).
- **Low Bias, Low Variance** (the dream): Tight cluster around the bullseye. This is the **ideal generalization** point — the global minimum of the total error curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The goal of a machine learning engineer is **not** to minimize bias or variance individually. The goal is to minimize their **sum** (plus the noise floor $\sigma^2$) by picking the optimal model complexity.

### 1.3 The Three Error Components — Glossary

| Component | Formal Symbol | Plain-English Meaning | Can We Reduce It? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Squared Bias** | $\left(E[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\right)^2$ | Error from wrong/oversimplified assumptions in the model class | Yes, by increasing model complexity or using a more flexible family |
| **Variance** | $E\left[\left(\hat{f}(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2\right]$ | Error from sensitivity to the *specific* training sample drawn | Yes, by regularization, more data, or ensembling |
| **Irreducible Noise** | $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(\epsilon)$ | Inherent randomness in the data-generation process | **No** — this is a hard lower bound on out-of-sample error |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Generative Story

We assume each data point is generated by the following process:

$$y = f(x) + \epsilon, \quad \text{where } E[\epsilon] = 0,\ \text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^2$$

The learning algorithm produces a hypothesis $\hat{f}(x)$ by minimizing some loss (e.g., squared error) over a training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ drawn i.i.d. from the data distribution.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown — How Each Error Behaves

- **Bias** quantifies the *systematic* deviation of the average prediction from the truth. If you retrained your model on 1,000 different datasets and averaged the predictions, bias measures how far that average lands from $f(x)$.
- **Variance** quantifies *how much* the predictions would fluctuate if you retrained on different datasets. A high-variance model is a fragile one: a single new data point could drastically change the decision boundary.
- **As model complexity $\uparrow$**:
  - Bias $\downarrow$ (the model can fit finer patterns)
  - Variance $\uparrow$ (the model starts chasing noise)
- **As training set size $N \uparrow$**:
  - Variance $\downarrow$ (estimators become more stable)
  - Bias is largely unchanged (a linear model remains linear)

### 2.3 The Tradeoff Curve — Conceptual Picture

The total expected error forms a characteristic **U-shaped (or convex) curve** as a function of model complexity:

- Far left → **High bias, low variance** (underfitting region)
- Far right → **Low bias, high variance** (overfitting region)
- Sweet spot → **Minimum total error** (optimal generalization)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE and module tests. Memorize the units and the meaning of each term.

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning / Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E[(y - \hat{f})^2] = (E[\hat{f}] - f)^2 + E[(\hat{f} - E[\hat{f}])^2] + \sigma^2$ | **Master decomposition** of expected test MSE |
| 2 | $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = E_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x)] - f(x)$ | Systematic error of the learner |
| 3 | $\text{Var}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)] = E_{\mathcal{D}}\left[(\hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x) - E_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x)])^2\right]$ | Estimation instability across datasets |
| 4 | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \approx \frac{\sigma^2}{N}$ (for simple estimators) | Variance shrinks as $1/N$ with more data |
| 5 | $E[(y - \hat{f})^2] = E[(\hat{f} - E[\hat{f}])^2] + (E[\hat{f}] - y)^2$ | Alternative additivity form |
| 6 | $\text{Bias}(\text{regularized estimator}) = \text{Bias}(\text{unreg.}) + \text{shrinkage term}$ | Regularization *increases* bias, *decreases* variance |
| 7 | $\text{Total Error}(x) = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Universal lower bound on test error |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bias-variance lens is the operational vocabulary used in:
- **Autonomous driving perception models**: Trading off latency vs. accuracy of a deep CNN.
- **Medical diagnosis models**: Where high bias (missing a disease) is catastrophic, so variance reduction via ensembling is preferred.
- **Financial forecasting**: A simple linear model has high bias on non-linear market regimes but extremely low variance → safer for risk-averse portfolios.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Rigorous Derivation of the Bias-Variance Decomposition

**Setup.** Let the true relationship be:

$$y = f(x) + \epsilon, \quad \text{with } E[\epsilon] = 0,\ \text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^2,\ \epsilon \perp \hat{f}(x)$$

The training set $\mathcal{D}$ is a random sample; therefore the learned predictor $\hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x)$ is a *random function* of $\mathcal{D}$. We seek to decompose the **expected test MSE at a fixed point $x$**:

$$E_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x)\right)^2\right] = \ ?$$

**Step 1 — Expand the square.**

$$E\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = E\left[y^2 - 2y\hat{f}(x) + \hat{f}(x)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $y = f(x) + \epsilon$.**

$$E\left[(f(x) + \epsilon)^2 - 2(f(x) + \epsilon)\hat{f}(x) + \hat{f}(x)^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Distribute and group terms.**

$$E\left[f(x)^2 + 2f(x)\epsilon + \epsilon^2 - 2f(x)\hat{f}(x) - 2\epsilon\hat{f}(x) + \hat{f}(x)^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply linearity of expectation term-by-term.**

Since $f(x)$ is deterministic (no expectation needed for it):

$$= f(x)^2 + 2f(x)\underbrace{E[\epsilon]}_{=\,0} + \underbrace{E[\epsilon^2]}_{=\,\sigma^2} - 2f(x)E[\hat{f}(x)] - 2\underbrace{E[\epsilon]}_{=\,0}E[\hat{f}(x)] + E[\hat{f}(x)^2]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using the zero-mean noise assumption.**

$$= f(x)^2 + \sigma^2 - 2f(x)E[\hat{f}(x)] + E[\hat{f}(x)^2]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the variance identity $E[Z^2] = \text{Var}(Z) + (E[Z])^2$ on $Z = \hat{f}(x)$.**

$$E[\hat{f}(x)^2] = \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \left(E[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back into the equation.**

$$= f(x)^2 + \sigma^2 - 2f(x)E[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \left(E[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2$$

**Step 8 — Recognize a perfect square.**

$$= \underbrace{\left(f(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Noise}}$$

**Final Result.**

$$\boxed{\,E_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] + \text{Var}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] + \sigma^2\,}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

Suppose at a fixed test point $x_0$, the true value is $f(x_0) = 5$, and we train 4 different models on 4 different bootstrap samples. The predictions are:

| Sample $i$ | Prediction $\hat{f}_i(x_0)$ | Residual $y_i - \hat{f}_i$ | Squared Residual |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 4.2 | 0.8 | 0.64 |
| 2 | 5.8 | $-0.8$ | 0.64 |
| 3 | 5.0 | 0.0 | 0.00 |
| 4 | 5.5 | $-0.5$ | 0.25 |

**Step A — Compute the mean prediction.**

$$E[\hat{f}(x_0)] = \frac{4.2 + 5.8 + 5.0 + 5.5}{4} = \frac{20.5}{4} = 5.125$$

**Step B — Compute the squared bias.**

$$\text{Bias}^2 = (E[\hat{f}(x_0)] - f(x_0))^2 = (5.125 - 5)^2 = (0.125)^2 = 0.015625$$

**Step C — Compute the variance across the 4 predictions.**

$$\text{Var} = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{4}(\hat{f}_i - 5.125)^2$$

Component-by-component:
- $(4.2 - 5.125)^2 = 0.855625$
- $(5.8 - 5.125)^2 = 0.455625$
- $(5.0 - 5.125)^2 = 0.015625$
- $(5.5 - 5.125)^2 = 0.140625$

$$\text{Sum} = 1.4675 \Rightarrow \text{Var} = 0.366875$$

**Step D — Assume the irreducible noise $\sigma^2 = 0.50$.**

**Step E — Total expected error.**

$$E[(y - \hat{f}(x_0))^2] = 0.015625 + 0.366875 + 0.500000 = 0.8825$$

> [!NOTE]
> The model above has *very low bias* (0.016) but *high variance* (0.367) — characteristic of an overparameterized model. A regularizer would shrink all predictions toward the mean, raising bias slightly but collapsing variance dramatically.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Effect of Regularization on the Tradeoff

Consider a polynomial regression of degree 10. The **ridge (L2) regularized loss** is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \mathbf{w}^\top \phi(x_i))^2 + \lambda \, \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w}$$

The closed-form solution is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{\lambda} = (X^\top X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}$$

- When $\lambda = 0$: $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ is the OLS estimate — **low bias, high variance** (overfitting).
- When $\lambda \to \infty$: $\hat{\mathbf{w}} \to \mathbf{0}$ — **high bias (underfitting), zero variance**.
- Optimal $\lambda^\*$ balances the two — found by **cross-validation**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of how model complexity influences error components** — the standard KTU visual aid for this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Model Complexity] --> B{Is Model Too Simple?}
    B -- Yes --> C[High Bias Region]
    C --> C1[Underfitting]
    C1 --> C2[Both Training and Test Error are HIGH]
    C2 --> D[Add Features / Increase Depth / Use Flexible Model]
    B -- No --> E{Is Model Too Complex?}
    E -- Yes --> F[High Variance Region]
    F --> F1[Overfitting]
    F1 --> F2[Training Error LOW but Test Error HIGH]
    F2 --> G[Apply Regularization: L1, L2, Dropout, Early Stop]
    G --> H[Cross-Validation to Find Sweet Spot]
    E -- No --> H
    D --> H
    H --> I[Minimum Total Error = Bias^2 + Variance + Noise]
    I --> J[Optimal Generalization Achieved]
```

### 4.2 The U-Curve Architecture of Total Error

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REGION1 [UNDERFITTING ZONE]
        A1[Simple Model: Linear Regression on Non-Linear Data]
        A2[Bias is HIGH]
        A3[Variance is LOW]
    end
    subgraph REGION2 [SWEET SPOT]
        B1[Optimal Complexity]
        B2[Total Error MINIMIZED]
        B3[Bias and Variance BALANCED]
    end
    subgraph REGION3 [OVERFITTING ZONE]
        C1[Complex Model: Deep Tree / High-Degree Polynomial]
        C2[Bias is LOW]
        C3[Variance is HIGH]
    end
    REGION1 --> REGION2
    REGION2 --> REGION3
```

### 4.3 Diagnostic & Remediation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Observe High Test Error] --> CHECK{High Training Error?}
    CHECK -- Yes --> UF[Diagnose: UNDERFITTING - High Bias]
    UF --> FIX1[Increase Model Complexity]
    UF --> FIX2[Add New Features / Feature Engineering]
    UF --> FIX3[Reduce Regularization Lambda]
    CHECK -- No --> OF[Diagnose: OVERFITTING - High Variance]
    OF --> FIX4[Collect More Training Data]
    OF --> FIX5[Apply Regularization L1 or L2]
    OF --> FIX6[Use Dropout in Neural Networks]
    OF --> FIX7[Use Ensemble Methods: Bagging]
    OF --> FIX8[Early Stopping During Training]
    FIX1 --> CV[Re-evaluate via K-Fold Cross Validation]
    FIX2 --> CV
    FIX3 --> CV
    FIX4 --> CV
    FIX5 --> CV
    FIX6 --> CV
    FIX7 --> CV
    FIX8 --> CV
    CV --> DONE[Optimal Model Deployed]
```

### 4.4 Bias-Variance Taxonomy Table (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Total Expected Test Error] --> L1[Bias Squared]
    L0 --> L2[Variance]
    L0 --> L3[Irreducible Noise Sigma Squared]
    L1 --> L1A[Model is too simple or has wrong assumptions]
    L1 --> L1B[Fix: Increase complexity or change model class]
    L2 --> L2A[Model is too sensitive to training data]
    L2 --> L2B[Fix: Regularize, get more data, or ensemble]
    L3 --> L3A[Inherent stochasticity in data]
    L3 --> L3B[Fix: Cannot be reduced - data collection limit]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
Define **bias** and **variance** in the context of a supervised learning algorithm. How are they related to underfitting and overfitting?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Bias** is the systematic error introduced by approximating a real-world problem with a simplified model. It is measured as the difference between the expected prediction of the model and the true value: $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = E[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$. **High bias causes underfitting** — both training and test errors are large.
- **Variance** is the amount by which the model's prediction would change if a different training dataset were used: $\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] = E[(\hat{f}(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)])^2]$. **High variance causes overfitting** — training error is small but test error is large.
- They are inversely related: as model complexity increases, bias decreases but variance increases.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
State the **bias-variance decomposition** of the expected prediction error. What is the role of the **irreducible noise term** $\sigma^2$ in this decomposition?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The expected squared test error at any point $x$ decomposes as:

$$E[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2 + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2$$

- The **bias squared** term is the systematic error of the average predictor.
- The **variance** term captures the instability of the predictor across different training sets.
- The **irreducible noise** $\sigma^2$ is the variance of the intrinsic random noise $\epsilon$ in the data-generation process $y = f(x) + \epsilon$. It represents a **hard lower bound** on the test error that no model can overcome, no matter how flexible, because the noise is part of the data and not the model.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **bias-variance decomposition** of the expected test mean-squared-error, starting from the assumption $y = f(x) + \epsilon$ with $E[\epsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **learning curves** and **validation curves** are used as practical diagnostic tools to identify whether a model suffers from high bias or high variance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The expected test MSE at a fixed input $x$ is:

$$E_{\mathcal{D}}\left[(y - \hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(x))^2\right]$$

**Step 1:** Expand the square:

$$= E[y^2 - 2y\hat{f}(x) + \hat{f}(x)^2]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $y = f(x) + \epsilon$:

$$= E[(f(x) + \epsilon)^2 - 2(f(x) + \epsilon)\hat{f}(x) + \hat{f}(x)^2]$$

**Step 3:** Use linearity of expectation and the fact that $E[\epsilon] = 0$, $E[\epsilon^2] = \sigma^2$, and $\epsilon$ is independent of $\hat{f}(x)$:

$$= f(x)^2 + \sigma^2 - 2f(x)E[\hat{f}(x)] + E[\hat{f}(x)^2]$$

**Step 4:** Apply the identity $E[Z^2] = \text{Var}(Z) + (E[Z])^2$:

$$= f(x)^2 - 2f(x)E[\hat{f}(x)] + (E[\hat{f}(x)])^2 + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^2$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the perfect square:

$$= \left(f(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2 + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^2$$

$$\boxed{= \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2}$$

**(b) Learning and Validation Curves (7 marks):**

A **learning curve** plots the **training error** and **validation error** as a function of the training set size $N$.

- **High-bias diagnosis:** Both training and validation errors are high and **converge** to a similar (high) plateau even as $N$ grows. Adding more data does *not* help. The remedy is to **increase model complexity** (more features, deeper model, fewer regularization).
- **High-variance diagnosis:** Training error is much lower than validation error, and a **large gap** persists between them. Adding more data *will* help close the gap. The remedy is to **decrease model complexity**, apply **regularization**, or use **bagging/ensembling**.

A **validation curve** plots the training and validation errors as a function of a **hyperparameter** (e.g., polynomial degree, regularization strength $\lambda$, tree depth).

- The point where the two curves are closest (and validation error is minimized) is the **optimal hyperparameter value**.
- If the curves are far apart, the model is overfitting → increase regularization or reduce complexity.
- If both curves are high and close together, the model is underfitting → increase complexity.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme, Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the **practical techniques** to manage the bias-variance tradeoff, with specific reference to **regularization (L1 and L2)**, **ensemble methods (bagging vs. boosting)**, and **early stopping**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **$k$-fold cross-validation** procedure and explain why it provides a more reliable estimate of the **expected test error** than a single train/test split. How does it connect to the bias-variance tradeoff? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Practical Techniques (7 marks):**

| Technique | Effect on Bias | Effect on Variance | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L2 Regularization (Ridge)** | Increases slightly | **Decreases substantially** | When model is overfitting |
| **L1 Regularization (Lasso)** | Increases slightly | **Decreases** + induces sparsity | When feature selection is needed |
| **Bagging (e.g., Random Forest)** | Roughly unchanged | **Decreases significantly** | High-variance base learners (deep trees) |
| **Boosting (e.g., AdaBoost, XGBoost)** | **Decreases significantly** | May increase if over-boosted | High-bias base learners (shallow trees) |
| **Early Stopping** | Slight increase | **Decreases** | Iterative learners (gradient descent on NNs) |
| **Dropout (NNs)** | Slight increase | **Decreases** | Deep neural networks |
| **More Training Data** | Unchanged | **Decreases (1/N rate)** | When variance is the bottleneck |

- **Regularization** adds a penalty term $\lambda \cdot \Omega(\mathbf{w})$ to the loss, shrinking weights and preventing the model from fitting noise.
- **Bagging** trains many models on bootstrap samples and averages them — averaging reduces variance quadratically while bias is roughly preserved.
- **Boosting** sequentially trains weak learners, each focusing on the previous one's errors — this systematically reduces bias.
- **Early stopping** halts training when validation error begins to rise, preventing the model from memorizing training data.

**(b) k-Fold Cross-Validation (7 marks):**

**Procedure:**
1. Shuffle the dataset randomly.
2. Split into $k$ equal-sized folds.
3. For $i = 1, \ldots, k$: train on the remaining $k-1$ folds and validate on fold $i$.
4. Average the $k$ validation scores to obtain the cross-validated estimate:

$$\text{CV}_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Score}_i$$

**Why it is better than a single train/test split:**

- A single split wastes a large portion of data for testing, and the resulting score has **high variance** — different splits give wildly different estimates.
- $k$-fold CV uses *every* data point for both training and validation, yielding a **lower-variance** estimate of the true expected test error.
- The estimate is also **less biased** because each fold is trained on $N(k-1)/k$ samples, close to the full $N$.

**Connection to bias-variance tradeoff:**

- A small $k$ (e.g., $k=2$, the holdout method) gives a low-bias but **high-variance** error estimate.
- A large $k$ (e.g., $k=N$, leave-one-out) gives a low-variance but **slightly high-bias** estimate (since each model is trained on $N-1$ samples, very close to the full data, but the models are highly correlated).
- **$k=5$ or $k=10$** is the practical sweet spot — a good empirical balance of bias and variance in the error estimate itself.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing the noise term:** Students often write the decomposition as $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}$ and *forget* the $\sigma^2$ noise term. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing bias of the estimator with bias of the prediction:** Bias is defined on the *predictor's expectation*, not on a single prediction. The formula $E[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$ must be squared, not absolute-valued.
> 3. **In derivation questions, students skip the linearity-of-expectation step.** Always show the substitution $E[\epsilon] = 0$ explicitly.
> 4. **Saying "regularization reduces both bias and variance"** is a common wrong answer. Regularization *increases* bias (slight underfitting) and *decreases* variance. Net effect on total error is the *only* thing that matters.
> 5. **Mixing up bagging and boosting:** Bagging reduces variance; boosting reduces bias. The examiner allocates marks for this distinction.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] The **expected test error** decomposes into $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- [x] **Bias** = systematic error from wrong model assumptions → causes **underfitting**.
- [x] **Variance** = error from sensitivity to training data → causes **overfitting**.
- [x] **Irreducible noise** $\sigma^2$ cannot be eliminated by any model.
- [x] As **model complexity $\uparrow$**: bias $\downarrow$, variance $\uparrow$ (the *tradeoff*).
- [x] As **training set size $N \uparrow$**: variance $\downarrow$, bias roughly unchanged.
- [x] **Regularization** (L1/L2/Dropout) trades a small increase in bias for a large decrease in variance.
- [x] **Bagging** primarily reduces variance; **Boosting** primarily reduces bias.
- [x] **Learning curves** diagnose the problem: convergent high errors = bias; large train-validation gap = variance.
- [x] **k-Fold Cross-Validation** (typically $k=5$ or $k=10$) is the standard tool for *estimating* the expected test error with low bias and low variance.
- [x] **Early stopping** is a regularization technique that controls the effective complexity of iterative models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line KTU Viva Answer:** *"Bias is what you get from being wrong on average; variance is what you get from being unstable; noise is what you cannot fix no matter what."*
